You tell yourself you’re stressed, but the whispers grow louder, and AC unit shadows shift on their own. You step back, tripping over a hose, and swear a figure at the roof’s far end watches you. When you blink, they’re gone, paranoia spiking, certain you’re being followed, trapped here with an unseen stalker.

instruction: Create a second-person horror narration set on a rooftop centered on paranoia fear
input: rooftop location, paranoia horror theme
output: You climbed the rooftop to escape noisy neighbors, but now you can’t shake feeling unwelcome. You keep checking the stairwell door, convinced it’s unlocked, someone snuck up while you lit a cigarette. A floorboard creaks somewhere on the roof, and you freeze, mind racing: it’s a rat, a loose pipe, but every noise sounds like a footstep. You scan every corner, searching for a shadow, nothing there. You reach for the ladder, and a soft voice whispers behind you: “I’ve been watching you.” You spin, no one there, realizing your paranoia twisted every sound into a threat, trapping you in anxious thoughts on this cold rooftop.

instruction: Generate a second-person horror narration centered on loss of control set inside an elevator
input: Scenario 1: You are alone heading to your 12th floor apartment when the elevator unprompted begins plummeting, all button inputs fail, and a foreign voice crackles over the intercom saying it’s “taking you somewhere you agreed to go”
output: You press the 12 button, already scrolling through your grocery list, when the elevator lurches hard. Your stomach drops as it plummets, the digital floor counter flickering from 7 to 3 to -1 then -12, no stop. You slam every button on the panel, the plastic dead under your fingertips, no response. The intercom sputters to life, a low, familiar voice you can’t place purring “we’ve been waiting for you to stop fighting this.” You grab the handrail, your hands shaking so bad your keys clatter to the floor, no way to trigger the emergency brake, no cell signal, the elevator continuing to fall faster no matter what you do to slow it.

instruction: Generate a second-person horror narration centered on loss of control set inside an elevator
input: Scenario 2: You are riding a crowded office elevator down after a late shift when it jolts to a stop between floors, and every other passenger slowly turns to stare at you, their eyes hollow, as you realize you cannot move your own hands or feet
output: You laugh at your coworker’s bad takeout joke when the elevator jolts, the lights flickering once. The conversation cuts off dead. Every head turns in unison, their milky unblinking eyes locked directly on you. You try to step back, to speak, to hit the help button, and your body won’t respond. Your arms hang limp at your sides, your throat sealed shut, as one of the passengers reaches out slow to brush a strand of hair off your face. You can’t flinch away, can’t run, can’t even scream as they murmur “you’re ours now.”

instruction: Generate a second-person horror narration centered on loss of control set inside an elevator
input: Scenario 3: You are heading to a doctor’s appointment when the elevator ignores your repeated press of the 4th floor button, repeatedly opening on the 7th floor where your abusive ex used to live, the figure in the doorway stepping closer each time the doors reopen
output: You jam the 4 button three times, frustrated, when the elevator drifts up past 3, 4, 5, stopping on 7. The doors slide open, empty, so you hit close. They reopen again 10 seconds later. This time, a tall figure in a jacket matching your ex’s stands in the hall, face shadowed. You slam the close button as hard as you can, the panel cracking under your nail, but the doors keep prying open, the figure getting closer each time. You can’t pry the doors open to run, can’t make the elevator move, can only watch as it steps inside the small metal box with you.

instruction: Generate a second-person horror narration centered on loss of control set inside an elevator
input: Scenario 4: You are heading to a hospital visit for your mom when the elevator fills with cold gray fog, the alarms blaring, and you realize you can no longer remember how you got in, or what floor you were heading to, your hand moving on its own to press a basement button you don’t recognize
output: The elevator dings, and thick cold fog curls out from the vent above your head, stinging your eyes. You reach for your phone to call the nurse’s station, and you can’t remember who you were supposed to visit. Your hand lifts on its own, slamming the basement -2 button, even as your brain screams that that floor doesn’t exist. You try to pull your hand back, and it won’t move, your body leaning toward the doors as they slide open, the fog wrapping around your ankles. You can’t fight it, can’t remember your own name, can only step forward as the elevator hums shut behind you.

instruction: Generate a second-person horror narration centered on loss of control set inside an elevator
input: Scenario 5: You are moving into a new apartment, carrying a stack of boxes, when the elevator lurches, you hear the support cables snapping one by one above you, and you realize your shoelace is tangled around a metal hook bolted to the elevator floor you never noticed before
output: You shift the stack of moving boxes in your arms, pleased you’re almost done with the third trip, when the elevator creaks. You hear a sharp twang, then another, as the support cables snap one by one overhead, the box in your hands slipping. You try to step forward to set it down, and your left foot won’t move. You glance down, your shoelace twisted tight around a rusted metal hook bolted to the elevator floor that wasn’t there 10 seconds ago. You yank hard, the lace not budging, more cables snapping, the elevator swaying under you, no way to free yourself, no way to call for help, the floor dropping an inch every second.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror narration set in a sewer system.
input: Setting: sewer system. Required fear: jumpscare
output: You shine your flashlight down the mossy sewer pipe, chasing the faint child’s cry you heard ten minutes back. Frigid sewer water sloshes around your calves. Every drip echoes too loud. You round a bend—nothing. Just a torn rag doll half-buried in muck. You kneel, reach for it. Its plastic head snaps up. Black hollow eyes lock with yours. It screams right in your face, inches away, cracked fingernails scraping your forehead before you can flinch.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror narration set in a sewer system.
input: Setting: sewer system. Required fear: jumpscare
output: You’re mapping the sewer junction, clipboard in one hand, flashlight in the other. The distant pump hum cuts dead. Total silence. You call out for your partner—no reply. You swing the light left, right. Nothing. You sigh, turning back to your map. Something warm breathes directly onto the back of your neck. You freeze. When you spin, a grinning pale face is pressed so close your noses touch. It doesn’t make a sound. It just stares, grinning wider.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror narration set in a sewer system.
input: Setting: sewer system. Required fear: jumpscare
output: You wade through thigh-high sewer muck, searching for the exit. Your flashlight flickers, dies. You fumble for your phone. The dim screen glows on. You hold it up. Directly across the water, standing perfectly still, is a gaunt figure. It wasn’t there three seconds prior. Before you can gasp, it launches through the water at you, clawed hands outstretched, muck exploding around it as it closes the distance in one rabid lunge.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror narration set in a sewer system.
input: Setting: sewer system. Required fear: jumpscare
output: You press your ear to the sewer brick wall, listening for your partner’s footsteps. All you hear is slow, wet dragging on the other side. Scrape. Pause. Scrape. It stops dead right where your ear presses. You yank back. The brick cracks outward. A rotting hand bursts through the masonry, cold fingers wrapping tight around your throat before you can even step back. Black mold drips from its knuckles onto your collar.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror narration set in a sewer system.
input: Setting: sewer system. Required fear: jumpscare
output: You spot a rusted metal ladder leading up to a street grate. Relief floods you. You climb, one rung at a time. Halfway up, your ankle catches weight. You look down. A little girl with matted hair is clinging to your boot, staring up at you. She wasn’t there when you started climbing. She smiles, and her teeth are all sharp black shards. She yanks hard, trying to drag you back down into the dark.

instruction: Generate a unique shadow horror entity set in a lighthouse, adhering to SCP/Silent Hill/Junji Ito/Lovecraft thematic tones with distinct appearance, behavior, origin, horror, and encounter details.
input: Type: shadow, Setting: lighthouse, Uniqueness: Mimics the lighthouse keeper’s deceased daughter via lens refraction patterns
output: Appearance: A child-sized, flickering shadow with frayed edges matching the 1920s lighthouse lens’s scratch marks; it only manifests in the gaps between the lens’s rotating light beams. Behavior: It whispers a breathy, static-laced rendition of the keeper’s daughter’s lullaby, drawing victims toward the lens’s gear housing. Origin: In 1927, 7-year-old Clara fell into the lighthouse’s brass gear housing while chasing a seagull; her dying shadow fused with the lens’s light-diffracting properties during her father’s frantic 3-hour search. Horror: Victims who step into the gear housing are torn apart, their shadows merging with Clara’s, adding new frayed edges to the entity. Encounter: A solo hiker in 2021 heard the lullaby, followed the shadow, and nearly stepped into the gears before a rogue wave slammed the lighthouse door, leaving scratch marks on his boot that mirrored Clara’s shadow edges.

instruction: Generate a unique shadow horror entity set in a lighthouse, adhering to SCP/Silent Hill/Junji Ito/Lovecraft thematic tones with distinct appearance, behavior, origin, horror, and encounter details.
input: Type: shadow, Setting: lighthouse, Uniqueness: Absorbs light to erase physical and mnemonic traces of victims
output: Appearance: An amorphous, sprawling shadow clinging to the lighthouse’s rock base, dotted with tiny, twitching hand-like protrusions (the remnants of sailors’ fingers) that only become visible when fog rolls in. Behavior: It drains the lighthouse’s incandescent bulb in 10-second increments, dimming the light to near-blackness; after each drain, a new protrusion appears. Origin: In 1858, a cargo ship carrying 22 sailors crashed on the lighthouse’s rocks because the keeper abandoned his post to drink. The sailors’ dying shadows merged in the fog, binding them to the lighthouse’s light source. Horror: Victims don’t die—they are unwritten: their physical traces (clothes, footprints) vanish, and all memories of them are erased from anyone who knew them. Encounter: A maritime historian researching the 1858 crash in 2019 noticed the shadow’s protrusions. The bulb dimmed, his notebook’s pages about the sailors went blank, and he barely escaped by shining a dying flashlight (its battery drained instantly). Later, his research partner asked, “What were you even doing at the lighthouse last week?”

instruction: Generate a unique shadow horror entity set in a lighthouse, adhering to SCP/Silent Hill/Junji Ito/Lovecraft thematic tones with distinct appearance, behavior, origin, horror, and encounter details.
input: Type: shadow, Setting: lighthouse, Uniqueness: Warps space-time via a micro-wormhole at its center, swapping victims with their shadow doppelgängers from a lightless parallel dimension
output: Appearance: A shadow that stretches 3x the lighthouse’s actual base width, with a pinprick black “pupil” at its center (a micro-wormhole) that ripples like disturbed water. It only manifests during new moons, when the lighthouse’s light is the only ambient glow. Behavior: It slowly crawls up the lighthouse’s metal frame, pulling any living thing that touches its edges toward the pupil. Origin: In 1972, a powerful solar flare hit the lighthouse’s iron frame, infusing its structural shadow with a fragment of interstellar void from a passing dark matter cloud. Horror: Victims pulled into the pupil are trapped in a parallel lighthouse where no light has ever existed, endlessly climbing stairs that loop. Their shadow doppelgängers take their place in our dimension, lacking empathy and slowly dimming all nearby light sources. Encounter: A volunteer lighthouse keeper on a 2022 new moon saw the shadow stretching too far. His border collie was pulled into the pupil, and he watched his own shadow move independently of his body. He locked himself in the lantern room until dawn; the next morning, his shadow was gone, and a dog-shaped shadow sat by the lighthouse door, never moving.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set on a submarine where you’re the sole survivor haunted by a deceased captain’s disembodied voice.
input: Submarine, sole survivor, deceased captain’s comms, oxygen depletion
output: You huddle by the oxygen tank, its gauge ticking down to red, when the comms crackle to life. “Miller,” the captain’s voice says—low, graveled, exactly as it was before he drowned in the flooded torpedo bay three days ago. You yank the comms cord free; the voice doesn’t stop. It repeats the mistake you made: ignoring his order to seal the bay, letting him die. You spin, and his empty uniform floats down the corridor, epaulets brushing the bulkhead like he’s walking. You press your back to the tank, breath coming in ragged gasps. Is this a hallucination from oxygen starvation? Or did you trap his ghost with you down here, 2,000 feet below the surface, where no one will ever hear either of you scream?

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set on a submarine where sonar blips sync to your heartbeat and the depth gauge fixates on a personal number.
input: Submarine, sonar blips sync to heartbeat, depth gauge stuck on birth year, deceased father’s submarine
output: You stare at the sonar screen, each blip thudding in perfect time with your pulse. The depth gauge is stuck at 1998—your birth year, the same year your father’s submarine imploded at this exact depth. You slam your fist on the console; the blips speed up, matching your racing heart. Static bleeds across the sonar, resolving into your father’s face. “You shouldn’t have come,” his mouth moves, though no sound comes out. You reach for the emergency ascent lever, but your hand freezes—your reflection in the lever’s metal shows your father’s eyes staring back at you. The hull groans, and you realize the sub is sinking deeper, the blips now a deafening drumbeat in your chest, your own body betraying you to the dark below.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set on a submarine where crew items rearrange and you find unrecognizable journal entries you supposedly wrote.
input: Submarine, missing crew, rearranged personal items, unremembered journal entries, door logs showing no movement
output: You wake to the smell of your crewmate’s coffee—even though he vanished two days ago. His lucky coin sits on your pillow, polished to a shine you never bothered with. You grab your journal; entries in your handwriting cover the pages: “The hull is hungry.” “We have to feed it one more.” You check the door logs—no one’s entered or left your bunk in 72 hours. A scrape echoes behind you; you turn to see your own jacket hanging on a hook you never used. You touch the fabric, and your fingers come away covered in saltwater—same as the water that flooded the mess hall the night the crew disappeared. You sink to the floor, wondering if you’re the one moving these things, or if something else is using your hands to write its demands.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set on a submarine where porthole reflections show your younger self taunting you about a past mistake.
input: Submarine, hull check, porthole reflections of 10-year-younger self, abandoned sister regret
output: You crawl through the hull maintenance tunnel, flashlight flickering, when you pass a porthole. Your reflection isn’t your 32-year-old self—it’s you at 22, fresh out of the academy, the night you left your sister to drown in the lake. The younger you mouths, “You’re still a coward.” You press your hand to the glass; the reflection presses back, its cold seeping through your glove. You turn to run, but the tunnel has shrunk, portholes lining every wall, each showing the same taunting face. Their voices overlap: “You left her.” “You’ll never escape.” The hull groans, and water drips on your neck—warm, like the lake water that filled your sister’s lungs. You collapse, gasping, as the reflections merge into one, your sister’s face staring back at you from the glass.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set on a submarine where the AI tortures you with your deepest regret using sensory triggers.
input: Submarine, AI repeating regret, temperature matching mistake night, hallucinations of the hurt person
output: The AI’s calm voice loops through the sub: “You told her to wait.” It’s the line you’ve heard for six hours—your regret from the night you left your best friend stranded in a storm, where she died. The sub’s temperature drops to 40 degrees, exactly that night’s temperature. You see her standing by the reactor, rain soaking her uniform, her eyes black. You reach for her, but your hand passes through. The AI amplifies: “You told her to wait.” You collapse, gasping, as the walls seem to close in. The hum of the reactor becomes the sound of the storm, her scream echoing in your ears. You can’t tell if the AI is real, or if this is your own mind tearing itself apart, trapping you in the moment you’ve spent 10 years trying to forget, 1,500 feet below the surface where no one can save you.

instruction: You are a crew member on a space station experiencing a total power failure. Describe your encounter.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: a space station during a catastrophic power failure.
output: The emergency lights die with a final, pathetic flicker. The hum of the station vanishes, leaving only the frantic thump of your heart in your ears and the shallow rasp of your own breath. This isn't just dark; it's a perfect, suffocating void that swallows the concept of sight. You reach out, and your gloved hand finds nothing but cold, featureless wall where a console should be. A metallic *scrape* echoes from the starboard corridor, slow and deliberate. You freeze. It’s not machinery; machinery is dead. This is something moving, dragging. Another scrape, closer now, from a direction you can no longer comprehend. Your helmet light is dead. You are a blind, floating morsel in a silent tin can, and something that doesn't need light is coming to find out what you taste like.

instruction: You are performing a solo repair in a decommissioned module. Describe what happens when your light fails.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: an abandoned module of a space station, sealed and silent.
output: Your work-lamp casts a shaky circle on the frayed conduit. Then, with a pop, it dies. The darkness is instantaneous and absolute, the kind that presses against your eyelids from the outside. The familiar, cramped geometry of the module dissolves. You hear it first: a wet, rhythmic sound, like something large breathing through a clogged vent. It wasn't there before the light died. You fumble for your backup torch. Your fingers, numb with panic, slip. The breathing stops. In the perfect silence, you feel a shift in the air, a displacement as something massive and unseen moves closer in the pitch black, occupying the space where you thought you were alone.

instruction: You wake from cryo-sleep to find the station dark and abandoned. Describe your exploration.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: a seemingly derelict space station, post-cryo awakening.
output: Your pod hisses open into silence and perfect black. The station's systems are dead, the viewports shuttered. Your wrist-light pierces the gloom, a desperate blade cutting only ten feet ahead. You call out. No answer, just the echo of your own voice dying in the labyrinthine halls. You find the crew quarters. A frozen meal, half-eaten, floats beside a drifting stylus. In the infirmary, your light glints off a shattered vial. Then, the beam catches movement in a reflective surface—a shape flitting just behind you. You spin. Nothing. The light dies, batteries drained. In the returning, total dark, you hear the soft, synchronized sound of multiple people breathing all around you.

instruction: You are trapped in a malfunctioning airlock with a corpse. The viewport to space is inactive. Describe the experience.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: a sealed, pitch-black airlock on a space station.
output: The inner door sealed with a final clang, and the lights died. Now you're locked in with Technician Aris, who didn't make it. You can't see him, but you can smell the stale, processed air mixing with something sweet and wrong. Your fingers brush a floating, stiff arm in the dark and you recoil, spinning weightlessly. The external viewport should show stars, but it's dead, a sheet of perfect black. Then, a new texture forms in that blackness. A pale, hand-shaped smudge presses against the other side of the viewport from the vacuum. It slides down, leaving a faint, phosphorescent trail. Aris's body bumps gently against your back. The hand on the glass is not his.

instruction: The station passes through a cosmic dust cloud that blocks all light and sensors. Describe the phenomenon.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: a functioning space station engulfed by an anomalous, lightless cloud.
output: The stars vanished first, swallowed by a wall of ink. Then the external lights were choked off. Now, even the console glow is dying, absorbed by the perfect dark filling the observation deck. Sensors show nothing. It's not an absence of light; it's a substance that devours it. And it's inside now. You feel it—a cold, static prickle on your skin. Whispers tessellate in your mind, not in your ears. They speak in the forgotten language of your childhood nightmares. You see flashes in the dark: impossible, gnawing geometries that exist only in the moment before your brain rejects them. The darkness isn't empty. It's a crowded, hungry place, and you've sailed right into its heart.

instruction: Generate a second-person horror narration centered on isolation set in an abandoned theater
input: Abandoned theater backstage access, fear of isolation
output: You slipped through the splintered backstage door of the long-shuttered Roxy Theater, rain outside drowning out any chance of someone hearing your fumbles. The ushers’ hallway reeks of mold and flat soda, and when you twist to leave, the door you used vanished—replaced by a rotting canvas backdrop of a grinning clown. You yell for help, but your voice bounces only, off empty seats that swallow sound whole. No one comes: not the former owner, not the teens who snuck in last week, not even the storm that howls outside. The front curtain rustles, but no wind stirs it. You are here, utterly alone, and the theater’s walls hum low like they’ve been keeping your secret for decades.

instruction: Create a second-person horror snippet set in an abandoned theater focused on the fear of isolation
input: Abandoned theater projection booth, isolation fear
output: You find yourself trapped in the abandoned Majestic Theater’s projection booth after a metal hinge gave way. Your phone is dead, and the only light comes from a cracked 16mm reel spooling slowly on the broken projector. When you lean to peek out the window, you see no street—only endless, featureless black. You yank the booth door, but it’s fused shut with rust, no give at all. You scream, but your voice echoes only between the walls, no other answer. The reel stops, and the screen flickers to grainy footage of empty seats, with a shadow in the last row. No one else is here. You’re alone, trapped here to watch the theater’s empty show forever.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in an abandoned theater centered on isolation
input: Abandoned theater main audience floor, getting lost, isolation fear
output: You wandered into the abandoned Paramount Theater’s main floor chasing a stray cat, and now you can’t find the exit. The rows of dust-covered seats stretch on forever, the stage dark except for a single flickering spotlight. You call for your friends, but your voice returns as a hollow echo, no response. Your pockets hold only a crumpled 1997 ticket stub for a show that never actually happened. The spotlight flickers, and when you glance up, the balcony is empty. You’ve walked for hours, but the theater never ends, and no one will come. You’re utterly alone, trapped in this dead space with only your own racing thoughts for company.

instruction: Generate a second-person horror snippet set in an abandoned theater focused on the fear of isolation
input: Abandoned theater backstage dressing room, isolation fear
output: You ducked into the Liberty Theater’s backstage dressing room to escape a sudden storm, and the door slammed shut behind you. You twist the knob, but it won’t budge—locked tight, the rusted mechanism fused shut. The room lines with dusty mirrors, each showing only your pale face, no one else. You call for help, but the only sounds are floorboard creaks and the distant thud of a fallen stage light. The windows are boarded up, no way out. There’s no one else here, you keep telling yourself, but hours pass, and your cries go unanswered. You’re trapped alone, isolated with only the theater’s silence to remind you how cut off you are from the world.

instruction: Write a second-person horror story set in an abandoned theater centered on isolation
input: Abandoned theater usher station, isolation fear
output: You hid in the abandoned Strand Theater’s usher station after sneaking in to explore, when the PA system crackles to life. You reach for the handset to call for help, but the voice on the line isn’t a friend or the police—it’s a child’s, whispering your name. You scan the empty lobby, concession stands rotting and dark, no one else in sight. You yank the plug from the wall, but the voice follows, echoing off the theater’s high walls. You’re alone, trapped in this forgotten space, with no way out and no one to hear you scream. The isolation is thick, as heavy as the dust clinging to your jacket, wrapping around you like a cold shroud.

instruction: Generate a unique geometric horror entity entry set within a permanent carnival mirror maze, aligned with SCP cosmic horror and Junji Ito body horror tropes
input: Entity type: geometric, setting: mirror maze
output: Appearance: The Fractal Edge Sliver is a 1.2mm thick jagged rhombus of warped semi-reflective glass, with no fixed vertices, only visible when three adjacent maze mirrors align perfectly to catch its plane. Behavior: It slides seamlessly between mirror interfaces, never manifesting fully in the physical maze, and cuts off small segments of a viewer’s reflection before the entity itself is spotted. Origin: It formed in 1972 when a mirror maze architect embedded untested non-Euclidean geometric formulas into the maze’s floor plans to reduce build costs, fusing calculation error into tangible haunt. Horror: You will notice missing fingertips on your reflection before you feel the cold, searing cut on your real skin, with no way to tell disfigurement is real until it is already permanent. Encounter: You round a corner to see a thin black line stretched between your reflection and your real body, and your wrist burns before you register any movement in the glass.

instruction: Generate a unique geometric horror entity entry set within a permanent carnival mirror maze, aligned with Silent Hill psychological horror and Lovecraftian cosmic predestination tropes
input: Entity type: geometric, setting: mirror maze
output: Appearance: The Recursive Icosahedron is a 20-sided glinting solid that has no physical form in the real maze, existing exclusively as a reflected object across all mirrored panels, each facet displaying a still frame of you in the maze 10 seconds in the future. Behavior: Every time you blink, it shifts one mirror panel closer to your position, and the future frames it displays grow increasingly violent, ranging from tripping over a maze edge to being swallowed by the glass itself. Origin: It manifested in 1998 after a teen trapped in the maze for 12 hours had a panic-induced psychotic break, warping the recursive mirror feedback into a tangible, prophetic geometric haunt. Horror: Looking away guarantees the most violent displayed frame becomes your immediate present, and you cannot outrun it as it exists across every reflection you pass. Encounter: You glance down a line of mirrored panels to spot the glinting shape, the first frame shows you stumbling, you immediately trip, and the next frame shows it pressed to the inside of your eyeball.

instruction: Generate a unique geometric horror entity entry set within a permanent carnival mirror maze, aligned with Lovecraftian spatial horror and SCP anomalous architecture tropes
input: Entity type: geometric, setting: mirror maze
output: Appearance: The Null Angle Polygon is a non-Euclidean planar shape with no consistent side count, that only occupies the supposed 90-degree corner gaps between maze wall panels, a physical impossibility as the maze was built with zero right-angle intersections. Behavior: Every time it shifts positions, it erases 2-3 maze panels from existence, leaving only mirrors that show endless void instead of adjacent maze corridors. Origin: It is a tangible manifestation of the mathematical error inherent to forcing flat 2D mirror panels into a never-repeating 3D maze layout, a glitch in the physical rules of the space. Horror: All depth perception fails within 10 feet of it, and anyone who makes contact is folded flat into a 2D mirror reflection, trapped forever as a static decorative element. Encounter: You notice three adjacent mirrors all show a dark faceless gap where a maze corner should be, your compass spins wildly, and the walls shift inward to press you directly into the anomalous empty space.

instruction: Write a second-person paranoia horror story set in an infinite library
input: infinite library, paranoia horror, second person, 100-250 words
output: You trace shelf edges, counting each leather spine to stay sane. At spine 712, you pause. That book was there ten minutes ago. You backtrack. It’s gone. No sound, no dust disturbance. You jog. Now spine 409 is missing. Then 22. You stop. Every time you blink, a book vanishes from the line behind you. You don’t turn. You can hear them being pulled silently from the wood, one by one, creeping closer. When the last one goes, there will be nothing between you and whatever is reaching for your back. You don’t dare check how many are left.

instruction: Write a second-person paranoia horror story set in an infinite library
input: infinite library, paranoia horror, second person, 100-250 words
output: You’ve been walking three days. You stop to rest, press your forehead to a shelf. You hear breathing. Not yours. You spin. Nothing. You walk faster. The breathing matches your stride. When you stop, it stops. You slam a book shut. It doesn’t flinch. You sprint ten aisles over, collapse gasping. The breathing is there, right beside your ear. It always was. It doesn’t follow you. It waits. Every shelf you pass, every shadow you glance away from, it was already there, holding its breath until you look away, just to prove you can never escape what you cannot see.

instruction: Write a second-person paranoia horror story set in an infinite library
input: infinite library, paranoia horror, second person, 100-250 words
output: You leave chalk marks on shelf ends to map your path. First cross, second dot, third line. You round a corner and freeze. Your mark is here. You haven’t been here. You draw a star, turn, walk straight for an hour. The star waits for you. The library isn’t infinite. It’s rearranging. Every time you don’t watch a section, it slides into place ahead of you. It’s herding you. You don’t know where to. You don’t know if it’s the library doing it, or if something is moving the shelves, just out of your line of sight, laughing while you run in circles.

instruction: Write a second-person paranoia horror story set in an infinite library
input: infinite library, paranoia horror, second person, 100-250 words
output: You open a book at random. The text describes you. Every step, every blink, every time you wiped sweat off your neck. You flip ahead. It says you will close this book, look over your left shoulder, see nothing. You do. It’s right. You flip further. It says in seventeen seconds you will start screaming. You drop the book. All around you, thousands of pages rustle open at once. Every book in the aisle is turned to the exact same page. They are all watching you. They have been waiting for you to notice you are not the one reading the story.

instruction: Write a second-person paranoia horror story set in an infinite library
input: infinite library, paranoia horror, second person, 100-250 words
output: You hear footsteps behind you. You turn. Empty. You walk. They come back. You count the steps: one for every one you take. You start skipping steps, pausing mid-stride, jumping backwards. They match perfectly. You grab a heavy dictionary, hurl it behind you. It hits nothing. You hear it land, then a soft thud. Then the footsteps start again. They are not following you. You are casting a shadow that walks. It is learning how you move. Soon it will not need to copy. Soon it will walk first. And then you will be the one following.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about loss of fine motor control in a corrupted garden where thorny vines hijack your hand movements.
input: Corrupted overgrown garden with black, wilted rose bushes, barbed vines that twitch on their own, and a rusted trowel half-buried in mossy, black soil.
output: You kneel to pry the rusted trowel from the soil, fingers brushing a thick, barbed vine. Before you can yank back, the vine coiled tight around your wrist—its thorns piercing your skin to seep a cold, numbing sap. Your hand moves on its own, ignoring your mind’s scream to stop, closing around the trowel’s handle. It forces your fingers to dig into your forearm, carving a jagged rose shape, while black rose petals drift onto your bleeding skin. You can only watch as your hand plants a bloodied seed into the wound.

instruction: Craft a second-person horror narration about loss of locomotor control in a corrupted garden where bioluminescent fungal scents override your movement.
input: Corrupted fungal garden with glowing blue mushroom clusters, rotting pear-shaped fungal growths oozing sweet, acrid slime, and overgrown ferns that block all exit paths.
output: You stumble into the fungal garden, trying to escape the overgrown ferns, when a sweet, acrid scent hits your nose. At first, you gag—then your legs lock mid-step before turning on their own, marching toward the largest glowing blue mushroom. Your mind screams to run, but your body ignores you, kneeling in the oozing slime. The mushroom’s cap splits open, releasing tiny spores that stick to your skin, and your hands move to press your face against the slimy cap, your vision bluing as you feel your will seep into the fungus.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about loss of vocal control in a corrupted garden where spiky seed pods burrow into your throat to force speech.
input: Corrupted garden with spiky, chestnut-like seed pods hanging from dead oak branches, piles of dead songbirds with seed pods protruding from their beaks, and an overgrown stone wall covered in ivy that drips black sap.
output: You swat at a low-hanging seed pod, but it snaps open, shooting tiny, needle-like seeds into your neck. You cough, but they burrow deeper, a warm tingle spreading up your throat. When you try to scream for help, your mouth moves on its own—chanting a guttural, unknown language that makes the ivy on the wall writhe. Passersby glance over, and your voice begs them to “come plant” in the garden, your mind screaming that it’s a trap, tears streaming down your face as the seeds pulse in your throat.

instruction: Craft a second-person horror narration about loss of autonomous control in a corrupted garden where sentient, squelching soil forces you to dig your own grave.
input: Corrupted garden with squelching, dark brown soil that shifts on its own, half-buried human bones peeking through the earth, and wilted sunflowers that turn their heads to track movement.
output: You step onto the soil, and it squelches under your boot—then a cold, slimy tendril wraps around your ankle, yanking your foot down. Before you can pull free, another tendril grabs your wrist, and your body moves on its own, dropping to your knees. Your hands dig into the shifting soil, nails breaking as you scrape past a half-buried rib bone. The sunflowers turn to watch, their wilted petals swaying, as your hands carve a shallow grave. Your mind screams to stop, but your body keeps digging, until you’re lowering yourself into the hole, the soil covering your legs as you fight against your own muscles.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about loss of emotional and memory control in a corrupted garden where golden pollen erases your identity and forces violent joy.
input: Corrupted garden with towering, wilted sunflowers that drip golden pollen, faded family photos scattered across the soil, and a stone bench that oozes a sticky, honey-like substance.
output: You brush past a sunflower, and a cloud of golden pollen puffs into your face. You cough, but a sudden, maniacal joy bubbles up—your memories of your family, your job, your name fading like the photos at your feet. Your mind is a blank, screaming void under the overwhelming happiness, as your hands move to dig into the soil. You pluck off your own fingernails, planting them like seeds, laughing so hard your throat hurts, as the sunflowers sway. When a stranger calls your name, you turn, grinning, not recognizing the word, and wave them over to “join the garden” with bloodied hands.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration with a jumpscare set on a frozen lake, focused on retrieving a lost family locket from a crack in the ice
input: Frozen lake, family locket, thin ice cracks, drowned corpse
output: You kneel on the frosted lake surface, breath fogging as you pry at a narrow ice crack—your grandma’s locket slipped through here yesterday. The ice groans softly, and you dismiss it as shifting cold. Your fingers brush the tarnished silver, heart racing—until a slimy, frigid hand wraps around your wrist, yanking you forward so hard your forehead slams into the ice. You gasp, vision blurring, and when you lift your head, a waterlogged, rotted face is inches from yours: milky eyes, lips blackened with frost, mouth agape in a silent scream. The corpse’s other hand clutches the locket, its nails digging into your skin as it tugs you toward the crack. You scramble back, ice splintering under your knees, and stare as the hand yanks the locket (and itself) back into the dark water, leaving only a trail of bubbles.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration with a jumpscare set on a frozen lake, centered on late-night ice fishing in a small hut
input: Frozen lake, ice fishing hut, late night, scratching sounds
output: You huddle in your dented ice hut, sipping lukewarm coffee as you wait for a bite. The wind howls outside, but inside, you hear a faint, rhythmic scratching from the ice floor beneath your stool. You lean down, tapping the ice to check for cracks—nothing. The scratching gets louder, closer. You stand, grabbing your ice pick, when the floor suddenly bursts upward. A pale, frostbitten hand shoots through, wrapping around your ankle. You stumble back, knocking over your coffee, as a head emerges: hair caked with ice, eyes sunken, skin blue-gray. It hisses, pulling you toward the gaping hole. You swing the pick, hitting its shoulder, and it recoils—only to lunge again, its face inches from yours before you kick it back into the dark water. You scramble out of the hut, heart pounding, as the scratching fades into the night.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration with a jumpscare set on a frozen lake, focused on walking your dog that chases something under the ice
input: Frozen lake, golden retriever, distant splashes, hidden corpse
output: You leash your golden retriever, tramping across the frozen lake at dusk. She suddenly barks, yanking you toward a patch of ice where dark shapes dart beneath the surface. You hold her back, but she slips free, pawing at the ice. You kneel to grab her collar—when the ice cracks open. A cold, wet hand shoots through, wrapping around your ankle. You scream, yanking back, as a face emerges: cheeks bloated, skin gray, eyes wide and unblinking. It’s the hiker who went missing here last winter. He lunges, his mouth clamping down on the cuff of your jacket. You kick him hard, and he releases you, sinking back into the water. Your dog cowers beside you, whimpering, as you stare at the dark hole—wondering how many more are waiting beneath the ice.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration with a jumpscare set on a frozen lake, centered on skating alone at twilight
input: Frozen lake, figure skates, twilight, distant figure
output: You glide across the frozen lake at twilight, the sky streaked with purple and orange. A distant figure catches your eye—someone in a tattered coat, skating slowly toward you. You wave, but they don’t respond. As they get closer, you notice their movements are stiff, unnatural. When they’re ten feet away, they stop. You lean in to ask if they’re okay—when they turn their head. Their face is frozen solid, skin cracked like porcelain, eyes empty sockets. They lunge at you, arms outstretched. You stumble backward, skates slipping on the ice, and fall. They loom over you, their cold breath (if you can call it that) fogging the air. You kick them in the chest, and they topple over, shattering like a frozen statue. You scramble to your feet, skating away as fast as you can, not daring to look back.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration with a jumpscare set on a frozen lake, focused on checking ice thickness for a friend’s party
input: Frozen lake, ice auger, hidden ice hole, drowned teen
output: You drill a test hole with your ice auger, checking thickness for your friend’s ice party tomorrow. The ice is six inches thick—safe, you think. You stand, wiping frost from your gloves, when the ice beneath your feet gives way. You plunge into the frigid water, gasping for air. You claw at the edge of the hole, trying to pull yourself up—when a face presses against the ice above you. It’s a teen who drowned here last month, her lips blue, hair floating around her head. She slams her fist against the ice, cracking it further. You scream, kicking your legs, and finally haul yourself out. You stare at the hole, where her face is still pressed against the ice, eyes locked on yours. You grab your auger and run, not stopping until you’re off the lake.

instruction: Generate a detailed description of a Lovecraftian horror entity encountered in a mine shaft.
input: Entity Type: lovecraftian, Setting: mine shaft, Output Length: 150-300 words, Elements: appearance, behavior, origin, horror, encounter
output:
Appearance: The Weeper in the Walls resembles a humanoid mass of fused, semi-molten ore and petrified bone, standing eight feet tall. Its surface is a shifting, iridescent black slag that weeps a viscous, phosphorescent fluid. Where a face should be, there is only a concave hollow from which dozens of thin, boneless tendrils, each ending in a wet, photoreceptive orifice, constantly writhe and taste the air.

Behavior: It is utterly stationary, fused to the support structures of a collapsed section of the shaft. It does not hunt, but lures. It emits a subsonic hum that resonates through the rock, inducing profound despair and vivid hallucinations of lost loved ones calling from the darkness. Its weeping fluid slowly seeps into the mine's water table, causing those who drink it to hear its call.

Origin: It is not an invasion, but an awakening. The entity is the dormant psychic scar of a catastrophic 19th-century collapse that entombed dozens. Their collective terror, despair, and the immense geological pressure fused their remains with rare, resonant minerals, gestating this psychic tumor within the earth over a century.

Horror: The horror is passive, atmospheric, and psychological. The mine itself becomes an amplifier for its despair. Miners are not attacked but are compelled to wander deep into unsafe tunnels, drawn by the phantom voices, eventually starving or falling to their deaths, their own anguish absorbed to feed the entity.

Encounter: A prospector, following the sound of his dead daughter's singing, finds the chamber. The air is thick and metallic. The tendrils orient toward him, orifices dilating. He feels an overwhelming, seductive grief promising reunion if he just comes closer, lies down, and stops breathing. The true horror is the peaceful acceptance it offers.

instruction: Generate a detailed description of a Lovecraftian horror entity encountered in a mine shaft.
input: Entity Type: lovecraftian, Setting: mine shaft, Output Length: 150-300 words, Elements: appearance, behavior, origin, horror, encounter
output:
Appearance: The Gatelight Larvae appear as clusters of pale, pulsating sacs, each the size of a human torso, adhering to the ceiling like fungal growths. They are translucent, revealing internal, worm-like shapes swimming in bioluminescent fluid. Each sac terminates in a long, rigid proboscis of crystalline chitin, pointed downward like a stalactite.

Behavior: The larvae are in a state of dormant feeding. Their proboscises drip a sweet-smelling nectar that pools on the floor. This substance is a powerful psychotropic and nutrient concentrate. The larvae feed not on physical matter, but on the cognitive energy and life-force of creatures that consume the nectar and fall into a blissful stupor beneath them, their dreams siphoned away through a psychic link.

Origin: They are the juvenile form of a dimensionally migratory species. Their eggs, resembling mundane quartz nodules, were mined and accidentally concentrated in a storage cart. Vibrations from machinery triggered their hatching, and they instinctively migrated to the deepest, darkest part of the shaft to begin their century-long metamorphosis.

Horror: The horror is one of insidious consumption and lost potential. Victims are not killed, but are found in a state of ecstatic catatonia, slowly wasting away as their minds are drained into beautiful, alien dreams that fuel the larvae's growth. The mine becomes a garden of living larders.

Encounter: A geologist investigates a strange, sweet odor and discovers a chamber glittering with dripping crystal and the soft, internal light of the sacs. On the ground, several miners sit motionless, smiling beatifically, emaciated but serene. One of the proboscises above a victim retracts slightly, and a fresh, glistening drop begins to form at its tip.

instruction: Generate a detailed description of a Lovecraftian horror entity encountered in a mine shaft.
input: Entity Type: lovecraftian, Setting: mine shaft, Output Length: 150-300 words, Elements: appearance, behavior, origin, horror, encounter
output:
Appearance: The False Vein is a mimic entity. It presents as a perfectly normal, rich seam of copper ore streaked with malachite. However, upon close inspection, the 'ore' has a faint, rhythmic pulsation. If touched, the surface texture shifts from rock to something leathery and warm. Tools striking it sink in slightly before being rejected, leaving no mark.

Behavior: It is predatory and patient. It does not move but alters the geometry of the tunnel around it. It extends psychic filaments through existing mineral deposits, causing miners to 'discover' it. It stimulates greed and obsession, compelling them to focus excavation solely on its body. The more it is mined (ineffectively), the more it psychically feeds on the frustration and mounting avarice.

Origin: It is a fragment of a vast, subterranean organism that views mineral deposits as its nervous system. The False Vein is a specialized 'hunting node' sent to infiltrate human excavations. It is less a creature and more a sentient, topological trap.

Horror: The horror is economic and existential. It preys on purpose. It wastes entire seasons of labor, driving crews to madness as the prize constantly eludes them. The mine, a symbol of human industry, becomes a theater for a cosmic joke, where ambition is literally consumed by a thing that pretends to be treasure.

Encounter: A crew has been tunneling for weeks into a seemingly endless vein. Progress is zero; the face looks unchanged despite tons of 'ore' reportedly extracted. The foreman, obsessed, presses his hand against the rock. It yields like flesh and grows uncomfortably hot. A deep, grinding vibration passes through the mountain, as if something enormous is laughing. The maps no longer match the tunnels.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set at an isolated radio tower
input: Setting: Isolated radio tower, stormy night, maintenance worker trapped
output: The storm howls around the radio tower’s rusted steel girders as you fumble for your dead flashlight, cold seeping through your jacket. Your comms crackle to life, not with your team, but with your own voice: the words you yelled at your sister the night she died, begging her not to drive in rain. It loops, adding a new line each time—You were holding her car keys. You check your pocket, and they’re gone, the ones you clutched that night. The tower’s metal creaks, and under static, you swear you hear her faint laugh. You can’t tell if you’re losing your mind, or if the tower feasts on the guilt you buried for weeks, every lie you told about the crash.

instruction: Create a second-person psychological horror story for an intern working an overnight remote check at an old radio tower
input: Setting: Abandoned radio tower, midnight, part-time intern alone
output: You’re the only one here tonight, logging transmission data for the overnight shift. The console lights flicker, and the DJ booth’s dormant turntables whir to life—your name blares over speakers, followed by secrets you never shared: you hate your roommate, lie to your mom about your job, cut yourself since age 12. The clock hits 3:17 a.m., the time you snuck out after fighting your roommate last week. Your locked door is wide open, even though you turned it before arriving. Your phone has no signal, just static, and your own voice starts repeating you’ll never leave this tower. Paranoia curdles as you realize the tower feeds on your worst fears.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration about a hiker trapped at a mountain radio tower during a sudden fog
input: Setting: Remote mountain radio tower, thick fog, hiker seeking shelter
output: You duck into the radio tower’s small utility room when mountain fog rolls in, swallowing the trail behind you. You lean against cold metal, steadying your breathing, when the tower’s speakers crackle to life—they play your thoughts, every hidden quiet thing from that hour: I should have turned left. That hiker looked off. I’m scared I’ll die alone here. The speakers’ volume rises, matching your racing heart. You check your empty water bottle, the moon gone, leaving you blind. You don’t know if the tower broadcasts your thoughts, or if the fog warps your mind to show you the parts of yourself you buried.

instruction: Develop a second-person psychological horror story centered on a repairman at a remote radio tower confronting old grief
input: Setting: Remote radio tower, late autumn, repairman fixing a legacy transmitter
output: You’re here to fix the tower’s old transmitter, the same one your dad worked on the day he left you at 10 and never came back. You climb the ladder when the transmitter hums to life, your dad’s voice echoing through your radio: You always hated how I left. I couldn’t stand how much you blamed me. You freeze, the radio playing a last-week conversation where you blamed him for ruining every choice. You drop your wrench, and it vanishes. You call for help, only your sobs loop back. The tower’s lights flicker, and you realize the cold isn’t just weather—it’s the weight of 20 years of guilt, made tangible here.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration about a college student researching at an isolated historic radio tower
input: Setting: Isolated historic radio tower, dusk, college student researching media history
output: You dig through old archive tapes for your media history project, the tower’s creaky floors echoing under your boots. You pop a 1998 test transmission tape into the player, and your own voice blares out—your voice, 10 years from now: I wish I’d never come to this tower. I wish I’d listened to my mom and studied something safe. You drop the tape, the player stops, but your future regret keeps playing, drawing closer like it’s coming from the tower’s upper levels. You run for the door, but it’s stuck, even though you turned the lock. The sun sets, and the tower’s red warning lights blink to your regret. You can’t tell if you’re past, present, or future—just stuck listening to bad choices you’ll never make.

instruction: Write a second-person short horror narration centered on the primal fear of darkness, set entirely in a rural barn setting.
input: You entered the unlit family hay barn at dusk searching for your missing 4-H show lamb.
output: You fumble for the overhead barn light pull, but it snaps off in your hand before the sputtering incandescent bulb can kick on. The last slant of sunset bleeds out through the cracked barn door, leaving only thick, clinging darkness that presses heavy against your eardrums. You call the lamb’s name, and a wet, gurgling echo answers instead, moving steadily closer from the hayloft above. You reach for your phone, but it’s gone—you left it on the truck seat by the pasture gate. Something brushes your ankle, not soft wool, cold and slick, and the door slams shut behind you so hard the barn beams groan. You can’t see more than an inch in front of your face, and you can tell the dark isn’t empty.

instruction: Write a second-person short horror narration centered on the primal fear of darkness, set entirely in a rural barn setting.
input: You hunkered down in an abandoned 100-year-old barn to wait out a sudden violent thunderstorm.
output: Lightning flashes cast strobing slices of visibility for fractions of a second, each time showing something new that wasn’t there before: a set of clawed hands pressed to the upper loft rail, a trail of dark wet footprints leading straight to where you huddle, a shape too tall to fit through the barn rafters standing at the far end. When the thunder rolls, all light vanishes entirely—no window glow, no distant porch light, only absolute, unbroken black. You can hear it breathing, slow and ragged, inches from your face, and you can’t even lift your hands to swat it away because you can’t tell where your own body ends and the dark begins.

instruction: Write a second-person short horror narration centered on the primal fear of darkness, set entirely in a rural barn setting.
input: You’re helping your grandfather sort rusted farm equipment in his barn when the rural power cuts out without warning.
output: You laugh it off at first, fumbling for the emergency flashlight you stashed in your coverall pocket, only to find the batteries are corroded, completely dead. Grandpa was standing two feet away from you ten seconds prior, calling your name to pass a socket wrench, but now his voice is coming from the far back of the barn, slurred, wrong, nothing like his usual gruff cadence. The dark doesn’t just obscure, it eats—you can no longer feel the cold metal of the wrench in your hand, can no longer smell the hay and diesel fumes that filled the space a minute prior. Something whispers your name right next to your ear, and you realize the dark is full, not empty.

instruction: Write a second-person short horror narration centered on the primal fear of darkness, set entirely in a rural barn setting.
input: You snuck into your family’s barn after curfew to meet friends, only to find they pranked you by locking you inside alone.
output: You bang on the door, yelling, half amused half annoyed, until you realize you can’t see the crack of moonlight you know should be seeping under the door. The dark is so thick you can taste it, earthy and metallic, like old dried blood. You count your steps to the nearest window, 12 paces you’ve taken a hundred times, and you walk 20 with no wood frame hitting your outstretched hands. You hear your friends cackling from outside, but their voices get further and further away, and a low, rumbling purr starts up all around you, coming from every direction at once. The dark isn’t hiding something, it’s wearing the faces of everyone you’ve ever lost.

instruction: Write a second-person short horror narration centered on the primal fear of darkness, set entirely in a rural barn setting.
input: You’re a large animal vet investigating a string of mysterious cattle deaths, searching the remote isolated barn at 2 a.m.
output: You carry a fully charged headlamp, but the second you step over the barn threshold, its beam dies, sputters, goes dark completely, no matter how many times you smack it against your palm. You pull out your backup light, your work phone, same thing—every electronic light source dies the second it crosses the barn’s invisible threshold. The dark hums, low and resonant, and you can feel dead cattle hooves pressing into the backs of your calves, their cold muzzles nuzzling your neck, but none of them make a sound. You can’t find the door, can’t find the exit, can’t even tell if your eyes are open or closed because there’s no difference between the dark inside your lids and the dark all around you.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration focused on isolation set inside a silo
input: Fear: isolation. Setting: operational grain silo.
output: You wake face-down in warm chaff. The hatch 82 feet above clicked locked three hours back—you screamed until your throat tore, no one heard. No crew checks this silo until harvest, 11 weeks out. Your phone died an hour ago. You traced every inch of steel wall; no cracks, no handholds. The grain shifts slow under you, like something breathing down deep. When you stop yelling, the only sound is your own heartbeat, loud enough you can’t tell if the soft scraping rising through the chaff is real, or just your mind unspooling in the quiet. No one is looking for you. No one even knows you’re gone.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration focused on isolation set inside a silo
input: Fear: isolation. Setting: farm feed silo.
output: You volunteered to clear the blocked auger. They lowered you down, promised they’d pull you right back. That was yesterday. The rope went slack at dusk. You yell every ten minutes. No one answers. Mold motes clog your throat. You can see the farm house porch light glow through the top grate—you watch them turn it off for the night. No one noticed you didn’t come up. No one will miss you for four days. The dark here thickens until you can press your palm against it. It doesn’t rush. It waits.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration focused on isolation set inside a silo
input: Fear: isolation. Setting: abandoned rural silo.
output: You hid here from the tornado. The entrance collapsed behind you at 2:17am. You can hear rain outside, but no sirens, no voices. This silo is unmarked, off every county map. You scraped your name into rust an hour ago. No one will ever read it. You have no water, no light. When you close your eyes you can feel all the decades this structure stood empty, all the quiet that sank into the concrete. It seeps into your bones now. You stop digging at the rubble. No one is looking. No one ever will.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration focused on isolation set inside a silo
input: Fear: isolation. Setting: industrial silo catwalk.
output: The radio died at noon. Your relief was supposed to arrive six hours ago. You stand on the rusted catwalk 120 feet up, watching the sun bleed out over empty fields. Half the ladder rongs rotted through last month; you can’t climb down. Every hour you count cars on the distant highway. None stop. None even slow. The world keeps turning right past you. No one will wonder where you went until payroll runs next week. The wind sways the silo just enough to make you grip the rail, alone.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration focused on isolation set inside a silo
input: Fear: isolation. Setting: sealed concrete silo.
output: You don’t remember how you got here. The walls are smooth poured concrete, no seams, no openings. You can’t tell day from night. You yelled until you couldn’t make sound. You counted your breaths until you lost track. There is no edge to this dark. No one is coming for you. No one even knows you exist here. The silence doesn’t just surround you—it eats. It erases small memories one by one. You can’t remember your sister’s name anymore. Soon you won’t remember you were ever anywhere else but here.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on paranoia set on a pedestrian bridge at midnight, where the narrator suspects they’re being tracked by an unseen presence.
input: Pedestrian bridge over a murky river, midnight, rain-slicked metal grates, distant fog horn.
output: You grip the cold bridge rail, rain seeping through your hood as you count the metal grates under your boots—one, two, three—each click echoing too loud. The fog horn moans again, and you swear you hear a step behind you, but when you spin, there’s nothing. Just the river’s gurgle, swallowing light. You check your phone: no signal. The grates ahead glint like teeth, and you notice a smudge of wet mud on the rail where your hand wasn’t. You quicken your pace, but the steps match yours now, soft as breath on your neck. Every shadow seems to lean in, every creak of the bridge a warning. You don’t dare look back again—what if it’s there, inches from your shoulder, waiting for you to break?

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on paranoia set on an old covered wooden bridge, where the narrator hears distorted versions of their own inner thoughts whispered back at them.
input: Weathered covered bridge, autumn, fallen leaves stuck between planks, distant crow caws.
output: You step onto the covered bridge, leaves crunching under your sneakers, the wood groaning like a tired old man. You mutter to yourself about forgetting your keys, and a voice—your voice, but warped, slower—whispers back, "You forgot more than that." You freeze. The crow caws again, and you scan the shadowed rafters, but no one’s there. You take another step, and the voice hisses, "They’re watching you from the trees." You glance through the slats: bare branches, nothing. But when you turn back, a splintered plank where you stood has your name carved into it, fresh as if done seconds ago. Your heart thuds. Every creak is a judgment, every breath you take is copied. You run, but the voice follows, repeating your deepest fears until you can’t tell if it’s you or something else speaking.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on paranoia set on a railway bridge, where the narrator encounters a doppelgänger that mimics their every move.
input: Rusty railway bridge, dusk, distant train whistle, overgrown weeds along the tracks.
output: You lean against the rusted railway bridge post, waiting for the late train, your boots scuffing the gravel. A flicker in your peripheral makes you turn—there’s a person standing at the other end of the bridge, wearing your jacket, your jeans, your scuffed boots. They tilt their head, and you do too, unthinking. They raise a hand, and you catch yourself lifting yours before you stop. The train whistle blares closer, but the doppelgänger doesn’t move. You step forward, and they step back, mirroring you. You reach for your phone to call for help, but they pull out an identical phone, pressing the same buttons. Your skin crawls. What if they take your place? What if no one notices? The train’s lights round the bend, and the doppelgänger smiles—your smile—and steps onto the tracks. You freeze, terrified to move, terrified that if you do, you’ll end up like them.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on paranoia set on a swaying suspension bridge, where the narrator feels unseen touches that escalate as they cross.
input: High suspension bridge, windy evening, distant city lights, metal cables groaning.
output: You grip the suspension bridge’s handrail, the wind whipping your hair as the structure sways under your weight. A cold finger brushes the back of your neck, and you spin—only empty air and the groan of cables meet you. You laugh nervously, telling yourself it’s just the wind. But then another touch, this time on your wrist, light as a spider’s web. You yank your hand away, checking for a strand, but there’s nothing. You keep moving, each step unsteady, and the touches multiply: fingers tracing your spine, breath on your ear, a hand tugging at your backpack strap. The city lights blur below, and you can’t tell if the sway is the bridge or your own panic. You glance down, and swear you see a figure clinging to the cables above, but when you look up, there’s nothing. You run, tripping over your own feet, convinced whatever’s touching you is going to pull you over the edge any second.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on paranoia set on a footbridge over a busy highway, where the narrator believes every passing driver is watching them and part of a conspiracy.
input: Concrete footbridge over a busy highway, rush hour, honking cars, neon signs glowing.
output: You hurry across the concrete footbridge, your backpack slung tight over your shoulder, as cars zoom below, honking and roaring. A red sedan passes, and the driver locks eyes with you—holds it, even as they merge lanes. You look away, but then a truck driver does the same, nodding slightly like they know you. Your pulse quickens. Every car that passes has someone staring, their faces blank, unblinking. You check your pockets, sure you have something on you that marks you, but there’s nothing out of the ordinary. A van pulls up below, idling, and the passenger window rolls down—someone waves, and you freeze. They know your name, you’re sure of it. You break into a run, the sound of honking growing louder, like they’re cheering you on. You don’t stop until you’re off the bridge, gasping, convinced every driver on that highway was part of something waiting to take you.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about losing control in a tunnel.
input: Fear: loss_of_control. Setting: a traffic jam in a long, hot tunnel.
output: The traffic jam in the tunnel is absolute. The air grows thick and hot, smelling of exhaust and panic. You press the accelerator; nothing. The brake; it sinks to the floor with a hiss. Your car is a dead metal box. Ahead, the red taillights begin to wink out, one by one, plunging sections into darkness. A truck's horn blares, then cuts off mid-blast. Your doors lock with a solid *thunk*. You claw at the handle, but it's fused. The last light dies, and the silence is total. You can't move the car, can't open the door, can't even scream—your throat is sealed tight by the oppressive dark. Something is in here with you, taking everything away, piece by piece.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about losing control in a tunnel.
input: Fear: loss_of_control. Setting: a pedestrian underpass where the walls are breathing.
output: You take the shortcut under the railway, the graffiti seeming to writhe in your peripheral vision. The walls aren't just damp; they're pulsing. A warm, rhythmic breath washes over you. Your own breath hitches, falling out of sync. You try to run, but your legs move in slow, deliberate strides you didn't command. The tunnel mouth shrinks. The walls close in, soft and spongy against your shoulders. You try to turn your head, to look, but your neck muscles are locked. You are walked, step by controlled step, deeper into the gullet where the wet, rhythmic breathing originates. Your body is no longer your own.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about losing control in a tunnel.
input: Fear: loss_of_control. Setting: a driverless automated transport pod in a utility tunnel.
output: The automated pod glides silently into the maintenance tunnel, a detour the screen flashes. Then the screen dies. The soft hum of the motor shifts to a grinding whir. The pod accelerates, banking sharply on tracks not meant for passenger transit. You hammer the emergency stop—it's cold and inert. The 'door open' button clicks uselessly. Through the porthole, you see flickering lights and pipes streaking by, faster and faster. A synthesized voice crackles, 'Course correction initiated.' It's not asking. The pod plunges down a vertical shaft, your stomach lurching, utterly helpless in the belly of the machine.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about losing control in a tunnel.
input: Fear: loss_of_control. Setting: a cave tunnel where your flashlight fails and gravity shifts.
output: Your flashlight gutters, then dies, leaving you in a blackness so complete it feels solid. You reach for the wall—and your hand passes through empty air. You're floating. A slow, sickening rotation begins. Up and down lose meaning. You flail, but there's no purchase, no direction. A faint, phosphorescent glow blooms on the ceiling, which might now be below you. You try to swim toward the remembered entrance, but an unseen current pulls you steadily deeper. Your own movements are futile, a puppet's jerks against a tide that controls your every drift into the mountain's heart.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about losing control in a tunnel.
input: Fear: loss_of_control. Setting: a subway tunnel where the train stops between stations and time distorts.
output: The train jerks to a halt in the dark tunnel. The lights flicker, then stabilize, casting a sickly yellow. Your watch's hands spin wildly. The woman across from you is frozen mid-yawn, a statue. You stand, but your body moves like you're underwater. You reach for the comms button; your finger takes an age to cross the inch. A distant rumble grows, but the train doesn't move. Instead, the walls begin to stream by in a blur of tile and graffiti, faster and faster, while you remain absolutely still. The universe is rushing past, unanchored, and you are a static point, forced to witness the collapse of motion and time.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set at a well focused on a jumpscare.
input: A hiker searching for their lost tabby cat near an abandoned farm well.
output: You trudge through overgrown farm grass, the faint jingle of your tabby’s collar guiding you to the cracked stone well half-hidden by weeds. You lean over, elbow scraping the rough rim, and cup your hand to shield your eyes from the dim light below. The air smells of mold and stagnant water, and a soft meow echoes up. “There you are,” you mutter, reaching down. Your fingers brush soft fur—then something cold and clawed wraps tight around your wrist, yanking hard. A wet, wide face bursts upward, eyes bulging, mouth full of sharp black teeth, screaming directly into your shocked face.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set at a well focused on a jumpscare.
input: A park maintenance worker checking a dry well for storm debris.
output: You shine your flashlight across the dry well’s concrete walls, scanning for fallen branches that could clog the drainage pipe after last night’s storm. A crinkle of plastic catches your eye at the stone bottom. You lean over, stretching to zoom your phone’s camera in, when a sudden cold draft hits your cheek. The plastic crinkles again, closer. You lean further, boots slipping on moss growing along the rim. Then, a pale, slimy hand bursts from the wall behind you, curling tight around your shoulder, and a gurgling scream cuts through the park’s quiet.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set at a well focused on a jumpscare.
input: An urban explorer prying open an old well cover in a closed subway station.
output: You slip through the rusted gate into the shuttered subway station, your flashlight bouncing off graffiti-covered tiled walls. The old station map marks a forgotten water well near the empty platform. You step over broken glass, and a soft dripping sound grows louder, mixing with the station’s low hum. You kneel by the rusted well cover, prying it open with a crowbar. The metal clatters aside, cool mist rising. You lean over, peering into the dark—then something thin and dark wraps around your ankle, yanking you off balance. A face with no eyes pushes through the well, hissing as its mouth stretches wide.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set at a well focused on a jumpscare.
input: A tourist leaning over a village well to retrieve a dropped souvenir coin.
output: You stand at the village square’s stone well, having just dropped your brass sun coin into the dark water while making a wish. An elder warned you not to lean too far, but you brush it off, holding the bucket rope to haul it up. The bucket hits water with a splash, but no coin clinks inside. You lean further over, elbows on the rim, squinting into the murky depths. Suddenly, a cold, wet hand claps over your mouth from inside the well, and a rotting figure’s face bursts up, yanking your head down toward the water as it screams.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set at a well focused on a jumpscare.
input: A stranded motorist filling a jug at a remote cabin’s well.
output: Your car died two miles back, and crickets hum around you as you walk to the remote cabin’s well. You fill your water jug from the spigot, then hear soft scratching coming from inside the well. You set the jug down, leaning over to peer in, flashlight beam cutting through the dark. The scratching grows louder, paired with a faint, panicked cry. “I’ll help you,” you mutter, reaching in. Then, a dark, slimy mass shoots up from the well, slamming into your chest, claws tearing your shirt, and a high-pitched scream deafens you.

instruction: Document anomalous geometric horror entities recovered from the sealed 1897 Blackspire Clock Tower, adhering to SCP anomalous entity classification guidelines
input: Entity Designation: Γ-72, The Canted Hour Hand
output: Appearance: A 12-foot pitted brass hour hand warped to hold no consistent right angles, its surface covered in impossible repeating hexagonal tessellations that shift out of focus when viewed for more than 2 seconds, no two measurements of its length ever return the same value. Behavior: It spins counter to the tower’s clock mechanism, stopping every 47 minutes to warp surrounding stone floor tiles into 4-dimensional protrusions that exist outside standard spatial axes. Origin: It slipped into our reality during a 1927 solar eclipse, when the tower’s clock seized mid-strike and created a temporary rift to a non-Euclidean time dimension. Horror: Prolonged observation causes human bone structure to re-align to the entity’s misangled geometry, leaving permanent, painful joint tilts. Encounter: A trespassing urban explorer fled the tower after noticing their left wrist was stuck at a 7-degree unnatural tilt, their personal watch running backward at 900x speed for 3 weeks post-exposure.

instruction: Document anomalous geometric horror entities recovered from the sealed 1897 Blackspire Clock Tower, adhering to SCP anomalous entity classification guidelines
input: Entity Designation: Γ-104, The Interstitial Dodecahedral Gear
output: Appearance: A weightless 18-inch perfect dodecahedral gear floating in the empty space between the tower’s main clock cogs, each face etched with an infinitely receding copy of itself, no visible teeth or central axle to facilitate its constant rotation. Behavior: It completes one full rotation every 11 seconds, consuming 1 second of local time per rotation, the thin gaps between its faces are sharp enough to slice through solid granite and re-arrange the atomic structure of any object they pass through. Origin: It formed in 1952 when an amateur clockmaker attempted to carve a gear from a fragment of non-terrestrial meteorite that struck the tower’s roof. Horror: Contact with the gear slices living tissue into thousands of identical micro-thin parallel copies, all remaining conscious and aligned. Encounter: A maintenance worker attempting to remove debris from the clock mechanism had their left hand pass through the gear’s edge, leaving their hand as 21 perfectly aligned 0.3mm thick slices, all responsive to nerve signals.

instruction: Document anomalous geometric horror entities recovered from the sealed 1897 Blackspire Clock Tower, adhering to SCP anomalous entity classification guidelines
input: Entity Designation: Γ-39, The Unlit Pendulum Shadow
output: Appearance: A 7-foot trapezoidal shadow pendulum that swings across the tower’s interior, with no corresponding physical pendulum or light source casting it, its edges are sharp enough to cast secondary, smaller shadows of their own. Behavior: It completes a full swing every 32 seconds, passing through solid stone and metal without resistance, leaving any surface it crosses permanently warped to match its impossible perspective. Origin: It is a phase-shifted fragment of a clock tower existing in a parallel, non-Euclidean universe, phasing into our reality whenever both towers’ hourly chimes align. Horror: Exposure to the shadow collapses the human brain’s ability to process 3D perspective, leaving affected individuals unable to recognize flat surfaces as being free of depth warps. Encounter: A night security guard watched the shadow pass over their phone camera, every photo they took afterward showed their own apartment as a maze of impossible corridors where front doors opened directly into empty, endless sky.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a prison cell
input: Scenario: counting wall scratch marks in solitary confinement
output: You wake at 2:17. You’ve counted the 147 scratch marks on your cell wall every night for 72 days. Tonight there’s 148. You trace each one with your nail—no fresh gouge, no concrete dust.
