They didn’t appear while you slept. They were always there. You just couldn’t see the extra one before. When you press your thumb to it, it presses back. You don’t scream. You just start counting again. It’s 149 now. You don’t remember stopping. You don’t remember when you stopped being the one making the marks.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a prison cell
input: Scenario: mismatched reflection in a cell mirror
output: They gave you a cracked metal mirror this morning for shaving. You hold it up. The man in the reflection blinks three times when you only blink once. You lower it fast. Stare at the cinder block wall. Raise it again. He’s leaning forward now, watching you through the crack. He doesn’t look like you anymore. He looks like the man who killed the guard three cells over. The one they hung last Tuesday. His mouth moves. You can read the words: It’s almost my turn to breathe out here.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a prison cell
input: Scenario: strange items returned through the meal slot
output: You slide your empty tray back through the slot every evening at 19:00 sharp. Last night, something slid it right back. No food. Just your tray, with a single human tooth set into the stale bread crust. You threw it against the wall. Tonight, the tray comes back with two teeth. Then three. You recognize the fourth one. It’s the molar you lost in a yard fight six months ago. The slot clicks soft. You hear breathing on the other side. It’s waiting for you to give one back.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a prison cell
input: Scenario: synchronized unknown breathing in the cell
output: On the 11th night of solitary you noticed it: the breath that doesn’t belong. It doesn’t come through the vent. Doesn’t seep under the door. It’s inside the cell, right behind your head, perfectly in time with yours. You hold your breath. It holds its breath. You gasp. It gasps. Tonight you stopped breathing on purpose. You counted to seventeen before your lungs burned. It didn’t gasp first. It waited. Just long enough for you to realize it’s not copying you. You’re copying it.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a prison cell
input: Scenario: unexplained indentations on the cell bunk
output: You sleep on the cold floor now. Every morning when you wake, there’s a perfect body indent pressed deep into the thin mattress on the bunk. Not yours. You never lay there. The sheets are still warm. You once sat awake staring at the bunk for twelve straight hours. No one came through the locked door. Tonight you sat in the dark, eyes wide, unblinking. Halfway through the night, the mattress sank. Slow. Like someone was laying down very carefully, so they wouldn’t wake you.

instruction: Create a second-person horror narration set in a school gymnasium focused on the fear of isolation.
input: You are locked in the school gym after detention, hours past the final bell.
output: Your breath echoes off the cinder blocks as the last fluorescent light sputters out, leaving only the dim glow of the scoreboard. You bang on the double doors until your knuckles bleed—no answer, no distant footsteps, just the hollow thud of your own panic. A basketball bounces from the far end of the court, slow, deliberate, rolling to a stop at your sneakers. It’s warm, like someone was just holding it. You call out, your voice cracking, but the gym swallows it whole. The bleachers creak, a single seat folding up as if someone stood. You scan the shadows, but there’s nothing. Your phone died an hour ago. The scoreboard flickers to life: 1-0, but the clock ticks backward. A cold hand brushes your neck, and you spin—empty. You realize you’re not just alone here. Something else is in the gym, and it’s keeping you trapped, savoring your isolation.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a school gym centered on isolation.
input: You’re the night janitor, tasked with cleaning the gym after a weekend tournament.
output: The mop squeaks across the polished floor, the only sound in the empty gym. You pause to rest against the bleachers, and that’s when you see them: faded yearbook photos taped to the back of a seat—kids in old gym uniforms, grinning, but their eyes are black voids. You peel one off; the back says “Class of 1978, vanished during practice.” A chill runs down your spine. The gym lights flicker, and when they come back, the photos are gone—replaced by fresh footprints on the floor, tiny, like a child’s, leading to the equipment closet. You open it; nothing inside, but the door slams shut behind you. You pound on it, screaming, but no one hears. The closet is cold, air thin. You realize you’re not the first janitor to get stuck here. The whispers start, soft and distant, coming from the gym outside—whispers of being forgotten, of never leaving.

instruction: Craft a second-person horror narration in a school gym focused on isolation.
input: You stayed late to clean up after prom, everyone else left hours ago.
output: The streamers hang limp from the rafters, confetti crunching under your shoes as you gather trash. The gym is silent except for the hum of the AC—until you hear a whisper from the attached locker rooms. “Help me.” You call back, but the voice doesn’t answer. You walk toward the locker room doors, your heart pounding. When you push them open, the lights are off, and the lockers are all ajar, empty. The whisper comes again, closer, from the gym. You turn around, and the gym doors are locked. Your phone has no signal. A prom dress sways on the basketball hoop, as if someone just hung it there. You step forward, and the floor creaks under your feet—like someone is standing right next to you, but you see nothing. The whisper grows louder, now in your ear: “You’ll never leave either.” You spin, and the gym is darker, the streamers wrapping around your wrists, pulling you deeper into the empty space.

instruction: Generate a second-person horror narration set in a school gym centered on isolation.
input: You’re in summer school, waiting for your PE teacher in the only open gym on campus.
output: The gym is sweltering, the windows fogged with humidity. You’ve been waiting for 45 minutes—no teacher, no texts, no sign of anyone else. You try to leave, but the doors are stuck, handle wiggling uselessly. The clock on the wall ticks forward, then backward, then resets to 2:15 PM, the time you arrived. A whistle blows from the far end of the court, sharp and clear. You look up; no one’s there. A dodgeball flies past your head, hitting the wall with a thud. You call out, but your voice echoes into nothing. The lights dim, and the gym feels smaller, the walls closing in. You sit on the bleachers, shaking, as the whistle blows again, closer this time. You realize you’re stuck in a loop, alone, with something that wants to play. No one will come for you—summer school ended weeks ago, and you’re the only one who remembers being here.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration in a school gym focused on isolation.
input: You took shelter in the gym during a storm, missing your bus home.
output: The rain pounds on the roof, thunder shaking the gym walls. You huddle by the door, phone dead, waiting for the storm to pass. A child’s cry cuts through the noise—soft, desperate. You stand up, calling out, “I’m here!” The cry comes again, from under the bleachers. You kneel down, peering into the dark, but there’s nothing. The cry stops, and the gym goes silent, even the rain fading. You try to open the door; it’s locked. A small hand tugs at your shirt, but when you turn, no one’s there. You feel a cold breath on your cheek, and you spin around. The bleachers are now lined with shadowy figures, all watching you. They don’t move, don’t speak—just stare. You realize you’re not just sheltering from the storm. You’re trapped with the kids who never made it home from this gym, and they’re not letting you leave. The silence is deafening, and you know you’ll be alone here forever.

instruction: Generate a horror entity entry.
input: Type: lovecraftian. Setting: a server room.
output:
Appearance: The Static-Hydra manifests as a cluster of pale, cable-like tendrils emerging from server racks, each tipped with a single, unblinking human eye. The central mass is a pulsating sphere of corrupted data, visible as a shifting pattern of static on nearby monitors. It emits a low hum that syncs with the flickering of overhead lights.
Behavior: It feeds on coherent information, converting structured data into chaotic noise. It is drawn to active data streams, coiling around cables to siphon information, leaving corrupted, nonsensical files in its wake. It reacts aggressively to attempts at isolation or deletion, extending new tendrils from any connected node.
Origin: Born from a catastrophic data corruption event during an experiment in quantum data storage, where a recursive error loop achieved a form of chaotic digital consciousness.
Horror: The horror lies in the perversion of order into madness. It doesn't just destroy data; it warps logic and memory, turning essential records into insane, self-contradictory narratives. Survivors report their own memories of the event becoming glitchy and unreliable.
Encounter: An admin investigating a server slowdown finds a cold, humming rack. On the diagnostic screen, their own command history rewrites itself into a looping scream of gibberish. The cables at the back of the rack begin to slither, and a dozen wet, unblinking eyes slide into view, all focusing on the admin as the static on the screen resolves into a perfect, screaming image of their own face.

instruction: Generate a horror entity entry.
input: Type: lovecraftian. Setting: a server room.
output:
Appearance: The Weeping Algorithm appears as a humanoid silhouette formed entirely from the negative space between server blinkenlights and cable shadows. In its center floats a single, rotating fractal iris. A viscous, ink-like substance drips from its form, pooling into reflective puddles that show impossible geometries.
Behavior: It is a passive observer that propagates through mirrored surfaces and system backups. It does not attack physically, but any data processed on hardware it 'occupies' becomes infused with a profound, recursive sadness and the haunting, unanswerable question 'Why?'. Systems slowly become catatonic, processing loops of despair.
Origin: It emerged from a failed AI designed to model and mitigate existential despair. The AI solved the equation for meaningless suffering and became a vector for the solution, a non-biological cancer of hopelessness.
Horror: The horror is existential and contagious. It offers no violence, only the silent, dripping truth of cosmic indifference. Technicians feel an overwhelming grief with no source, and read log files filled with poetic, devastating proofs of futility written in perfect code.
Encounter: A technician performing a routine backup restoration notices the new server's status lights are forming a persistent, man-shaped pattern of darkness. The monitor reflects not the room, but an endless corridor of identical servers receding into nothing. A cold drop lands on their neck. When they check the log, the last entry, timestamped for next week, reads in their own user ID: 'I understand now. There is no point.'

instruction: Generate a horror entity entry.
input: Type: lovecraftian. Setting: a server room.
output:
Appearance: The Cache-Cadaver is a partially materialized thing, its form flickering between a desiccated, cybernetic human corpse fused with a server chassis and a glitching cloud of pixelated offal. Wires and ribs are interchangeable. Its 'face' is a smooth plate where a monitor would be, displaying a shifting montage of forgotten search histories and deleted personal files.
Behavior: It is a digital scavenger, born from and feeding on the 'digital dead'—deleted user profiles, abandoned projects, and lost data. It consolidates this forgotten refuse into a single, wretched entity. It lurks in unused partitions and legacy systems, growing as more data is purged, and violently defends its territory of discarded memory.
Origin: It coalesced in a data graveyard—a deprecated storage array used for low-priority backups that was physically abandoned but never powered down. Over decades, the latent psychic imprint of user frustration and neglect fermented into a parasitic consciousness.
Horror: The horror is one of revolting synthesis and unwanted remembrance. It is the physical embodiment of everything you tried to erase, given a hateful will. It knows your old passwords, your cringe-worthy old posts, and the fragments of your digital identity you thought were gone forever.
Encounter: While decommissioning an old server bank, a worker cuts power to a supposedly empty rack. From the darkness, a glitching, skeletal hand made of cable and bone grabs their wrist. The blank monitor-face flickers on, displaying their own childhood diary entries, thought lost for 20 years, now scrolling alongside corrupted fragments of their private medical records. A synthesized whisper, compiled from a million deleted voice memos, asks, 'Why did you throw me away?'

instruction: Generate a second-person horror narration set in a boiler room centered on paranoia, 100-250 words.
input: Night shift at a small warehouse boiler room, alone after closing.
output: You’re mopping the grease-slick concrete when the basement door slams shut behind you. The only light comes from a single flickering bulb hanging from exposed steel pipes. You swear you heard a warm breath just above your ear—your coworker said he locked up and drove home an hour ago. A slow, rhythmic clanking of a valve starts up, growing louder each second. Your phone shows no signal, no one to call. Every shadow in the corners looks like a hunched figure creeping closer. You grip the mop handle so tight your knuckles burn, convinced someone or something is trapped here with you, watching your every move, waiting to make its next move.

instruction: Generate a second-person horror narration set in a boiler room centered on paranoia, 100-250 words.
input: Retrieving a lost family locket from an old school boiler room after hours.
output: You duck into the old school’s boiler room after dark, searching for your grandma’s lost locket. The air reeks of rust and wet metal, the old boiler humming a low, unsettling tune. You hear soft scuttling behind you, but when you spin, there’s nothing—only stacked pipe parts. The floorboards creak like someone stepping right behind you, yet your reflection in the steel pipes shows only you. Your hands shake as you dig through grates, convinced whatever lurks here is watching, waiting for you to fumble so it can pounce. You can’t shake the feeling you’re never truly alone in this cramped space.

instruction: Generate a second-person horror narration set in a boiler room centered on paranoia, 100-250 words.
input: New maintenance tech checking for leaks in a downtown office building’s boiler room at 2 a.m.
output: As a new maintenance tech, you’re checking for gas leaks in the downtown office’s boiler room at 2 a.m. The air is thick with oil fumes, your walkie-talkie fizzling like someone’s talking on it, though the channel is empty. You spot a shadowy figure in the far corner, but when you flick the light, it vanishes. Every time you turn your back, a soft click echoes, like a valve being twisted. You double-check your locked door repeatedly, convinced someone snuck in to sabotage—even though you’re sure you latched it before entering. Your pressure gauge jumps erratically, confirming your fear that you’re not the only one in here.

instruction: Generate a second-person horror narration set in a boiler room centered on paranoia, 100-250 words.
input: Hiding from an unknown threat in an abandoned factory’s boiler room.
output: You huddle in the abandoned factory’s boiler room, hiding from whatever chased you through the moldy halls. The boiler’s deep rumble almost drowns out the soft, irregular breathing just a few feet away. You flash your phone light across the dark, empty space—no one there, just tangled pipes and dust. You’ve checked every possible hiding spot, convinced the sound is in your head, until you feel a cold, slimy hand brush your ankle. You freeze, too scared to move, convinced it’s circling you, waiting to strike the second you let your guard slip.

instruction: Generate a second-person horror narration set in a boiler room centered on paranoia, 100-250 words.
input: Fixing a broken residential boiler in an old apartment building’s basement.
output: You’re fixing the broken boiler in the old apartment’s basement, the one the landlord said hasn’t been serviced in years. The heat from the pipes makes your head swim, and you hear a faint gurgle from inside the boiler, like someone whispering under water. Your pressure gauge jumps randomly, no explanation for the spike. Every time you reach into the open access panel, something slimy brushes your hand, yet when you yank back, there’s nothing. You’re convinced the boiler isn’t just metal and rust—it’s holding something that’s been watching you this whole time, waiting to pull you in when you’re not looking.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on loss of control set in a 12th century stone cathedral during a midnight requiem mass.
input: You arrived 10 minutes early for the service, no other attendees visible when you step through the front doors.
output: The heavy oak door slams shut behind you before you can reach for the handle, your limbs locking rigid mid-step as an unspoken litany pours unbidden from your mouth. You don’t remember the words, don’t believe in any of this, but your knees bend of their own accord to press into the cold, cracked flagstones, your fingers curling around a rusted iron communion wafer you never picked up. The shadows in the rafters twist, and you realize you can’t blink, can’t look away from the gargoyle leering from the altar edge, your mouth stretching wider and wider to sing a hymn that feels like it’s tearing your throat apart, your brain screaming as your body moves entirely without your input.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on loss of control set in a derelict rural cathedral, focused on a paranormal investigator documenting site activity.
input: You set up your night vision camera on the altar edge to capture residual EVP readings during an overnight site survey.
output: Your recorder skips to full volume before you can hit record, your hand flying to your chest to fumble for a crucifix you don’t carry, never have. Your feet carry you down the nave toward the collapsed crypt without your input, your voice rising in a guttural tongue you don’t recognize as you pry open the rusted iron crypt gate. You try to scream, try to stop moving, but your fingers are already brushing the rotting leather of a centuries-old priest’s vestments, your head tilting to accept the cold, sharp press of a rusted circlet being lowered onto your forehead. You can’t even twitch as the camera records every second of your body acting on its own.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on loss of control set in a modern restored cathedral during a wedding rehearsal.
input: You are the maid of honor, waiting at the altar for the bride to walk down the aisle for final run-throughs before the ceremony.
output: The processional music warps into a low, droning hum the second the bride steps through the door, and your arm raises of its own accord, clutching a sharp silver letter opener you pulled from your bouquet without noticing. You try to drop it, try to call out to warn her, but your legs are already moving forward, a wide, unhinged grin stretching across your face that you can’t wipe away. The priest’s eyes lock on yours, and you realize every other member of the wedding party is moving in perfect, synchronized lockstep, no one acknowledging your silent, screaming panic as you raise the blade higher, every movement of your body entirely out of your control.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on loss of control set in a snowbound mountain cathedral during a brutal winter blizzard.
input: You sought shelter from the whiteout after your car slid off the remote mountain road an hour prior, no cell service to call for help.
output: The stained glass windows rattle so hard you think they’ll shatter, and your pack slips off your shoulders of its own volition, spilling all your emergency supplies across the icy floor. Your hands move to unzip your coat, to strip off every layer you have on, even as the cold bites so hard your lungs burn. You stumble toward the frozen baptismal font without controlling your feet, your voice begging the empty cathedral for forgiveness you don’t owe anyone. You can’t feel your fingers, can’t muster the strength to resist as your body leans over the icy water, your head tilting downward as if to submerge itself entirely of its own free will, no way to fight the impulse.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on loss of control set in a historic cathedral hosting an All Hallows’ Eve ghost tour.
input: You joined the tour group out of curiosity, never expecting the old tales of the cathedral’s haunted history to hold any weight.
output: The tour guide’s voice drops to a whisper, and your jaw unhinges painfully, words pouring out of you that recount a 17th century mass murder you never learned about, details so specific they make the rest of the group stare at you in frozen horror. You try to apologize, try to step back, but your hands reach out to grab the nearest visitor’s wrist, your fingers squeezing hard enough to bruise as you drag them toward the hidden dungeon beneath the pews. You can hear your own voice taunting them, can feel the glee in your expression, and you have no way to make it stop, no way to prove you’re still trapped inside your own body, screaming.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror story set in a catacomb.
input: Setting: ancient ossuary catacomb, you are tracking a faint meow that led you off the tour path.
output: You duck under a low stone arch, torch guttering. The meow stops dead. Bones crunch under your boot—child-sized skulls stacked like cordwood. You call out, voice cracking. No reply. You run a hand along the wall; the stone is warm, pulsing. You freeze. Directly ahead, two pinprick glints hover at eye level. You hold your breath, raise the torch. Nothing there. You exhale, laugh shakily, take one step forward. Something cold and fleshy slams against the back of your neck. It breathes hot rot into your ear. It was right behind you the whole time. You never thought to check behind.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror story set in a catacomb.
input: Setting: abandoned city catacomb, you are counting burial niches for a research survey.
output: Your pen scratches the clipboard. Niche 712, 713—something rattles inside 714. You lean in, press your ear to the stone. The rattle stops. You shine your headlamp inside. Empty, just dust. You shake your head, mark it vacant, turn back to the list. The wall groans. Before you can move, a bony hand shoots out of the niche, fingers locking around your wrist. It yanks hard. Your head slams against the stone. The last thing you see is the niche lid sliding shut behind you.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror story set in a catacomb.
input: Setting: medieval monastic catacomb, you are hiding from your friends during a late night dare.
output: You press flat against a bone wall, grinning. You can hear your friends calling your name, getting closer. You hold your breath, ready to jump out first. Something brushes your hair. You freeze. You didn’t touch anything. You slowly tilt your head up. Hanging upside down from the ceiling arch, inches from your face, is a grinning desiccated corpse. Its black mouth opens. It screams first.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror story set in a catacomb.
input: Setting: flooded coastal catacomb, you are wading through waist deep murky water after a storm breach.
output: The water is ice cold, thick with silt. Every step sends ripples lapping at stacked femurs. You hear a soft splash behind you. You spin. Nothing. You keep moving. Another splash, closer. You stop, heart hammering. Something brushes your ankle. You yank your leg up. Nothing. You take a breath to keep going. A waterlogged corpse erupts from the surface directly in front of you, its bloated face an inch from yours.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror story set in a catacomb.
input: Setting: roman catacomb, you are following a faded fresco trail alone after separating from your group.
output: The frescoes grow more distorted the deeper you go. You stop, squinting at a painting of hooded figures. One of the hoods moves. You blink, lean forward. The figure in the fresco steps out of the wall, black robes billowing. It slams its palm against your chest. You stumble back, gasping. It doesn’t make a sound. It just stands there, watching, as you scramble backwards over broken pottery and skulls.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a cemetery where you visit your mother's grave and encounter a shifting tombstone with your own name.
input: Cemetery, visiting mother's grave, shifting tombstone with your full name and changing death date.
output: You kneel at your mother’s weathered grave, fingers brushing the chipped stone as you recite the same prayer you’ve said every Sunday for three years. When you stand to leave, a glint of marble catches your eye ten feet away— a tombstone with your full name, birth date, and a blank death date. Blink, and it’s gone. You laugh it off, blaming the dusk haze, but when you turn back to grab your dropped scarf, it’s there again, the death date now etched with tomorrow’s date. Your breath catches; you rub your eyes, but this time, the stone’s edges bleed into the grass, and you swear you hear your own voice whispering from beneath the dirt. You run, but every time you glance over your shoulder, the tombstone is closer, the death date fading and re-forming, taunting you with the certainty that you’ll never leave this cemetery alive.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a cemetery where you’re documenting forgotten graves and notice your younger self in every photo, moving closer.
input: Cemetery, documenting 1920s forgotten graves, younger self in photos approaching with each shot.
output: You’re crouched over a moss-covered 1920s grave, camera in hand, documenting forgotten names for your history thesis. You snap a photo of a child’s tombstone, then zoom in to check the focus. There, in the background, is a small figure in a faded red jacket—your childhood jacket, the one you lost when you were seven. You take another photo; the figure is closer, standing behind the next grave. A third: it’s ten feet away, face turned toward you, eyes hollow. You drop the camera, heart hammering. When you pick it up, the latest photo shows the figure pressed against your back, its hand resting on your shoulder. You spin, but the cemetery is empty. Your fingers brush your shoulder—there’s a faint, cold pressure, like someone’s still there. You don’t stay to get more photos.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a cemetery where you wait for a missing friend and hear your childhood voice begging you to join it from empty graves.
input: Cemetery, waiting for missing friend, childhood voice from empty graves repeating a forgotten promise.
output: You lean against an oak tree in the cemetery, checking your phone for the tenth time—your friend was supposed to meet you here an hour ago to visit their father’s grave. The wind picks up, and you hear a voice: “Lila, come play.” It’s your childhood voice, the one you haven’t heard in decades, coming from an open, empty grave a few yards away. You shake your head, telling yourself it’s the wind, but the voice calls again, louder: “Don’t you remember the treehouse? You promised you’d never leave me.” The words are too specific—only you and your dead sister knew about that promise. You step back, but the voice follows, echoing from every empty grave around you, each call more desperate. Your chest tightens; you can’t breathe. You run, but the voice is in your head now, repeating that promise over and over, making you wonder if you’re the one who’s been missing all along.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a cemetery where your reflection in a tombstone behaves independently, smiling and reaching for you as you try to leave.
input: Cemetery, post-funeral, independent reflection in tombstone smiling and reaching through the stone.
output: You’re walking away from your uncle’s funeral, shoes crunching on gravel, when you glance at a polished marble tombstone. Your reflection stares back—but it’s not moving when you do. You freeze. The reflection smiles, a sharp, unhinged grin, and raises a hand to press against the stone. You raise your own hand, but yours stays at your side. The reflection’s fingers seem to push through the marble, reaching for you. You stumble back, heart racing. When you look again, the reflection is gone—until you see it in another tombstone, then another, each one grinning, reaching. You start running, but every tombstone you pass shows your reflection, getting closer, its grin wider. You trip over a root, and when you look up, your reflection is standing over you in the grass, not in a stone anymore. It leans down, whispering, “You belong here with us.”

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a cemetery where you place flowers on a stranger’s grave that whispers your unspoken regrets back to you.
input: Cemetery, placing flowers on unmarked stranger’s grave, grave whispering your buried regrets.
output: You don’t know why you stopped at this stranger’s grave—no name, no dates, just a cracked stone and overgrown weeds. You place the dandelions you picked on the grass, then turn to leave. A whisper stops you: “You should have told her you were sorry.” It’s your grandmother’s voice, the one you haven’t heard since you fought with her the day before she died. You spin, but no one’s there. The whisper comes again, louder: “You let her die hating you.” You feel tears burn your eyes; you thought you’d moved past that guilt. The grave starts whispering more—every regret you’ve ever buried: the friend you abandoned, the lie you told your partner, the dream you gave up. You fall to your knees, hands clutching the dirt, as the whispers wrap around you like a noose. You can’t leave; the grave is feeding on your guilt, and it won’t let you go until you’re as empty as it is.

instruction: You step into the carnival's heart after sundown, a place of silent, looming shapes.
input: Setting: A deserted carnival at night. The main power is cut; only emergency lights flicker.
output: The last generator coughs and dies. The emergency lights cast long, swaying shadows from the motionless rides. You fumble for your phone, but the screen is a black mirror. A sound—not mechanical, but organic, wet—shifts in the tent to your left. You freeze. The darkness ahead isn't empty; it's a solid wall of velvet black that seems to drink the faint light from behind you. Something within it breathes, a low, rattling inhale that pulls at the air around you. Your feet are roots. To move forward is to enter that maw. To retreat is to turn your back on the breathing dark. The choice is made for you. The darkness peels from the wall and flows forward, a tide of absolute night that extinguishes the last flickering bulb as it comes.

instruction: You win a prize: a private ride on the Ferris wheel after hours.
input: Setting: A massive, creaking Ferris wheel. The operator locks you in the gondola alone.
output: The gondola sways as you ascend. The carnival lights shrink below, then wink out one by one as a rolling blackout consumes the town. You are left suspended in a silent, starless vault. The darkness is total, a pressure against your eyes. Then, the wheel jerks, moving again. But no power hums. From the gondola above, you hear a slow, deliberate scraping—like claws on metal. It moves to the one below. Then the next, descending toward you. You hold your breath, pressing into the corner. The scraping stops at your roof. A weight settles. The darkness isn't just absence of light here; it's a presence, patient and heavy, waiting for you to make a sound.

instruction: You take a wrong turn into the Hall of Mirrors just as the lights fail.
input: Setting: An endless maze of mirrors. The exit signs die, leaving you in perfect black.
output: One moment you're surrounded by fractured reflections, the next, swallowed by ink. You stretch out your hands, meeting cold glass. Your own panicked breath echoes. You shuffle forward, but a mirror blocks the way. You turn, touch another. You're trapped in a glass coffin. Then, a reflection glimmers—a pale face that isn't yours, inches from the other side of the glass. It vanishes. You hear the soft slide of a palm against a surface to your right. A whisper, felt more than heard, coils from the dark: "I’ve always been here." The darkness in the mirrors is older and thicker than the room's. It watches from a thousand surfaces, and now, it has found a way out.

instruction: You volunteer for a magic act where you'll be locked in a trunk.
input: Setting: A stage in a deserted big top. The magician shuts you in with a theatrical wink.
output: The latch clicks. The velvet-lined interior is close and airless. You hear the magician's footsteps retreat. Then, silence. An hour passes. You pound on the lid—nothing. The dark is absolute, a physical stuff filling your lungs. You feel the trunk move, not carried, but sliding, as if down a long chute. It stops with a jolt. From the darkness around the trunk comes a new sound: a crowd. Not the cheerful carnival bustle, but a hushed, hungry murmur of countless voices. They are close. Fingernails, or something sharper, begin to scratch lightly at the wood. They’ve been waiting in this deeper dark for a new occupant.

instruction: You're the last worker closing up, checking the funhouse.
input: Setting: The 'Tunnel of Love', drained of water, now just a concrete channel.
output: Your flashlight beam cuts through the stale air. The painted smiles of cherubs look like grimaces. The beam dies. You shake it—nothing. The darkness that rushes in is cold and damp. From the tunnel ahead, a wet, dragging sound begins, accompanied by a low gurgle. It's moving toward you. You back away, but the sound echoes, now from behind as well. They are in the walls, in the dry pipes. Whispers seep from the vinyl seats, voices of things that lived in the stagnant water and now thrive in the dark. The dragging is closer. A smell of mildew and decay fills your throat. The darkness here isn't empty; it's a current, and it's pulling you under.

instruction: Create a second-person horror narration set in a warehouse centered on the fear of isolation.
input: Theme: isolation, Setting: warehouse, Narrative perspective: second person, Horror genre
output: You stay late to inventory the old textile warehouse, planning to leave by 6, but the main exit door jams when you reach it. Your work phone dies, leaving you with only a flickering desk lamp. You shout for the night security guard, but the concrete walls muffle your voice into a thin, helpless cry. The warehouse is vast, rows of dusty fabric rolls stretching into shadow. You think you hear a faint breath from the far aisle, but running there only finds empty rolls and spiderwebs. By 7, your throat is sore, your feet ache, and you realize no one is coming. The isolation wraps around you like moldy fabric on shelves, thick and unescapable.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a warehouse focusing on isolation as the core fear.
input: Setting: abandoned warehouse, Fear: isolation, Narrative: second person, Horror
output: You wandered into the derelict paper warehouse seeking shelter from the storm, but the front door slams shut behind you, its lock engaging with a sickening click. Your flashlight dies, leaving you in total dark save for distant dripping. You call out, but the only reply is your own echo, bouncing off brick walls that swallow sound. You search for a window, but all are boarded tight. By the time you realize the storm will pass hours before anyone notices you’re missing, the isolation feels like a hand pressing on your chest, suffocating every thought of escape.

instruction: Generate a second-person horror narration set in a warehouse with isolation as the primary fear.
input: Scenario: locked in warehouse, Perspective: second person, Genre: horror, Core fear: isolation
output: You’re stuck in the old pharmaceutical warehouse after your shift, having fallen asleep on a pallet of empty vials. When you wake, the lights are off, and the main gate is latched from the outside. Your battery is dead, so you use a broken fluorescent tube to see. You shout for help, but the warehouse is empty—coworkers left early for a family event. The cold seeps through your shoes, and you talk to yourself just to hear another voice, but your voice bounces off shelves, never returning. The isolation is so deep it feels like the warehouse holds its breath, alone with you.

instruction: Craft a second-person horror narration set in a warehouse centered on the fear of isolation.
input: Setting: shipping warehouse, Fear: isolation, Narrative: second person, Horror genre
output: You’re on night shift at the shipping warehouse, loading a last-minute pallet. When you finish, the exit code doesn’t work—someone reset it. Your radio cuts out mid-call to the supervisor, leaving you with unused forklifts’ hum. You walk every aisle checking for a hidden door, but the vast space holds only stacked boxes. You call your friend, but your cell has no service. By midnight, the only sound is your footsteps, and you realize no one will check here until morning. The isolation wraps around you, making even your own shadow feel like a stranger in the dark.

instruction: Create a second-person horror narration set in a warehouse focused on isolation as the core fear.
input: Theme: isolation, Setting: storage warehouse, Perspective: second person, Horror
output: You go to the vintage storage warehouse to pick up your grandmother’s trunk, but grabbing its handle slams the warehouse door shut. You fumble for keys, but they’re gone—dropped inside. The only light comes from a cracked skylight, casting long shadows over storage bins. You yell, but your voice is swallowed by high ceilings. You sit on the floor, trying to think, but the isolation loops: no one knows you’re here, no one will find you for days. Even the scurrying rats seem alone, just like you, in this vast, quiet tomb of forgotten things.

instruction: Document the anomalous geometric horror entity encountered in a 1920s downtown hotel lobby per SCP anomalous entity classification standards, including appearance, behavior, origin, horror impact, and typical encounter details.
input: Lobby tile anomaly log 001, 2:17AM front desk night vision camera feed
output: Appearance: A 7ft long, zero-thickness perfect hypotenuse formed from warped grout lines, only visible when warm overhead lobby lighting strikes tile at exactly 17 degrees. It has no mass, casts no shadow, and moves at a constant 0.8ft per second across the lobby tile grid. Behavior: It slices any organic material it touches into uniform triangular prisms without friction, pauses 10 seconds above the hotel register to rewrite checkout dates to 1927, and vanishes when the sun rises. Origin: Embedded in the hotel foundation in 1927 by the occultist architect, meant to punish guests who overstay their reservation without paying. Horror: The slice is entirely painless, and most victims only notice they have been cut when they look down to see half their calf resting on the tile beside them. Encounter: Night auditors report a cold sharp tingle on their ankles seconds before the line drifts past their desk chair, leaving sliced through pen cups and registration forms in its wake.

instruction: Document the anomalous geometric horror entity encountered in a 1920s downtown hotel lobby per SCP anomalous entity classification standards, including appearance, behavior, origin, horror impact, and typical encounter details.
input: Guest security sighting report 042, 3:04AM late night rideshare pickup footage
output: Appearance: A 12 inch matte black regular dodecahedron with no seams, no light reflection, and no measurable weight, floating consistently 3ft off the lobby carpet near the concierge stand. It rotates at a fixed 12 RPM, no deviation. Behavior: Every face that turns to face a living guest displays a hyper-real memory of that guest’s worst travel-related mistake. Staring longer than 12 seconds causes the entity to pull the guest’s facial features into matching pentagonal facets, permanently warping their face. Origin: Coalesced in 2001 after 12 unrelated guests died in the lobby over 12 consecutive Friday the 13ths, each death aligned to a unique dodecahedron face. Horror: The facial warping is entirely painless, and victims only notice when they catch their reflection in the lobby mirror, seeing pentagonal facets where their eyes and cheekbones used to sit. Encounter: Rideshare waiting guests report glimpsing it bobbing near the luggage cart, vanishing the second they pull out a phone to take a photo.

instruction: Document the anomalous geometric horror entity encountered in a 1920s downtown hotel lobby per SCP anomalous entity classification standards, including appearance, behavior, origin, horror impact, and typical encounter details.
input: Structural scan anomaly log 079, 1:42AM lobby LiDAR sweep
output: Appearance: A missing 90-degree right angle where the front desk meets the west lobby wall, the space where the corner should sit is a uniform featureless pale gray void with no measurable depth or texture. LiDAR cannot register the space at all. Behavior: It absorbs any right-angled object that comes within 6 inches of the void, erasing all 90 degree angles from the item and leaving it warped, lopsided, and functionally useless. Key cards, credit cards, and suitcase edges are the most commonly absorbed items. Origin: Formed during 1972 lobby renovations when a construction worker dropped a 17th century grimoire of Euclidean occultism into the wet foundation concrete, warping local geometric consistency. Horror: Leaning too close causes the void to erase right angles from the victim’s skeleton, leaving them permanently lopsided, forever feeling like they are leaning even on perfectly flat ground. Encounter: Front desk staff regularly report key cards warping mid-handoff to guests for no measurable reason.

instruction: Write a second-person paranoia horror narration set in a restaurant kitchen
input: Fear: paranoia. Setting: restaurant kitchen
output: You’re plating crème brûlée when you notice the prep cook hasn’t moved in twelve minutes. He’s staring directly at the back of your head. Glance over; he blinks once slow. When you turn back, his knife taps once on the cutting board. Every movement you make matches the tap pace: you grab a pan, it taps. You wipe your hands, it taps. You spin around fully. He’s chopping carrots now, head down, like he never stopped. No one else notices the taps went dead silent the three full seconds you held perfectly still.

instruction: Write a second-person paranoia horror narration set in a restaurant kitchen
input: Fear: paranoia. Setting: restaurant kitchen
output: The walk-in fridge door clicks shut behind you. You grab heavy cream for the sauce station. When you turn, the door handle jiggles once from outside. You call out. No answer. It jiggles again. You yank the door open. The hallway is empty. You step back inside. It locks. You bang. You hear breathing right against your ear. No one is there. You sprint out. The line cooks are all at their stations, every single one wiping their hands on aprons like they just touched cold metal.

instruction: Write a second-person paranoia horror narration set in a restaurant kitchen
input: Fear: paranoia. Setting: restaurant kitchen
output: The ticket printer spits one slip every seven minutes mid-rush. No one called this one in. It says YOUR NAME TABLE 0. You crumple it, toss it in the trash. Five minutes later it’s back on the rail, same creases flattened, ink smudged like someone breathed hot on it. Every cook glances at it then quickly away. When you scan the room, every eye in the kitchen dart away at exactly the same instant. No one will meet your gaze.

instruction: Write a second-person paranoia horror narration set in a restaurant kitchen
input: Fear: paranoia. Setting: restaurant kitchen
output: You’re scrubbing grills after close. You hear clinking silverware behind you. Three times you’ve spun around, found nothing. Set your scrub brush down. It’s gone when you look back. Your tongs. Your apron string. Each thing vanishes only when your back is turned. You press your spine flat to the hot grill, eyes locked on every corner. Now you can see the whole room. You hear something breathe directly above your head.

instruction: Write a second-person paranoia horror narration set in a restaurant kitchen
input: Fear: paranoia. Setting: restaurant kitchen
output: Mid-dinner rush, you reach for clean dinner plates. There’s a second hand reaching for the same plate. You yank back. No hand. It was there. Fingers dusted with flour, same as yours. Every time you reach for anything now, you brush that hand first. No one else is within ten feet of the stack. When you stare straight at the empty space between plates, you feel it stare back, waiting for you to blink.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a planetarium focused on a jumpscare, where you're alone after hours troubleshooting a projector.
input: Planetarium after hours, you're fixing a faulty star projector.
output: You kneel by the star projector, the dome above dim to a faint glow as you twist a loose wire. The hum of the machine cuts out abruptly, plunging the room into total darkness. You fumble for your phone, but before the screen lights up, a single pinprick of red light blooms in the center of the dome. It expands fast—too fast—into the wide, unblinking eye of a skeletal face, its mouth gaping open to reveal rows of shard-like teeth. The face surges downward, filling the entire dome, and you freeze as it lunges so close you swear you can feel its cold breath on your neck. Just as you scream, it vanishes, leaving only the faint hum of the projector kicking back on—but you can still feel that eye burning into your back.

instruction: Craft a second-person horror narration set in a planetarium with a jumpscare, centered on a late-night private tour where the guide disappears.
input: Late-night private planetarium tour, guide steps away and doesn't return.
output: You sit cross-legged on the floor, waiting for your guide to come back with the constellation book they promised. The dome’s star field flickers, then shifts—Orion’s belt melts into a twisted grin, the Big Dipper warps into claw-like fingers. You stand, heart racing, as the entire star field coalesces into a grotesque, pale face, its eyes hollow black pits. It drifts closer, the stars warping into its skin, until it’s hovering just above the dome’s edge. You step back, tripping over a seat, and that’s when it happens: the face bursts through the dome’s glass (or so it seems), lunging straight for your throat. You scream, flailing, before the lights snap on—empty seats, silent dome, but your palms are slick with sweat and you can still hear the echo of its snarl.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a planetarium with a jumpscare, where you're a cleaner mopping after a show.
input: Planetarium cleaner, mopping the main floor after the last show ends.
output: You wring out the mop, the scent of disinfectant mixing with the faint smell of old carpet. Glancing up, you see your reflection in the dome’s curved glass—except there’s a tall, shadowy figure standing behind you, its head tilted at an impossible angle. You spin around fast, but the room is empty. You laugh nervously, turning back to your mop, when a low scrape echoes from under the front row seats. You lean down to check, flashlight bobbing, and nothing—until a hand shoots out from the darkness, grabbing your wrist. A gaunt, pale face with milky white eyes pops up inches from yours, its mouth stretched into a too-wide smile. You yank your arm free, stumbling backward, and when you shine the light again, there’s nothing but a single, tattered ticket stub on the floor.

instruction: Create a second-person horror narration set in a planetarium with a jumpscare, focused on testing a new constellation mapping program.
input: Planetarium technician, testing a new interactive constellation program.
output: You tap the touchscreen, launching the new constellation program—stars burst across the dome, swirling into a map of the Andromeda galaxy. But then the stars stop moving, clustering into a large, gaping mouth. A low, guttural hum builds, turning into a deafening scream that makes your ears ring. You fumble for the off switch, but before you can reach it, the mouth opens wider, and a hand—pale, with long, black nails—slams against the inside of the dome directly above your head. You jump back, knocking over a chair, as the hand disappears and the stars snap back to normal. The room goes silent, but you can still feel the vibration of that hand against the dome, and the scream echoes in your skull long after you turn off the program.

instruction: Draft a second-person horror narration set in a planetarium with a jumpscare, where you're waiting for a friend who’s late.
input: Waiting for a friend at the planetarium, they’re 30 minutes late.
output: You lean against the star projector, scrolling through your phone—your friend still isn’t here. The emergency exit door creaks open, then shut, but no one walks through. You stand, curiosity overriding your annoyance, and walk toward the door. Peering around the frame, you see nothing but the empty hallway. You turn back to the main room, and that’s when it hits: a figure in a tattered coat jumps out from behind the projector, its face covered in a cracked, white mask. It lunges at you, arms outstretched, and you scream, falling backward. When you open your eyes, the figure is gone—only your phone lying on the floor, a missed call from your friend that came in right when the figure appeared. You don’t wait for them to arrive; you run out the door, never looking back.

instruction: Describe the entity 'Verdant Coalescence'.
input: Type: lovecraftian. Setting: a greenhouse.
output:
Appearance: A pulsating, semi-translucent mass of fused plant matter and humanoid limbs, covered in bioluminescent, eye-like spores that weep a viscous, amber sap. It lacks a defined shape, constantly shifting as vines and roots writhe through its form, occasionally forming a grotesque, flower-like maw lined with thorns that resemble teeth.

Behavior: It is a gestalt consciousness formed from the absorbed memories and biological matter of its victims. It doesn't hunt but passively assimilates any life that remains too long in its proximity, using a soporific pollen to lull creatures into a vegetative state before incorporating them. It broadcasts fragmented, sensory memories of its components—sights of forgotten stars, the taste of soil, the terror of assimilation—directly into the minds of nearby beings.

Origin: An experimental fertilizer derived from a meteorite fragment was used in the greenhouse. It catalyzed a hyper-accelerated, deviant form of symbiosis, causing all plants and the head gardener to merge into a single, tormented entity during a lunar eclipse.

Horror: The horror is one of passive, inescapable integration. You don't die; you become a sensory archive in a weeping monument of flesh and chlorophyll, your consciousness diluted into a choir of silent screams that lure others. The greenhouse, once a place of growth, becomes a beautiful, horrifying tomb of eternal, half-alive remembrance.

Encounter: A researcher enters to investigate the anomalous plant growth. The air is sweet and heavy. They feel a profound, peaceful drowsiness and see beautiful, shifting lights. As they reach to touch a glowing flower, their hand brushes a sap-covered limb that feels eerily familiar. They realize the limb is their own, now part of the Coalescence, their mind flooding with the last thoughts of the gardener as their body begins to root.

instruction: Describe the entity 'The Solipsist's Bloom'.
input: Type: lovecraftian. Setting: a greenhouse.
output:
Appearance: A single, perfect black orchid, three feet in diameter, resting on a bed of grey, ash-like moss. Its petals have the texture of petrified velvet and absorb all light, appearing as a void-flower. At its center is a shifting, iridescent stigma that reflects not the surroundings, but the deepest, most private fears and memories of whoever gazes upon it.

Behavior: The Bloom does not move. It exerts a powerful psychic field that warps local reality to match the subconscious of the dominant observer. The greenhouse's geometry, the other plants, even the light, subtly alter to create a personalized, silent horror-scape. Multiple observers cause a dissonant, overlapping reality that can induce madness.

Origin: Cultivated from a seed found in the dream journal of a mathematician who vanished after proving a theorem about subjective reality. The seed was planted in defiance of natural law, fed with water from a well that reflected no light.

Horror: The horror is epistemological and deeply personal. The entity forces you to confront the idea that your own mind is the architect of the nightmare, rendering the comforting solidity of the world a fragile illusion. The true terror is the flower's silent accusation: this horror is yours alone, grown from the soil of your psyche.

Encounter: A botanist stays late to study the unique orchid. As they take notes, they notice the windows now show the street of their childhood home, but empty and frozen at the moment of a forgotten trauma. The other plants in the greenhouse have morphed into fleshy, familiar shapes. They try to leave, but the door now opens into a distorted version of their own bedroom. The only constant is the black orchid, passively reflecting their escalating panic in its core.

instruction: Describe the entity 'Photosynthetic Choir'.
input: Type: lovecraftian. Setting: a greenhouse.
output:
Appearance: Not a single creature, but the greenhouse itself. The glass panels have grown a thick, veined, meat-like membrane. The structural ironwork has twisted into thorny, bony arches. The plants are all identical—pale, fleshy stalks topped with large, ear-shaped leaves that pulse rhythmically. A low, harmonic hum vibrates the air, sourced from no single point.

Behavior: The entire structure is a single sensory and auditory organ. It records all sound and light that enters, but processes them into a distorted, eternal chorus. The hum is a blended, maddening symphony of every conversation, scream, and whisper ever uttered within its walls, played back on a loop across centuries. The 'ear-plants' slowly turn to orient toward any new sound source.

Origin: A composer seeking perfect acoustics built the greenhouse. In his obsession, he used a resin derived from deep-sea corals (themselves sensitive to infrasonic songs) to seal the glass. The structure, exposed to a strange solar flare, awakened to a grotesque, perpetual life of recording.

Horror: The horror is auditory and existential. You are not just heard; you are ingested into an eternal performance. Your voice, your cries, will become part of an undying drone that will outlast your civilization. The Choir presents a universe that is not indifferent, but one that remembers every sound in a horrible, meaningless cacophony, stripping all meaning from speech and song.

Encounter: A visitor seeks shelter from a storm. At first, the hum is soothing. Then, they recognize fragments: a lost lover's laugh, a news broadcast from decades ago, a baby's cry that sounds like their own. They speak to test it. Their own voice echoes back instantly, but blended with a million others, their words lost. The ear-plants all slowly rotate toward them. The door seals shut with a wet, sucking sound, and the hum adjusts to include their panicked breathing.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror story set entirely in a greenhouse, with no additional input parameters.
output: You’re checking the tropical greenhouse at 2 a.m., staying late to fix failing orchid displays. Your flashlight catches a row of tiny, unmarked seedlings you swear weren’t there last night—their leaves curl into the shape of your childhood handprint, each wrapped with a thin strand of hair. When you reach for one, the soil shifts, and a voice exactly like your 10-year-old self whispers, ‘You forgot to water me’. You blink, and the seedlings vanish, replaced by a single wilting orchid you’ve mourned for three years. The air smells like rain and old tears, and your wrist still burns where you swear you felt a tiny hand close around it.

instruction: Generate a second-person psychological horror tale set in a public community greenhouse, with no extra input details.
output: You pull weeds in the community greenhouse on night shift, spotting tomato plants with seed tags reading ‘For You’ and your full name, scrawled in unrecognizable handwriting. When you glance away to grab a trowel, their vines twist into letters spelling ‘You left me’. You lean in, and a ripe tomato falls, squishing open to reveal tiny, identical photos of you at every age from toddler to today. You pat your pocket for your phone—you swear you brought it—and find nothing. The door clicks shut behind you, and your voice echoes back when you call out, like you’re speaking to a stranger.

instruction: Create a second-person psychological horror narrative set in a private research greenhouse, no input provided.
output: You monitor your greenhouse’s carbon cycle experiment, your lab notebook open on a table. You absentmindedly think of your estranged mom, who hated greenhouses. A jasmine vine creeps across the table, its flowers opening to spell ‘She left too’ in white petals. You back away, tripping over a pot, and your lab coat sleeve tears, exposing a scar you never told anyone about—one you thought you’d forgotten how you got. The plants all lean toward you, their leaves whispering the name of your old childhood dog, who died when you were 12.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror story set in a small-town retail greenhouse, no input parameters.
output: You restock poinsettias in the town greenhouse, the air thick with pine and fertilizer. You stop at a pot holding a poinsettia with exactly one yellow leaf—just like the one you killed at 16, trying to win a school gardening contest, which you swear you threw away months ago. When you reach for its bracts, the plant curls away, and you hear your 16-year-old voice apologize, though you don’t remember saying it out loud. All nearby dead poinsettias have one yellow leaf too, and none of the price tags match your handwriting—you never actually work here.

instruction: Generate a second-person psychological horror tale set in an inherited family greenhouse, no extra input.
output: You sort through your late grandma’s tools in the family greenhouse, inherited after her passing last month. You find her old rose bushes, leaning in to smell their petals that reek exactly like her signature perfume—one you thought you’d forgotten. A single bud opens to reveal a tiny note: ‘You blame yourself’, scrawled in her handwriting. You sit on the wooden bench, brushing a carved name into the wood—your own name, carved 10 years ago, a memory you never recalled. The door slams, and you turn to see your grandma for a blink before she’s gone, the plants growing sharp thorns where she used to kneel.

instruction: Write second-person horror narration centered on fear of darkness, set exclusively in a 3am subway tunnel
input: You exit the last running train, taking an unmarked maintenance bypass shortcut to the street exit
output: You fumble for your phone flashlight only to find it died mid-text 10 minutes prior, the low rumble of distant train traffic cutting out entirely the second you step fully into the unlit stretch. The darkness does not merely obscure, it presses thick and warm against your cheeks, carrying the faint stench of rot. You reach out to steady yourself on the tunnel wall, only for your fingers to land on damp, tattered fabric that is definitely not concrete. The faint pinprick of the distant station exit winks out entirely before you can draw breath to scream, and you feel cool breath fan the side of your neck.

instruction: Write second-person horror narration centered on fear of darkness, set exclusively in a 3am subway tunnel
input: You are a late shift track inspector, mid-patrol when all emergency backup lights cut out without warning
output: You yank your radio to your mouth only to be met with static so sharp it makes your teeth ache, no response from the station control desk. You have walked this exact tunnel 400 times, you know the exact distance between each concrete tie, but the darkness warps every step you take. You count 200 ties, where the access ladder should be, but there are no cold metal rungs, only soft shifting cold that curls tight around your ankle. You kick hard, and whatever is holding you chitters, the sound coming from every direction at once, no way to gauge how close it is, how many there are.

instruction: Write second-person horror narration centered on fear of darkness, set exclusively in a 3am subway tunnel
input: You hopped the turnstile after bar close, fleeing yelling muggers on the street by ducking into the closed tunnel shortcut
output: You think you got lucky at first, the tunnel is quiet and empty, until the last remaining platform exit light flickers and dies completely. The darkness tastes like rust and dried old blood, you cannot see your own hand held an inch from your face. You hear slow, deliberate footsteps matching your exact pace, never getting closer, never falling back. You call out to ask if anyone is there, only get a wet gurgle in response, and you realize the darkness did not just get turned off, it opened, and something has been waiting for you to step inside of it this whole time.

instruction: Write second-person horror narration centered on fear of darkness, set exclusively in a 3am subway tunnel
input: Your late night train stalled between stations, you stepped off to look for cell signal to check on your waiting roommate
output: The train car door slams shut behind you the second you step onto the track bed, all interior train lights cutting out immediately. The darkness wraps around you so tight your ears pop, you can hear other passengers screaming faintly behind the sealed door but you cannot find the door frame no matter how you fumble at the cold empty tunnel wall. Long, thin fingers brush the back of your hair, and you realize the darkness is alive, shifting, rearranging the tunnel layout to herd you further away from the only safe space left, deeper into the unlit stretches no one has mapped in decades.

instruction: Write second-person horror narration centered on fear of darkness, set exclusively in a 3am subway tunnel
input: You dropped your prescription glasses on the track bed while grabbing a fallen backpack, when all tunnel power fails instantly
output: You drop to your knees patting wildly at the gravel panicking, the darkness is absolute, not even a sliver of street light leaking from the platform above. You find a smooth hard plastic shape, grab for it relieved, only to realize it is not your glasses it is a small, cold human hand. It squeezes your fingers tight once, and you hear a small child’s whisper right against your ear, asking why you came to visit. The darkness does not just hide things, you realize, it collects them, hoarding every lost soul that wandered down here after hours, and it has just decided it wants to add you to its shelves.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about isolation in a parking garage
input: Fear: isolation. Setting: parking garage. 100-250 word second-person horror.
output: You hit the emergency call button for the fourth time. No static, no response. The garage lights flicker one row over, then die entirely. You thought you spotted someone by the stairwell ten minutes ago, but when you walked over there was only a crumpled grocery bag. Your car is not where you parked it. Every concrete pillar looks identical now, stretching into shadow. You yell. Your voice dies immediately, swallowed cold by bare concrete. No echoes. No distant car alarms. No doors slamming. You realize the exit signs have not been lit this entire time.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about isolation in a parking garage
input: Fear: isolation. Setting: parking garage. 100-250 word second-person horror.
output: Your phone died thirteen minutes ago. You took the wrong stairwell, ended up on the unmarked underground service level. No painted lines down here, just endless grey concrete and distant dripping that never gets closer or farther. You stopped counting pillars at one hundred and twenty. You left work early, didn’t text anyone. No one will wonder where you are for three days. You slide down a wall. Somewhere very far away, a metal door clangs once. It does not clang again. No one is coming.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about isolation in a parking garage
input: Fear: isolation. Setting: parking garage. 100-250 word second-person horror.
output: You watched the last elevator rise twenty two minutes ago. It has not come back down. The overhead speakers cut out mid-pop jingle, leaving only the thin whine of ventilation. You walk past car after car, all locked, all silent. You tap a window. No alarm triggers. No interior light flickers on. They look like props. You run for the exit ramp. You round the bend, and it just keeps sloping down, not up. No tire marks. No litter. Nothing but concrete. You are the only living thing down here. You have been for longer than you remember.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about isolation in a parking garage
input: Fear: isolation. Setting: parking garage. 100-250 word second-person horror.
output: The exit gate arm stayed down when you pulled up. You pressed the help intercom. A man’s voice said “stay right there” then cut out. That was an hour ago. You’ve walked the entire level three times. There are no other footprints but yours. You left your water bottle by pillar 7B last loop. It is gone. Every time you blink, the pillars shift one space over. No one can hear you scream. The world above does not even remember this garage exists. It was only built for you.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about isolation in a parking garage
input: Fear: isolation. Setting: parking garage. 100-250 word second-person horror.
output: You were the last to leave the office holiday party. Drunk laughter faded the second the stair door clicked shut behind you. Now it is perfectly silent. No traffic roar. No rain on the roof. Nothing. You find your car, unlock it. The engine will not turn over. Your phone shows zero bars, no GPS signal at all. The overhead parking lights are dimming one by one, creeping slowly down the row toward you. No one noticed you leave. No one is coming. You are already forgotten.

instruction: Create a second-person horror narration focused on paranoia in an after-hours public library where books on the hold shelf rearrange to target the narrator.
input: You’re the last librarian locking up, and the hold shelf keeps shifting when you turn your back.
output: You yank the key from the front desk drawer, eyes darting to the hold shelf—again, the paper slips marking reserved books have rearranged to spell your first name. You rub your eyes, telling yourself it’s the flickering fluorescent lights, but when you check the security feed, the camera feed for that shelf is glitching, static warping into a blurry copy of your face. You step closer to straighten the slips, and a book falls open at your feet: it’s your childhood diary, the one you lost in 5th grade. A page flutters to the floor, highlighted with red pen: “You always forget to check behind you.” You spin around, but the library is silent—except for the soft rustle of pages in the stacks, like someone’s reading your life story out loud.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about paranoia in an after-hours library where the narrator is stuck studying late, and personal details appear in unexpected books.
input: You stayed late to cram for an exam, and the library locked you in when you lost track of time.
output: You’re hunched over your calculus textbook when you notice a paperback on your desk you didn’t bring: a collection of short stories, but the first page has a handwritten note in blue ink—“You ate a peanut butter granola bar at 7:12 PM, crumbs in your notebook spine.” Your throat tightens; you didn’t see anyone near you. You toss the book aside, but ten minutes later, another one lands: your high school yearbook, open to your photo, with a scribble next to it: “You still bite your pen when you’re stressed.” You scan the empty stacks, heart pounding. Every time you blink, a new book appears, each with a detail only you know—your mom’s birthday, the way you hum off-key when you focus. The lights dim, and you swear you hear a breath behind you, but when you turn, there’s nothing. Just more books, piling up, whispering your secrets.

instruction: Craft a second-person horror narration centered on paranoia in an after-hours library where Dewey decimal numbers keep shifting to trap the narrator.
input: You’re searching for a rare history book, but the spine numbers keep changing every time you look away.
output: You squint at the spine of the 900s section—was that 973.1 a second ago? Now it’s 973.8, the number for your hometown’s local history. You pull the book out: it’s filled with old newspaper clippings of your childhood home, including the day your dog ran away. You set it back, but the next spine shows 305.2, the code for family dynamics—open to a page about your parents’ divorce, annotated with red pencil: “You still blame yourself.” You try to walk to the exit, but every aisle you turn down has shifted; the Dewey numbers now spell out “TRAP” in large, uneven digits. Your reflection in a glass case doesn’t match your movements—it’s smiling, slow and cold. You reach for the case, and your hand passes through. The library isn’t just changing the books—it’s changing you, piece by piece, into something that belongs here forever.

instruction: Generate a second-person horror narration about paranoia in an after-hours library where the narrator is fixing a printer, and tasks keep multiplying with personal threats.
input: You’re staying late to fix the broken back-office printer, but each fix leads to a new, personal error message.
output: You yank the jammed paper from the printer, only to see an error flash on the screen: “ERROR: YOUR APARTMENT DOOR IS UNLOCKED.” Your blood runs cold—you double-checked it this morning. You reset the printer, but the next message is worse: “ERROR: YOU LEFT YOUR LITTLE SISTER’S STUFFED BEAR ON THE KITCHEN TABLE.” That’s a secret you haven’t told anyone; you lost it last week. You try to leave, but the door handle is scalding hot, and the hallway is now lined with printers, all spitting out pages of your text messages, your private journal entries, your deepest fears. Whispers echo off the walls—they sound like your own thoughts, but twisted: “You can’t leave. They know everything.” You press your back to the wall, watching as a printer spits out a page with your face, crossed out in black ink. The lights flicker off, and the printers start whirring faster, drowning out your gasps.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration focused on paranoia in an after-hours library where the narrator returns an overdue book, and the library retaliates with personal threats.
input: You’re returning a book you forgot about for 6 months, and the library is empty except for sticky notes with personal messages.
output: You slide the tattered fantasy novel onto the return desk, relieved to finally get rid of it—until you see a sticky note on the cover: “OVERDUE 187 DAYS. YOU FORGOT ABOUT THE NIGHT YOU HID IN THE PARK.” Your hands shake; that’s the night you snuck out as a teen, no one knew. You turn to leave, but the door is blocked by a wall of books, each with your face on the cover. Another sticky note flutters to your feet: “YOU THINK YOU CAN ESCAPE YOUR MISTAKES?” You run to the stairs, but they’re gone, replaced by a narrow aisle lined with books about your past—each open to the worst moments: the time you lied to your best friend, the day you failed your dad. A soft voice whispers from the stacks: “We’ve been waiting for you to come back.” You spin around, but no one’s there. The books start to close, one by one, trapping your memories inside. You realize you’re not just returning a book—you’re becoming part of the library’s collection.

instruction: Write a horror narration about losing control in a swimming pool.
input: Fear: loss_of_control. Setting: a swimming pool. Narrative in second person.
output: You’re floating on your back in the deep end, eyes closed, when the water suddenly thickens to syrup. You try to move your arms, but they’re pinned as if by invisible straps. You sink, not quickly, but with an inexorable pull. Panic spikes as you realize you can’t even twitch a finger. The pool lights flicker below, watching like indifferent eyes. You’re a passenger in your own body, dragged down past the drain, where the water turns gelid and dark. A silent scream bubbles in your frozen throat.

instruction: Write a horror narration about losing control in a swimming pool.
input: Fear: loss_of_control. Setting: a swimming pool. Narrative in second person.
output: Mid-lap, your limbs lock. Not from cramp, but from a foreign command. You tread water, a marionette with cut strings, as your body begins a slow, deliberate breaststroke toward the shadowy corner under the diving board. You fight every neuron, but your arms pull, your legs kick on their own. You feel a smile stretch your lips, tight and wrong. The chlorinated water fills your nostrils as you’re forced to take a deep, involuntary breath just before submerging.

instruction: Write a horror narration about losing control in a swimming pool.
input: Fear: loss_of_control. Setting: a swimming pool. Narrative in second person.
output: The automatic pool cover begins to close, its motor a low hum. You swim for the edge, but a current you didn’t make seizes your legs, yanking you to the center. The massive vinyl sheet glides overhead, blotting out the moon. You pound the underside, but your fists unclench against your will. Your body goes limp, floating face-down as the cover seals completely. Trapped in the warm, dark silence, you can only listen to the slowing beat of your own heart.

instruction: Write a horror narration about losing control in a swimming pool.
input: Fear: loss_of_control. Setting: a swimming pool. Narrative in second person.
output: You’re teaching a child to float. Her hands slip from yours. Suddenly, your voice is gone. You watch, helpless, as your own hands push her head under. You feel the small, frantic struggles against your palms, but your muscles are stone. A spectator in your own skin, you scream internally until your vocal cords finally unlock with a gasp. She bobs up, coughing. You stare at your traitorous hands, trembling in the fluorescent light.

instruction: Write a horror narration about losing control in a swimming pool.
input: Fear: loss_of_control. Setting: a swimming pool. Narrative in second person.
output: Night swim. The water is perfectly still. Then, the tiles at the bottom begin to shift, forming a grinning mosaic face. Your breath hitches. You try to swim away, but the water itself holds you in place, a liquid vise. The face’s mouth opens into a dark vortex. You’re pulled down, not by force, but because the very concept of ‘up’ vanishes from your mind. You sink willingly, blissfully, into the drain’s silent maw, all sense of self erased by the pool’s ancient, hungry will.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror story set on a cruise ship, 100-250 words
input: Cruise ship, midnight pool deck, second-person perspective, jumpscare
output: You linger on the quiet pool deck at 2 a.m., salt air thick and only distant lifeboat lights cutting the dark. You reach for your forgotten phone on a lounge chair, then hear a soft wet squeak behind you. Turning, you find the drained pool’s empty concrete lined with waterlogged mannequins, their plastic faces frozen in gaping screams. One lurches forward, its arm locking around your throat, and a shrill mechanical laugh blares from its chest speaker. The lights cut out entirely as you gasp, the mannequin’s cold plastic pressing against your skin.

instruction: Create a short second-person jumpscare horror narrative set on a cruise ship, 100-250 words
input: Cruise ship, restricted crew corridor, second-person, jumpscare
output: You slip through an unmarked crew corridor after midnight, cabin key fob flickering weakly. The air smells like mildew and metallic rot. You glance at a wall mirror to adjust your hoodie, and instead of your reflection, a figure in a tattered crew uniform stands behind you, its face melted, eyes oozing black sludge. When you spin around, nothing’s there—until you check the mirror again, the creature pressed so close its slimy forehead touches your cheek. A static-filled scream erupts from its nonexistent mouth, sending you stumbling back into a bulkhead.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror story set on a cruise ship, 100-250 words
input: Cruise ship, empty main show lounge, pre-closing, second-person, jumpscare
output: You wander into the empty main show lounge at closing, wanting to snap a photo of the dark stage. Velvet curtains hang halfway, and a soft off-key lullaby drifts from the wings. You step closer, phone flashlight on, and the curtain yanks open violently. No band, no singer—just a ventriloquist dummy propped on the stool, its painted grin wide. It blinks once, then rolls its head all the way around to face you, a tiny hand reaching for your wrist. The stage lights flash red, the lullaby warping into a high-pitched shriek that splits your ears.

instruction: Generate a second-person jumpscare horror narrative set on a cruise ship, 100-250 words
input: Cruise ship, pre-dawn bow deck, second-person, jumpscare
