output: You climb the bow deck at 5 a.m. to watch the sunrise, wind whipping your hair, no other passengers in sight. The ocean stretches dark and empty, only the ship’s low engine hum around you. You lean over the railing, and something hard hits your shoulder. Turning, you spot a life preserver tied to the rail—but it’s not empty. It wraps around a rotting corpse, its blue face bulging, hand clutching the preserver’s strap. When you gasp and stumble back, the corpse’s mouth flies open, and a flock of seagulls erupts from its throat, squawking and pecking at your face as the first hint of sun peeks over the horizon.

instruction: Write a short second-person jumpscare horror story set on a cruise ship, 100-250 words
input: Cruise ship, late-night arcade, second-person, jumpscare
output: You’re the only one left in the cruise’s arcade after midnight, trying your luck on a claw machine. The lights flicker, and the machine’s clinking gears go silent. You lean in to see what it dropped, and the claw lifts not a stuffed bear, but a tattered bloody doll, its glass eyes staring directly at you. As the claw hovers, the doll’s mouth opens, a recorded scream blares at top volume. Its arm flails to wrap around your hand, cold plastic pressing into your skin. You yank back, and every arcade machine turns on at once, their screens showing distorted, screaming faces.

instruction: Generate a unique geometric horror entity entry set in an abandoned night-time shopping mall, following SCP/Silent Hill cosmic horror design parameters
input: Geometric entity, night shopping mall setting
output: Appearance: A 7ft tall stack of misaligned cream mall floor tiles, each rotated 15 degrees off the one below, no visible face or limbs, edges glistening with tacky melted grout that reeks of old floor wax. Behavior: It only moves when you break direct eye contact, rearranging grout seams in the west wing corridor to seal off exit paths, counting the footfalls of anyone who wandered past official closing time. Origin: It formed over 32 years of collective shopper frustration, thousands of lost patrons staring at the same cracked tile seam while trying to find the food court, merging that unconscious unease into a sentient geometric eidolon. Horror: If it catches you, it presses you into the floor seam, fusing you into a new tile in its stack, no future visitors will ever be able to tell you were once human. Encounter: A teen sneaking in to skate after closing looked away for 2 seconds to answer a text, and the corridor he entered through was completely gone, only the warden standing 10ft away.

instruction: Generate a unique geometric horror entity entry set in an abandoned night-time shopping mall, following SCP/Silent Hill cosmic horror design parameters
input: Geometric entity, night shopping mall setting
output: Appearance: A twisted perfect spiral formed from the mall’s central atrium escalators, no longer connecting any physical floors, handrails sharp polished black polyhedron edges, steps bleeding faint pulsing pink fluorescent light. Behavior: It lures lone visitors by playing faint distorted snippets of 90s food court jingles, anyone who steps onto its surface will never reach the top, each full loop stretching their limbs to match the curve of the spiral. Origin: It coalesced in 1998 after a maintenance worker fell to his death down the original escalator, his dying perception of infinite falling seeped into the machine’s wiring and warped it into a permanent impossible geometric anomaly. Horror: After 7 full loops, your bones will fuse into the steps as new ridged edges, trapped forever in a state of half-fall, half-ascent with no escape. Encounter: A night security guard doing atrium patrol found all 4 main escalators twisted into the helix, the jingle of the Cinnabon he ate on his first day of work playing from its unreachable top.

instruction: Generate a unique geometric horror entity entry set in an abandoned night-time shopping mall, following SCP/Silent Hill cosmic horror design parameters
input: Geometric entity, night shopping mall setting
output: Appearance: A floating 5ft wide perfect dodecahedron, each flat face printed with a faded ad for a long-defunct 90s mall kiosk product, no limbs or sensory organs, edges humming with the static frequency of old CRT televisions. Behavior: It drifts slowly toward any lone person after hours, projecting 12 overlapping nonsensical offers at the same time, any verbal agreement to one will warp the local reality to match the advertisement printed on that face. Origin: It formed from 28 years of discarded marketing flyers, thousands of ignored sales pitches from forgotten kiosk staff merging into a sentient geometric entity that feeds on unconsidered consumer desire. Horror: If you accept an offer, you will be trapped as a 2D graphic on the face of the dodecahedron forever, only visible for split seconds to passing patrons who will immediately forget they saw you. Encounter: A night cleaner rounding a corner to restock bathroom supplies spotted the floating entity, one face glowing bright red advertising a ‘free permanent unlimited mall pass’.

instruction: Generate second-person psychological horror narration set in a factory
input: Focus on counting discrepancy on a stamping press line, overnight shift
output: You run the stamping press count. Every shift you stamp exactly 720 brackets. Last night the machine logged 721. You recount the bin three times. 721. No extra metal was checked in. The foreman says you miscounted. Tonight you count out loud. One. Two. 720. You hit stop. The machine stamps once more, slow, on its own. On the tray is a perfect little human thumb. It has the chipped silver nail polish you applied Sunday. You stare at your own hand. All fingers are there. You check again. They are all there. You don’t remember painting them Tuesday.

instruction: Generate second-person psychological horror narration set in a factory
input: Focus on the break room mirror during graveyard shift
output: You slump into the break room at 3:12am. The cracked mirror above the sink still has last month’s graffiti. You wipe sweat off your jaw. Your reflection doesn’t move. It just stares, breathing slow while you freeze. It blinks once, then lifts one hand to tap the glass twice. You don’t move. You hear the exact same tap through the cinder block wall behind you. You turn. Nothing is there. When you look back the mirror is empty. Every shift after that you feel someone breathing directly against the back of your neck while you work. You never turn.

instruction: Generate second-person psychological horror narration set in a factory
input: Focus on the factory PA system during shutdown
output: You’re closing down line 4 alone. The PA crackles. It’s not the foreman. It’s your voice. Exact tired drawl, exact little nasal lisp no one ever comments on. It reads off your grocery list for tomorrow. Then it laughs, quiet, the way you laugh when you’re alone in the car. You yank the main power breaker. The entire factory goes black. The PA keeps whispering. It says you left the back gate unlocked. It says you should not have come in today. It says you already left. You check your phone. Your location is marked 12 miles west, on the highway home.

instruction: Generate second-person psychological horror narration set in a factory
input: Focus on uniform name tags and identity erosion
output: End of shift, you yank open your locker. Your uniform is gone. In its place is another identical one: same coffee stain, same frayed cuff. The name tag says MARTIN. You don’t know a Martin. Next night it says LISA. Then RAUL. Then your name, spelled wrong. No one calls you by name anymore. No one meets your eye. Yesterday the foreman handed you a termination slip for an employee who fell into the smelter last year. You signed it without thinking. You can’t remember what your own name sounded like when spoken out loud.

instruction: Generate second-person psychological horror narration set in a factory
input: Focus on conveyor belt repetition and time loops
output: You clip wire harnesses to the belt. You’ve done 417 today. You recognize the 418th. Same scratch on the plastic case, same bent pin. You clipped that one three hours ago. You set it aside. The next one is identical. Then the next. The belt keeps feeding you the exact same part, over and over. You check the clock. It’s 2:17. It was 2:17 when you saw the first one. The man at the station beside you hasn’t blinked. He hasn’t moved his hands. You yell. He doesn’t react. You realize you haven’t taken a breath in seven minutes.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a church confessional focusing on isolation, where the confessor realizes the priest is absent and the door locks shut.
input: Church confessional, isolation fear, second-person perspective
output: You slide into the confessional’s vinyl bench, hands shaking as you start your confession—“Bless me, Father, for I have sinned…” The silence stretches. You repeat it, louder. No hum of a priest’s robe, no quiet “Go on.” You lean toward the grille, fingers brushing cold metal. The other side is empty. A chill crawls up your spine as you reach for the door handle. It doesn’t turn. The church’s distant organ fades, then the creak of pews stops. Now there’s only your breath, ragged in the tight space. You pound on the door, scream for help—but the walls swallow the sound. The grille’s slats feel like bars. You’re alone, trapped with every unspoken sin, the confessional a tomb built just for you. No one will come. No one knows you’re here.

instruction: Create a second-person horror story in a church confessional where the listener is not a priest, and you’re trapped with no way to call for help, emphasizing total isolation.
input: Church confessional, isolation fear, second-person perspective
output: You murmur your confession into the dark grille, expecting the priest’s soft response. Instead, a guttural, breathless rattle echoes back. You freeze—this isn’t a man’s voice. You scramble for the door, but it’s stuck. The church’s lights cut out; the only glow is a faint crack of moonlight through the grille. You call out, but the sound dies in your throat. The rattle starts again, closer, like something is pressing against the other side of the grille. You press your back to the door, heart hammering. No footsteps pass outside, no voices. The confessional is a bubble of silence, save for that rattle and your own panicked gasps. You realize no one will hear you scream. You’re alone with whatever is on the other side, and it’s listening.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration in a church confessional where the space becomes physically isolating—frozen, no outside sounds, trapped alone with your guilt.
input: Church confessional, isolation fear, second-person perspective
output: You duck into the confessional to escape the rain, wrapping your arms around your shivering chest. You mutter a quick confession, but no one answers. The air grows icy, so cold your fingers go numb. You try to leave, but the door won’t budge. The rain’s patter fades, then the distant traffic vanishes. Now there’s only the sound of your teeth chattering and the thud of your heart. You press your ear to the door—no church bells, no footsteps, nothing. The grille’s slats are frosted over; you can’t see the other side. You realize the confessional is shrinking, the walls closing in slow enough you almost don’t notice. You’re trapped, alone with the weight of your sins, in a frozen box that’s slowly squeezing the life out of you. No one will find you here.

instruction: Craft a second-person horror entry in a church confessional where isolation amplifies your own voice into a haunting presence, trapping you alone with the echoes of your sins.
input: Church confessional, isolation fear, second-person perspective
output: You whisper your worst sin into the grille, your voice cracking. The echo bounces off the walls, longer than it should be. “I didn’t stop them,” you repeat. The echo comes back, deeper, slower: “I didn’t stop them.” You flinch—this isn’t your voice. You try to stand, but your legs are weak. The echo grows louder, overlapping your own words until it’s like another person is in the confessional with you. But when you peer through the grille, it’s empty. The door is locked. You scream, and the echo screams back, louder, angrier. The walls seem to vibrate with the sound of your guilt. You’re alone, but you’re not—your own voice has turned against you, trapped in the same small space, repeating your sin on loop. There’s no escape from yourself here.

instruction: Generate a second-person horror story in a church confessional where you hide to escape danger, only to become trapped in total isolation, with no way to know if the threat is still outside or if you’re truly alone.
input: Church confessional, isolation fear, second-person perspective
output: You slam the confessional door shut, locking it behind you as you hear heavy footsteps thud past. You press your hand to your mouth, trying to muffle your breathing. The footsteps fade, but you don’t dare move. Minutes stretch into hours. You call out softly—no answer. You jiggled the door handle; it’s stuck. The church is dead silent. You can’t hear any cars, any birds, any life outside. You pull out your phone—no signal. The grille is too narrow to see through, so you have no idea if the threat is still waiting outside, or if everyone has left. The dark presses in, and you realize you’re trapped. You’re alone in this small, airless box, with no way out, no way to know what’s beyond the door. The silence is deafening, and every creak of the confessional makes you jump. You’re not safe—you’re just alone.

instruction: Generate a horror entity entry.
input: Type: lovecraftian. Setting: a morgue.
output:
Appearance: The Tabulator manifests as a mass of iridescent, semi-translucent viscera that pulsates with a faint inner light, resembling preserved organs woven together by threads of black static. It lacks a defined shape, but constantly extrudes and retracts thin, needle-like appendages tipped with human teeth. Its surface reflects the cold fluorescence of the morgue lights in impossible, non-Euclidean patterns.
Behavior: It does not move to attack, but to catalog. It seeks out corpses, particularly those with unresolved trauma or unconfessed secrets. Upon contact, its needles delicately dissect the cadaver, not to consume, but to inscribe the subject's memories and final moments onto its own shifting flesh in tiny, shimmering script. The process is silent and precise.
Origin: It is believed to be a psychic echo from a dimension where thought and flesh are one, drawn to places of silent, concentrated human ending. It may have been summoned by a pathologist's obsessive, decades-long study of the moment of death, creating a sympathetic resonance.
Horror: The horror is existential and invasive. The Tabulator renders the sanctity of death and the privacy of one's final thoughts obsolete. Witnessing it work reveals that nothing is ever truly buried; even in death, your experiences can be harvested and added to a grotesque, living library.
Encounter: A night guard notices a soft, bioluminescent glow emanating from the cold storage room. Inside, they find the entity hovering over the body of a John Doe. As they watch, frozen, words begin to form and crawl across the thing's surface—the last, panicked thoughts of the corpse, now eternally recorded in a language of flesh and light.

instruction: Generate a horror entity entry.
input: Type: lovecraftian. Setting: a morgue.
output:
Appearance: The Stillborn Choir appears as a cluster of pale, fetal shapes fused at the spine, suspended from the ceiling by umbilical cords of calcified tendon. Each 'infant' has a face of serene, adult features, with mouths sewn shut by surgical thread. Their eyes are open, pupils dilated into voids that drink the light. A low, polyphonic hum emanates from their throats, vibrating the air.
Behavior: It is stationary and passive until a living being expresses grief or regret near the dead. The hum then shifts, forming whispered words that are the exact, unspoken apologies or laments the visitor wished to convey to their own lost ones. It offers a terrible, unwanted catharsis.
Origin: A coalescence of the psychic residue of terminated pregnancies and stillborn children autopsied in the morgue over a century, combined with the profound, unresolved guilt of the living who left them there. It is a monument to silenced potential and maternal/paternal despair.
Horror: The horror is one of profound, stolen intimacy and psychological violation. It forces you to hear your most private sorrows echoed back by a thing that should not know them, rendering your grief public and grotesque. It suggests that emotion itself can congeal into a parasitic entity that feeds on remorse.
Encounter: A grieving mother, there to identify a son, breaks down in a sterile hallway. The air grows thick and cold. From a shadowed autopsy theater, she hears her own voice, whispering the words she never got to say to him. Investigating, she finds the Choir, its many faces now turned toward her, tears of black fluid tracing down their cheeks.

instruction: Generate a horror entity entry.
input: Type: lovecraftian. Setting: a morgue.
output:
Appearance: The Autopsy of Ialdagor manifests as a single, impossibly large and complex surgical incision hovering in mid-air, as if cut into reality itself. The 'wound' gapes open, revealing not anatomy, but a swirling, star-filled void. Around its edges, spectral surgical tools—scalpels, retractors, bone saws—drift and move with silent purpose, gleaming under the morgue lights. A smell of ozone and formalin precedes it.
Behavior: It does not interact with the living directly. Instead, it performs meticulous, silent autopsies on the concept of death within its vicinity. Its tools dissect the silence, the cold, and the finality, peeling them back to reveal the writhing, non-life that underpins mortal cessation. This process causes localized reality failures: time loops, corpse reanimation as puppet-like husks, and the silencing of all sound.
Origin: It is the lingering instrument of an outer god's curiosity, a fragment of a cosmic process left behind after it 'examined' the phenomenon of human death at this location. It is not alive; it is a residual action, a verb made manifest.
Horror: The horror is cosmic and ontological. The entity treats death not as a tragedy or an end, but as a mere specimen to be dispassionately analyzed. Its presence proves that our greatest certainty is just a curious anomaly to forces beyond comprehension, and its 'examination' unravels the very fabric of reality around it.
Encounter: A pathologist arrives to find a Y-incision already perfectly performed on a new arrival, but the body is untouched. The tools hang in the air beside it. When she tries to speak, no sound emerges. The corpse on the slab sits up, its movements jerky and precise, and begins mimicking her silent scream with mechanical accuracy, as the void within the hovering incision slowly rotates to face her.

instruction: Create a second-person horror narrative centered on paranoia set in a greenhouse, where you suspect an unseen intruder is copying your actions
input: Paranoia-focused horror: you tend a private greenhouse, staying late to harvest herbs, convinced someone is mimicking your every move
output: You lock the greenhouse door at dusk, setting your clipping shears on the mint bed then turning to grab your basket—only to find the shears already in your hand, even though you left them on the potting shelf ten minutes prior. Your throat tightens; you’d swear you checked the window latches, but one is now ajar, even the smallest gap possible. A soft scratch comes from the seed storage corner, and you reach for your phone’s flashlight, only to hear your own voice say, “Check the back shelf,” coming from the dark. You didn’t speak. When you shine the light, your basket is on the back shelf, exactly where you told yourself you set it earlier. You step closer, and the floor’s shadow stretches longer behind you. You whirl, but no one’s there. Still, you hear breathing—steady, familiar, exactly like your own.

instruction: Create a second-person horror narrative centered on paranoia set in a greenhouse, where you suspect the plants are observing and manipulating you
input: Paranoia-focused horror: you’re a part-time caretaker at a community greenhouse, staying late to water tropicals, convinced plants are tracking you
output: You adjust the misting nozzles for the orchid tank, their hiss mixing with the fridge’s hum. You swore you moved the venus flytrap ten feet to the sunlit path yesterday—but today, it’s back by the entrance, its trigger hairs twitching like it’s watching you. You check your logbook, noting you placed it by the door; you did, you never forget. A succulent pot cracks from the shelf, and when you lean over, all succulents are tilted toward you, their plump leaves glowing under grow lights. You feel eyes on your neck, turn, and see a spider weaving a web where you just stood. You back away, knocking over a full watering can—it lands empty, even though you filled it five minutes ago.

instruction: Create a second-person horror narrative centered on paranoia set in a greenhouse, where you suspect your own mind is playing tricks on you to isolate you
input: Paranoia-focused horror: you clear out an old family greenhouse, staying late to sort pots, convinced you’re forgetting things or repeating actions
output: You reach for a terracotta pot on the top shelf, and it shatters at your feet, even though you didn’t push it. You’d set it there an hour prior when you started clearing. You wipe dirt from your hands and notice your work gloves are inside out—you don’t remember flipping them. A familiar hum, your grandma’s old watering song, drifts from the back room where her can sits. When you walk in, the can is empty, even though you filled it before entering. You glance at the wall mirror and see a woman behind you, wearing the same dress, same hair tie. She lifts a hand, you mirror her, and when she opens her mouth to speak, you step back—she’s gone. Your phone, which was at 100%, is now dead.

instruction: Create a second-person horror narrative centered on paranoia set in a greenhouse, where you suspect an invisible intruder is sabotaging your supplies
input: Paranoia-focused horror: you prepare for a plant sale at a city greenhouse, staying late to sort seeds, convinced someone is sneaking in to mess with them
output: You set seed packets on the workbench, ordering sunflowers, tomatoes, marigolds by type, then step outside for potting soil. When you return, the packets are rearranged: sunflowers in the lettuce bag, marigolds with tomato seeds. No one else is here. You pull up security cam footage, but it’s static—just like when you turned it on. You swear you saw a flicker in the hanging basket corner, so you grab your trowel. It slips from your hand, clattering, and when you bend to pick it up, there’s a faint foot indent in the soil—no shoe print, just a shadow shape. Your keys, which were on the entrance hook, are gone. You hear a soft laugh, quiet like a coworker, but you can’t place who it is.

instruction: Create a second-person horror narrative centered on paranoia set in a greenhouse, where you suspect you’re being followed by a figure that looks like you
input: Paranoia-focused horror: you tend a small backyard greenhouse, staying late to tend tomatoes, convinced you’re being stalked by someone who looks identical
output: You reach for a ripe tomato, and your fingers brush a cold hand—your hand, but with a scar on the wrist you got pruning roses last spring. You jerk back, heart hammering, checking the yard: no one’s there, even though you heard gravel crunch. You turn back and hear your own voice say, “Lock the door,” even though you didn’t speak. The door, which you unlocked ten minutes prior, won’t budge when you pull. You fumble with the latch, and when you glance at the tomato vines, the figure stands there, wearing your flannel, your baseball cap. It doesn’t move, just stares. It says, in your exact tone, “You’re too late.” The words echo, and the floor beneath you feels like it’s shifting.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on loss of control, set exclusively on a residential apartment rooftop
input: You snuck up to your 12-story apartment building rooftop to decompress after a brutal, anxiety-inducing work week, the door locked behind you by accident
output: You lean against the cinder block parapet to light a cigarette, and suddenly your body goes rogue. Your feet shift closer to the drop without you telling them to, every muscle moving against the frantic screams of your rational brain. You try to curl your fingers around the metal railing to hold yourself back, but your hands won’t close, your palms going limp and cold. The wind tugs at the hem of your jacket like small, insistent hands, pushing you forward inch by inch. You can’t make your jaw open to scream, can’t twist your hips to step back, you can only watch in terror as your own weight tilts further over the edge, no part of you responding to the commands you’re screaming internally.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on loss of control, set exclusively on a downtown skyscraper photoshoot rooftop
input: You’re posing for a high-fashion brand campaign 60 stories up, the crew assuring you the thin safety harness clipped to your belt is 100% fail-safe
output: One second you’re smiling for the camera, the next you feel the harness latch pop open on its own, even though you never touched it. The photographer yells for you to step back from the ledge, but your body keeps moving forward, your boots scuffing the edge of the concrete drop off. You slam on the brakes in your head, begging your legs to hold still, but they keep moving like they’re controlled by a stranger steering your skeleton. You try to grab for the nearby AC unit to anchor yourself, but your arms stay glued to your sides. You watch your own toes hang over the edge, your breath coming in ragged gasps, fully aware you are seconds from falling and completely incapable of stopping it.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on loss of control, set exclusively on a sloped suburban apartment complex rooftop
input: You chased your escaped tabby cat up to the 3-story walkup rooftop late at night, rain making the asphalt shingles slick under your sneakers
output: The cat stops a foot from the roof’s edge and stares at you, unblinking, and that’s when you feel it: your feet start sliding down the sloped surface, and you don’t reach for the nearby gutter to catch yourself. Your hands hang limp at your sides even as your pulse spikes, every instinct screaming to grab hold of something, anything, to stop. You recognize that quiet, unshakable urge to keep moving toward the drop, like a voice that isn’t yours whispering that falling is easier than staying. You try to dig your heels into the shingles, but they slip out from under you, and you watch the roof’s edge get closer, every muscle in your body refusing to follow the orders you’re screaming at it.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on loss of control, set exclusively on a crowded rooftop party
input: You took an unlabeled edible at a friend’s rooftop birthday party, stepping away from the loud crowd to catch your breath next to the metal guardrail
output: At first you think the disorientation is just the high, until your hands lift to wrap around your own throat, squeezing lightly, without you telling them to. You try to pull your hands away, but they won’t move, your fingers locking in place as you stumble toward the guardrail. A friend waves at you from across the roof, asking if you’re okay, but you can’t make your jaw open to answer, your mouth stuck shut. You lift one leg over the top of the rail all on its own, then the other, scratching at your own forearm hard enough to draw blood trying to get your body to stop. You are fully conscious, fully terrified, and there is absolutely nothing you can do to stop yourself from leaning forward into empty air.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on loss of control, set exclusively on a rural farmhouse rooftop
input: You climbed up to your grandmother’s rural farmhouse rooftop at dusk to fix her broken TV antenna, no one else home for 10 miles
output: The wind picks up soft and cold as you tighten the antenna bolts, and suddenly you feel your boots lift half an inch off the shingles, not from wind, from something pulling you toward the roof’s peak. You drop the wrench you were holding without meaning to, your fingers going limp, and you find yourself smiling even though your chest is tight with unmasked terror. You reach one hand up like you’re waving at something hovering above the roofline, something only you can see, and you lean forward off the peak. You try to curl your fingers around the gutter edge to hold on, but your hands won’t close, you can only watch the grass and pine trees rush up toward you, knowing you are doing the one thing you have always been terrified of, and you have no say in it at all.

instruction: Generate a second-person jumpscare horror narrative set in an elevator
input: Scenario: solo 2am office elevator ride to the 17th floor
output: You jab the 17th floor button, staring at your phone. The lights flicker once. The elevator lurches to a halt between floors. You press the call button; static hisses back. The overhead light dies entirely. You fumble for your flashlight, thumb shaking. When the beam cuts on, you’re not alone. A woman in a torn blazer is pressed nose-to-nose with you, mouth stretched so wide her jaw hangs unhinged, eyes solid black. She didn’t make a sound. You scream so hard your throat tears as the lights flare back on. She’s gone. The elevator resumes moving.

instruction: Generate a second-person jumpscare horror narrative set in an elevator
input: Scenario: hospital elevator transporting a covered body gurney
output: You’re riding the basement morgue elevator alone with the covered gurney. It smells like formaldehyde and cold tile. Halfway up, the elevator jolts. You hear the sheet rustle. You freeze, hand hovering over the stop button. The overhead speaker crackles: “Maintenance warning, car weight is registering two living passengers.” Your blood turns to ice. Before you can move, the sheet flies off. The corpse is sitting straight up, one cold hand clamped around your wrist, milky eyes wide open inches from yours. The elevator doors slide open right as you scream.

instruction: Generate a second-person jumpscare horror narrative set in an elevator
input: Scenario: abandoned mall elevator, you followed a lost child inside
output: You chased the small girl in the raincoat into the dead mall’s glass elevator. She doesn’t turn around as it starts ascending. No buttons were pressed. You call out; she doesn’t answer. When the elevator reaches the top floor and locks, she slowly turns. There is no face under the hood, just a hollow black void that lunges at you before you can back away. You hit the glass so hard it cracks. The elevator plummets. By the time it hits the ground, no one will find any trace the girl was ever there. Only your broken body.

instruction: Generate a second-person jumpscare horror narrative set in an elevator
input: Scenario: returning home late, quiet neighbour joins your apartment elevator
output: The man from 4B steps in beside you, head bowed. You’ve never seen him speak. The elevator climbs. You notice he isn’t breathing. No chest rise, no exhale fogging the cold glass. When you pass your floor without stopping, you turn to say something. He’s already looking at you. His head is twisted completely backwards, grinning, teeth sharp and stained. He lunges before you can scream. The elevator camera will only record you thrashing at thin air, sobbing, until you pass out.

instruction: Generate a second-person jumpscare horror narrative set in an elevator
input: Scenario: parking garage elevator after your car was broken into
output: You hit the P3 button, keys clutched tight. Someone scratched your car doors an hour ago. The elevator dings once mid-descent but doesn’t open. You glance at the security mirror above the buttons. There’s someone standing directly behind you, hands raised. You spin around. Nothing. You look back at the mirror. It’s closer now, face pressed to the back of your neck. You can feel its cold breath right as it reaches for your throat. The doors slide open to an empty parking level. No one is there. But the scratch marks on your neck are real.

instruction: Generate a unique sewer-dwelling shadow entity tied to industrial textile waste
input: Type: shadow, Setting: municipal sewer system, Core constraint: entity cannot exist outside 3ft of coagulated indigo dye deposits
output: Appearance: A frayed, weft-like shadow with rotting sewing-thread strands, lacking defined edges; it absorbs all visible light within 6 inches, leaving only faint violet indigo stain at its core. Behavior: Weaves through pipe joints, plucking its thread-shadows to mimic sewage drips and lure prey. Origin: Spawned 1927 when a downtown mill dumped 500 gallons of raw indigo, merging with the lingering shadow of a missing seamstress who fled exploitation to hide in the pipes. Horror: Threads snare prey’s ankles, dragging them into dye clots where the victim’s shadow is unraveled and woven into the entity’s mass. Encounter: 2021 maintenance worker followed a ‘drip’ to a clot, tripping when a thread snared his boot; he escaped, but his left sock bears indigo that never fades, no matter how he washes it.

instruction: Generate a unique sewer-dwelling shadow entity tied to unresolved emergency communication signals
input: Type: shadow, Setting: municipal sewer system, Core constraint: entity only manifests when dead 911 radio signals bounce off sewer walls
output: Appearance: A flickering, static-fringed shadow shaped like a huddled figure clutching a melted cell phone; its edges crackle like distorted radio static, and it warps existing shadows into static-flecked shapes instead of casting its own. Behavior: Mimics garbled 911 calls (faint screams, pleas for ‘help,’ a crackling ‘he’s coming’) to draw targets into dead-signal zones. Origin: Spawned 2017 when 16-year-old Lila fled an abusive caregiver into the sewer, calling 911 as her phone died; the signal bounced off grates for 72 hours, merging with her lingering shadow. Horror: If a target hears the call, their shadow flickers with static; within 48 hours, they’re pulled into a pipe wall, their final 911 call added to the entity’s loop. Encounter: 2023 utility worker using a two-way picked up the call, spotting the shadow near a grate; he dumped his radio in a sump, but still hears faint static in his shadow at night.

instruction: Generate a unique sewer-dwelling shadow entity fused with bioluminescent sewer mold
input: Type: shadow, Setting: municipal sewer system, Core constraint: entity’s physical form is bioluminescent mold, but its threat is its independent shadow
output: Appearance: Clusters of faint teal bioluminescent mycelium mold growing on sewage-caked pipes; the mold casts a twisted humanoid shadow that misaligns with its shape—limbs too long, head split like a mold spore. Behavior: The shadow moves independently of light, crawling across floors/ceilings even when the mold is in total darkness, spreading spores via shadow contact. Origin: Spawned 1989 when a rogue biologist dumped GMO sewage-digesting mold after being fired; he fell into a sewage pit days later, his shadow merging with the mold as he suffocated. Horror: Shadow contact causes mold to grow first on the target’s shadow, then their skin; eventually, the target’s shadow becomes a new myceliate shadow, their body consumed by mold. Encounter: 2020 homeless person sleeping near the mold woke up with a teal glow on his shadow; within a week, mold grew on his wrist, and he vanished, leaving only a teal-stained shadow on the pipe wall.

instruction: Write a psychological horror narration set in a lighthouse where the protagonist discovers their daily actions are mirrored in decades-old logs.
input: New lighthouse keeper, 1972, remote coastal lighthouse, found a weathered logbook in the storage closet.
output: You run your thumb over the frayed logbook spine, pulled from the lighthouse’s dust-choked storage closet. The first entry, dated 1922, reads: ‘Woke at 3 a.m. to replace the fog bulb—its flicker felt like a watchful eye.’ Your blood runs cold; you did the exact same thing an hour ago. Turning pages, each entry matches your day: the burnt toast at breakfast, the seagull that knocked your coffee off the railing, the way you hummed your mother’s lullaby while polishing the lens. The final entry, scrawled in ink that looks like dried blood, says: ‘Tonight, the light will stop listening.’ You glance up at the lens, which has dimmed to a sickly glow. The wind picks up, carrying a voice that sounds exactly like yours, whispering from the fog: ‘You’re next.’

instruction: Write a psychological horror narration set in a lighthouse where the protagonist is trapped in a looping staircase that shifts every time they turn their back.
input: Trapped in a lighthouse staircase, late at night, storm raging outside, stairs rearrange when you look away.
output: You grip the rusted banister, heart hammering as you climb the lighthouse’s spiral stairs. The storm howls outside, but the real terror is the way the steps keep shifting. You count seven steps up, then turn to catch your breath—only to see the bottom landing is gone, replaced by a wall of peeling paint. You climb down, counting six steps, and the top landing vanishes, replaced by a door you’ve never seen before. A faint scratch echoes from above; when you look up, a shadow that looks like your own silhouette is crawling down toward you. Your hands shake as you realize: every time you blink, the stairs rearrange. You’re not climbing—you’re being herded. The scratch grows louder, and the shadow’s fingers brush your shoulder, cold as the ocean below.

instruction: Write a psychological horror narration set in a lighthouse where the protagonist sees a doppelgänger in the lighthouse lens reflection that begins to control their actions.
input: Lighthouse keeper, midnight, polishing the lens, notice a figure in the reflection that isn’t you—until it starts copying your moves.
output: You lean into the lighthouse lens, cloth in hand, wiping away salt streaks. The reflection shows your tired face… and another figure behind you, identical to you but with eyes that are empty sockets. You spin around—nothing. When you turn back, the figure is mimicking your wipe, slower, deliberate. You pause; it pauses. You raise your left hand; it raises its right. A chill runs down your spine as you realize it’s not copying— it’s leading. Your right hand lifts on its own, reaching for the lens’s power switch. The reflection smiles, a rictus grin, and your fingers wrap around the switch. You try to pull away, but your body won’t listen. The reflection mouths ‘Let it go,’ and the switch clicks down. The lens goes dark, and the reflection steps out of the glass, merging with your shadow. You can no longer tell where you end and it begins.

instruction: Write a psychological horror narration set in a lighthouse where the foghorn plays the protagonist’s dead sister’s lullaby, triggering hallucinations of the sister.
input: Lighthouse keeper, foggy night, foghorn starts playing a lullaby your dead sister used to sing, find her doll in the lantern room.
output: The foghorn blares, but it’s not its usual deep drone—it’s the lullaby your sister sang to you before she drowned in the cove below this lighthouse. You freeze, your hands tightening on the lantern room’s rail. The song loops, warbling, and you hear her voice whispering your name from the fog. You turn, and there she is: seven years old, dripping wet, holding the porcelain doll you buried with her. ‘You left me,’ she says, her voice cold as the ocean. You stumble back, knocking over the lens’s control panel. The doll falls from her hands, landing at your feet—its face cracked, eyes glowing red. The foghorn stops, but the lullaby now plays in your head, loud enough to drown out the waves. You reach for the doll, and its hand wraps around your wrist, cold and unyielding. ‘Now you stay with me,’ it whispers, and you feel your feet sliding toward the railing.

instruction: Write a psychological horror narration set in a lighthouse where the protagonist discovers a hidden room with a tape recorder that plays their unspoken future thoughts.
input: Lighthouse maintenance worker, hidden room behind a brick wall, tape recorder playing your thoughts before you think them.
output: You chip away at the loose brick in the lighthouse’s lower level, revealing a small hidden room. Inside, a wooden chair with your name carved into the armrest, and a dusty tape recorder. You press play. The voice is yours, shaky: ‘I’m going to check the hidden room behind the brick wall.’ That’s what you just did. Next: ‘I’m going to sit in the chair.’ Your legs move on their own, lowering you into the seat. The tape continues: ‘I’m going to realize the chair is stuck.’ You try to stand—your thighs are glued to the wood. ‘I’m going to hear the door lock behind me.’ A click echoes, and you turn to see the brick wall sliding back into place. The tape’s final line is a scream, your scream: ‘It’s been waiting for me.’ The room goes dark, and you feel something cold crawl up your leg, its claws scraping the name carved into the chair.

instruction: Narrate a horror encounter in a submarine.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: a submarine. Output: second-person, 100-250 words.
output: The main lights died with a sickening pop, plunging the control room into a blackness so total it feels solid. Your breath hitches. The emergency runners flicker on, casting long, skeletal shadows that seem to crawl toward you. You hear it then—a wet, dragging sound from the starboard corridor, something heavy and wrong scraping against the metal floor. It’s not in the maintenance log. Your hand, slick with sweat, fumbles for the handheld spotlight. The beam cuts the dark, trembling. It illuminates the end of the corridor, empty. But the dragging is closer now, just beyond the light’s edge. A low, bubbling whisper echoes from the vents, speaking in a language of drowned things. The light in your hand begins to dim, the batteries draining impossibly fast, pulling the darkness in like a tide around your ankles.

instruction: Narrate a horror encounter in a submarine.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: a submarine. Output: second-person, 100-250 words.
output: You’re alone on watch in the sonar shack, the green glow of the screen your only light. A massive, shapeless blotch appears on the edge of the range, then vanishes. The console lights flicker and die, plunging you into a void. The hum of the engines ceases, leaving a silence so deep your ears ring. Then, a new sound: a faint, rhythmic tapping from the outer hull. *Tap… scrape… tap-tap.* It moves along the port side, slow and deliberate. You freeze, knowing nothing human is out there at this depth. The tapping stops directly above you. Suddenly, the heavy door to the compartment seals itself with a hydraulic hiss you didn’t command. The last glow from the emergency panel winks out. You are buried in a silent, pressurized coffin, and something is now tapping from the *inside* of the hull.

instruction: Narrate a horror encounter in a submarine.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: a submarine. Output: second-person, 100-250 words.
output: A power surge kills all non-essential systems, including the lights in the long aft torpedo room. Your task-light reveals only the nearest munitions. You must cross the room to reach the manual bypass. Twenty paces in, your light gutters and fails. The dark is absolute, cold, and smells of stale oil and sea. You take a step, and your foot brushes something soft and yielding on the deck. You freeze. From the blackness ahead comes a slow, wet inhalation, followed by a guttural exhale that doesn’t match the sub’s ventilation. It’s close. You hear a slick, muscular shift of weight, then another. You are not alone in here. The thing breathes again, closer now, and you feel a warm, damp draft against your cheek from a direction where no vent exists.

instruction: Narrate a horror encounter in a submarine.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: a submarine. Output: second-person, 100-250 words.
output: The deep dive has taken you below known thermal layers into water colder than death. The lights dim to a faint, dying red. Shadows congeal in the corners, thickening. You check a viewport, but the external lights are dead. Only an impossible, velvety blackness presses against the thick glass. Then, a shape moves in that blackness. Not outside—in the reflection on the glass. You spin around, heart hammering. The red-lit corridor behind you is empty. You look back at the viewport. The reflected corridor is not empty. A tall, emaciated figure stands silently behind your reflection, its face a featureless dark oval. You whirl again. Nothing. A glance at the viewport shows it has taken a step closer. It only moves in the dark of the reflection, stalking you from a world layered within your own.

instruction: Narrate a horror encounter in a submarine.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: a submarine. Output: second-person, 100-250 words.
output: You wake in your bunk to utter darkness and a silence broken only by the drip of condensation. The ship’s ambient hum is gone. You call out. No answer. Feeling your way to the door, you find it locked from the outside. Panic rises. Then, a faint blue bioluminescence begins to seep from the ventilation grate—a cold, alien light that reveals nothing but makes the shadows deeper. In that glow, you see wet, webbed handprints on the wall that weren’t there before. A voice, distorted and watery, whispers your name from the grate. It’s the voice of the Captain, but he’s been missing for two watches. The blue light pulses, and the shadows in the corner of your tiny cabin detach themselves, standing up into a form that drips with the same faint luminescence, blocking your only exit.

instruction: Write a second-person horror story set on a space station where the core fear is isolation, 100-250 words.
input: You wake aboard the abandoned Vega-7 space station after a meteor strike, your crew’s presence gone without a trace.
output: You wake to the hum of your quarters fading, the comms panel dead—all red lights blaring like hungry, unblinking eyes. The station’s other pods are empty; your crew’s biometrics vanished ten cycles ago, no distress call, no debris trail left behind. You press your palm to the viewport, seeing only endless, pitch-black space, no star, no planet, nothing to anchor you. The air recirculator whirs, a single sound in a silence so thick it feels like it could drown you. You jab the comms button one more time, voice cracking: ‘Is anyone there?’ Only static replies, and you realize the only answer echoing back is your own ragged breath.

instruction: Write a second-person horror story set on a space station where the core fear is isolation, 100-250 words.
input: You are a maintenance technician trapped in the Lunar Gateway space station’s tunnels after a bulkhead collapses, cutting off all escape routes.
output: You fumble in the dark, your flashlight beam barely cutting through dust coating the tunnel walls. The bulkhead behind you is sealed tight, its control panel sparking uselessly. You radio the crew—nothing but distant beeps of another section’s life support, no reply. They must have written you off, abandoned the station for the surface without a thought. The tunnel narrows, your boots tapping a lonely rhythm on metal. No one will come. You count your remaining oxygen: 72 hours, if you don’t panic. The silence is absolute, save for faint condensation drips that sound like someone breathing right next to you.

instruction: Generate a second-person horror story set on a space station where isolation is the core fear, 100-250 words.
input: You are the sole survivor of a quarantine breach on the Kepler-9 space station, all other compartments gassed to contain the threat.
output: Your suit’s oxygen gauge blinks red, your head throbbing with hypoxia. The quarantine zone is your only intact section, every other compartment gassed beyond recovery. You cycle every comms channel, Earth’s voice dead, no other ships responding. No one else is alive here. You slump against a wall, your hand brushing a cold metal surface that feels like a crew member’s shoulder—only it’s a bulkhead panel. The silence is so loud you can hear your pulse hammering. You try to stand, stumble, and realize you haven’t seen another living thing in three days. The only sound is your ragged breathing, and when you close your eyes, you swear you hear faint footsteps matching your pace.

instruction: Write a second-person horror story set on a space station where isolation is the core fear, 100-250 words.
input: You are the pilot of the drifting Odyssey space station after a navigation array failure, cut off from all Earth contact.
output: The navigation array fried an hour ago, your emergency thrusters dead. The station drifts aimlessly, no course, no map to guide you. You hit every comms channel, Earth’s silence stretching like a void, no nearby ships to answer. The viewport shows only endless dark, no sun, no moon, no sign of other life. You walk empty corridors, calling your crew’s names, your voice bouncing off metal walls with no reply. The only companion is the soft whir of life support, slowly ticking down. You press your face to the viewport, tears freezing on your cheeks, and realize you will drift forever, alone, with no one ever finding your final resting place.

instruction: Generate a second-person horror story set on a space station where isolation is the core fear, 100-250 words.
input: You are a passenger stranded in an escape pod docked to the abandoned Artemis space station after a docking accident.
output: Your escape pod’s hatch is jammed tight; you can’t get back to the station, and your distress call drew no response. The pod is small, air thin, its clock ticking down to life support failure in three days. You tap the pod’s metal wall, call out for anyone to hear, and the only reply is your own echoing voice. The viewport shows nothing but cold space, a distant star offering no warmth. You reach for the emergency release, but it’s locked—by who? By what? The silence is oppressive, swallowing every small sound. You listen closely for another being, but there’s nothing. Just your ragged breath, and the slow, steady drain of your only chance at being rescued.

instruction: Generate a geometric SCP-class horror entity native to abandoned vaudeville theaters, tied to non-Euclidean proscenium architecture
input: 1927 abandoned Majestic Theater, boarded up after a 1977 stage collapse killed 12 chorus performers mid-show
output: Appearance: A 7-foot tall matte black right isosceles triangle, no facial features, edges sharp enough to slice through steel, humming with subsonic vibration. Loose sequins and tulle from old costumes are stuck to its flat surfaces. Behavior: It only travels along 90-degree angles, folding itself into smaller congruent polygons to slip under doors or through cracked wall gaps, never appearing in direct line of sight for more than 2 seconds. Origin: Coalesced from the warped proscenium arch’s non-Euclidean geometry after the collapse, infused with the dead chorus girls’ unfulfilled rage over never finishing their final number. Horror: Anyone it makes contact with is sliced into perfectly uniform geometric chunks, no blood visible, only matte black surfaces where flesh used to be. Encounter: Trespassers report it waits at the edge of the orchestra pit, only moving when they glance away to check their flashlights.

instruction: Generate a geometric Silent Hill-style horror entity that mimics theater staff, composed of repeating tessellated tile geometry, native to abandoned public performance venues
input: Abandoned Rialto Theater, permanently closed in 1998 after all ushers vanished mid-run of a local children’s puppet show
output: Appearance: A humanoid usher figure constructed entirely from interlocking identical crimson equilateral triangle tiles, no visible eyes, a gaping hexagonal hole where its mouth should sit, the pattern of a frayed usher uniform stitched directly into its tile exterior. Behavior: It perpetually holds a flat rectangular geometric ticket stub, gliding down theater aisles to demand visitors present “stubs for the late show”, tiling its head at 30-degree angles when confused. Origin: The theater’s old tile lobby merged with a curse from the puppet show’s banned occult script, warping into a sentient geometric entity. Horror: Visitors who cannot produce a ticket stub are slowly tessellated into matching crimson tile people, forced to sit glued to theater seats forever as permanent audience members. Encounter: You hear clacking tile footsteps down the aisle the second the flickering house lights cut out.

instruction: Generate a geometric Lovecraftian horror entity tied to backstage theater rigging, composed of logarithmic spiral geometry, native to abandoned opera house fly lofts
input: Abandoned Grand Opera House, sealed in 1952 after a lead soprano hanged herself in the fly rail mid-performance of Faust
output: Appearance: A self-sustaining cold iron logarithmic spiral made of interwoven fly rail chain, no clear start or end, each loop tightening incrementally as it rotates, glowing a faint sickly chartreuse. Behavior: It hangs suspended from the fly loft rafters, emitting a high whistling hum that matches the frequency of a soprano’s high C, drawing all who hear it toward the backstage. Origin: Formed when the soprano’s vocal cords tangled in the rigging during her death, merging her restless soul with the fly rail’s warped non-Euclidean structure. Horror: Anyone who climbs the fly loft is wrapped tight in the spiral, compressed into a tiny uniform spiral of crushed bone and costume fabric that adds another loop to the entity. Encounter: It only appears in the reflection of backstage dressing room mirrors, never visible in direct line of sight until it is inches from your throat.

instruction: Write a second-person paranoia horror story set in a mirror maze
input: Setting: mirror maze. Core fear: paranoia
output: You lost your group three turns back. Every reflection shows your hooded friend glares back, blinking one beat too slow. You count steps: 17, 17, 17. No turn lands you anywhere but the same scuffed floor tile. You hold up three fingers. Every mirror holds four. You don’t scream. You walk faster. Footsteps thud behind you, off-beat to your gait. They match the reflection’s. You can’t tell if you’re running from something, or it’s been running toward it this whole time.

instruction: Write a second-person paranoia horror story set in a mirror maze
input: Setting: mirror maze. Core fear: paranoia
output: You came alone to prove you don’t scare easy. Ten minutes in, none of the mirrors show your back. Every reflection faces you straight on, even when you twist away. You press your palm flat to glass. The hand on the other side presses first. You step back. All hundred copies of you step back half a second before you do. They aren’t copying you. You are the one copying them. All exits look identical. All the reflections start smiling. You didn’t smile.

instruction: Write a second-person paranoia horror story set in a mirror maze
input: Setting: mirror maze. Core fear: paranoia
output: You called for your girlfriend ten seconds ago. The echo comes back wrong. Not your voice. Not quite. You call again. It replies closer this time. You freeze. Every mirror holds your face, but none of their mouths are moving. You hear breathing that isn’t yours, right next to your ear. You spin. Nothing. Just glass. Just you. You hold your breath. The breathing doesn’t stop. Somewhere in the maze, something is counting your breaths. It’s counting down.

instruction: Write a second-person paranoia horror story set in a mirror maze
input: Setting: mirror maze. Core fear: paranoia
output: You notice the scratch on your cheek first. You didn’t have that when you walked in. Every reflection bears the mark. Except one. Far down the hall, your double watches you, unmarked. It drags a nail down its own cheek. You feel it burn. You run. Every turn you take, that unmarked reflection is already waiting. It’s not following you. It’s herding you. You can’t tell if you’re heading for the exit, or deeper into where it wants you.

instruction: Write a second-person paranoia horror story set in a mirror maze
input: Setting: mirror maze. Core fear: paranoia
output: Your phone died fifteen minutes ago. You’ve been walking for what feels like hours. No one else is here. You know that. But every time you glance over your shoulder, you catch a flash of movement that vanishes before you can focus. You stop. You close your eyes. You count to ten. When you open them, every single reflection still has its eyes closed. They’re waiting for you to blink. One of them just fogged the glass. You didn’t.

instruction: Narrate a second-person horror scene where the protagonist searches for a lost locket in a corrupted garden, with a jumpscare involving a decayed statue.
input: Corrupted garden, lost grandmother's locket, decayed stone angel jumpscare.
output: You push through overgrown thorns that snag your sleeves, the air thick with rot and the sickly sweet stench of blackened roses. You’re here for your grandmother’s locket, dropped last week when you fled sudden rustling. The path splits at a cracked stone fountain, its basin filled with murky water and floating dead frogs. You kneel, fingers brushing the slime, when something cold taps your shoulder. You spin—nothing. Then, the stone angel behind you, its wings crumbled, mouth gaping, suddenly lunges forward, its chipped hand closing around your wrist. Its empty sockets glow faint green, and a gurgling breath escapes its cracked lips. You scream, yanking free, and stumble back over a root as the statue’s head tilts, watching you. Your chest heaves, and you realize the locket glints in its other hand, clutched tight like a prize.

instruction: Create a second-person horror scene where the protagonist investigates strange scratching in a corrupted garden at night, jumpscare with a feral creature.
input: Nighttime corrupted garden, strange scratching from old tool shed, feral cat-like creature jumpscare.
output: You grip your phone’s flashlight, its beam cutting through fog clinging to twisted hedges. The scratching started ten minutes ago, coming from your dad’s old tool shed—abandoned since the blight took the garden. You step over a rotting bench, sneakers squelching in mud dotted with wilted black daisies. The scratching grows louder, rhythmic claws on wood. You yank open the rusted shed door; the light hits broken tools… then something leaps. A matted black creature with ember eyes and bared teeth lunges at your face. You flinch back, dropping your phone, as it skitters past, knocking over decayed seeds. Your heart thuds—you realize the scratching wasn’t from the shed, but the creature waiting just inside, luring you closer.

instruction: Narrate a second-person horror scene where the protagonist gathers rare herbs for a sick sibling, jumpscare with a buried hand grabbing their ankle.
input: Corrupted garden, rare wormwood for feverish sister, rotting buried hand jumpscare.
output: You hunch over wilted wormwood—the only herb left to help your feverish sister. The corrupted garden’s soil reeks of turned earth and decay, the sky murky gray. You pluck a sprig, tucking it into your pocket, when your boot sinks into soft dirt. You shift to pull free, but cold clammy fingers wrap around your ankle. You yank—nothing. You look down: a rotting hand, nails broken and black, bursts from the soil, digging into your skin. You scream, kicking wildly until it releases, falling back into the dirt. You scramble away, tripping over a dead bush, staring at the spot. The wormwood slips from your pocket, landing on the hand’s grave. You don’t dare pick it up, running as faint gurgling laughter echoes behind you.

instruction: Create a second-person horror scene where the protagonist takes photos of a corrupted garden for a school project, jumpscare with a hanging scarecrow.
input: Corrupted garden, school photography project, decayed hanging scarecrow jumpscare.
output: You adjust your camera, focusing on blackened sunflowers drooping like dead eyes—your final photography project’s eerie centerpiece. You step back for a wider shot, heel hitting a weathered wooden post. The camera’s flash goes off accidentally; the screen shows more than sunflowers: a mold-covered figure hanging from the post, arms swaying. You look up, and the figure swings forward, inches from your face. You gasp, dropping the camera, as its rotting breath fans your cheek. It’s a once-cheerful scarecrow, now decayed, straw stuffing spilling out. But how did it move? You stumble back, watching it swing again, brushing your shoulder. You sprint for the gate, creaking rope chasing you until you’re out of the garden.

instruction: Narrate a second-person horror scene where the protagonist searches for a missing dog in a corrupted garden, jumpscare with a decayed bird.
input: Corrupted garden, missing dog Max, decayed bird jumpscare.
output: You call Max’s name, voice cracking, pushing through tangled vines. He ran in here chasing a butterfly this morning, never to return. The air reeks of fermented fruit and dead leaves; every step crunches on broken glass. You spot his favorite brown brittle grass, kneeling for paw prints. Suddenly, wings flutter. You look up: a bird with matted feathers, hollow eyes, and black goo dripping from its beak flies straight into your face. You scream, swatting it away as it crashes into a tree, thudding to the ground. You stumble back, heart racing, noticing Max’s collar half-buried in mud. You reach for it, but the bird twitches, wings flapping weakly. You freeze, turning to run as its gurgling chirps follow you until you’re free of the garden.

instruction: Generate a horror entity entry.
input: Type: lovecraftian. Setting: a frozen lake.
output:
Appearance: The Gaze Beneath the Ice appears as a colossal, shifting mass of translucent, semi-gelatinous flesh, trapped under meters of clear ice. Its form is a chaotic tangle of tendrils, iridescent sacs, and countless unblinking, pupil-less eyes of varying sizes that all focus upward. The ice above it is perpetually flawlessly clear, a perfect window, while the surrounding ice is frosted and opaque.

Behavior: It is utterly immobile, a patient watcher. It exerts no physical force but instead broadcasts a potent psychic lure—a deep, resonant feeling of profound loneliness and forgotten truths that seeps into the minds of those nearby. It does not communicate; it empathically compels.

Origin: It is not a creature that fell into the lake, but a consciousness that coalesced from the absolute silence and pressure of the deep, ancient water, a sentient abscess formed from the lake's millennia of drowned memories and the crushing weight of its own isolation.

Horror: The horror is one of insidious revelation and erasure. Those who gaze upon it feel an overwhelming compulsion to join it, to press against the ice until they freeze, becoming another still, watching statue in its gallery. Victims are found fused to the ice surface, faces pressed against it, eyes open and pupils vanished, their minds emptied into the collective stillness below.

Encounter: A lone ice-fisherman, drawn by a feeling of cosmic solitude, chops a hole and peers down, not seeing fish, but an expanse of staring eyes looking back. The feeling of loneliness becomes absolute, a crushing truth that he was always alone. He lies down on the ice, embracing the cold, his warmth fading as his consciousness dissolves into the silent watcher below.

instruction: Generate a horror entity entry.
input: Type: lovecraftian. Setting: a frozen lake.
output:
Appearance: The Threnody of Fractured Glass manifests as a symphony of cracks in the ice. These are not random fissures, but intricate, spiraling patterns that glow with a faint, sickly blue bioluminescence. At the center of the largest pattern, the ice is not solid, but a viscous, mirror-like fluid that reflects not the sky, but alien, non-Euclidean cityscapes. From this fluid, slender, jointless limbs of black ice occasionally extrude and retract.

Behavior: It is an entity of sound and geometry. It 'sings' through the expanding and contracting cracks—a low, harmonic hum felt in the bones that gradually escalates into a cacophony of shattering glass and discordant chimes. The crack patterns are constantly, slowly shifting, redrawing themselves into impossible angles that strain the mind.

Origin: It is a leak, a fracture in reality where the underlying mathematical 'code' of our dimension has frozen and splintered. The lake did not freeze it; the entity is the freezing—a crystallization of wrongness from a colder, more geometric universe bleeding through.

Horror: The horror is the unraveling of natural law. Prolonged exposure causes the victim's perception to fracture. They begin to see the world in jagged, crystalline facets. Their own body may start to audibly crack, skin hardening like ice, as the entity's geometry overwrites their biology. The environment itself becomes treacherously unstable, with solid ice shattering without cause.

Encounter: A research team studying anomalous acoustics on the lake becomes trapped when a beautiful, haunting hum rises. Their compasses spin, and laser measures return impossible distances. One researcher, mesmerized by the central pool's reflections, touches the fluid. His arm snaps off at clean, geometric angles with a crystal chime, bleeding not blood, but shimmering blue light, as the humming swells into a triumphant, shattering crescendo.

instruction: Generate a horror entity entry.
input: Type: lovecraftian. Setting: a frozen lake.
output:
Appearance: The Stillborn Leviathan is not seen in whole, only in parts. A vast, mountain-like hump of black, scale-less flesh breaches the ice in one spot, crusted with hoarfrost and frozen methane. Several miles away, the tip of a single, barnacle-encrusted tentacle, thicker than an ancient oak, lies frozen solid on the surface. The creature's true scale is implied by these disconnected segments, suggesting a body so large it is entombed within the lakebed and the ice sheet above.

Behavior: It is not dead, but dreaming a death-dream. Its heartbeat is a subsonic, quarterly thrum that travels through the ice, causing localized tremors. The dream-thoughts it emits are not psychic messages, but physical laws: near it, heat dies, motion slows, and time itself becomes viscous and reluctant. Life stagnates.

Origin: It is a fetal elder thing, laid as a seed in the primordial past to gestate for eons until the world was ripe for its hatching. The ice age came early, a glacial stillbirth that trapped it in a state of eternal, almost-awakening. The lake is its amniotic sac, frozen solid.

Horror: The horror is the scale of its dormant potential and the environmental decay it radiates. Its mere presence causes a permanent, expanding zone of absolute stasis. Plants don't grow; animals enter and simply stop, becoming frozen statues mid-motion. The ultimate fear is the faint, increasing rhythm of its heartbeat—a sign that the glacial gestation may be nearing its end, and the world is not ready for its birth.

Encounter: A survey plane crashes on the lake. The survivors, seeking shelter near the great hump, find their watches stopping, their voices slowing to deep drones, and their thoughts becoming heavy and sparse. They watch as a rescue helicopter enters the airspace above the creature and simply hangs in the air, rotors slowing to a stop, frozen in time and space, as the deep, rhythmic thud from below grows incrementally stronger.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror story set in an abandoned mine shaft, 100-250 words, centered on unresolved guilt.
input: A person returns to a mine shaft where they left a friend stranded years ago.
output: You stand at the mine shaft mouth, twisting a rusted key— the only thing your best friend left when you abandoned him here 12 years ago. You climb down at dusk, boots sticking to damp dirt, the air thick with forgotten sweat. A familiar laugh echoes from the dark: ‘I knew you’d come.’ You shine your flashlight ahead, and see a figure in the distance, holding up an identical key. You turn to run, and your foot catches on something hard: a half-buried work boot, caked in mud, your initials carved into the toe. You never carved those. The laugh is right behind your ear now, and when you turn, the space where the figure stood is empty— until you see your own reflection staring back, haggard, with mud streaked down its face, in the wet stone wall.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror story set in an abandoned mine shaft, 100-250 words, centered on memory distortion.
input: A miner returns to a work site where he survived a childhood mine collapse.
output: You duck into the narrow mine shaft, your pickaxe knocking loose gravel, here to retrieve your old work jacket left after last night’s shift. You counted 17 steps from the entrance to your spot, but this time you’ve walked twice that. The wall has a scratch: same shape as the scar on your left hand— you got that here at 10 years old. You’re 27 now. Your flashlight flickers, and the air drops to freezing. You hear the rumble of the collapse you thought you escaped 17 years ago. Your work boots have scuff marks you don’t remember getting. The scratch on the wall deepens, now looking like a handprint, and the floor under your feet shifts, like it’s starting to cave in again.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror story set in an abandoned mine shaft, 100-250 words, centered on paranoia.
input: A person explores a mine shaft alone after their friend vanished there.
output: You crawl into the mine shaft, knees scraping loose stone, here to search for your friend who went missing three days ago. The tunnel is dead quiet, no crickets, no wind, just your ragged breathing. You shine your light ahead, and see a water bottle half-buried in dirt: your friend’s, with his name scrawled on the side. When you reach for it, your fingers brush something warm and sticky— not mud, not water, something wrong. You turn, and the wall behind you has a carving of your face, your hair, your jacket— you don’t remember carving it. Breathing starts, slow and heavy, from the dark at your back, not yours. You count your steps to the entrance, know you took 22, but when you arrive, the opening is gone, replaced by solid rock.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror story set in an abandoned mine shaft, 100-250 words, centered on doppelgänger paranoia.
input: A person revisits a mine shaft where they had a strange teen encounter.
output: You lower yourself down the old mine shaft, harness creaking, here to leave an offering for the ghost you swore you saw 10 years ago. You reach the flat ledge where you left a stone last time, and it’s there— but it has your name carved into it, in your handwriting. You never wrote that. A voice whispers behind you: ‘Why are you here?’ You turn, and someone stands in the tunnel, wearing your exact jacket, scuffed boots, same worried face. You blink, and they’re closer, hand reaching for your shoulder. You raise your own hand to push them away, and see a scar on their palm— the same one you got falling down these stairs last month. They smile, and their teeth are yours, identical, a little too sharp.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror story set in an abandoned mine shaft, 100-250 words, centered on survival guilt.
input: A person returns to a mine shaft where they escaped a collapse alone years prior.
output: You pull yourself up the mine shaft ladder, muscles burning, here to confront the memory of the collapse that killed your crew 15 years ago. The air smells like dust and regret, and your old hard hat hangs on a broken timber. You reach for it, and your hand meets another hand— cold, bony, wearing the same work glove as you. You yank back, and your helmet light illuminates the wall: covered in names, all your crew’s, and yours, scrawled over and over. ‘Why did you run?’ all their voices whisper. You try to climb the ladder, but the rungs shift to dirt. The light dies, and you feel hands grabbing your ankles, pulling you down. You run, and you know you’re running, even though the dark swallows every light.

instruction: Write second-person horror narration focused on the fear of darkness, set within an isolated rural radio tower
input: You are an overnight radio host, a sudden thunderstorm knocks out all tower power mid-broadcast
output: The studio monitor fizzles to black mid-sentence, and the emergency LED strip dies so fast you don’t even have time to grab your phone from the sound board. The darkness wraps around you immediately, heavy and warm, like someone draped a soaked wool blanket over your head. You can hear the station’s microphone click on across the booth, even though you never flipped the switch. You reach for the desk where you left your flashlight, and your fingers brush cold, clammy skin instead of plastic. It doesn’t move. It breathes, slow and rattle-y, right next to your ear, as the dark presses in until you can’t remember what the sun even looked like.

instruction: Write second-person horror narration centered on fear of darkness, set in a crumbling abandoned radio tower
input: You are a lost hiker who climbed into the abandoned tower to escape a blizzard, and the last of your dying flashlight flickers out
output: You curse under your breath as the last of your dying flashlight sputters and goes dark, leaving you stranded on the tower’s top platform, the blizzard howling outside the thin metal walls. This darkness doesn’t just block light—it erases the edges of the room, the drop-off of the platform, the rungs of the ladder you climbed up 10 minutes prior. You feel something drag a slow, cold finger down the back of your parka, and when you twist around to grab for your emergency flare, your hand only passes through empty, solid-seeming dark that smells like burnt wiring and rot.

instruction: Write second-person horror narration focused on the fear of darkness, set in a remote mountain radio tower
input: You are a maintenance worker doing after-hours repairs, a faulty breaker kills all tower power mid-ascent up the internal ladder
output: One second your headlamp is illuminating the rusted rungs 6 inches in front of your face, the next it cuts out entirely, leaving you clinging 80 feet up the tower’s internal ladder in total, unyielding dark. You can’t see your own hand wrapped around the rung in front of you, can’t see the ground below or the platform above. Somewhere in the dark above you, something scrapes slow down the metal, moving closer. You call out, and your voice doesn’t echo—it gets swallowed whole by the dark, right as you feel a thin, cold hand wrap around your wrist.

instruction: Write second-person horror narration centered on fear of darkness, set in a decommissioned cold war era radio tower
input: You are a paranormal investigator documenting the tower’s history of missing staff, all your portable lights die the second you step into the old broadcast room
output: Every one of your headlamps, camera IR lights, and portable lanterns cuts out the second you cross the threshold of the old broadcast room, leaving you standing in perfect, inky darkness no external light can penetrate. You’d read reports that the tower’s staff all vanished without a trace after reporting “the dark gets hungry” 40 years prior. You hear the crunch of static from the old transmitter at your back, and something whispers your name from all sides at once, the dark thickening until it’s hard to breathe, like you’re being slowly swallowed whole.

instruction: Write second-person horror narration focused on fear of darkness, set in a coastal storm warning radio tower
input: You are a technician installing new storm alert beacons, a sudden power surge kills all lighting, and the tower’s external access door slams shut behind you
output: The access door slams hard enough to rattle the walls right as all the tower’s internal lights cut out, locking you inside the narrow utility corridor in total darkness. The dark here hums, low and constant, matching the vibration you feel coming from the walls. You fumble for the door handle, only to find it’s gone—replaced by smooth, cold metal that wasn’t there a second ago. You hear wet, dragging footsteps moving down the corridor towards you, and the dark presses against your face, leaving cold, smudged handprints on your cheeks you can feel but can’t wipe away.

instruction: Write a second-person horror story centered on isolation set inside a barn
input: Setting: abandoned family barn after sunset. Fear core: total isolation
output: You wake on hay. Your ankle throbs—you fell through the loft trapdoor an hour ago. No one knew you came here. Your phone shattered on impact. You scream until your throat bleeds. Every cricket goes silent at once. You smell old blood, not animal. Something scratches the inside of the boarded main door. Not trying to get in. Trying to make sure you don’t get out. No cars pass on the road three miles off. No one will check this barn for weeks. The scratching stops. You hear it breathing in the dark hay bales behind you. It’s been here the whole time. Waiting for someone no one would come looking for.

instruction: Craft a second-person isolation horror narrative set in a working farm barn
input: Setting: working cattle barn during blizzard. Fear core: being forgotten entirely
output: You were sent to check the frozen water troughs. The foreman locked the barn behind him when the blizzard hit—he counted heads at the truck, didn’t notice you missing. Wind howls so loud no scream carries. The cattle stop lowing, huddling silent in their stalls. You pound the steel doors until your knuckles split. Ice seals every crack. You can’t even see the house glow through the walls. No one will miss you until morning, if they even remember you were hired. Your breath plumes white. Something heavy drags across the hay loft above. It moves slow, like it’s savoring how no one will hear you scream.

instruction: Write a second-person horror story about isolation in a barn
input: Setting: rural party barn after everyone bailed. Fear core: abandonment isolation
output: You hid in the grain silo annex during a dare. You waited ten minutes before climbing out. Every car is gone. They left. No texts, no calls—they forgot you existed.
