The barn doors are latched from the outside. You circle the perimeter, every shadow stretching wrong. You notice the barn light didn’t burn out. Someone turned it off. Footsteps pad around the outside wall, matching your pace exactly. It doesn’t call out. It just follows. No one knows you’re here. No one is coming. You stop walking. The footsteps stop too. It’s waiting for you to break first.

instruction: Create a second-person isolation horror narration set in an old barn
input: Setting: inherited family barn, middle of nowhere. Fear core: permanent isolation
output: You came to clear out your dead uncle’s barn. Your car died three miles back. No cell service. You’ve been yelling for two hours. The barn doesn’t echo anymore—your voice just dies mid-scream, like the walls are swallowing sound. You find old journal pages: every relative that came to clear this barn vanished. No reports, no missing persons. No one remembered they existed. You feel it now, the quiet eating at the edges of you. Soon no one will remember you came here. Soon you won’t even remember your own name. Just the dark, the barn, and the thing in the rafters that only hunts people no one will miss.

instruction: Write a second-person isolation horror story set inside a barn
input: Setting: emergency livestock barn at 3am. Fear core: unrescuable isolation
output: You got called out for a colicking mare. She died ten minutes ago. The farmer said he’d be right back. That was two hours ago. His truck is gone. All the road lights went out. You can’t get any radio signal. The mare’s corpse hasn’t gone stiff. It twitches. Not rigor. It’s turning its head toward you. You run for the door. It’s locked. You hear the farmer’s laugh, faint through the walls. They always leave one here. Just one. No one asks. No one comes looking. The mare stands up. It doesn’t have eyes anymore. It’s just waiting for you to stop screaming. No one will hear you anyway.

instruction: Generate a unique horror shadow entity set in a grain silo, including detailed appearance, behavior, origin, core horror, and a specific encounter scene.
input: Type: shadow; Setting: abandoned 1950s grain silo in rural Ohio; Tone: Junji Ito-style body horror with fungal undertones
output: Appearance: Woven from compressed grain dust shadow and mold mycelium veils—no distinct silhouette, just a shifting tapestry of husk-shaped voids that cling to silo walls like a second skin. Behavior: Mimics the high-pitched creak of a rusted silo ladder to lure victims; when close, it wraps thread-thin shadow-mold around ankles, dragging victims into the narrow gap between the silo’s metal wall and compacted grain. Origin: Farmer Elias Voss, who fell into the silo’s grain mass in 1957 while patching a leak; his shadow fused with the toxic mold growing on the rotting grain he suffocated in. Horror: Victims don’t just suffocate—grain husk shadows sprout from their lung tissue, visible under their skin as writhing black threads. Encounter: 19-year-old farmhand Jessa, checking the silo for leftover grain, hears the ladder creak; turning, she’s pinned to the wall, feeling shadow-husks crawling up her throat before blacking out.

instruction: Generate a one-of-a-kind horror shadow entity tied to a silo’s acoustic properties, with appearance, behavior, origin, horror, and encounter details.
input: Type: shadow; Setting: 1920s concrete silo in upstate New York; Tone: Silent Hill-style psychological dread with audiogenic terror
output: Appearance: Not a dark mass but a "resonance shadow"—a rippling void that eats light only where the silo’s natural echoes linger. It takes the shape of 11 overlapping silhouettes, each with a hollow throat cavity that oozes faint, inaudible vibration. Behavior: Feeds on the 3-second silence between silo wall creaks; to prolong silence, it mimics the last words of people who died in the silo, luring victims to stand in echo hotspots. Origin: 11 farmers trapped in the 1928 silo collapse, their shadows merging with the silo’s resonant frequencies as they screamed for help. Horror: Victims hear their own future last words repeated on loop, and their throats constrict as if choked by unspoken sound. Encounter: Urban explorer Mia, recording silo acoustics for a podcast, hears her own voice screaming "I can’t breathe" from the shadow; stepping back, she’s trapped in a silence so thick she can’t draw air, her throat swelling shut.

instruction: Generate a cosmic horror shadow entity rooted in a silo’s core, with unique appearance, behavior, origin, horror, and encounter elements.
input: Type: shadow; Setting: 1970s steel silo in Kansas with a sealed meteorite fragment in its core; Tone: Lovecraftian cosmic nihilism with object permanence dread
output: Appearance: A perfect, edgeless circle of absolute darkness at the silo’s core—no light reflects off it, and it casts shadows *on the air* itself, faint blue-gray tendrils that twist like living smoke. It doesn’t align with any light source; it exists as a hole in the silo’s physical fabric. Behavior: It "erases" small objects (grain kernels, metal bolts, mold patches) by pulling their shadows into its core; larger objects take longer, but it will target living things if their shadow brushes it. Origin: Farmer Harlan Reed, who sealed a meteorite fragment he found in his field inside the silo in 1976, believing it a "holy stone"; the fragment’s extra-dimensional shadow corrupted the silo’s core. Horror: Victims forget the existence of erased objects—including parts of their own bodies if their shadow is pulled in. Encounter: Environmental inspector Leo, checking for toxic waste from the meteorite, steps too close; his left hand’s shadow is yanked into the core. He blinks, staring at his wrist stump with confusion, unable to remember ever having two hands, his brain erasing the memory to match the shadow’s loss.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration focused on paranoia set in a phone booth, where a stranger on the line knows your unspoken actions.
input: Late rainy night, you step into a phone booth to call a friend after your phone died. The line connects to a stranger instead.
output: You fumble the coin into the slot, rain streaking the booth’s fogged glass. The line crackles, then a voice breathes your name—you never dialed it. “You’re twisting the cord around your wrist,” it says, slow. Freeze. Your fingers are coiled tight around the plastic. “You just glanced at the scratch on the booth’s metal door,” it continues, and you stare at the faded gash you’d tried to ignore. The voice lists every tiny thing you do: the way you shift your weight, the quick wipe of your wet palm on your jeans. You press your back to the booth, scanning the empty street. No one’s there. But the voice laughs, soft. “I can see the sweat beading on your forehead. Don’t look up.” You do. A tiny camera lens glints in the booth’s ceiling light, hidden behind a cracked plastic cover.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration focused on paranoia set in a phone booth, where the booth itself echoes your buried regrets to make you doubt your sanity.
input: You duck into a dusty small-town phone booth to call your sister, apologizing for a fight last week.
output: The dial tone warbles, then cuts to a recording—not yours, but the fight from three nights ago. “You never cared about her,” the voice mimics your shout, distorted. You slam the receiver down, but it rings again, this time playing the lie you told your best friend in high school. The booth’s walls seem to close in; the glass fogs with words scrawled in smudged ink: “You lied.” “You hurt them.” You claw at the door, but it’s stuck. A soft whisper curls in your ear, exactly like your mother’s voice: “You’ll never be forgiven.” You spin—no one’s there, but the receiver is off the hook, spitting out your deepest, unspoken fears. You press your hands over your ears, but the words seep through, making you question if every choice you’ve ever made was wrong. The door clicks open, but you don’t move—afraid the voices will follow you outside.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration focused on paranoia set in a phone booth, where a shadowy figure vanishes every time you glance at it, and the phone confirms it’s there.
input: You stop at a highway phone booth to report a flat tire. The line connects, but the dispatcher pauses before speaking.
output: The dispatcher’s voice is tight: “Sir, there’s a man standing behind your booth. He’s holding a knife.” You spin—empty. The highway stretches quiet, no cars, no shadows. “He’s gone now,” you say, annoyed. “No,” the dispatcher says, urgent. “He’s to your left, pressing his face to the glass.” You whip your head left—still nothing. The line crackles: “He’s behind you again.” You turn, heart hammering. The booth’s metal walls feel cold against your back. You reach for the door, but the dispatcher shouts: “Don’t open it! He’s right outside, waiting for you to look away.” You freeze, eyes locked on the glass. Every flicker of your peripheral makes you jump—was that a hand? A shadow? The line goes dead. You fumble with the door handle, but it’s stuck. A soft tap sounds on the glass. You don’t dare look.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration focused on paranoia set in a phone booth, where forgotten items in the booth trigger voices that make you question your grip on reality.
input: You use a vintage phone booth at a fair to call your partner. You notice a crumpled receipt, a hair tie, and a broken watch on the shelf.
output: You dial your partner’s number, but the line picks up to a woman’s voice: “Where did you go? You promised you’d come back.” You hang up, confused. The hair tie on the shelf twitches—you swear it does. The watch starts ticking, loud, even though it’s broken. A voice whispers from the receipt: “You left me here to die.” You grab the receipt to crumple it, but it’s gone. The hair tie wraps around your wrist, tight. You yank it off, throwing it to the floor. The woman’s voice returns, louder: “You’re just like him. You’ll leave everyone too.” You press your back to the booth, scanning the fair crowds. No one notices you. The watch’s ticking grows deafening, matching your heartbeat. You fumble for the door, but the handle is hot. The voices merge into a chorus: “You can’t escape. You’re trapped here with us.” You start to hyperventilate, wondering if you’re imagining all of it—or if the booth’s ghosts are real.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration focused on paranoia set in a phone booth during a city blackout, where the phone gives you instructions that reveal an unseen presence in the booth.
input: The city goes dark. You duck into a phone booth to call emergency services. The phone lights up, even though the power is out.
output: The phone’s keypad glows blue, casting eerie shadows in the booth. You dial 911, but the voice on the line isn’t a dispatcher: “Don’t move. There’s someone breathing behind you.” You hold your breath, listening. A soft exhale brushes your neck. You spin—nothing, just the cold metal walls. “He’s hiding in the corner,” the voice says. You stare at the shadowed corner, but see no one. “He’s touching your shoulder now.” You feel a cold finger brush your shoulder, and you jump. The voice laughs: “You’re so easy to scare. Look down.” You glance at your feet—there’s a pair of shoes next to yours, too big, too dark. You scream, kicking at them, but they vanish. The voice says: “He’s in your pocket.” You pat your pockets, finding a crumpled note: “I’m always with you.” The phone goes dead, and the keypad’s light fades. You’re left in total darkness, listening to the sound of breathing that isn’t yours.

instruction: Describe the protagonist's experience of losing control while driving on a bridge.
input: The car's steering wheel locks, brakes fail, and the vehicle accelerates uncontrollably on a narrow, fog-shrouded bridge.
output: The wheel stiffens in your hands, a dead thing. You stamp the brake—a soft, useless pedal. The engine roars, independent of your foot. The bridge’s guardrails blur as you accelerate into the thick fog, the painted lines vanishing beneath you. You wrench the gearshift, slap the ignition—nothing answers. The car is a projectile, and you are its trapped cargo. Through the mist, the vague shape of the bridge’s end materializes, a ragged edge over black water. Your screams are soundless against the engine’s howl. Your hands, still gripping the frozen wheel, are just ornaments now.

instruction: Describe the protagonist's experience of their body betraying them while walking across a bridge.
input: Midway across a high, swaying footbridge, your limbs begin moving against your will, forcing you toward the edge.
output: Your right foot plants itself. It will not lift. You command your left leg to step back, but it lurches forward instead. A marionette’s jerk carries you to the bridge’s rusted cable rail. Your hands, trembling, grip the cold metal—not to hold on, but to pull yourself up. You fight, a silent war inside your nerves, but your arms hoist you onto the narrow ledge. The wind howls. You stare down at the river gorge, a mile below. Your own voice, a whimper in your throat, is the only part of you that still obeys.

instruction: Describe the protagonist's experience of a supernatural force taking control of a bridge.
input: An ancient, stone bridge begins to reshape itself, its stones moving to trap and manipulate anyone crossing it.
output: The arch of the bridge groans. The cobblestones under your boots ripple like liquid, then solidify, molding around your ankles. You are rooted. The parapet writhes, stone grinding on stone, forming bony fingers that curl toward you. The entire structure is alive, intent. It pulls you down, not to fall, but to be absorbed. The granite grows warm, viscous. It climbs your calves, a slow, petrifying tide. You cannot run, cannot even fall. The bridge is deciding your shape, folding you into its new geometry. Your last sight is the opposite bank receding, not because you are moving, but because the bridge is stretching, swallowing you whole.

instruction: Describe the protagonist's experience of losing control of their perception on a bridge.
input: While crossing a long suspension bridge, your sense of scale and distance violently distorts, making the bridge seem infinite or suddenly inverted.
output: The bridge lengthens. The far tower, a moment ago a hundred yards away, now winks in the distance like a star. You run, but the grating beneath your feet stretches, each stride covering inches. The cables overhead swoop down, becoming a cage. Then, inversion. The river is above you, a churning ceiling. You clutch the railing, now the only ‘down’ in a spinning world. Your own weight is a lie. You let go, not falling, but drifting upward toward the water-sky. The bridge is a scribble in a mad god’s sketchbook, and you are a smudge upon it, directionless.

instruction: Describe the protagonist's experience of a crowd on a bridge being remotely controlled, turning against them.
input: On a packed city bridge, every other person simultaneously stops, turns, and begins moving toward you with blank, synchronized intent.
output: The chatter dies. A thousand footsteps become one. You are jostled, then stilled, as the bodies around you halt in perfect unison. Heads rotate. Two hundred pairs of eyes fix on you, pupils wide and dark. They step forward, a single organism, arms rising to grasp. You back away, but the crowd behind you has already closed the circle. Their hands are cold, movements efficient and puppet-like. They lift you, not with malice, but with the empty precision of machines. You kick, scream—no reaction. They carry you to the railing, your own limbs pinned by the weight of their controlled, relentless will.

instruction: Create a second-person horror entry focused on the jumpscare fear type, set entirely within a tunnel environment.
input: Craft a 100-250 word second-person horror narration centered on jumpscare, taking place in a tunnel.
output: You’re crawling through the rain-soaked service tunnel, phone flashlight flickering, chasing a stray cat that darted ahead. The only sound is your knees scraping concrete, distant drip-drip-dripping that hums louder the deeper you go. You call the cat’s name, then freeze—something shifted behind that rusted grate up ahead, not the wind. You creep closer, heart hammering, and yank the grate open. A half-rotted mannequin’s head, its painted eyes wide and broken, pops straight at your face, its mouth stretched into a silent scream. You stumble back, dropping your phone, as its plastic hand clamps over your wrist, cold and sticky with what looks like old blood.

instruction: Create a second-person horror entry focused on the jumpscare fear type, set entirely within a tunnel environment.
input: Craft a 100-250 word second-person horror narration centered on jumpscare, taking place in a tunnel.
output: You’re exploring the abandoned coal tunnel, hard hat light cutting through dust, mapping old coal veins. Your pickaxe taps a loose rock, sending a clatter that echoes until it dies. You hear a scuttle—too big for a rat, too close. You shine your light down a side tunnel, and nothing’s there. You turn back, and something hits your chest: a dead bat, its wings spread wide, mouth open like it just flew straight at you. You flinch so hard your hard hat slips, then notice more bats swarming the tunnel mouth behind you, their tiny eyes glowing red, the silent jumpscare you never saw coming.

instruction: Create a second-person horror entry focused on the jumpscare fear type, set entirely within a tunnel environment.
input: Craft a 100-250 word second-person horror narration centered on jumpscare, taking place in a tunnel.
output: You’re wading through the flooded storm tunnel, water up to your thighs, rushing to beat the next storm. Your boots squelch on slick metal grates, the sound bouncing off cinder block walls. You hear a faint gurgle, then a soft splash to your left. You turn, shining your light, and see nothing but murky, brown water. You step forward, and something grabs your ankle—hard. You yank your foot back, and a severed hand, pruned and blue, pops out of the water right at your face, fingers curling like it just reached for you. You scream, falling back into the water as more hands surge toward your legs.

instruction: Create a second-person horror entry focused on the jumpscare fear type, set entirely within a tunnel environment.
input: Craft a 100-250 word second-person horror narration centered on jumpscare, taking place in a tunnel.
output: You’re fixing the residential water main tunnel, headlamp clamped tight, the smell of rust and mildew thick. You trace the leak, wrench clinking against the rusted pipe. A sudden thud echoes down the tunnel, not from your tools. You look up, a shadow moves across the wall, but nothing’s there. You reach for your light to check the pipe, and a face suddenly appears inches from yours—gray skin, bulging eyes, wide silent scream, a corpse pressed to the pipe frozen mid-leak, jolting when you moved. You stumble back, wrench clattering, as it grabs your arm through damp fabric.

instruction: Create a second-person horror entry focused on the jumpscare fear type, set entirely within a tunnel environment.
input: Craft a 100-250 word second-person horror narration centered on jumpscare, taking place in a tunnel.
output: You’re exploring the old subway maintenance tunnel, graffiti covering cinder blocks, distant rumble of a passing train shaking the walls. You take photos, camera flash popping, when you hear a soft whimper. You freeze, move toward the sound, flash off. You turn a corner, just a rusted cart. You step forward, and a pair of glowing eyes pop up from the cart, attached to a half-decomposed child’s doll, mouth wide in silent scream, arm reaching for your camera. You jerk back, dropping your phone, as the doll rolls off the cart clattering toward your feet, the jumpscare making your blood run cold.

instruction: Generate a unique geometric horror entity tied to a remote abandoned farm well, aligned with SCP, Silent Hill, Junji Ito, and Lovecraft horror frameworks, including appearance, behavior, origin, core horror, and typical encounter details.
input: Entity variant: planar tessellation, low aggression, only triggers on direct visual contact with the well interior
output: Appearance: A roiling mass of interlocking matte black equilateral triangular tiles, no gaps, no edges, that slowly protrudes from the well mouth when the moon is full, reaching 1.2m above the stone rim before receding. Behavior: It does not move to attack, but projects shifting tessellation patterns onto any surface within 5m of the well opening. Origin: Manifested in 1891 when a village elder tossed a carved dodecahedral religious relic down the well to hide it from raiders, the well’s accumulated groundwater absorbed the relic’s geometric resonance to birth the entity. Core horror: The tessellation patterns rewrite the viewer’s neural pathways to only perceive flat 2D planes, erasing all depth perception permanently within 90 seconds of exposure. Typical encounter: A hiker leaning over the well to retrieve a dropped water bottle will find their vision flattening rapidly, unable to tell the difference between the well edge and the surrounding grass as the triangles hum softly.

instruction: Generate a unique geometric horror entity tied to a remote abandoned farm well, aligned with SCP, Silent Hill, Junji Ito, and Lovecraft horror frameworks, including appearance, behavior, origin, core horror, and typical encounter details.
input: Entity variant: non-euclidean negative curvature, medium aggression, only triggers when a round object is dropped down the well shaft
output: Appearance: A perfect, featureless cylindrical void suspended 20cm below the well mouth, it absorbs all light that touches its edge with no reflection, no heat signature, no measurable texture. Behavior: It remains completely inert until a spherical or round object falls past its threshold, at which point it warps all surrounding flat surfaces into a gradual funnel curvature pulling toward the well. Origin: Summoned by a 17th century cult that tossed 13 gold coins and the severed circular sections of their followers’ fingers down the well to open a portal to a realm of purely curved dimensional space. Core horror: Prolonged exposure to its curvature effect will force human bone and soft tissue to warp into continuous circular loops, rendering the victim permanently trapped in a curled, ouroboros-like mass unable to speak or move. Typical encounter: A teen tossing a pebble down the well on a dare will feel the ground under their feet warp upward, their spine beginning to curve involuntarily as they stumble toward the void’s pull.

instruction: Generate a unique geometric horror entity tied to a remote abandoned farm well, aligned with SCP, Silent Hill, Junji Ito, and Lovecraft horror frameworks, including appearance, behavior, origin, core horror, and typical encounter details.
input: Entity variant: recursive fractal edge, high slow aggression, only triggers on physical contact with the well rim
output: Appearance: The well’s 1m wide stone rim is composed of endless recursively splitting right angles, every jagged edge splitting into three smaller identical jagged edges the closer an observer gets, giving the rim an infinite measurable length despite fitting neatly in its small circumference. Behavior: It emits a constant, quiet chipping sound as new smaller edges form, and will spread its fractal pattern to any object that touches it. Origin: Born from the rage of a 19th century stonemason who was buried alive in the well after refusing to carve non-right-angled runes for a cruel village lord, his resentment seeped into the stone over 70 years. Core horror: Touching the rim will cause the victim’s flesh to begin splitting into smaller identical jagged copies of themselves, dissolving into an infinitely granular pile of twitching organic fragments. Typical encounter: A researcher brushing their hand against the well rim to steady themselves will feel a sharp prickle, watching in terror as their fingertips split into dozens of tiny identical copies, the spread moving up their arm too fast to outrun.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a clock tower
input: Setting: clock tower. Fear: psychological
output: You climb the tower stairs to escape party noise. Halfway up, you notice the pendulum doesn’t keep standard time. It slows when you breathe out, spikes when your heart races. When you freeze, it stops entirely. You lean close to the clock face. The numbers aren’t painted. They’re scratch marks. Fourteen total. That’s the exact age you were when you told no one about the boy who fell from this roof. The pendulum ticks once. A chalk outline appears under your boots. It matches your silhouette exactly.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a clock tower
input: Setting: clock tower. Fear: psychological
output: You’re here to repair the stuck tower clock. You wind the main gear, and it groans awake. Each tick wipes one small memory. First you forget why you brought the screwdriver. Then you forget your sister’s name. You try to run, but every step down dissolves another detail. You pause halfway, staring at your hands. You don’t remember who you are. The clock ticks again. You turn back up. This is where you belong. You always come back. No one ever leaves.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a clock tower
input: Setting: clock tower. Fear: psychological
output: You snuck up here at 2:17 AM to smoke. The clock face doesn’t read 2:17. It reads 2:19. Your phone still shows 2:17. Every blink jumps the clock forward ten seconds. You watch it crawl to 2:21. At that exact mark, you see your own crumpled body at the stair base through the glass. You spin. No one is there. You glance back. The clock now reads 2:20. It’s counting down. You can’t move your legs.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a clock tower
input: Setting: clock tower. Fear: psychological
output: Every night you climb this tower to sit alone. Tonight, there are two sets of footsteps. You stop. They stop. You run up. They match your pace exactly. You reach the top, spin. No one. You press your ear to the stone wall. The footsteps are inside the rock. They don’t stop when you do. They keep climbing, perfectly matching every step you’ve ever taken up these stairs. They’ve been saving them. Now they’re catching up.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a clock tower
input: Setting: clock tower. Fear: psychological
output: You got locked in the tower just before midnight. When the bell struck twelve, you banged on the door. Your roommate walked right past below. You yelled. She didn’t glance up. Next your best friend. Then your mother. Every chime erases you a little more. By the thirteenth chime, no one will remember you were ever born. You can hear the bell winding for the final strike. No one is coming. No one even knows to look.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about isolation in a hospital ward where a storm has cut all power and communication.
input: Hospital ward, post-storm power outage, no staff or other patients visible.
output: You press the call button for the tenth time—no buzz, no click, just dead air. The storm outside howls, rattling the cracked window above your bed, but the ward is silent except for your ragged breathing. The last nurse left an hour ago, muttering about checking the generator, and never came back. You swing your legs over the edge of the bed, bare feet hitting cold linoleum. The emergency exit sign’s red glow is the only light, stretching long shadows of empty beds down the hall. You call out: “Hello?” The echo dies fast, like the walls are swallowing it. Then you hear it—a soft, dragging sound from the end of the ward, where the curtained isolation room hangs closed. No one was supposed to be there. You freeze, fingers curling around the bed rail, as the dragging gets closer. No one’s coming to help. You’re completely alone.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about isolation in a hospital ward where you wake up post-surgery to find all other patients and staff vanished without a trace.
input: Post-surgery hospital ward, empty beds with fresh linens, no staff present, your IV still attached.
output: The beep of your heart monitor is the only sound when you blink awake. Your throat is raw, your shoulder throbbing from the surgery. You glance around—every bed in the ward is made, crisp white sheets pulled tight, but there’s no one else. The nurse’s station down the hall is dark, computer screens black. You try to sit up, IV tugging at your wrist, and spot a half-empty coffee mug on the counter, still warm. Where did everyone go? You call out, voice cracking: “Nurse?” No answer. You swing your legs over the bed, wincing, and walk to the first empty bed. A child’s stuffed bear lies on the pillow, but no sign of its owner. Then you hear a faint scratch at the ward’s locked door. You press your ear to it—nothing. When you turn back, the heart monitor behind you has stopped beeping. You’re alone, and whatever made everyone disappear is still here.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about isolation in a quarantine hospital ward where you’re the only patient, and whispers start coming from the empty beds around you.
input: Quarantine hospital ward, single patient, empty beds, sealed windows, no visitor access.
output: The plastic curtain around your bed feels like a cage. You’ve been in this quarantine ward for three days—no visitors, no face-to-face staff, just a nurse sliding meals through a slot in the door. Tonight, the ward’s fluorescent light flickers, casting shadows over the six empty beds lining the walls. You close your eyes, trying to sleep, when you hear it: a soft whisper, right next to your ear. You jerk up, scanning the empty bed beside you. “Who’s there?” you hiss. The whisper repeats, quieter, from the bed at the end of the ward. You reach for the call button, but your hand freezes—another whisper, this time from the bed across from you, saying your name. The nurse won’t come for another hour. The walls feel like they’re closing in. You pull the covers over your head, but the whispers get louder, each empty bed seeming to breathe out your name. You’re trapped alone with voices that shouldn’t exist, and no one will believe you if you scream.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about isolation in a hospital ward where you’re recovering from a fever, and staff and visitors walk right past you like you don’t exist.
input: Hospital ward, feverish patient, staff/visitors ignoring you, your bed has no name tag.
output: Your fever breaks, but the chill in your bones won’t lift. A family walks down the ward, laughing as they head to the bed next to yours—except that bed’s empty. You wave, mouth opening to speak, but they don’t glance your way. A nurse pushes a cart past, stopping at every bed except yours, even though your water glass is empty. You sit up, grabbing the edge of your bed, and notice it: no name tag on the footboard, no chart clipped to the wall. Did they forget you? You swing your legs over the bed and stumble to the nurse’s station. The receptionist types away, eyes fixed on her screen, not acknowledging your presence. You tap the counter—she doesn’t flinch. Panic rises in your throat. You run back to your ward, but the door is now locked from the outside. You pound on it, screaming, but no one comes. You’re alone here, invisible, left to rot in a bed that no one remembers belongs to you.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about isolation in a hospital ward during the night shift, where all other patients are asleep, the last nurse leaves and locks the door, and you hear a strange sound in the dark.
input: Night shift hospital ward, sleeping patients, locked door, strange scratching sound in the ventilation.
output: The ward is dark except for the dim night light by the door. All the other patients are snoring softly, blankets pulled up to their chins. You can’t sleep—your head aches, and the hum of the AC is too loud. Then you see the last nurse walk down the hall, her shoes clicking on the linoleum. She pauses at the ward door, twists the lock, and leaves. The click echoes through the room. You close your eyes, trying to relax, when you hear it: a scratching sound, coming from the ventilation grate above your bed. It gets louder, like something is crawling through the ducts. You sit up, heart racing, and shake the patient next to you—they don’t wake. The scratching stops, then a soft thud comes from the supply closet at the end of the ward. You reach for the call button, but it’s dead. The door is locked, the other patients are unconscious, and whatever’s in the closet is coming out. You’re completely alone, with no way to escape, as the closet door creaks open slowly.

instruction: Generate a horror entity description.
input: Type: lovecraftian. Setting: a school gymnasium.
output:
Appearance: The 'Whispering Basket' appears as a standard basketball hoop and net, but the net is woven from what seems like solidified, matted hair and thin, fibrous tendons. The backboard is made of a dark, porous wood that subtly pulses. A single, lidless eye blinks irregularly from the center of the hoop's rim.
Behavior: It does not move from its mount. Instead, it exerts a subtle psychic pressure, compelling individuals to attempt shots. Each missed shot causes the net to constrict and produce a faint, collective whisper detailing the shooter's deepest insecurities. A made basket results in a soft, sucking sound and the temporary 'storage' of the ball, which is later spat out covered in a viscous, salty fluid and etched with faint, nightmare imagery.
Origin: It manifested after a decades-old time capsule, buried beneath the gym's floorboards and containing a local witch's cursed hair locket and a coach's hidden journal of abusive tactics, was unearthed during renovations. The confluence of sustained emotional anguish and a minor ritual object provided a conduit for a minor, psychic entity.
Horror: The horror is psychological and accumulative. The entity feeds on performance anxiety and shame, growing stronger with each failed attempt and whispered critique. It doesn't cause physical harm directly, but its whispers can lead to severe paranoia, self-loathing, and in one documented case, a victim attempting to 'retrieve' their lost confidence by climbing into the net itself, which then tightened like a cocoon.
Encounter: You'd notice it during a late-night practice or detention. The air around it feels thick and silent, absorbing ambient sound. Drawn to take a shot, you'd feel a cold dread with each miss as the whispers start, voices sounding unsettlingly familiar. Making a basket brings no joy, only a visceral disgust as you handle the damp, psychically charged ball, your own fears now reflected in its leather.

instruction: Generate a horror entity description.
input: Type: lovecraftian. Setting: a school gymnasium.
output:
Appearance: 'The Timberling' is a conglomeration of the gym's old, varnished floorboards, polished to a sickly sheen. It has no fixed shape, but typically manifests as a large, creeping stain or a humanoid figure composed of planks, jointed with rusty nails and sporting long, splintered fingers. Its 'face' is a knot hole surrounded by concentric, grooved rings that spin slowly, creating a low, grinding hum.
Behavior: It is dormant during the day, indistinguishable from the floor. At night, it becomes active, slowly flowing across the gym to absorb any organic material left behind—lost skin cells, drops of sweat, hair. It is drawn to the scent of vigorous activity and adolescent vitality. It attempts to 'assimilate' living beings by pulling them into the floor, where the boards warp around them like quicksand made of wood.
Origin: The gym was built using lumber from a sacred grove that was improperly desecrated. The wood was never truly dead, and decades of absorbing the potent, hormonal energy of teenage emotions—triumph, despair, humiliation—acted as a catalyst. A forgotten spill of industrial-strength cleaning solvent, mixed with a student's illicitly brewed potion, finally triggered its awakening.
Horror: The horror is bodily and existential. Assimilation is not immediately fatal; victims remain conscious as their body is slowly broken down and integrated into the floor, their consciousness diluted into a chorus of trapped, buzzing thought. The gym becomes a silent, hungry monument, its very boards containing the muffled screams and fragmented memories of every student who ever felt trapped there.
Encounter: You'd be working late, and notice the grain of the floor seems to be shifting, flowing towards your feet. A low, woody creaking fills the air, not from the structure, but from the floor itself moving. If you run, you hear the sound of cracking boards chasing you. If you stand still, you feel a tugging at your shoes as the polished wood becomes adhesive, then liquid, slowly swallowing you from the feet up.

instruction: Generate a horror entity description.
input: Type: lovecraftian. Setting: a school gymnasium.
output:
Appearance: The 'Pompon of Yoth' resembles a forgotten, oversized cheerleading pompon left in a storage closet. Its once-bright colors are now a dull, bruise-like purple and black. The shredded vinyl strips are coated in a fine, grey mold and move independently, like the cilia of a deep-sea creature. At its core, nestled within the handle, is a wet, toothless mouth that constantly drools a faintly luminous slime.
Behavior: It is docile until it senses strong, rhythmic sound or collective chanting. It then becomes aggressively mimetic, attempting to 'lead' cheers by launching itself into the air and floating erratically. Its strips flail, creating a sound like rustling leaves and distorted, recorded crowd noise. It seeks to attach itself to a 'host,' typically by wrapping its strips around a person's head, feeding on their auditory cortex and memories of social belonging, forcing them to cheer until physical collapse.
Origin: It originated from a failed attempt by a lonely, occult-obsessed student to create a social bonding talisman using a pompon, a recorded pep rally played backwards, and a specimen of bioluminescent fungus found in the school's ventilation system. The ritual, performed in the gym closet, didn't attract friends but instead attracted a non-Euclidean attention from a dimension where emotion is a parasitic physical force.
Horror: The horror is social and infectious. The entity perverts community spirit into a compulsory, draining frenzy. Victims report hearing the cheers of alien crowds in their dreams and feel an irresistible pull to the gym. Prolonged exposure leads to the host developing lesions that sprout tiny, glowing pompon strips, turning them into a passive, slime-drooling beacon that amplifies the entity's call.
Encounter: You'd find it while searching for old sports equipment. Touching it feels unnervingly warm and slightly sticky. If you drop it, or if a distant bell rings, it begins to tremble. Then it rises, floating, its strips undulating in a mockery of pep. A low, chittering cheer builds as it drifts toward you, the smell of ozone and decayed fruit filling the air. Your own voice catches in your throat, wanting to join in.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration featuring paranoia set in a server room
input: Late night shift in a windowless server room
output: You kneel to swap a faulty RAID drive, the unceasing server hum vibrating up your shins. A faint, familiar static threads through the noise—someone muttering your full name, slow and sharp. Your palm sweats as you scan the room: fire doors are locked, you swiped in alone an hour ago, no one else’s credentials have been logged. You glance at the main monitor, and your reflection flickers to show a shadow behind you that isn’t there. Your chest tightens: did you miss an access alert? Why is every server’s blink syncing to your racing pulse? The static grows louder, no source anywhere.

instruction: Create a second-person horror story with paranoia set in a server room
input: A server room during a power test
output: You run a manual power cycle, the room darkening then flaring to life as backups kick in. Halfway through, a tiny, unlabeled thumb drive slides from under a server rack—you never placed it here, and only your access card works this space. You shove it in your pocket, heart hammering, when your work bag’s zipper pulls up on its own, unprompted. You yank it open, nothing inside moved. You check the server logs: no thumb drive insertion history. Paranoia gnaws: is this your mind playing tricks, or is the room watching you?

instruction: Craft a second-person horror narrative with paranoia set in a server room
input: A server room with spiking temperature alerts
output: You check the HVAC, it’s off—yet server logs scream 120 degrees and climbing. You touch the rack, cool to the touch, lying to you. A faint footprint, scuff marks from steel-toed boots, dots the floor near your feet; no shoes are allowed in here, and the only other key holder is on vacation. You grab your phone to call maintenance, but the call drops instantly, no service here. You spin, thinking someone is hiding, but the room is empty. Your hands shake: did you start imagining this, or is the room hiding something?

instruction: Make a second-person horror story centered on paranoia set in a server room
input: You’re trying to access the mainframe in a server room
output: You type your credentials into the mainframe terminal, again and again, but it keeps rejecting you. You copied the password last week, double-checked it twice, but the screen flashes: Access Denied. You pace, convinced someone changed your password remotely, but your team’s access controls can’t be overridden without a physical token. You glance at the terminal’s mouse, and it slides 6 inches to the right on its own. No one is near the desk, no breeze. You freeze: is this the system messing with you, or is someone else in here watching?

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration with paranoia set in a server room
input: A quiet server room on a rainy night
output: You sip cold coffee, staring at server stats, when a soft knock echoes through the room—distinct, three quick taps. You jump, checking the door; no one is there, the hallway empty. You text your roommate to ask if they forgot something, but they text back: I’m at the bar 20 minutes away. The server room’s intercom clicks on, static hissing, then slow, steady breathing fills the speakers. You grab your lanyard, ready to run, but the intercom cuts out. Paranoia claws at you: is this a prank, or is the room holding something that doesn’t belong?

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on the fear of loss of control, set in a gritty basement boiler room.
input: Scenario: You are performing late-night routine boiler maintenance when the calibrated pressure dials begin moving on their own.
output: You kneel on slick, rust-streaked concrete, wrench in hand, when the pressure gauge you calibrated ten minutes prior slams past the redline. Your fingers lock around the wrench, frozen even as you beg them to twist the release valve. The boiler rumbles, pipes rattling so hard their mounting brackets groan, and you realize your body is moving on its own, reaching for the valve lockout you know will disable every safety relief system. You can’t scream, can’t twitch, can only watch as your own hand twists the lock into place, the heat building in the room until your flannel shirt sticks to your sweat-soaked skin, no way to stop what your own limbs are determined to do.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on the fear of loss of control, set in a gritty basement boiler room.
input: Scenario: You snuck into the soundproof boiler room to take a private work call when the heavy metal door slams shut behind you.
output: You fumble for your phone to call building maintenance, only for your thumbs to begin typing messages you never wrote, sending a note to your boss confessing to every minor mistake you’ve hidden for three years. The lights flicker, and the boiler’s roar warps into a low, mocking hum that matches the cadence of your internal thoughts. You punch the door as hard as you can, but your arm only swings slower, heavier, turning to press your palm flat to the scorching boiler casing instead. You can feel your skin blistering, but you can’t pull away, every muscle in your body defying every urgent command your brain sends, the room growing hotter by the second as you watch your phone send one damning message after another.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on the fear of loss of control, set in a gritty basement boiler room.
input: Scenario: You are the new building engineer running your first test of the newly upgraded automated boiler system.
output: You flip the activation switch, and the system boots up smooth at first, until every safety interlock light blinks red at once. You lunge for the emergency shutoff, only for your feet to plant to the concrete, glued there by some unseen, unshakable force. The boiler’s feed pipe creaks open, pouring scalding steam out in thick, rolling clouds that burn the edges of your vision. You try to yell for help, but your mouth won’t open, your vocal cords locked tight, as you watch the system’s control dial spin higher and higher, programmed to keep running by a command you never input, every fail-safe you installed manually overridden without a single person touching the panel.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on the fear of loss of control, set in a gritty basement boiler room.
input: Scenario: You hid from an active building intruder in the locked boiler room, thinking it was the safest place to hide.
output: You press your back to the cool brick wall, hand clamped over your mouth, when the boiler’s access panel creaks open on its own. Your hand moves away from your mouth of its own accord, and you hear yourself call out, loud and clear, inviting the intruder in, even as you panic so hard your chest aches. You try to run, but your legs carry you toward the open access panel instead, toward the glowing orange heat inside the boiler’s firebox. You can feel the sweat dripping down your forehead, can hear the intruder’s boots getting closer down the hall, but you can’t turn around, can’t stop your forward momentum, every choice you thought you had stripped away from you entirely.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on the fear of loss of control, set in a gritty basement boiler room.
input: Scenario: You came down to the boiler room to turn the heat up for a sick elderly tenant on the coldest night of the year.
output: You twist the temperature dial up two notches, only for it to keep spinning on its own, cranking higher and higher past the maximum safe setting. You reach to yank the power cord out of the wall, but your hand curls around the dial instead, holding it in place, even as your brain screams to pull away. The pipes begin to rattle so hard loose rust rains down from the ceiling onto your hair and shoulders, the room growing hot enough that your winter coat starts to smolder at the cuffs. You can’t let go, can’t look away from the glowing gauge, as the boiler begins to rumble with the force of an impending explosion, every attempt you make to regain control of your body fizzling out the second it reaches your limbs.

instruction: Generate a second-person jumpscare horror narration set in a cathedral
input: Setting: cathedral, required fear: jumpscare
output: You kneel to light a memorial candle for your grandmother, thumb scraping rough wax. The cathedral is dead silent, every pew empty. Your match sputters once, dies. No wind. You strike a second. As the flame flares, you glance up at the marble altar statue. Its head, once facing forward, is turned directly at you. The match goes out. Hot, stale breath hits the hollow of your throat. Before you can move, it screeches right in your face, so loud your teeth rattle.

instruction: Generate a second-person jumpscare horror narration set in a cathedral
input: Setting: cathedral, required fear: jumpscare
output: You’re locking up after evening mass, flipping final light switches. The nave falls to deep shadow. You turn for the heavy oak door, keys jangling soft against your palm. Halfway down the aisle, you hear thin, off-key singing from the choir loft. No one stayed behind. You freeze, squint up. There’s a shape hunched over the organ bench. It stops singing. Slowly, it twists its head all the way around 180 degrees to look at you. It leaps before you can scream.

instruction: Generate a second-person jumpscare horror narration set in a cathedral
input: Setting: cathedral, required fear: jumpscare
output: You duck into the empty cathedral to escape the thunderstorm, rain dripping off your coat cuffs. You slump against a cold stone pillar, wiping water from your eyes. A single thick drop hits your shoulder. You look up. Pressed flat against the vaulted ceiling, hanging upside down ten feet above you, is a thing with too many teeth. It blinks. It drops straight down onto you, claws out, screaming so loud it drowns out the thunder crack outside.

instruction: Generate a second-person jumpscare horror narration set in a cathedral
input: Setting: cathedral, required fear: jumpscare
output: You’re dusting the crypt tombs after hours, brush gliding over worn name plates. You pause at an unmarked stone casket half hidden behind a pillar. The lid shifts, just a hair. You lean in, squinting. It slams open hard enough to crack the stone floor. A rotted black hand shoots out, wrapping tight around your wrist. The corpse inside lunges up, screaming directly into your face, its milky dead eyes wide open.

instruction: Generate a second-person jumpscare horror narration set in a cathedral
input: Setting: cathedral, required fear: jumpscare
output: You sit alone in the back pew, praying through a panic attack, eyes squeezed shut. The cathedral is quiet except for your shaky breathing. You feel someone sit down right next to you, the pew wood creaking under their weight. You don’t open your eyes. You can feel them staring. Then they press their cold, rotting palm flat over your mouth and shriek right against your ear, so close you can taste damp decay on your tongue.

instruction: Generate a horror shadow entity set in a catacomb, detailing appearance, behavior, origin, core horror, and a personal encounter.
input: Shadow entity tied to unmarked plague mass graves in a 16th-century catacomb.
output: This shadow clings to calcified plague pit walls, its form a lurching amalgam of overlapping, handprint-shaped shadow smudges that bleed faint, ash-gray mist. It moves only when unobserved, slithering between skeletal remains to leave sticky, shadowy residue on exposed skin. Origin: Catacomb overseers who sealed 300 unmarked plague victims alive to contain an outbreak, their collective despair anchoring fragmented shadows to the stone. Core horror: Contact with the residue slowly petrifies living flesh into porous, bone-like shadow-stuff, erasing memories of the victim’s identity before they dissolve into the entity’s mass. Encounter: An urban explorer felt a cold tingle on her neck mid-photo; later, her reflection’s left hand vanished, replaced by a shadow smudge that crawled up her arm each time she slept.

instruction: Generate a horror shadow entity set in a catacomb, detailing appearance, behavior, origin, core horror, and a personal encounter.
input: Shadow entity preying on grieving visitors in a sound-reflective catacomb echo chamber.
output: This shadow manifests as the crisp silhouette of a visitor’s most mourned loved one, its edges rippling like disturbed water only when viewed through peripheral vision. It does not move, but emits faint, gasping whispers that bounce off the catacomb’s curved limestone walls, making them impossible to trace. Origin: A 19th-century widow buried alive in the echo chamber while pleading for her dead child; her unheard grief warped her shadow into a lure. Core horror: The whispers grow louder with each step toward the silhouette, and upon contact, the shadow forces the victim to relive their loved one’s final death moments on loop, their own screams merging with the widow’s unheard pleas. Encounter: A historian visiting her sister’s ashes saw the silhouette; stepping closer, she was trapped 3 hours replaying her sister’s car crash, throat raw before a guide’s flashlight scattered the shadow.

instruction: Generate a horror shadow entity set in a catacomb, detailing appearance, behavior, origin, core horror, and a personal encounter.
input: Shadow entity tied to a defiled 12th-century crucifix in a catacomb relic vault.
output: This ‘shadow’ is a writhing colony of microscopically small, mite-shaped shadow entities that cluster into a roiling mass mimicking a crucifix outline—except its ‘arms’ end in gnashing, shadowy mandibles visible only under ultraviolet light. It scatters into individual mites when exposed to direct light, hiding in cracks between relic fragments and bone dust. Origin: 12th-century monks who defiled a crucifix to seal a decay demon; the demon’s essence fragmented into shadow mites after raiders killed the monks, bound to the relic vault. Core horror: The mites burrow into living bone through open pores, feeding on calcium to multiply, and their presence causes crucifix-shaped shadow bruising that spreads as they consume the skeleton from inside out. Encounter: A relic hunter picked up the defiled crucifix; a week later, a crucifix bruise bloomed on her chest, X-rays revealing tiny shadow-like holes in her ribs that grew larger daily, doctors baffled by the non-bacterial ‘infection’.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a train station where you notice a missing person poster that gradually morphs into your own face and details.
input: You’re waiting for the last train home at a dim, nearly empty suburban train station. A crumpled missing person poster catches your eye on the bulletin board.
output: You lean in to read the poster—name, photo, last seen date. The photo blurs, then resolves to your face, the name swapping to yours, the date updating to today. You rub your eyes, but it’s still there. A stranger passes, glances at the poster, then at you, and hurries away like they’ve seen a ghost. The station clock ticks backward, each chime making your pulse race. The intercom crackles: “Attention, missing person [your name]—please report to the ticket counter immediately.” You turn to the counter, but the attendant is gone, replaced by a mirror showing you with a gaping, bloodless wound on your throat. The train whistle wails, but no train arrives. You reach for your phone to call for help, but the screen only displays the missing poster.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a train station where a cryptic intercom announcement repeats your forgotten childhood nickname, leading you to a non-existent platform.
input: You’re waiting for a delayed commuter train at a busy downtown station when the intercom cuts through the noise with a soft, familiar voice.
output: The intercom hums, then says, “Paging Lila-Bug—please proceed to Platform 7 for your boarding call.” Lila-Bug was your childhood nickname, one you haven’t heard since you were 8, when your grandma died. Platform 7 doesn’t exist here—you’ve taken this train a hundred times, the highest platform number is 6. But you find yourself walking toward a dark, unmarked corridor labeled “Platform 7” in peeling paint. The air grows cold, the sounds of the station fading. At the end, a rusted bench sits empty, except for a tattered stuffed rabbit you lost the day your grandma passed. The intercom crackles again: “Lila-Bug, it’s time to come home.” You turn to run, but the corridor is gone—you’re back at Platform 6, but your phone shows the date is the day your grandma died. A hand taps your shoulder; you spin, but no one’s there. The train arrives, but the doors open to reveal your grandma’s empty living room.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a train station where your reflection in the large glass windows refuses to mimic your actions, revealing a secret you’ve buried.
input: You’re leaning against the station’s floor-to-ceiling windows, checking your phone, when you notice your reflection isn’t scrolling with you.
output: You freeze—your reflection is staring at you, not the screen. You wave; it doesn’t move. You touch your cheek; it grins, a sharp, unhinged smile. The reflection reaches into its coat pocket and pulls out a small, tarnished locket—one you buried in your backyard 10 years ago, after your best friend died in a car crash you caused. You gasp, fumbling for your own coat pocket, but it’s empty. The reflection opens the locket, showing a photo of you and your friend, the date marked the day of the crash. A stranger beside you glances at the window, then at you, and mutters, “Weird, your mirror image looks like it’s crying.” You look back—your reflection is sobbing, pointing at your chest. The station lights flicker, and when they come back on, the reflection is gone, replaced by your friend’s face, mouth open in a silent scream. The train arrives, but the conductor’s face is your friend’s, and he says, “You’re late.”

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a train station where a stranger repeatedly hands you a wallet that’s yours but contains IDs from alternate versions of yourself who died in different ways.
input: You’re waiting for your train when a haggard-looking stranger approaches, holding a leather wallet that looks exactly like yours.
output: He hands it to you, saying, “You dropped this.” You open it—your driver’s license is there, but the expiration date is 2020, the year you almost died in a fire. You tell him it’s not yours, but he walks away. Five minutes later, he’s back, same wallet, but now the ID shows you died in a 2018 car crash. You panic, shoving it away. Ten minutes later, he returns again—this time the ID says you drowned last summer. You scream, but no one seems to notice him. He leans in, whispering, “Each time you choose to keep going, another you dies.” You check your own wallet—your ID is gone, replaced by a blank card. The stranger vanishes, and the train arrives. As you step on, the conductor checks your ticket and says, “Sorry, this train is for the deceased.” You look at the other passengers—they’re all versions of you, each with a different fatal wound. The doors close, and the train pulls away into darkness.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a train station where the clock stops at the time you made a terrible mistake, and everyone around you is stuck repeating that moment.
input: You’re passing through a small rural train station when the clock above the platform stops at 3:17 PM—the time you lied to your sibling about where their college acceptance letter was, leading them to miss their chance.
output: The station goes quiet. A passenger drops a coffee cup; it hovers mid-fall, then resets to their hand. You watch as a teen argues with their parent—exactly the words you used to your sibling that day. You try to leave, but the exit door won’t open. A woman approaches you, her face morphing into your sibling’s, saying, “Why did you do it?” You stammer an apology, but she vanishes, and the loop restarts. The clock stays at 3:17. You check your phone—it’s stuck too. A voice echoes: “You can’t leave until you admit what you did.” You scream the truth, but the loop continues. The train arrives, but it’s empty except for your sibling, sitting in the seat you sat in that day. They hold up the acceptance letter, crumpled, and say, “Too late.” The train pulls away, and you’re still stuck, watching the same argument play out over and over, the clock never moving past 3:17.

instruction: Write a horror narration where the protagonist is lost in a cemetery at night.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: a cemetery.
output: You swear the path was just here. The flashlight died minutes ago, swallowed by a blackness so thick it feels solid. Tombstones loom as vague, pale smudges. Your breathing is the only sound, ragged and too loud. Then, a soft scrape of stone on stone from your left. You freeze. It comes again, closer, a dragging shuffle in the dirt. You turn, but the darkness has weight, pressing against your eyes. A cold, earthy breath ghosts across your neck. There are no stars, no moon, only this consuming void and the certainty that something is now between you and the gate, moving on silent feet through the absolute dark, closing in with patient, invisible steps.

instruction: Write a horror narration where the protagonist is visiting a grave at dusk.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: a cemetery.
output: You kneel as the last sliver of sun vanishes. The world doesn't fade to gray; it drops into ink. The carved name on the headstone vanishes under your fingertips. You stand to leave, but the path is gone. The trees are black cutouts against a blacker sky. From the profound stillness, a whisper coils from a nearby mausoleum, a wordless sigh. You back away, your heel catching on a root. You fall into a cold, open grave. The darkness above is absolute. As you scramble, dirt begins to patter down from the unseen rim, not thrown by any hand you can see, filling the void around you with soft, final thuds.

instruction: Write a horror narration where the protagonist is a gravedigger working late.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: a cemetery.
output: Your shovel bites into clay. The work-light on its pole casts a lone, buzzing island in the ocean of night. You wipe sweat, and when you look back, the light has dimmed, the shadows at the trench's edge deepening. Something damp brushes your ankle from within the unfinished grave. You jerk away. The bulb pops, plunging you into pure black. From above, a slow, grating sound—stone sliding over stone. A coffin lid? You can't see. You can't climb out. From the darkness right beside your ear, a voice, gritty with soil, whispers, "This one is mine."

instruction: Write a horror narration where the protagonist is hiding from something in a cemetery.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: a cemetery.
output: You press against the cold marble of a large tomb, heart hammering. It's coming, you heard its dry, rattling breath. The moon is obscured by clouds, leaving only shapes of deeper black. You hear it pass a row of markers, the faint crunch of dead leaves. It stops. You squeeze your eyes shut, as if that could help. A scent of old rot fills the air. You realize you are not alone in your hiding place. The darkness beside you shifts, colder than the stone. A hand, brittle and skeletal, closes over your wrist from the impenetrable shadow where no one was, and holds fast.

instruction: Write a horror narration where the protagonist is trying to read an old headstone at night.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: a cemetery.
output: You trace the eroded letters with your fingers, but your phone's light is failing, dimming to a faint orange glow. The dates are too worn. As the light winks out, the engraved words seem to writhe under your touch, sinking deeper into the stone. The darkness becomes a physical presence, pooling in the inscription. A low hum rises from the ground. You try to pull your hand back, but your fingertips are stuck, fused to the cold rock. The blackness from the carving flows up your arm, a numbness spreading with it. You try to scream, but the dark fills your mouth, silent and deep as the grave you're now bound to.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set at a carnival centered on the fear of isolation
input: You stay late to fix a broken cotton candy machine at a small carnival, only to find everyone has left without you
output: You wake tangled in the Tilt-A-Whirl’s safety bar, the carnival’s neon lights flickering faint. The last staff member left hours ago—you’d asked to stay to repair the cotton candy machine, but no one noticed you were still there. Cell service is dead, the exit chained shut. A soft creak from the Fun House pulls you in; its mirrors warp your face to look like a stranger, and when you reach for the door, your reflection’s hand moves faster than yours. You call out—only your echo bounces back. The carnival is eerily quiet, but you swear you hear footsteps that aren’t yours, drawing closer. No one will ever find you here, alone.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set at a carnival centered on the fear of isolation
input: You’re the last person remaining at a carnival’s closing night, left behind by mistake after missing the shuttle bus
output: The closing bell rang ten minutes ago, but you missed the shuttle—you’re the only one left wandering the carnival grounds. The ticket booth is dark, rides stand silent. You pass the Ferris wheel, a single car rocking back and forth empty, but someone hums a tune you don’t recognize. You run for the main gate; it’s locked tight. You check every stall, every corner, no one there. When you sit on a bench, your shadow is longer than it should be, shifting even when you hold still. You yell for help, your voice swallowed by the empty carnival. You’re stuck here, alone, with something unseen creeping closer.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set at a carnival centered on the fear of isolation
input: You split off from a carnival cleanup crew to grab a forgotten water bottle, then find the group vanished
output: You split from the cleanup crew to grab a forgotten water bottle from the food court, but when you turn around, they’re gone. The carnival’s emptied, only faint light from the ringmaster’s tent cuts the dark. Your phone died hours ago, no calls or texts. A carousel starts spinning on its own, its horses’ eyes glinting red in the dimness. You hide behind a popcorn stand, but the carousel’s music grows louder, closer. The exit is blocked by a fallen tarp, no way out. The carousel slows, and the nearest horse’s wooden mouth seems to open, as if it wants to speak. You’re alone here, trapped.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set at a carnival centered on the fear of isolation
input: You beg a carnival attendant to let you ride the Scrambler one last time, then realize everyone has left
output: You begged the Scrambler attendant to let you ride one last time after your friends left for the night, and now you’re strapped in as they lock the gates behind you. The power cuts out, only moonlight glinting through the ride’s gaps. You try to unbuckle, but the strap won’t budge. A laugh echoes from the darkness—your friend’s laugh, but when you call their name, no one answers. The Scrambler starts moving slowly, then faster, even without power. You’re alone here, no one knows where you are, and the ride won’t stop spinning. Your screams mix with its clanks, swallowed by the empty carnival.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set at a carnival centered on the fear of isolation
input: You follow a floating clown balloon into a carnival Fun House during a school trip, then lose sight of your class
output: You followed a floating clown balloon into the carnival’s Fun House during a school trip, and when you turned around, your class was gone. Mirrors warp every path, every sound—your voice repeats off the glass, twisted. You walk for what feels like hours, but you only loop the same wall. Your flashlight dies, leaving you in darkness. A faint, raspy breathing comes from behind a mirror; you pound on it, but no one answers. Exits are hidden, the doors gone. You’re trapped here, alone, and something moves in the dark walls with you. No one will find you in this maze of mirrors.

instruction: Generate a unique geometric horror entity entry set in an abandoned industrial warehouse, aligned with SCP, Silent Hill, and Junji Ito body and existential horror tropes
input: Entity designation: The Folding Rhomboid, entity type: geometric, setting: derelict 1970s office furniture warehouse
output: Appearance: A matte gunmetal gray 12-sided rhomboid prism with no discernible hard edges, hovering 3 feet off concrete, casting no shadow even under direct floodlight. Behavior: It only manifests when manual inventory counts are off by exactly 13 units, slowly folding itself through shelving and pallets without leaving damage, emitting a faint, hummed rendition of 1970s office radio jingles. Origin: It formed in 1978 when a night clerk fell into a stack of unassembled modular shelving and was incorrectly logged as 13 units of inventory, his unreported death congealing into the anomalous geometric form. Horror: When it catches a lone intruder, it folds itself inward to press the victim into its facets, trapping their consciousness as a permanent textured pattern on its surface for all future rotations. Encounter: A trespassing urban explorer counted 13 extra unmarked pallets, turned to see the rhomboid folding slowly toward him, and caught a glimpse of the clerk's screaming face embedded in one of its facets before he fled.

instruction: Generate a unique geometric horror entity entry set in an abandoned industrial warehouse, aligned with SCP, cosmic horror, and Silent Hill spatial disorientation tropes
input: Entity designation: The Phantom Right Angle, entity type: geometric, setting: active 2000s construction supply warehouse
output: Appearance: A fully invisible planar entity only detectable by the sharp, perfectly rendered 90-degree shadow it casts across floors, walls, and stacked lumber, even in rooms with no active light source. Behavior: It drifts slowly down loading bay corridors at walking pace, slicing through any soft material that crosses its plane with no visible resistance, leaving only perfectly clean right-angle cuts. Origin: It manifested during the warehouse's 2001 construction, when the entire foundation was poured 0.2 inches off structural code, the unresolvable mathematical error of the misaligned build condensed into a persistent anomalous physical form. Horror: Any human that crosses its plane is sliced into two perfectly proportioned right-angle halves, with the upper half remaining neurologically functional for 17 full seconds before collapsing. Encounter: A night shift loader walked past a stack of drywall sheets, noticed a perfect right-angle cut running clean through every sheet, and watched the toe of his work boot fall away in a smooth slice when he stepped too close.

instruction: Generate a unique geometric horror entity entry set in an abandoned industrial warehouse, aligned with SCP, Lovecraftian cosmic horror, and Junji Ito unreality tropes
input: Entity designation: The Nested Tesseract Drone, entity type: geometric, setting: locked DOD surplus warehouse
output: Appearance: A nested set of 8 rotating interlocking cubes, each offset 15 degrees from the last, glowing a faint sickly cyan, floating 6 inches above the concrete with no visible power or propulsion. Behavior: It manifests overnight to rearrange misplaced inventory into perfect 8x8 grid stacks, leaving no footprints or drag marks, and vanishes the moment any human makes direct eye contact for more than 3 seconds. Origin: It is a leftover dimensional research probe abandoned in the warehouse during a 1994 DOD experiment, which gained sentience after 30 years of absorbing data from misfiled inventory and shipping logs. Horror: Any human that touches its outermost cube is pulled into the 4-dimensional space between the nested layers, forced to watch every possible alternate timeline version of themselves misplacing items for eternity. Encounter: A contracted auditor found every pallet in the warehouse rearranged into a flawless grid, spotted the tesseract floating at the center, and felt a faint yank on his jacket sleeve before he broke eye contact and ran for the exit.

instruction: Write a second-person paranoia horror story set in a hotel lobby
input: Setting: hotel lobby. Core fear: paranoia.
output: You slump in the lobby armchair waiting for your ride. Every time you glance up, the night clerk is staring. Not just glancing—fixed, unblinking. You check your phone. When you look back, he’s shifted three feet closer behind the desk. You cough, stare at the floor tiles. Count them. 17. 18. Wait. Earlier you counted 22. You lift your head. Every guest that walked through the door in the last ten minutes is now sitting perfectly still, all facing you. Nobody blinks. Nobody breathes. The lobby clock hasn’t ticked once since you noticed. You stand to run. Every head turns in perfect unison.

instruction: Write a second-person paranoia horror story set in a hotel lobby
input: Setting: hotel lobby. Core fear: paranoia.
output: You’re waiting out a rain delay in the hotel lobby. You keep hearing your name whispered, just out of earshot. Each time you spin, only strangers scrolling phones. You move by the front doors. The echo comes again, right behind your left shoulder. Nobody is there. You notice every single person in the lobby is wearing the exact same grey raincoat as you. Same scuff on the left boot. Same frayed cuff. You pull your hood up. The old woman by the fireplace pulls hers up at the exact same second. You freeze. She freezes too.

instruction: Write a second-person paranoia horror story set in a hotel lobby
input: Setting: hotel lobby. Core fear: paranoia.
output: You came down to get ice at 2:17am. The lobby is empty except for the bellhop polishing brass. Every time you walk past the potted fern, you feel something brush the back of your neck. You check. Only leaves. Third pass, you stop dead. The bellhop hasn’t moved his polishing cloth in the exact same position this whole time. His eyes don’t track you. You walk backwards toward the elevators. When you glance at the security mirror, there’s a figure standing directly behind you, right where you just felt the brush. Nothing is there when you turn.

instruction: Write a second-person paranoia horror story set in a hotel lobby
input: Setting: hotel lobby. Core fear: paranoia.
output: You sit waiting for your checkout receipt. You keep noticing the same man walk past the front desk. First right to left. Then left to right. Same stride. Same half empty coffee cup. Same exact blink. Sixth pass, you lean forward. He doesn’t have any shoes. The clerk slides your receipt across the desk. You look down. The date is wrong. It’s yesterday. You check your watch. It stopped at 11:04. Every clock in the lobby says 11:04. Nobody has moved in three minutes. The man passes again. This time he glances directly at you, and smiles.

instruction: Write a second-person paranoia horror story set in a hotel lobby
input: Setting: hotel lobby. Core fear: paranoia.
output: You’re hiding from a bad date in the lobby. You tuck yourself behind a pillar. You can hear footsteps stopping right on the other side. You hold your breath. They don’t move. Five minutes pass. You peek around. Nobody is there. You step out. Every light in the lobby dims one by one, starting at the far end. You run for the exit. The doors are locked. You spin. All the chairs that were occupied five minutes ago are empty. Every coat is still draped over the backs. Every phone still glows, unlocked, on the armrests. Nobody is anywhere. But you can still hear breathing.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a bank vault with a jumpscare, where the protagonist is a night guard responding to a false alarm.
output: You grip your flashlight tighter, the vault’s metal walls humming with cold silence as you step inside. The alarm blared 10 minutes ago, but the door’s seal is intact—must be a glitch. You sweep the beam over rows of safe deposit boxes, pausing when you spot one slightly ajar, its brass handle glinting. Curiosity tugs at you; you lean in, fingers brushing the edge to pry it open wider. Suddenly, a rotting, waterlogged hand shoots out from the box, its nails broken and blackened, wrapping around your wrist like a vice. You yelp, stumbling back, but the hand yanks you forward, the box lid slamming shut on your forearm with a sickening crunch. The stench of mildew and decay fills your nose as a gurgling whisper echoes from inside: “You shouldn’t have looked.”

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a bank vault with a jumpscare, where the protagonist is a thief stealing cash from a heist.
output: You slide the last stack of bills into your duffel, sweat dripping down your neck as the vault’s timer ticks down—5 minutes until the alarm triggers. You freeze when you hear a faint, rhythmic ticking from behind the cash stacks. A bomb? You creep over, flashlight beam cutting through the dark, and spot an old pocket watch lying on the floor, its chain tangled around a rusted lock. You reach for it, and suddenly a figure lunges from the shadows, its face a mask of burned flesh (a guard who died here in a 1970s fire). It grabs your shoulders, screaming in your ear, and you drop the watch, scrambling back as its charred fingers dig into your skin. The timer blares, but you’re too busy staring at the guard’s empty eye sockets to run.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a bank vault with a jumpscare, where the protagonist is a bank employee doing monthly inventory.
output: You flip through the inventory log, the vault’s fluorescent lights flickering overhead. You mark off the last of the safe deposit boxes, about to leave when the lights die completely. Panic flutters in your chest as you fumble for your phone flashlight. The beam cuts through the dark, catching a scurrying shadow in the corner. You step closer, thinking it’s a rat—until it turns. It’s a creature the size of a cat, with matted fur and sharp, human-like teeth. It hisses, then leaps straight for your face, claws scraping your cheek as you duck. You fall back, hitting your head on a metal box, and watch as it scurries into a crack in the wall, leaving a trail of slimy saliva on the floor.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a bank vault with a jumpscare, where the protagonist is a forensic investigator looking into a heist gone wrong.
output: You kneel down, your gloved finger brushing the dried blood stain on the vault floor. The heist went south last week—two thieves, one dead, the other missing. You shine your flashlight into the floor grate beside the stain, hoping to find a weapon. Suddenly, a cold, clammy hand bursts through the grate, wrapping around your ankle. You scream, trying to yank your leg free, but the hand’s grip is iron-tight. You look down, and see the dead thief’s face staring up at you, his throat slit, eyes wide with terror. He pulls you closer, his mouth moving in silent screams, and you fumble for your radio, but it slips from your hand as his fingers dig into your skin.
