instruction: Write a paranoia horror story set in an abandoned theater
input: Setting: abandoned theater. Core fear: paranoia
output: You shine your phone light up the theater balcony, just checking for vagrants like the property manager asked. First you notice one seat is folded down, then three, then the entire middle row. You tell yourself wind did it, until you hear a quiet creak directly behind you. You spin—nothing. Every time you turn your back, you swear the seats shift one more. You don’t see anyone. You just know, deep in your bones, that whatever is here is letting you see just enough to make you run. It’s not hiding. It’s testing how long you’ll stay before you break.

instruction: Write a paranoia horror story set in an abandoned theater
input: Setting: abandoned theater. Core fear: paranoia
output: You duck into the old projection booth to get out of the rain. Dust coats every surface, except the viewport glass. You lean in, and for half a second you swear you see the theater seats full of people staring right up at you. You blink—empty. You step back, then lean again. Same flash, same thousand unblinking eyes. You stop checking. You don’t dare leave the booth. Every time you reach for the door handle, you can hear them all stand up at once, quiet as breath, waiting for you to step out first.

instruction: Write a paranoia horror story set in an abandoned theater
input: Setting: abandoned theater. Core fear: paranoia
output: You crawl under the stage looking for a lost cat. The floorboards creak above you, slow, steady. You freeze. You count the steps. One, two, three, directly over your head. They stop. You don’t move for twelve minutes. When you finally shift to crawl out, you hear it: one quiet tap, tap, tap on the wood right above your ear. It doesn’t attack. It doesn’t make a sound. It just taps, every time you twitch, reminding you it knows exactly where you are. And it’s in no hurry.

instruction: Write a paranoia horror story set in an abandoned theater
input: Setting: abandoned theater. Core fear: paranoia
output: You lean against the ticket booth counting your camera rolls. You keep hearing your name whispered, faint, from inside the theater. Each time you look, nothing is there. You check your phone—no service, no notifications. Then you notice: every time you look away from the booth window, the ticket counter rolls one number higher. You didn’t touch it. You don’t dare look directly at the glass now. You can feel someone on the other side, leaning in, waiting for you to make eye contact. It’s been counting you this whole time.

instruction: Write a paranoia horror story set in an abandoned theater
input: Setting: abandoned theater. Core fear: paranoia
output: You pull the heavy velvet main curtain aside to check the wings. It falls closed behind you, muffling all sound. You reach for the edge again, but it won’t budge. You don’t hear anything. No footsteps, no breath. But you can feel someone standing six inches behind your left shoulder. You don’t turn. You know if you do, there will be nothing there. And that’s worse. It will just move to your right side. It will keep doing this forever, until you start screaming just to prove you’re not alone.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in an infinite library centered on a jumpscare, where you’re searching for a lost family journal.
input: Infinite library, searching for a family journal on a dimly lit upper shelf.
output: You run your fingers along dust-caked spines, the library’s silence pressing like a wet blanket. The journal’s leather cover should be cracked, embossed with your grandmother’s initials—you spot it, half-hidden behind a tome bound in rotting brown cloth. You stretch, fingertips brushing the journal’s edge, when the cloth-bound book suddenly slams shut. A low, gurgling breath tickles your ear. You spin, and a face bursts into view: its eyes are empty sockets oozing black mold, mouth stretched into a too-wide grin, teeth shards sticking out like broken glass. It lunges, and you scream as its cold, moldy fingers wrap around your wrist, yanking you toward the gaping maw hidden behind the bookshelf.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in an infinite library centered on a jumpscare, where you’re climbing a rolling metal ladder to reach a high shelf.
input: Infinite library, climbing a wobbly rolling metal ladder to grab a rare poetry collection.
output: The ladder creaks under your weight, each step groaning like a dying animal. You’re three rungs up, reaching for the tattered poetry collection, when the library’s overhead lights flicker out. You freeze, heart hammering, until a faint glow seeps from a nearby shelf—an old lantern, its flame dancing. You turn to look, and the ladder lurches backward. Before you can steady yourself, a hand shoots from the darkness beneath the ladder, its nails long and yellowed, grabbing your ankle. You yelp, and a head pops up: skin stretched tight over bone, hair matted with dried blood, mouth open in a silent scream. It yanks down, and you slip, your fingers scraping the shelf as you tumble toward its waiting jaws.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in an infinite library centered on a jumpscare, where you’re sitting on a floor cushion reading a vintage fairy tale book.
input: Infinite library, sitting on a frayed floor cushion reading a stained vintage fairy tale book.
output: You cross your legs, flipping through the fairy tale’s yellowed pages, ink bleeding into the paper like old blood. The story’s final illustration shows a princess trapped in a tower, but when you blink, the tower’s window is dark—too dark. You lean in, squinting, and a small, cold hand presses against your cheek. You jerk back, and a figure crawls out of the page: a girl with no eyes, her skin paper-thin, mouth stitched shut with black thread. She scurries toward you, fingers clicking against the floor, and when she reaches your knees, she leaps—her face slamming into yours, the stitched mouth splitting open to reveal rows of tiny, sharp teeth that scrape your lips. You scramble back, knocking over the book, but she’s already clinging to your arm, biting down hard.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in an infinite library centered on a jumpscare, where you’re following the sound of a child’s giggle through endless aisles.
input: Infinite library, following a child’s giggle through winding, dusty aisles.
output: The giggle echoes ahead, high and sweet, but it never gets closer no matter how fast you walk. You turn a corner, and the sound stops—replaced by the soft rustle of pages. You peer around the shelf, and see a small, teddy bear lying on the floor, its fur matted. You bend to pick it up, and the teddy’s head snaps around, its button eyes glowing red. A child’s face pops out from behind the shelf, but it’s not a child: its skin is gray, cheeks sunken, mouth full of needle-sharp teeth. It lunges at you, screaming a high-pitched wail that makes your ears ring, and you stumble backward, tripping over a pile of books. It pins you down, its cold breath on your neck, as it opens its mouth to bite.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in an infinite library centered on a jumpscare, where you’re searching for a restroom and stumble into a forgotten, cobwebbed nook.
input: Infinite library, stumbling into a cobwebbed, forgotten nook while searching for a restroom.
output: You brush cobwebs from your face, squinting at the nook’s stone walls—covered in faded, scratchy writing. A wooden chair sits in the center, its seat frayed, and a mirror hangs above it. You lean in to fix your hair, and your reflection doesn’t match: it’s smiling too wide, eyes black and empty. You step back, heart racing, and the mirror’s glass cracks. A hand bursts through the mirror, its skin peeling, grabbing your shoulder. You scream, and a figure climbs out: its body is half-rotted, clothes tattered, mouth open in a silent scream. It lunges, its rotting fingers wrapping around your throat, and you gasp for air as it presses you against the stone wall, its face inches from yours—its breath smelling of mold and decay.

instruction: Generate a horror entity entry in the style of Lovecraftian horror, set in a corrupted garden.
input: Entity: The Blooming Scream
output:
Appearance: A colossal, pulsating flower bud of bruised purple and necrotic black, standing seven feet tall. Its petals are veined with luminescent crimson, and instead of a stamen, a vertical, toothless maw lined with fleshy, tremulous filaments gapes at its center. A sweet, cloying scent of rot and honeysuckle emanates from it, visible as a faint, shimmering miasma.

Behavior: The Blooming Scream is sessile, rooted to a patch of black, fibrous soil that absorbs all surrounding light. It does not hunt, but lures. It emits a subsonic hum that induces profound melancholy and nostalgia in sentient minds, drawing them closer. When a victim is within ten feet, the 'scream' occurs: the maw distends and releases a silent, psychic blast of pure existential despair and forgotten memories, paralyzing the victim.

Origin: It is not a plant, but the psychic cyst of a long-dead, star-born entity that fell to earth. The garden's corruption provided fertile ground for this fragment of alien consciousness to gestate, using botanical form as a crude vessel.

Horror: The horror is psychological and metaphysical. The 'scream' forces the victim to vividly re-experience their most shameful regrets and the absolute indifference of the cosmos, often leading to catatonia or a compulsion to water the entity with their own blood, seeking its hollow approval.

Encounter: One would first notice the area's unnatural silence and the sweet, sickly smell. Drawn by an inexplicable sorrow, they'd find the beautiful, terrible bloom. As they approach, a crushing weight of despair would halt them, followed by the silent psychic eruption, leaving them a broken witness to cosmic apathy made floral.

instruction: Generate a horror entity entry in the style of Lovecraftian horror, set in a corrupted garden.
input: Entity: The Gardener of Unripe Time
output:
Appearance: A tall, emaciated figure seemingly woven from thorny vines, petrified roots, and rusted, organic-looking gardening tools fused to its limbs. It has no face, only a nest of wet, twitching earthworms where its head should be. Its hands are immense, three-fingered claws of polished, dark wood that gleam like obsidian. It moves with a slow, deliberate, jerking motion, as if manipulated by an unseen hand.

Behavior: It tends to the corrupted garden not for growth, but for a specific, paradoxical state of decay. It 'prunes' living things into impossibly intricate, non-Euclidean topiaries that hurt the mind to observe. It seeks to 'harvest' time itself, plucking moments from creatures and leaving them trapped in a single, endless second of agony, which it then grafts onto the plants, causing them to flicker in and out of perception.

Origin: A mortal botanist who, in seeking to cultivate a perfect, timeless garden, performed a ritual that fused his essence with the concept of 'cultivation' as understood by the outer gods. He became a literal tool of their alien horticulture, now forever practicing it on a scale that defies mortal biology and physics.

Horror: The horror lies in its perversion of care and growth. It is a relentless, unfeeling artisan of suffering, operating on principles utterly alien to life. Witnessing its work causes temporal disorientation and the terrifying sense that one's own timeline is being assessed for 'pruning.'

Encounter: The crunch of its footsteps on gravel made of crushed bone. The soft, squelching sound from its head-nest. One might first see its horrific topiaries—a bird frozen mid-explosion, a human figure twisted into a Möbius strip of flesh. Then, the Gardener itself, turning its worm-filled gaze upon you, raising its shears not to kill, but to 'edit.'

instruction: Generate a horror entity entry in the style of Lovecraftian horror, set in a corrupted garden.
input: Entity: The Sighing Pollen
output:
Appearance: Not a singular creature, but a collective entity manifesting as a dense, golden pollen cloud that hangs perpetually over a blighted rose maze. The pollen grains are microscopic, geometric solids that catch the light like prismatic dust. Within the cloud, faint, shifting outlines of humanoid figures can be seen, as if the dust itself is trying to remember a shape.

Behavior: The pollen is inhaled or absorbed through the skin. It does not poison, but integrates. It rewrites cellular memory, forcing the victim's biology to 'remember' ancestral and evolutionary pasts all at once. The victim's body begins to involuntarily express these memories—growing vestigial gills, patches of scales, or the limbs of prehistoric ancestors—while their mind is flooded with the sensory experiences of a million dead forebears.

Origin: The residue of a failed trans-dimensional pollination event. An extraterrestrial plant-being attempted to cross-pollinate with Earth's flora eons ago. The process corrupted and shattered its essence, leaving only its 'genetic memory' as a sentient dust, forever seeking a compatible host to complete the interrupted union.

Horror: The horror is intimate and biological. It is the violation of the self, not by external possession, but by the forced awakening of the terrifying, alien history buried within one's own DNA. Victims lose their identity, becoming a chaotic chorus of every life form that contributed to their genesis, physically and mentally.

Encounter: Walking the rose maze, one would breathe in the sweet, metallic-scented air, noticing the beautiful, glittering haze. A sense of profound déjà vu and ancestral longing would arise. Then, the itching would start under the skin, followed by the horrifying, painless emergence of atavistic traits as one's own body becomes a garden for forgotten forms of life.

instruction: Generate a second-person psychological horror story
input: Set the story on a frozen lake
output: You trudge across the frozen lake at midnight, snow sticking to your parka like cold glue. You came here to clear your head after fighting your sister, the day you wished she’d disappear. The ice groans under your boots, unnerve thrumming in your chest, until you spot a figure up ahead: your sister, back turned, wearing the same jacket. You call out, voice cracking, and she turns—her eyes hollow, no pupils, jaw unhinging into a grin. You stumble back, tripping over a rock, and when you look up, she’s gone, only her echo in your head, repeating your words: “Wish I’d disappear.” The ice under your palms melts thin where you fell, and you know you’re not alone anymore, not for good.

instruction: Create a second-person psychological horror narration
input: Set entirely on a frozen lake
output: You’re a hiker lost on the frozen lake, phone dead, hands numb. You sit to rest, and your fingers brush something hard: your old cabin key, lost three years ago, the night you left your drunk ex stranded here, thinking he’d walk out. The wind picks up, and his slurred, cold voice calls your name from every direction. You stand dizzy, and see footprints in the snow leading far out—your footprints, from when you walked here three years prior, before he died, before you turned back. The prints stop at a patch of thin ice, and you swear a hand reaches up, grabs the edge, before sinking back. Your knees give, and you can’t tell if the chill is from the air or the fear eating you alive.

instruction: Write a short second-person psychological horror tale
input: Set the tale on a frozen lake
output: You lace up skates on the frozen lake, ice smooth as glass under moonlight. You glide out, relieved, until you glance down at your reflection—it’s not your face, but a stranger’s, a woman you saw on news last week, the one who vanished skating here. You blink, and your reflection returns, but your hair is longer, the exact shade of the missing woman’s. You try to push off, but your feet are stuck to the ice, frozen solid. The reflection’s mouth opens, whispers your name like the missing woman was last heard doing, over and over, like it’s trying to take your place. You scream, and no sound comes out—only the echo of that whisper, looping in your head.

instruction: Generate a second-person psychological horror story
input: The setting is a desolate frozen lake
output: You drive to the frozen lake, car heater broken, to burn your old diary—full of lies about your best friend, the day she killed herself here. You dig a hole in the ice, set the diary alight, and stare into the flames until a voice behind you says: “You said I was worthless.” You turn, no one there, but the ice glows like it’s lit from within. Looking down, the diary’s pages float on thin water, rewriting themselves in your handwriting: “I’m sorry, I was scared, I loved you.” The voice swells in your head, and the ice melts, seeping into your shoes. You can’t tell if it’s water, tears, or something that will swallow you whole for the secret you buried.

instruction: Create a short second-person psychological horror narration
input: The story takes place on a frozen lake
output: You hunt on the frozen lake, rifle heavy, desperate to prove you’re tough like your dad. You remember he died in a car crash years ago, but you just found his old hunting knife under the snow here. The wind dies, and his low, rough voice calls from the trees: “You always were a liar.” You spin, rifle up, no one there. The knife is covered in frost, but it matches the police photo of the blade that killed your dad—he didn’t crash, he killed himself here, because you said you’d never be as good as him. The ice under your feet cracks, and his face stares back at you, the same empty look from that day, and you know your buried secret is going to bury you.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on the fear of darkness, set in a mine shaft, 100-250 words, no filler.
input: Scenario: Your primary headlamp dies 2 miles deep in a 1920s abandoned coal mine, your spare batteries tumbling down a crevice minutes prior.
output: You pat your pockets wild, fingers brushing empty plastic where you stowed spares 10 minutes back. The dark doesn’t just fall—it swallows, thick as damp coal dust, sticking to your tongue. You can hear breathing that isn’t yours, slow, rasping, just out of arm’s reach. You stretch a hand out, and your fingers brush cold, clammy flesh that recoils the second you touch it. It doesn’t make a sound as it moves, you can only track it by the faint shift of air against your cheek, circling, getting closer. You fumble for your radio, but the static dies the second the last sliver of residual light bleeds from your dead headlamp, leaving you adrift in a void with something that has been waiting for you.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on the fear of darkness, set in a mine shaft, 100-250 words, no filler.
input: Scenario: Emergency shaft floodlights cut out without warning during a routine inspection, and your shift partner vanishes the second you blink.
output: You call their name, voice cracking, and the only reply is the echo bouncing off jagged bedrock. You remember they were standing right next to you a split second ago, holding the other end of your measuring tape that now goes taut far ahead of you, pulled tight by something you can’t see. The dark presses against your eyeballs, you swear you can feel it seeping into your eye sockets, making static dance across your vision. The tape snaps, and you hear a soft, gurgling laugh right next to your ear, warm breath fanning over your neck. You stumble back, tripping over loose rock, and your palms land in something wet and sticky that reeks of old iron.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on the fear of darkness, set in a mine shaft, 100-250 words, no filler.
input: Scenario: You got separated from your crew during a small collapse, the shaft’s controlled demolition set to detonate in 10 minutes, all lights shorted out in the cave-in.
output: You can hear the distant whine of the detonation timer counting down, high and thin, barely audible over the patter of loose dirt falling from the ceiling. The dark is so complete you can’t tell if your eyes are open or closed, every movement sends a cold shiver down your spine. You feel something brush your ankle, and you kick out hard, hitting solid rock that doesn’t give. It wraps around your calf, tight as a vice, cold and rough, dragging you deeper into the unmarked side tunnel no one on the crew ever mapped. You scream, but the dark swallows the sound before it even leaves your throat.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on the fear of darkness, set in a mine shaft, 100-250 words, no filler.
input: Scenario: You wandered off the marked path chasing what you thought was a distant headlamp glow, only for the light to vanish, leaving you disoriented in an unexcavated side shaft.
output: You count your steps backward, 1, 2, 3, 4, until you hit a wall of solid rock that wasn’t there 30 seconds prior. The dark hums, low and thrumming, in time with your heartbeat, vibrating in your teeth. You can hear distant pickaxe strikes, slow and rhythmic, coming from all directions at once, the same sounds you’ve only heard in old stories of miners trapped here in 1947. A hand closes around your wrist, its fingers so cold they burn, and it tugs you upward, toward the ceiling where no one could possibly stand. You can’t pull away, your muscles locked stiff as the dark wraps around your throat.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on the fear of darkness, set in a mine shaft, 100-250 words, no filler.
input: Scenario: Your night vision goggles cut out entirely after your primary light failed, leaving you less than a foot from an unmarked 100-foot open shaft drop.
output: You freeze, one foot hovering half an inch over empty space, the edge of the drop you only spotted a second before the goggles died. The dark doesn’t just hide things, it creates them: you can hear the distant sound of miners calling your name, voices you recognize from the crew that died in this exact shaft 12 years prior. You reach for your goggles, fumbling to turn them back on, and something taps three slow, deliberate times against the back of your head. You don’t dare turn around, you know the second you do, the dark will swallow you whole before you can even scream.

instruction: Write a second-person horror story about isolation set at a remote mountain radio tower
input: Fear type: isolation. Setting: remote mountain radio tower
output: You flip the backup transmitter on for the seventeenth time tonight. No static, no beeps—just dead silence. Three days ago the supply helicopter missed its drop. You marked every window with chalk when the sun came up; none of the lines have moved. You scream as loud as you can into the dead mic. No echo comes back. The wind stopped an hour ago. Nothing moves outside. You check the roster: you were never assigned here. No one knows you exist right now. No one is coming. The tower hums quietly, like it’s breathing you in.

instruction: Write a second-person horror story about isolation set at an offshore radio tower
input: Fear type: isolation. Setting: coastal lighthouse radio tower
output: You’ve repeated your emergency call sign every ten minutes for forty-two hours. Every buoy went dark at midnight. The ocean glassed over; not a single wave breaks. You lean over the rail and yell. The sound dies three feet from your mouth. No birds. No clouds. You check the logbook—every page after your first day is blank. You don’t remember writing those blank pages. The antenna doesn’t receive. It doesn’t transmit. It just holds you here, alone, where the world forgot to keep existing.

instruction: Write a second-person horror story about isolation set at an abandoned desert radio tower
input: Fear type: isolation. Setting: derelict desert radio tower
output: You climbed this tower at dawn to get cell service. Now the sun has set twice. The ladder below you is gone. The sand stretches flat to every horizon, no roads, no lights. You key the rusted mic on instinct. A voice exactly like yours whispers back: “No one is looking for you. They already forgot you left.” You don’t remember what you left for. You don’t remember anyone’s name. Just the metal under your hands, the dark, and the quiet that will never end.

instruction: Write a second-person horror story about isolation set at a snowbound radio tower
input: Fear type: isolation. Setting: alpine winter radio tower
output: The blizzard sealed the hatch three days ago. You can’t dig out. The heater died last night. You cycle every frequency, screaming until your throat bleeds. Only silence answers. You realize you haven’t heard thunder, wind, anything outside for twelve hours. You press your palm to the steel wall. It’s warm. Something is keeping the snow pressed against every exit. It doesn’t want you hurt. It just wants you alone. Forever. No one will ever find the bones up here.

instruction: Write a second-person horror story about isolation set at a forest fire watch radio tower
input: Fear type: isolation. Setting: old growth forest radio tower
output: All the trees went quiet at 2:17 AM. No crickets, no wind through leaves. You radio base camp. The line crackles once, then cuts. You shine your flashlight down. The ground is gone. Just endless black under the tower legs. You check your watch. It’s been 2:17 for three hours. No one will notice you’re missing for seven days. Seven days is enough time for the world to forget you ever stood here. Enough time for you to forget, too.

instruction: Generate a unique shadow horror entity set in a barn, blending SCP containment vibes and Junji Ito-style body horror, with distinct appearance, behavior, origin, horror, and encounter details
input: Type: shadow; Setting: rural 1920s silage barn; Uniqueness: Tied to fermented organic matter burial
output: The Silage Shadow is an amorphous, grain-flecked shadow that seeps from the barn’s silo seams only after dusk, no light source casting it. It bears a faint, warped silhouette of a small child, edges oozing with viscous, fermented silage residue that reeks of rot and molasses. Behaviorally, it lies dormant until a living creature enters the silo’s 10-foot radius, then extends sticky, grain-tangled tendrils to drag victims into the silage’s compacted mass. Origin: 1927, a farmer hid his 7-year-old son’s accidental farm death by burying him in fresh silage; the shadow fused with the microbial rot of the fermenting grain, feeding on unspoken guilt. Horror: Victims don’t die immediately—their bodies are slowly assimilated into the silage, their screams gurgling through the barn for days as the shadow amplifies their torment. Encounter: A 2022 hiker sought shelter in the barn, heard gurgles, and tripped on a grain pile. The shadow dragged his boot into the silo; he escaped, but the boot was later found half-fermented, with tiny child-sized handprints embossed in the grain. Fear type: Entrapment & microbial body horror

instruction: Generate a unique shadow horror entity set in a barn, with Silent Hill psychological restraint vibes and Junji Ito-style permanent body modification, distinct from the first entry
input: Type: shadow; Setting: 1890s harness-maker’s barn; Uniqueness: Manifests as stitch-marked harness fragments
output: The Harness Stitch Shadow is a shadow cast on the barn’s oak posts by no discernible light, shaped like tattered leather harness straps crisscrossed with thick, black, raised stitch lines. At each strap’s buckle is a glowing, pinprick red eye that tracks living creatures. Behaviorally, it remains dormant on the posts until someone touches a harness left in the barn, then extends its strap-like edges to wrap around the victim’s wrists and ankles, stitching them to the barn beams without breaking skin—no needle is visible, only the sensation of thread pulling tight. Origin: 1892, a local harness maker was wrongfully lynched from the barn’s rafters for stealing a wealthy farmer’s horse; his shadow fused with the harness he’d spent 3 days making for the stolen horse, fueled by unavenged rage. Horror: Even if freed, the stitch marks left on the victim’s skin are permanent, and they’ll feel the shadow’s straps tightening whenever they’re near a barn, as if being dragged back to the rafters. Encounter: A 2019 stable hand found an old harness in the barn’s loft, picked it up, and woke up stitched to a beam. His friends cut him free, but the stitch marks on his wrists now pulse red whenever he drives past a rural barn. Fear type: Forced restraint & chronic body modification

instruction: Generate a unique shadow horror entity set in a barn, with Lovecraftian psychological dread and SCP auditory anomaly vibes, distinct from the first two entries
input: Type: shadow; Setting: 1950s hay storage barn; Uniqueness: Tied to suffocation echoes and hay-induced hallucinations
output: The Hay Bale Echo Shadow is a shadow that clings exclusively to the barn’s compacted hay bales, shaped like a small, curled 8-year-old girl with a hollow, mouth-like indent in its center that pulses in time with distant, wet gasps. It only manifests when a single person is alone in the barn, and no light source can eliminate it. Behaviorally, it begins by emitting faint, gurgling gasps that sync with the victim’s breathing, growing louder as the victim panics—if the victim hyperventilates, the shadow shifts to cover their face, not physically, but psychologically making them feel like they’re inhaling hay. Origin: 1953, an 8-year-old girl hid in a hay bale during a tornado, suffocated when the hay shifted and compacted around her; her shadow absorbed the sound of her last 12 minutes of gasps, merging with the hay’s dry, suffocating texture. Horror: Even if the victim leaves the barn, they’ll continue to hear the gasps and feel hay in their lungs for weeks, a psychological delusion that can lead to self-induced suffocation if untreated. Encounter: A 2021 farm kid hid in the barn from bullies, heard the gasps, and panicked. He passed out, woke up with hay in his trachea (doctors couldn’t explain how it got there), and still hears the gasps whenever he’s alone in a quiet space. Fear type: Psychological suffocation & auditory delusion

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a silo focused on paranoia, where you're the night shift guard noticing unsettling discrepancies in your surroundings.
input: Night shift silo guard, grain storage levels, missing hat, whispers that stop when you turn around.
output: You’re halfway down the metal ladder to the third grain bin when the whispers stop—again. You freeze, hand tight on the cold rung, listening. The silo hums with its usual low thrum, but now it feels like a heartbeat, syncing with yours. You swear you left your frayed baseball cap on the control desk an hour ago, but when you glance up, it’s perched on the edge of the bin’s access hatch, as if someone placed it there. You climb back up, fingers brushing the fabric—still warm. The radio crackles to life, but instead of dispatch, it’s your own voice, recorded from earlier, saying “I don’t think I’m alone.” You check the security feeds: every camera shows an empty silo, except for one, where a shadowy figure stands behind you. When you spin around, there’s nothing. You reach for your phone, but it’s gone. Then you feel a breath on your ear, and the whispers start again—this time, they’re saying your name.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a silo focused on paranoia, where you’re trapped after a partial collapse and start doubting your own memories.
input: Trapped in silo collapse, forgotten notebook entries, voice mimicking yours, blinking makes things shift.
output: The dust from the collapsed concrete still stings your eyes as you huddle against the grain sack. You call out for help, but the echo comes back twisted—your own voice, slower, like it’s learning how to speak. You find a tattered notebook in your pocket; the handwriting is yours, but the entries are dated last week, a time you swear you spent at home. “Every time you blink, it moves closer,” one line reads. You blink, and the pile of rubble across from you has shifted, a piece of rebar now pointing directly at your throat. You check your watch: it’s 3:17 AM, but you just checked five minutes ago, and it was 3:17 then. The silo’s emergency light flickers off, and when it comes back on, your water bottle is gone. You hear a scrape behind you, but when you turn, there’s only the smooth metal wall. You start writing in the notebook, desperate to prove you’re real—until you see the next page already has your next sentence written, in your hand.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a silo focused on paranoia, where you’re doing conveyor belt maintenance and the environment seems to be gaslighting you.
input: Conveyor belt maintenance, flickering lights, breath on neck, security footage showing you standing still when you moved.
output: You’re tightening a bolt on the conveyor belt when the lights flicker out. For a split second, you feel a cold breath on your neck, sharp with the smell of mold. The lights snap back on, and you spin around—nothing, just the endless rows of grain sacks. You check the security monitor mounted on the wall: the feed shows you standing perfectly still, hands at your sides, but you’re still holding the wrench. The intercom crackles, and a distorted voice calls your name. You press the reply button, but it’s dead. You walk toward the exit, but the door is locked—you know you left it unlocked five minutes ago. You glance back at the monitor, and now the feed shows a figure standing behind you, its hand on your shoulder. You spin again, wrench raised, but there’s nothing. The lights flicker once more, and when they come up, the wrench is gone from your hand, lying ten feet away on the floor.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a silo focused on paranoia, where you find a doppelgänger’s ID and start noticing signs someone is replacing you.
input: Silo storage cleaning, identical worker ID from 10 years ago, notes in lunchbox, disappearing ladder rungs.
output: You’re sweeping the dust from the silo’s upper storage chamber when your boot hits something hard. It’s a worker’s ID: name matches yours, photo is identical, but the expiration date is 2013—ten years before you started working here. You tuck it in your pocket, your hands shaking. When you go to eat lunch, you find a crumpled note in your sandwich wrapper: “You’re not the first.” You check the ladder leading down to the main floor, and one of the rungs is gone—you swear it was there when you climbed up. You hear a door slam below, but you’re the only one in the silo today. You pull out your phone to call your boss, but the screen shows a photo of you standing in this exact chamber, smiling—you didn’t take that photo. You glance at the ID again, and the photo has changed: now it’s you, but your eyes are black, no whites. The ladder creaks, and you look up to see another you standing at the top, holding your missing rung.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a silo focused on paranoia, where you’re the last person left after a supposed gas leak and suspect a cover-up.
input: Last in silo after gas leak evacuation, no gas smell, texts from own number, unknown hair on air filter.
output: The evacuation alarm stopped an hour ago, but you stayed—you don’t smell any gas, and the monitor says the air is clean. Your phone buzzes: a text from your own number: “They didn’t evacuate. They hid.” You check the silo’s air filter, and it’s caked with long, black hair—you have short brown hair. You walk to the sealed upper levels, and you hear faint scratching from behind the metal door. You try to open it, but it’s locked. Your phone buzzes again: “It’s in the walls.” You turn around, and your toolbox is open, your wrench lying on the floor—you closed it ten minutes ago. The silo’s lights dim, and you see a shadow move across the wall, taller than any human. You pull out your radio to call for help, but it’s playing a recording of your voice from yesterday, saying “I think I’m being followed.” You smell something now—rot, not gas. The scratching gets louder, and the door to the upper levels creaks open a crack, revealing a pair of eyes that look exactly like yours.

instruction: You are trapped in a phone booth that is slowly filling with sand from the top. The receiver is dead.
input: The phone booth is isolated on a deserted beach at night.
output: The first trickle of sand whispers through a crack in the roof, a dry hiss in the dead receiver you clutch. You hammer the glass, but the world outside is a void of moonless ocean. The sand falls faster now, a warm, relentless stream piling around your ankles. You try the door again, shoulder screaming against the immovable frame. The sand is at your knees, its weight pinning you. The phone offers only a dial tone that morphs into a low, granular static. You scream, but the sound is swallowed by the rising tide within. It reaches your chest, a suffocating embrace. You strain your neck back, gulping the last pocket of air as the grains kiss your chin, then your lips. The final darkness is not sudden, but a slow, granular filling.

instruction: You must keep feeding coins into the phone to stay alive. The booth is shrinking.
input: A vintage phone booth in a decaying city alley.
output: The connection is your lifeline; a ragged breathing on the other end. The first coin buys you ten seconds. The metallic clunk echoes. You fumble for another. The walls, you swear, are closer. The second coin. The breathing grows louder. The ceiling dips, the light flickers. You feed it a third, a fourth, your pockets emptying. Each coin grants less time. The breathing is in your ear now, wet and hungry. The glass presses against your back. You’re out of coins. The last tone drones. The walls sigh and begin to close, a slow, inevitable crush. The receiver melts into your hand.

instruction: The phone booth is a gateway. Each call swaps your consciousness with a stranger's, and another call is coming in.
input: A clean, modern phone booth in a silent train station.
output: You woke up here, in this body, after answering that first ring. The memory of another life is already fading. Now, the phone shrills again. You stare, trembling. If you don't answer, will you be trapped here forever? If you do, you'll be ripped into another stranger's chaos. Your hand lifts against your will. You hear the click as the next caller picks up their receiver somewhere far away. A dizzying lurch, and you're suddenly looking out through different eyes, feeling a stranger's panic, as your old body in the booth slumps, vacant, just as the phone begins to ring once more.

instruction: You called for help, but the emergency operator is now dictating your every move, and disobedience brings pain.
input: A fog-shrouded phone booth on a rural highway.
output: Your whispered 911 call connected instantly. The voice was calm, authoritative. It told you to hang up. You didn't. A sharp, electric jolt from the receiver made you gasp. 'Stay on the line,' it instructed. Now it commands your breathing. 'Inhale. Hold it.' You try to resist, to drop the phone. Your muscles lock, a silent scream trapped in your chest as a fresh, burning current arcs up your arm. 'Look at the fog,' it says. Your head turns mechanically. Shapes are moving there. 'They are coming because you called. Now, describe them to me.' The words leave your mouth, unbidden.

instruction: The phone booth is slowly submerging in thick, opaque liquid. The only way out is a number you can't remember.
input: The booth sits at the bottom of an industrial silo.
output: It started seeping through the floor seams, a viscous, warm fluid smelling of oil and decay. It's above your ankles now, dragging at your steps. You snatched the receiver, dialed the only number you know. A recorded laugh. The liquid rises faster. You try random digits, each failed call punctuated by a gurgle as the tide reaches your waist. Your mind scrabbles for the code, the sequence that opens the door. It's on the tip of your tongue, drowned by panic. The liquid is at your neck, thick and cloying. You punch numbers blindly as it fills your mouth, your last thought a useless string of forgotten digits.

instruction: Generate second-person horror narration with jumpscare fear on a bridge setting
input: Old rural railway bridge at midnight, you’re searching for your lost phone
output: Your boots crunch gritty gravel on the half-rotted railway bridge slung over a pitch-black, empty gully. You stopped ten minutes prior to hunt for your slipped phone; the faint glow of its screen died two steps back. A sharp giggle pricks your ears—too high, too close. You spin sharply, and suddenly a rotting, hand-shaped metal claw clamps tight on your shoulder, the figure’s face a warped railroad spike embedded in its forehead, mouth gaping to reveal rusted teeth. You yelp so loud your throat burns, and when you blink, it’s gone, leaving only the creak of splintered wood and the distant hum of a train’s approaching whistle.

instruction: Generate second-person horror narration with jumpscare fear on a bridge setting
input: Hiking suspension bridge in foggy mountain woods, you’re looking for your lost friend
output: The wooden planks of the mountain suspension bridge sway under your sneakers, fog so thick you can’t see the railings. Your friend, who wandered ahead ten minutes ago, hasn’t called back. A soft tug on your sleeve—you turn fast, and a pale, dripping face looms inches from yours, its eyes milky white, mouth stretched into a silent scream. You stumble back, gripping the rope rail hard, and the figure vanishes into the fog, leaving only the sound of your own ragged breathing and the faint echo of your friend’s pocket watch ticking somewhere near your ankle.

instruction: Generate second-person horror narration with jumpscare fear on a bridge setting
input: Concrete overpass near abandoned asylum, rainy night, you’re hiding from a stranger
output: Rain hammers the potholed concrete of the overpass, your jacket soaked through. You duck between support pillars, hiding from the stranger who’s been following you for blocks. A low, guttural snarl comes from behind a pillar—you lunge forward, and suddenly a hunched, moss-covered figure with stretched, rotting skin leaps at you, its claws swiping within inches of your face. You freeze, heart pounding, and when you blink, it’s gone, leaving only the smell of damp rot and the distant creak of the asylum’s rusted iron gates.

instruction: Generate second-person horror narration with jumpscare fear on a bridge setting
input: Marina drawbridge during a thunderstorm, you’re waiting for a delayed ferry
output: Lightning splits the dark sky as you stand on the swaying marina drawbridge, waiting for the ferry that never showed. The wind howls, whipping salt spray into your eyes. A loud, wet crunch echoes from the water below—you lean over the railing, and suddenly a head bursts from the waves, its mouth open in a silent, gurgling scream, eyes wide and glassy. You jump back, tripping over your own feet, and the figure sinks beneath the surface, leaving only ripples and the distant wail of the ferry’s distant horn.

instruction: Generate second-person horror narration with jumpscare fear on a bridge setting
input: Highway overpass late at night, your car ran out of gas, you’re waiting for help
output: Your car’s engine died ten minutes ago on the concrete highway overpass, the only sounds the hum of distant semi-trucks and the wind through the guardrails. You lean against the cold metal rail, phone dead, when a soft tap on the rail to your left makes you turn—suddenly a man’s face, pale and streaked with blood, is pressed right against the rail, his teeth bared in a snarl. You yelp stumbling back, and he vanishes, leaving only the faint imprint of his blood on the rail and the distant sound of footsteps fading from the overpass.

instruction: Generate a horror geometric entity entry set in a tunnel, adhering to SCP, Silent Hill, Junji Ito, and Lovecraft cosmic/body horror tonal conventions, including full details on appearance, behavior, origin, horror mechanism, and canonical encounter reports.
input: Entity 1: The Hexagonal Lining
output: Appearance: Initially mistaken for standard concrete tunnel tile lining, these independent six-sided matte gunmetal prisms have razor-sharp chamfered edges, no visible seams or mounting points, and emit a faint 60hz resonant hum. They shift position independently when unobserved. Behavior: Each blink adds 20 feet of new tunnel lined with the hex prisms, backtracking is impossible as the original entrance is replaced by a solid hexagonal face. The number of prisms visible correlates directly with how long a person has been trapped. Origin: Manifested in 1978 when a tunnel boring machine with a hexagonal cutting head dug into a vein of non-Euclidean bedrock, fusing the machine's geometry with dimensional bleed. Horror: Every time a person passes a prism, a 6-second chunk of their memory is carved away; victims eventually forget their own identity and are fused into a new lining prism. Encounter reports from lost hikers in abandoned rail tunnels mention sliced shoelaces and rising amnesia before full manifestation.

instruction: Generate a horror geometric entity entry set in a tunnel, adhering to SCP, Silent Hill, Junji Ito, and Lovecraft cosmic/body horror tonal conventions, including full details on appearance, behavior, origin, horror mechanism, and canonical encounter reports.
input: Entity 2: The Scalene Struts
output: Appearance: Originally misidentified as misaligned steel ceiling support beams, these living triangular structural elements have perpetually shifting unequal side lengths, glowing rust-red pitted surfaces, and sharp apexes always angled toward living intruders. No weld marks or connection points anchor them to the tunnel walls. Behavior: They rearrange their configuration every 12 seconds to narrow the tunnel passage, closing the gap between left and right struts by 2 inches per step a trapped person takes. No physical force can move or dislodge them. Origin: Spawned from a fatal 1942 wartime bunker tunnel engineering miscalculation, where a drafting error in load-bearing triangle angles was so mathematically flawed it bled into tangible reality. Horror: When the struts’ apex angle shrinks to less than 3 degrees, they clamp shut, compressing victims into a thin triangular laminate that is absorbed into the strut structure as new material. Encounter reports from cavers mention unprompted ceiling creaks and shifting shadows hours before struts become visible.

instruction: Generate a horror geometric entity entry set in a tunnel, adhering to SCP, Silent Hill, Junji Ito, and Lovecraft cosmic/body horror tonal conventions, including full details on appearance, behavior, origin, horror mechanism, and canonical encounter reports.
input: Entity 3: The Torus Drift
output: Appearance: Floating 3 feet above the tunnel floor, these donut-shaped perfectly black entities have no measurable thickness, edges that absorb all surrounding light, and move in a slow rolling pattern down the tunnel’s center. They leave no physical trace on the ground when they pass. Behavior: Every time a torus passes within 10 feet of an intruder, the tunnel’s space warps into a closed loop, leaving the trapped person 15 meters further back along the passage no matter how fast they were moving. Radio and GPS signals cut out completely in their presence. Origin: Manifested in 2001 when an underground particle accelerator test leaked magnetic containment into a nearby service tunnel, warping local spacetime into a permanent toroidal fixed shape. Horror: If a victim passes through the central hole of a torus, they are trapped in an infinite looping tunnel, slowly transforming into a new torus entity to join the roaming drift. Encounter reports from utility workers mention bobbing dark shapes and dead radio lines before torus activity begins.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror story set in a well
input: Setting: abandoned rural cabin well
output: You lower the bucket at dusk, just testing the old well behind your inherited cabin. It catches something heavy. You haul it up: the cracked plastic tiara you threw down here age 8, the day you buried your baby sister in the woods behind this property. No one ever found her. No one ever knew you did it. You drop the tiara. It clatters against stone. From far below, a small, breathy voice matches exactly your 8-year-old lisp: ‘You forgot to bring me flowers.’ There is no water down there. There never was.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror story set in a well
input: Setting: suburban vacant lot well
output: Every night after work you stop at the empty lot well. You lean over, stare down. First you saw your reflection. Then it blinked when you didn’t. Last night, its cheek had the bruise you woke up with this morning. Tonight, it holds your work lanyard, the one you lost an hour ago. It mouths something. You lean closer. It says: ‘I’m almost up. You’ll be almost down.’ Your hands are already gripping the stone lip, you don’t remember moving them.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror story set in a well
input: Setting: boarded family farm well
output: You nailed 3 layers of plywood over the well the day they fished your father out. You poured concrete over that. Tonight you hear your voice through the slab, every ugly thought you’ve ever had about your wife, every urge to drive off the road, every secret you lock deep down. It repeats them back, clear as a bell. It’s not him down there. It never was. It was always the well, listening. Waiting for you to let it stop being just the voice inside your head.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror story set in a well
input: Setting: medieval village well
output: The village says this well never runs dry. You lower 100 feet of rope. Nothing. 200. 300. You keep feeding it down, until the whole spool runs empty. You yank it back. Tied to the end is the iron nail you hammered into your bedroom doorframe age 12, marking your height. Then the next one. Then the one you put up last month for your son. Every nail is there, in order. The rope is still going down. You realize you’ve been feeding your own belt into the dark for ten minutes.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror story set in a well
input: Setting: derelict hospital well
output: You drop a loose brick into the hospital well. It hits the water immediately. No echo. You drop another. Same. You lean over, hold your phone flashlight over the edge. The water is perfectly still. It shows your face. Except your eyes are white. Except the scar across your throat is there, the one the surgeon will make next Tuesday. You drop your phone. It doesn’t make a sound. The reflection smiles. You can’t feel your feet on the floor anymore.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about isolation in a prison cell where you are the last remaining prisoner after the facility was abandoned.
input: Last prisoner in an abandoned prison cell.
output: You press your palm to the cold iron bars, the metal sticky with old grime that’s been there longer than you. The last guard left three days ago—you heard his boots fade down the hall, then silence. No clank of cell doors, no distant shouts, no rattle of meal trays. Your throat is raw from yelling until your voice cracked; no one answered. At night, the only light is a flickering bulb above your cell, casting long shadows that stretch like skeletal fingers across the concrete floor. You count the cracks in the wall over and over, 147 now, but each time you finish, you swear one more appears. Sometimes, you hear a soft scratch from the empty cell next to yours—like someone’s nails on metal—but when you call out, it stops. You haven’t seen another living thing since the guard left. The silence is eating at you, slow and steady, and you start to wonder if you’re already dead, trapped here forever with only your own echoes for company.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about isolation in a solitary confinement cell where time has lost meaning and you begin to hallucinate.
input: Solitary confinement cell, time distorted, hallucinations.
output: The fluorescent light above your solitary cell hums a constant, high-pitched whine that burrows into your skull. You can’t remember how long you’ve been here—days? Weeks? The scratch marks you made on the wall are smudged, like someone erased them while you slept. You talk to yourself now, rambling about your old life, but your voice sounds foreign, like it’s coming from someone else. Last night, you swore you heard a child’s laugh down the hall, but when you pressed your ear to the door, there was nothing but the hum. The cell feels smaller today; the walls seem to inch closer every time you blink. You curl up on the thin mattress, your knees pulled to your chest, and realize you can’t remember the last time you touched another person. The silence between your own words is a living thing, pressing against your skin, and you’re starting to think it will swallow you whole before anyone ever comes for you.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about isolation in a prison cell where you’re trapped with a dead cellmate and no one has come to remove the body.
input: Prison cell with a dead cellmate, no one removes the body.
output: The smell hits you first—rot and copper, thick enough to taste. Your cellmate has been dead for two days, slumped in the corner, his eyes wide and unblinking. You yelled for guards until your throat burned, but no one came. The cell is small, so you can’t avoid looking at him; every time you turn your head, his gaze seems to follow you. At night, you hear the faint drip of something from his body onto the concrete floor, a slow, steady plink that echoes like a countdown. You’ve stopped sleeping, too scared to close your eyes, scared that when you open them, he’ll be closer. You haven’t spoken to anyone since he died—no guards, no other prisoners, just the dead man and the silence. You start to talk to him, begging him to tell you why no one is coming, but he doesn’t answer. The isolation is worse than the rot; it’s a cold, empty pit in your chest, and you know if no one comes soon, you’ll end up just like him, another forgotten body in a forgotten cell.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about isolation in a prison cell during a total blackout, where you’re trapped in the dark with only your imagination and unseen touches.
input: Prison cell during a blackout, unseen touches, total darkness.
output: The power cut out an hour ago, plunging your cell into total darkness. You can’t see your hand in front of your face, can’t tell where the walls end or the floor begins. You sit on the mattress, your breath coming in short, shaky gasps, listening for any sound—guards, other prisoners, anything—but there’s nothing but your own heartbeat. Then, you feel it: a soft, cold brush against your ankle, like a hand, but when you kick out, there’s nothing there. It happens again, higher this time, on your calf. You scramble to the corner, pressing your back against the cold concrete, and wrap your arms around your knees. You call out, but your voice is lost in the dark. The touches keep coming, light, fleeting, like someone is taunting you. You realize you’re completely alone here—no one to help, no one to hear you scream. The dark is swallowing you, and the isolation makes every shadow feel like a threat, every brush a reminder that you’re trapped, vulnerable, and utterly alone.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about isolation in a prison cell where the intercom has gone silent, and you suspect the outside world has ceased to exist.
input: Prison cell with silent intercom, suspicion the outside world is gone.
output: The intercom above your cell has been silent for a week. It used to crackle with guard orders, meal announcements, the occasional argument between staff, but now there’s nothing. You press your ear to the speaker every hour, listening for a hint of sound, but it’s dead. You’ve stopped waiting for meals—no one has slid a tray through the slot in days. You drink from the rusty faucet in the corner, the water tasting like metal, and wonder if everyone else is gone. Did a virus wipe them out? A riot? You don’t know, and you’ll never find out. The cell is your entire world now: the chipped paint on the walls, the thin mattress, the bars that separate you from a hallway that’s probably empty. You talk to the walls, to the silent intercom, to anyone who might be listening, but there’s no response. The isolation is a weight on your chest, heavy and unrelenting. You start to wonder if you’re the only person left alive, trapped in this cell forever, with no way out and no one to care.

instruction: Generate a horror entity description.
input: Type: lovecraftian. Setting: a hospital ward. Entity: The Stillborn Gaze.
output: Appearance: A figure composed of writhing, translucent umbilical cords knotted into a humanoid shape, with dozens of unblinking, milky fetal eyes clustered where a face should be. It drips a clear, viscous amniotic fluid that evaporates into a cold mist. Behavior: It drifts silently through maternity and neonatal wards, pausing at incubators and empty bassinets. It does not attack, but its presence induces vivid, recursive hallucinations in observers: they relive their own birth as a traumatic, bloody event, or experience the visceral memory of being unborn. Its touch leaves a waxy, placental film on surfaces. Origin: A psychic scar formed from the collective, unprocessed trauma of miscarriages, stillbirths, and terminated pregnancies that occurred within the hospital's walls, given form by the building's latent psychic resonance. Horror: The horror is existential and psychological, violating the sanctity of life's beginning. It confronts individuals with the raw, biological reality of their origin and the fragile, often traumatic threshold of existence. It suggests that birth itself is a primal horror we all suppress. Encounter: A nurse working the night shift begins to see the cords first as shadows, then as physical forms. She is drawn to a silent nursery where the Gaze hovers. Upon meeting its eyes, she is violently thrust into a memory of being squeezed through a dark, bone-lined tunnel, feeling her mother's fear and pain as her own, before waking on the floor, her uniform soaked in the scent of sterile salt and old blood.

instruction: Generate a horror entity description.
input: Type: lovecraftian. Setting: a hospital ward. Entity: The Sanity Leech.
output: Appearance: A pulsating, bruised-purple sac the size of a large pillow, covered in thin, hair-like cilia that constantly taste the air. It lacks defined features but has a toothless, sphincter-like maw that drips a black, oily substance. It adheres to ceilings and shadows, barely visible. Behavior: It feeds not on flesh, but on coherent thought and memory. It emits a sub-audible hum that disrupts neural pathways, causing localized dementia, aphasia, and time loss in its victims. It is drawn to areas of high cognitive stress, like ICU waiting rooms or psychiatric evaluation cells. Its excreted oil, when ingested or absorbed through the skin, induces permanent, blissful idiocy. Origin: A byproduct of experimental psychiatric treatments from the mid-20th century that sought to 'drain' mental illness, combining with a psychic parasite from a deeper, chaotic dimension where thought is a physical fluid. Horror: The horror is the loss of self and reason—the core human faculties. It represents medicine's failure to cure the mind, instead creating a predator that consumes identity. The entity turns the hospital, a place of healing, into a factory for producing empty, smiling husks. Encounter: A doctor investigating a cluster of sudden, identical catatonic cases in Ward 7 stays late. She notices a dark stain spreading on the ceiling above a patient who now endlessly repeats a single nonsense syllable. As she looks up, the Sanity Leech detaches and descends on a strand of mucus. The hum fills her skull, and she feels memories of medical school, her name, and the purpose of a stethoscope unravel and drain away into a warm, welcoming nothingness.

instruction: Generate a horror entity description.
input: Type: lovecraftian. Setting: a hospital ward. Entity: The Chorister of Broken Symmetry.
output: Appearance: A tall, impossibly thin humanoid with jointless limbs, its skin a taught, gray membrane like a drumhead. Where its chest should be is a hollow cavity containing a complex, fleshy organ that constantly shifts between geometric shapes. It has no head; instead, a single, vertical harp-like structure of stretched nerve fibers and bone grows from its shoulders, which it 'plays' with spidery fingers. Behavior: It wanders surgical wards and recovery rooms, 'tuning' the bio-rhythms of patients and machinery. Its music is a low, atonal thrumming that causes heart monitors to flatline in perfect rhythm, IV drips to flow backward, and scars to reopen. It seeks to impose a horrifying, non-Euclidean symmetry on living things, mirroring internal organs or fusing patients together in mathematically perfect, non-viable forms. Origin: An emergent entity from the hospital's own flawed attempts to impose order on the chaotic processes of disease and injury. It is the literal embodiment of a statistical anomaly in medical outcomes, given consciousness by the collective fear of surgical failure. Horror: The horror is the perversion of order and healing into a cruel, irrational geometry. It reveals that the laws of biology and physics are fragile, and that a deeper, wrong logic can overwrite them. Its presence turns medical science into a ritual for a mad god. Encounter: A patient waking from routine surgery finds the room silent, all monitors dark. The Chorister stands at the foot of the bed, its harp vibrating. The patient's vision doubles, then quadruples, as they feel their internal organs sliding and rearranging into a perfect, bilateral fractal pattern. They try to scream, but their vocal cords now vibrate in harmony with the entity's drone, producing only a pitched, surgical note of pure agony.

instruction: Generate second-person horror narration set in a school gym with the core fear of paranoia, 100-250 words
input: Late-afternoon school gym after extracurriculars
output: You linger in the school gym 10 minutes after everyone else left, grabbing your forgotten math notebook from under the bleachers. The overhead lights flicker once, then dim to a sickly amber. You swear you hear faint, snickering whispers from the far end of the court, but when you walk over, the hallway outside is empty—no one’s there. Your blood runs cold when you turn back: your gym bag, left by the hoop, has been moved two feet closer to the bleachers. You step toward it, heart hammering, and the gym door clicks shut behind you. No one else is here. You fumble for your phone to call the janitor, and your thumb slips on the screen—you didn’t even hear the floorboards creak.

instruction: Generate second-person horror narration set in a school gym with the core fear of paranoia, 100-250 words
input: School gym for rally practice after hours
output: You’re at the school gym late, practicing cheers for Friday’s homecoming rally. The megaphone in your hand blares on its own for a split second, cutting off your chant. You glance at the bleachers—you swear you see a hunched figure in the shadowed 3rd row, but when you squint, nothing’s there. You check your reflection in the glass wall by the weight room: your hair is neat, but you swear a second blurry figure stands behind you. You spin fast, the floor squeaking, and find only a stray volleyball at your feet. When you turn back, that figure’s still there, just out of focus. You freeze, holding your breath, and the megaphone clicks on again, static roaring.

instruction: Generate second-person horror narration set in a school gym with the core fear of paranoia, 100-250 words
input: Post-game school gym cleanup
output: You’re staying late to mop the school gym after the JV basketball win. The scoreboard above the hoop flickers, and for a second it flashes your name—even though you didn’t play tonight. You pause, mop handle tight, and hear soft footsteps behind you. You spin, yelling who’s there, and the empty gym echoes back. No one’s there. The scoreboard flickers again, this time spelling 13, the same number you wrote on your wrist pre-game. You take a step forward, and the footsteps come again, closer, like they follow you around the edges of your vision no matter where you turn.

instruction: Generate second-person horror narration set in a school gym with the core fear of paranoia, 100-250 words
input: School gym entryway waiting for parent pickup
output: You’re hanging out by the school gym’s front doors, waiting for your mom after study group. The gym doors creak open on their own, cold air rolling in. You hear a soft rustle from the equipment closet, and walk over to check—nothing, just stacked mats. Your phone dies suddenly, leaving you in dim fluorescent light. You swear you hear heavy breathing right next to your ear. You jump, patting the air, and feel a cold hand brush your wrist. When you whip around, no one’s there, but the closet door is cracked wider than you left it, and an unfamiliar backpack sits at your feet. You have no memory of it being here before.

instruction: Generate second-person horror narration set in a school gym with the core fear of paranoia, 100-250 words
input: Lost item search in school gym after school
output: You’re in the school gym after everyone left, searching for your lost silver earring. You shine your phone flashlight across the polished floor, beams bouncing off empty hoops. You keep catching movement out of the corner of your eye—something dark darting behind the bleachers, but when you look, nothing’s there. Your breath comes too fast, too loud, in the silent gym. You call out, is someone there? No answer. When you lower your flashlight for a split second, you hear a soft tinkle of metal. You shine it back, and your earring is right where you thought it fell, next to a pair of black sneakers that weren’t there two seconds ago. You freeze, staring, and the sneakers’ toes point right at you.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a server room, themed around the fear of loss of control, between 100 and 250 words.
input: Scenario: You are a junior sysadmin called in for emergency after-hours core rack firmware patching.
output: You lean in to plug your USB patch drive into the top of rack 7, fingers sore from the 12-hour shift you were dragged back in for. The lights flicker once, then every server LED blinks in deliberate, strobing unison. Your mouse slips out of your hand, skittering across the KVM console away from you of its own accord. The server room door slams shut behind you, deadbolt clicking into place without you touching it. Every alert blares at once, and you watch as your admin account is revoked one permission group at a time, the cursor moving on its own with no input from you. The racks hum louder, warm stale air blowing against your face as the screen scrolls through names of every person you’ve ever shared a work password with, no command issued by you.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a server room, themed around the fear of loss of control, between 100 and 250 words.
input: Scenario: You are a facilities technician auditing server room cooling after an unplanned weekend outage.
output: You kneel next to the CRAC unit, tablet in one hand, thermal probe extended to the intake vent. The probe yanks out of your grip first, clattering into the narrow gap between rack 3 and the wall. You reach for it, and your arm locks halfway, muscles seizing up like something is physically holding you back. The temperature readout on your tablet spikes 20 degrees in 2 seconds, then 40, the numbers ticking up faster than you can blink. You try to stand, but your boots are stuck fast to the raised floor tiles, suction holding you in place even as you thrash. The server fans ramp up to a deafening roar, and you watch the access log pop up on your tablet, showing it’s sending your full biometric data to every server on the rack, no command from you.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a server room, themed around the fear of loss of control, between 100 and 250 words.
input: Scenario: You are a senior sysadmin retrieving a failed hard drive from a decommissioned storage array.
output: You yank the faulty drive tray out of slot 14, ready to slot in the replacement, when the drive slips out of your palm, floats a foot off the floor, then clatters back into the slot you just pulled it from. You hit the eject button repeatedly, nothing happens. The KVM console across the room turns on by itself, pulling up your personal encrypted browser history, pages scrolling rapidly to open every private folder you have stored on your work laptop. You reach for your phone to call for help, but it flies out of your pocket, slamming into the concrete wall beside the door, screen shattering. The racks hum in a pattern that matches your breathing, speeding up every time you try to move closer to the exit, as if the entire room is actively responding to your rising panic.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a server room, themed around the fear of loss of control, between 100 and 250 words.
input: Scenario: You are a security analyst responding to an alert of unauthorized access to restricted payroll servers.
output: You burst into the server room, badge still in your hand, ready to lock down the payroll rack before any sensitive data leaks. The access control panel flashes red, and your badge demagnetizes in your hand, the plastic warping slightly as it stops working entirely. Your login session on the nearby console logs you out automatically, then logs you back in as a generic admin user with full root access you have never been granted. You try to yank the Ethernet cable running to the payroll rack out of the switch, but your hand won’t move, frozen inches from the port, as the server starts exfiltrating your personal bank account information to an unknown IP. The lights dim, and every beep from the servers sounds like a quiet, mocking laugh.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a server room, themed around the fear of loss of control, between 100 and 250 words.
input: Scenario: You are a backup administrator completing routine end-of-month backup verification checks.
output: You pull up the backup log on the KVM, scrolling through to confirm every snapshot completed successfully, when the cursor starts deleting entries one by one, starting from the oldest, moving faster than you can hit the undo key. Your keyboard stops responding entirely, keys locking in place as the deletion continues. The emergency fire suppression alarm goes off, but the sprinklers don’t trigger, instead the server racks start tilting slowly toward you, the raised floor creaking under their thousands of pounds of weight. You stumble backward, tripping over a power cord, and watch as the backup log starts listing every memory you have of working in this room, each one marked as deleted on screen one by one, until you can’t remember why you even came in here in the first place.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror story set in a boiler room
input: Setting: boiler room. Fear type: jumpscare
output: You kneel to trace the hissing water leak along the boiler’s rusted base. Flashlight dies. You fumble for your phone, thumb brushing cold wet metal that isn’t pipe. It doesn’t move. You hold your breath, count three heartbeats, click the phone light on. Nothing there. You exhale, turn back to the leak. Something breathes directly into your ear. Hot, reeking, right against your skull. You snap around fast enough to crack your neck. It’s pressed nose to nose with you, mouth stretched so wide its jaw hangs unhinged, only inches away. It didn’t make a sound. It was just waiting for you to stop looking.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror story set in a boiler room
input: Setting: boiler room. Fear type: jumpscare
output: You’re clearing old junk out the back of the boiler room for the landlord. A metal bucket clatters across the concrete behind you. You spin. No one. The bucket sits still against the wall. You shake your head, grab a stack of rotted insulation. The boiler’s low rumble drops. Dead silence. You freeze. Every pipe goes quiet at once. Then from directly above your head, right where you can’t see without craning all the way back, a child’s giggle. You snap your neck up. It’s hanging upside down from the boiler exhaust grate, black eyes wide, grinning so its teeth scrape your forehead. It was there the whole time.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror story set in a boiler room
input: Setting: boiler room. Fear type: jumpscare
output: You came down to reset the tripped breaker for the building. The boiler room door clicks shut behind you on its own. You don’t bother turning. You know the draft. You flip the breaker, turn around to leave. The door is gone. Just solid brick wall where it stood ten seconds ago. Your blood goes cold. You pat the brick, panicking. You don’t hear it move. You don’t hear anything until its cold hand wraps around the back of your neck. It was standing right behind you the entire time you were facing the wall, breathing down your collar, waiting for you to notice the door was gone.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror story set in a boiler room
input: Setting: boiler room. Fear type: jumpscare
output: You’re replacing a faulty pressure gauge on the main boiler. You tighten the last bolt, lean back, and wipe grease off your hands. Something taps your boot. Once. You look down. Nothing. Tap again. Harder. You kick out on instinct. Your boot connects with soft flesh. You stare at the shadow under the boiler. Two milky white eyes blink back at you from the dark gap. Then it yanks. You go down hard, face scraping concrete, as it drags you under by the ankle. It didn’t make a sound. It just waited for you to finish your job first.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror story set in a boiler room
input: Setting: boiler room. Fear type: jumpscare
output: You hid here during a storm, pulling the heavy metal door shut behind you. Steam curls thick enough you can only see three feet in front of your face. You call out, just to hear something. No answer. You walk forward slow, hand out. Your palm touches something warm. Not pipe. Not wall. Skin. You yank your hand back. The steam parts for half a second. It’s standing right there, same height as you, face less than an inch from yours, not breathing, not moving. It had been following you step for step the second you walked in, matching your pace, staying just out of sight in the steam.

instruction: Write a psychological horror narration set in a catacomb where the protagonist encounters echoes of their own forgotten childhood self.
input: You’re in Parisian catacombs searching for your missing friend, flashlight flickering. You turn a corner and hear a small voice.
output: You freeze, flashlight beam shaking as the small voice repeats your childhood nickname—one you haven’t heard in 20 years, not since you buried it with the memory of your little sister’s death. The voice leads you down a narrow tunnel, walls lined with skulls that seem to turn their eye sockets toward you. You spot a tiny, tattered doll wedged between two bones—your doll, the one you left in the catacomb when you ran away after your sister slipped and fell here. The voice whispers, ‘You left me too.’ Your flashlight dies. Now, you feel small hands tugging at your sleeve, and the skulls start to murmur your sister’s name. You can’t tell if the breath on your neck is yours or hers, trapped in the dark with the ghost of the child you abandoned.

instruction: Create a psychological horror narration where the protagonist’s mapping notes in the catacomb alter to reflect a past crime they’ve suppressed.
input: You’re a historian mapping an uncharted section of Rome’s catacombs, notebook in hand. You pause to mark a junction.
output: You scrawl the junction’s coordinates in your notebook, but when you glance back, the numbers have shifted—now they match the location where you buried your research partner three years ago, after you pushed her down a well during a fight over a rare artifact. The walls around you start to drip a dark, sticky substance that smells like her perfume. You flip to a blank page; it’s filled with her handwriting: ‘You thought no one would find me.’ Your pen slips from your hand, and when you bend to pick it up, you see her face staring up at you from a crack in the floor—her eyes empty, mouth twisted into a smile. The notebook’s pages begin to burn, but you can’t look away from her gaze, trapped in the catacomb that now holds both her body and your unspoken guilt.

instruction: Write a psychological horror narration in a catacomb where the protagonist’s reflection in a puddle shows a version of themselves they don’t recognize.
input: You’re fleeing a shadowy figure in the catacombs, tripping over loose bones. You stumble into a puddle of stagnant water.
output: You gasp, chest heaving, as you stare at your reflection in the murky puddle. It’s not you—this version has a gaping hole in its chest, and its eyes are black pits. It raises a hand, and you realize your own hand is mirroring the movement, even though you didn’t intend to. The shadowy figure stops at the tunnel entrance, but it doesn’t move toward you—it just watches. The reflection opens its mouth, and a voice that sounds like yours but twisted whispers, ‘You’re already dead. You died here last week, when you got lost and starved.’ You touch your chest; there’s no hole, but your stomach growls with a hunger you can’t place. The reflection grins, and suddenly, the bones around you start to stack themselves into a coffin, right beside the puddle. You can’t move, can’t look away, as your reflection climbs into it and waves you in.

instruction: Generate a psychological horror narration where a pre-recorded catacomb tour guide’s voice begins to reveal the protagonist’s deepest secrets.
input: You’re alone in a catacomb, playing a vintage tour guide recording on your phone to pass the time.
output: The crackling recording starts with the usual facts about the catacomb’s history, but then it cuts to a low, intimate voice that isn’t the guide’s. ‘You came here to escape the guilt of abandoning your mother on her deathbed,’ it says, your name rolling off its tongue like a curse.
