You beg it to stop, but the voice repeats every detail of that night, every shaky lie you told, and you realize you can’t run from this truth—this booth is holding it all, every mistake you’ve ever made, and there’s no way out.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a morgue focused on the loss_of_control fear archetype.
input: You are a solo night shift morgue attendant wrapping a recent overdose victim for transport.
output: You reach for the body bag zipper when your fingers seize, curling tight around the edge of the steel table before you can pull back. You try to call for help, but your throat locks shut, no sound coming out as the corpse’s hand lifts to rest on top of yours. You feel your arm move without your permission, pulling the scalpel from your waist holster, and you watch in frozen dread as you press the sharp edge into your own forearm, slow, deliberate, no part of you able to intervene. The corpse’s mouth stretches into a grin beneath its sheet as you keep cutting, your brain screaming stop while your body follows every silent, unbidden command.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a morgue focused on the loss_of_control fear archetype.
input: You arrived 10 minutes prior to identify your deceased older sibling for the coroner.
output: You lean in to confirm the tattoo on their wrist when your head snaps back of its own accord, your neck cracking loud in the cold quiet. You can’t look away as your hands reach up to undo the clasps holding the body bag closed, even as you beg yourself to stop. You watch, helpless, as your fingers curl around your sibling’s cold, stiff hand, bringing it up to rest against your own cheek. You can feel your mouth moving, whispering apologies you never meant to say, your face streaking with tears you don’t remember crying, every part of your autonomy stripped away by some unseen force lingering in the exam room.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a morgue focused on the loss_of_control fear archetype.
input: The morgue just suffered a total power outage mid-routine inventory check.
output: You fumble for your flashlight when your feet lift off the tile, hovering a foot above the ground without you making any movement. You kick, trying to right yourself, but your legs won’t respond, hanging limp as you drift slowly toward the bank of body drawers. You hear a low gurgle in the dark, and you feel your own mouth open wide, your jaw stretching until it aches, no sound coming out even as you continue to force it wider. A drawer slides open, and you drift toward it of your own volition, knowing you have no way to stop yourself from being pulled inside with the corpse stored there.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a morgue focused on the loss_of_control fear archetype.
input: You are a trainee mortician running your first solo autopsy on an unidentified Jane Doe.
output: You line up your scalpel to make the first Y-incision when your hand twists, driving the blade straight into your own palm before you can react. The pain is white-hot, but you can’t drop the scalpel, your fingers clamped around it so tight your knuckles blanch. You watch in unmitigated terror as your other hand reaches up to grip your jaw, prying your mouth open wide enough to cramp, and you feel yourself lean forward over the corpse of your own accord. You can taste the formaldehyde fumes getting stronger as you lower your face toward the dead woman’s open chest, every instinct screaming to run while your body refuses to obey.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a morgue focused on the loss_of_control fear archetype.
input: You are the lead morgue attendant logging new arrival tags at the front desk after hours.
output: You type a tag for a new drowning victim when your keyboard goes dark, and your head turns on its own to stare at the swinging exam room door. You try to stand, but your chair rolls backward of its own accord, slamming into the bank of body lockers hard enough to rattle the handles. You feel your throat tighten, and you start to confess to a patient you accidentally misidentified last month, words spilling out of your mouth unbidden, even as you try to bite your tongue to stop. One of the body drawers clicks open, and you know whatever is inside is forcing you to admit every mistake you’ve ever made, with no way to silence yourself.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror story set in a greenhouse
input: Setting: greenhouse. Fear: jumpscare
output: You kneel to pull dead tomato vines, breath fogging the cold greenhouse glass. Water drips steady from the broken gutter above. Your brush catches something soft, not vine. You lift it: a child’s mitten, caked in moss. You freeze. You’d locked the door. You scan the rows—nothing moves, only swaying fern fronds. You stand, turn to leave. A face is pressed directly to the glass six inches from yours, eyes black, mouth stretched open. It doesn’t breathe. It doesn’t blink. It was there the whole time, watching you kneel.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror story set in a greenhouse
input: Setting: greenhouse. Fear: jumpscare
output: You’re counting orchid blooms at dusk, headlamp cutting narrow beams through mist. Every so often you hear rustle, but it’s just wind. You reach for a specimen tag. Your hand closes over something warm and living. You yank back. There’s nothing on the shelf. You laugh shakily, wipe your palm on your jeans, and tilt your head up. Hanging upside down between the hanging baskets, one inch from your forehead, is a woman grinning so wide her lips split at the corners. She drops as you scream.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror story set in a greenhouse
input: Setting: greenhouse. Fear: jumpscare
output: You came to find your missing cat. The greenhouse reeks of rot. You call her name, flashlight bouncing off terracotta pots. Behind the giant pothos, you spot her curled tail. You step forward. It’s not her. It’s a pile of discarded garden gloves arranged to look like her. Before you can step back, something slams both palms hard against your shoulder blades from behind. You lurch forward. When you spin, the greenhouse is empty. The door is still locked from the inside.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror story set in a greenhouse
input: Setting: greenhouse. Fear: jumpscare
output: Thunder booms, killing the greenhouse lights. You fumble for your phone. Rain hammers the polycarbonate roof so loud you can’t hear anything else. You get the flashlight on, sweep it slowly across the rows. Every single potted plant has been turned to face you. Every one. You gasp, and the light jolts. Right at the far end, standing perfectly still between the lemon trees, is a tall figure that wasn’t there one second ago. It lunges before you can scream.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror story set in a greenhouse
input: Setting: greenhouse. Fear: jumpscare
output: You’re pruning roses, gloves sticky with sap. You keep catching movement out the corner of your eye, but it’s just shadow through the leaves. You snip a thorny stem. It doesn’t bleed sap. It bleeds dark, warm blood. You drop the shears. You look up. The rose bush you’ve been cutting for ten minutes has a human hand sticking out of the soil, fingers curled. The hand grabs your wrist hard before you can run.

instruction: Write a psychological horror narration set in an elevator where the mirrors show a slowly changing version of yourself.
input: Elevator stuck between the 12th and 13th floors, lined with full-length mirrors.
output: You jab the emergency button for the third time, static hissing back. Glancing at the mirror, you spot a purple bruise blooming on your left jaw—you didn’t have that five minutes ago. You rub at it, but the reflection doesn’t move. It smiles, a sharp, unrecognizable curve, and its right index finger is gone, the stump clean. Your heart thuds; you check your own hand, intact. The elevator lurches, dinging like it’s arrived, but the doors stay shut. The reflection leans in, breath fogging the mirror, and its mouth moves: “You’ve been lying to yourself about the accident.” You step back, tripping over your own feet, as the reflection’s hand presses through the glass, cold fingers wrapping around your wrist.

instruction: Narrate a psychological horror scene in an elevator with a stranger who reveals your unspoken guilt.
input: Late-night elevator ride; a quiet stranger joins you on the 7th floor, heading to the basement.
output: You stare at your shoes, avoiding eye contact with the stranger in the corner. They clear their throat, and their voice is low: “You left the window open that night. The cat got out, and it never came back.” Your blood runs cold—no one knew that. They lean forward, eyes glinting: “You cried in the shower for an hour, then told your mom it ran away.” You press the basement button harder, but the elevator stalls between floors. “You thought no one saw,” they say, reaching into their coat pocket. You flinch, but they pull out a crumpled collar—your cat’s collar, the one you buried in the backyard. The elevator lights flicker, and their face shifts to match yours.

instruction: Create a psychological horror story in an elevator where the floor count expands and your memory fades.
input: You enter an office building elevator to go to the 5th floor; the panel suddenly adds floors 10 through 47.
output: You press the 5th floor button, but it flickers out, and 17 lights up instead. You try to recall why you’re here—no name, no appointment, just a vague sense of urgency. The elevator climbs, floors ticking past 25, 30, 40. The walls hum a familiar tune, but you can’t place it. Glancing at the mirror, you’re wearing a crisp suit you don’t own, a name tag reading “Mr. Hale” pinned to the lapel. The intercom crackles: “Welcome back to floor 47, Mr. Hale. The board is waiting.” The doors open to an empty office with your name on the desk. You step out, and the elevator doors slam shut behind you. You reach for the handle, but it’s gone—you can’t remember how to leave.

instruction: Write a psychological horror narration in an elevator that keeps opening to the same altered hallway.
input: You’re leaving your 3rd-floor apartment; the elevator keeps opening to the same hallway, but small details shift each time.
output: You press the lobby button, but the doors slide open to your 3rd-floor hallway. The rug’s frayed at the edge where it was perfect this morning, and your neighbor’s door is ajar—they never leave it open. You call their name, no answer. The elevator doors close, and you press the button again. This time, the hallway is darker; your neighbor’s door is closed, but there’s a smudged handprint on the glass. You step back, heart racing, and press all the buttons. The elevator dings, doors open again—same hallway, but the clock above the door reads 3:14, frozen. You hear a soft meow from inside your neighbor’s apartment, but you know their cat died last month. The elevator doors close, and the panel now only shows the 3rd floor as an option.

instruction: Narrate a psychological horror scene in an elevator where you hear a future version of yourself begging you to stay inside.
input: You’re heading to the 10th floor for a job interview; a disembodied voice sounds from the elevator speaker.
output: The elevator dings at the 10th floor, and you reach for the door handle. A voice identical to yours crackles over the speaker: “Don’t get out. They’re not hiring—they’re waiting.” You laugh it off as a prank, but the voice grows desperate: “I tried to tell myself this too. They’ll lock you in the supply closet, make you watch the tapes.” You step out anyway, the door closing behind you. The hallway is empty, but you hear your own scream echoing from down the hall. You turn to run back to the elevator, but the panel shows the elevator is still on the 10th floor—inside, you see yourself staring back, mouth open in a silent scream. A hand clamps down on your shoulder, and you hear the same voice whisper in your ear: “Too late.”

instruction: Write a horror narration in second-person.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: a sewer system.
output: The grate slams shut above you, swallowing the last sliver of streetlight. Absolute blackness presses in, thick and cold. Your flashlight flickers, dies. You stand frozen, the only sound the drip of distant water and the frantic thud of your heart. Then, a new sound: a wet, sliding shuffle from the tunnel ahead. It’s not water. Something is moving in the dark, something that doesn’t need light. You feel a warm, damp breath against your ankle. It smells of rot and stagnant earth. You don’t dare breathe. The thing exhales, a low, guttural sigh that echoes off the slime-covered walls. It’s right there. In the perfect dark, you are utterly seen.

instruction: Write a horror narration in second-person.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: a sewer system.
output: Your boat drifts into a service chamber, the weak beam of your headlamp cutting through the gloom. The light sputters, then winks out. The darkness that follows isn’t empty. You hear clicking, like chitinous legs on brick, skittering across the ceiling. You paddle blindly, the oar scraping against something soft and yielding. A choked sob escapes you. Immediately, the clicking stops. Silence, deeper than the dark. Then, from directly beside your ear, a voice, wet and whispering, ‘Shhh… we can hear you in the dark.’ The water around your boat begins to churn.

instruction: Write a horror narration in second-person.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: a sewer system.
output: The maintenance tunnel narrows, forcing you to crawl. Your headlamp battery finally gives out, plunging you into a blackness so complete it feels solid. You inch forward, hands sinking into cold, unidentifiable muck. Then, your fingers brush against something smooth and curved—like a tooth, but far too large. You recoil, but your shoulder touches a wall that isn’t brick. It’s warm and pulsates faintly. The darkness around you isn’t an absence of light; it’s a living membrane. You realize you’re not in a tunnel at all. You’re inside something. And it just swallowed.

instruction: Write a horror narration in second-person.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: a sewer system.
output: You’re lost. Your phone died an hour ago. The only light now is the faint, greenish glow of strange fungus on the walls. It illuminates just enough to see the outlines of the colossal, circular tunnel. You follow it, hoping for a ladder. The fungal light begins to dim, patch by patch, as if being consumed. The darkness advances behind you, a rolling wave of nothing. You run, but the light dies faster ahead, sealing you in a shrinking pocket of gloom. The last patch winks out at your feet. In the final second of light, you see them: pale, elongated figures standing silently in a circle where the dark now reigns.

instruction: Write a horror narration in second-person.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: a sewer system.
output: The floodwaters forced you into this overflow pipe. It’s pitch black, the air stale and heavy. You feel your way along, each step a gamble. Suddenly, the ground gives way. You fall into a deeper, colder channel. The impact knocks the wind from you. As you lie there, stunned, you feel the water around you stirring. Something sinuous and immense brushes past your leg. You scramble back against a wall. Then you hear it: a deep, resonant hum that vibrates in your bones, coming from the abyss ahead. It’s a sound that doesn’t belong in this world. The hum grows louder. The dark water begins to rise.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration focused on isolation set in a lighthouse.
input: Stranded keeper, dead radio, old keep logs, empty ocean.
output: You clutch the cracked radio, its static cutting off mid-coast guard rescue update—they said the storm would pass in six hours, that was three days ago. The lighthouse’s beam swings slow over empty waves; no ships, no gulls, just the creak of rusted cast iron and your own shallow breathing. You flip through the last keeper’s moldy log: Page 17: ‘Day 12, still no one. The light still works.’ Page 22: ‘Day 28, I think I hear someone. No one’s here.’ Your hand freezes on page 41, a scrawled note here says ‘They don’t come.’ The beam hits the fogged window, and for a split second, you spot a pale figure in the empty keeper’s quarters. You yank the log shut. Rain streaks the glass. The radio stays dead.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration focused on isolation set in a lighthouse.
input: Repairing the light lens, no one around, sound doesn’t carry.
output: You’re on the catwalk, tightening bolts on the lighthouse’s rotating lens, when you notice: your shouts to test the mic never echo back. Below, the waves crash, but no sound reaches your ears—just the low hum of the light turning. You’ve been here a week, no signs of the relief crew. When you climb down, the keep’s door is locked, even though you left it ajar. You press your palm to the cold wood; the only other mark is a faint smudge, like a child’s small handprint, high on the frame. You call out again, loud, clear. Nothing. The light’s hum is getting louder. You’re alone.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration focused on isolation set in a lighthouse.
input: Running low on supplies, talking to the lighthouse, persistent silence.
output: Your last can of beans is empty, and the rain has stopped for three days, so your only water is stale rain caught in buckets. You start talking to the lighthouse just to fill the quiet: ‘How many days till someone comes?’ You mention your sister, the trip you planned before this assignment. The light keeps spinning, unblinking. Last night, you swore you heard a voice answer: ‘No one comes here.’ When you turned, the only thing there was the beam’s glow on the wall. Now, you sit at the table, no food, no signal, no way out. The light keeps turning. You’re not just stranded—you’re forgotten.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration focused on isolation set in a lighthouse.
input: Fog rolls in, can’t see beyond the beam, no contact.
output: The fog rolls in at dusk, thick enough to swallow the lighthouse’s lower reaches. You lean against the rail, scanning the white wall of mist, but there’s nothing—no siren, no distant ship horn, not even a gull’s cry. Your phone died an hour ago; the only power left runs the lighthouse beam. You call the coast guard frequency again, your voice bouncing back distorted, swallowed by the fog. The beam swings, and for a split second, you think you see a shape moving in the mist, gone before you blink. You’re stuck in this bubble of light and silence, cut off from every living thing. No one knows where you are. No one will look.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration focused on isolation set in a lighthouse.
input: Found old toys, past keepers’ secrets, no escape.
output: While cleaning the lighthouse attic, you find a chipped wooden doll, its painted face faded, tucked behind old maps. Next to it is a tattered child’s notebook, pages filled with drawings: a lighthouse, a person alone, a cross through the moon. You remember the last log entry: ‘I brought my niece here. She doesn’t want to leave.’ The attic door slams shut behind you. You twist the handle, it won’t budge. You shout, bang on the wood, no response. The lighthouse beam cuts through the floor, and you spot a small pale handprint on the planks—just like the one on the kitchen door. You’re not just alone here. You’re trapped with someone left behind long before you.

instruction: Classify and document a newly encountered geometric anomalous entity recovered from deep submarine mission logs
input: Entity ID GEO-SUB-001, retrieved from black box of lost Royal Navy submarine HMS Valiant, depth 8200m
output: Appearance: GEO-SUB-001 is a contiguous sheet of interlocking, perfectly uniform rhomboid facets that spontaneously replace sections of submarine hull and bulkhead, with no visible seams, iridescent like pressure-forged obsidian. Behavior: It crawls across interior surfaces at 2cm per minute, automatically sealing hatches and access panels when crew attempt to traverse between sections. Origin: It formed from the cumulative spacetime warping of 1400+ extreme deep dives, where ambient pressure warps local geometric rules. Horror: Each facet displays the final 10 seconds of a crew member crushed by historic hull failure, looping endlessly. Encounter: Three engineers attempted to chisel the entity free during the Valiant’s final mission; their fully flattened, two-dimensional bodies were integrated as new facets by the next crew shift. No remains were recoverable.

instruction: Classify and document a newly encountered geometric anomalous entity recovered from deep submarine mission logs
input: Entity ID GEO-SUB-002, observed during USS Odyssey abyssal survey mission, depth 9100m
output: Appearance: GEO-SUB-002 are weightless, 3cm wide perfect tetrahedra with no measurable mass, that drift freely through solid submarine bulkheads, wiring, and equipment as if physical matter does not exist for them. Behavior: They cluster within 1m of living crew, emitting a subsonic hum that matches the submarine’s hull resonant frequency, increasing in volume the deeper the sub dives. Origin: They spontaneously manifest below 7700m, where the fundamental Euclidean rules of the observable universe break down in the abyssal zone. Horror: If a tetrahedron makes contact with a living crew member, their soft internal organs immediately reconfigure into identical tetrahedral shapes, while the crew remains fully conscious for the entire process. Encounter: A sonar technician reported 19 tetrahedra floating in the control room 90 seconds before all his vitals flatlined, with no external signs of injury on his body.

instruction: Classify and document a newly encountered geometric anomalous entity recovered from deep submarine mission logs
input: Entity ID GEO-SUB-003, recovered from debris of lost Soviet submarine K-278 Komsomolets, depth 10200m
output: Appearance: GEO-SUB-003 is a self-repeating Koch snowflake fractal pattern that spreads across all metal submarine surfaces, each razor-sharp branch glowing faint icy blue, with identical self-similar structure at every magnification. Behavior: It grows 1cm per hour, cutting through pipe, wiring, and hatch seals without leaving metal shavings or debris, ignoring all physical barriers to its spread. Origin: It was created when an experimental geometric navigation computer was exposed to unfiltered cosmic radiation at extreme abyssal depth. Horror: Every section of pipe the fractal cuts through reduces the submarine’s total breathable air supply by the exact volume of the removed pipe segment, with no detectable leak or pressure drop to alert crew. Encounter: The Komsomolets crew reported the fractal spread across the main oxygen line 2 minutes before all onboard life signs ceased, with no external hull damage observed on the recovered wreck.

instruction: Write a paranoia horror story set on a space station
input: Fear theme: paranoia. Setting: isolated research space station
output: You check the air scrubber readouts for the seventh time in ten minutes. No anomalies. No one else is aboard—you confirmed the evacuation lock cycled closed three days ago. But every time you turn your back, you hear breathing just outside your helmet mic. Not static. Breathing. You spin. Empty corridor. You mute your mic. The breathing doesn’t stop. It gets closer. You yank the helmet off. The station air is silent. You re-don it. The breathing returns, right at your ear. No one is here. No one can be here. You don’t dare take the helmet off again.

instruction: Write a paranoia horror story set on a space station
input: Fear theme: paranoia. Setting: cargo bay of a resupply space station
output: You’re mopping condensation off the cargo bay bulkhead when you see it: a fresh palm print, pressed right into the frost. Your gloves leave ridged impressions. This is bare skin. You scan for life signs. Zero. You wipe it away, keep working. Ten minutes later there’s two. Then five. Each time you look away they multiply. You don’t remember blinking. You press your own bare palm against the cold metal. It matches exactly. You don’t remember taking your glove off. You don’t remember ever touching this wall.

instruction: Write a paranoia horror story set on a space station
input: Fear theme: paranoia. Setting: station residential corridor
output: There are 17 overhead lights on this corridor. You counted them every shift for 11 months. Tonight there are 18. You walk back. 17. Walk forward again. 18. No maintenance logs. No power fluctuations. You stand dead still, staring. One light winks out. Now 17. Then another turns on, further down. 18 again. It’s moving. It’s counting you. You press your back to the wall. You can’t look away. You don’t know when it will stop counting and start coming.

instruction: Write a paranoia horror story set on a space station
input: Fear theme: paranoia. Setting: station galley
output: You restock rations after every sleep cycle. Last night you locked the galley, verified the biometric seal. This morning one protein pouch is missing. Then tomorrow, two. You install a camera. The footage shows nothing. No one enters. The pouches just vanish, one by one, right off the shelf. You eat the next ration while you watch the feed. Halfway through, you glance down. Your hand is empty. The pouch is gone. You don’t remember swallowing. You don’t remember putting it down.

instruction: Write a paranoia horror story set on a space station
input: Fear theme: paranoia. Setting: observation cupola
output: You’re running the nightly stellar survey when you notice it: your shadow on the viewport, cast by the station’s running lights. Only you’re standing straight. The shadow is hunched. You lift your left hand. The shadow lifts its right. You turn around. No one is behind you. You turn back. The shadow is closer now. It isn’t cast by anything in this room. It’s on the other side of the glass. It’s been watching you. It’s been waiting for you to notice.

instruction: Write a tight, second-person horror narration focused on loss of control, set in an abandoned theater, 100–250 words, no filler.
input: fear: loss_of_control, setting: abandoned theater
output: You slip into the abandoned theater’s projection booth, chasing a faint flicker. The reel spools to a memory you buried: you leaving your little sister stranded on a dark road. You yank the reel off the spindle, but your hands lock to the metal—fingers curling against your will, cranking the handle faster. The projection light sears your eyes; you can’t blink, can’t look away. Each loop twists the memory: her screams grow guttural, her form melts into the theater’s shadowy seats. The booth door slams shut, the crank whirs on its own, and you realize you’re not just watching—you’re trapped, forced to relive your worst choice with zero control over your body or the nightmare unfolding.

instruction: Craft a second-person horror narration centered on loss of control, set in an abandoned theater, 100–250 words, no filler.
input: fear: loss_of_control, setting: abandoned theater
output: You sink into a threadbare velvet seat in the abandoned theater’s orchestra, and the fabric oozes over your wrists, gluing you down. The curtain rises on a play you don’t recognize—actors are your missing roommate, your dead grandma, your lost dog. They speak lines that mirror your unspoken guilt: *“You didn’t look hard enough”* *“You forgot to feed me”*. You try to scream, but your jaw locks. Your lungs burn, but you can’t gasp. The actors step off the stage, reaching for you, and your body goes rigid—no control over your voice, your movement, even your breath. The curtain falls, and you’re still stuck, forced to endure their accusations without a single way to fight back.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration focused on loss of control, set in an abandoned theater, 100–250 words, no filler.
input: fear: loss_of_control, setting: abandoned theater
output: You find a dented 1950s stage mic on the abandoned theater’s splintered platform. Curious, you pick it up—your quiet, unspoken thought *“I wish my toxic ex would vanish”* booms through the empty hall. A shadowy figure slithers from the wings, matching the shape of your ex, and melts into the floor. Panicked, you think *“I didn’t mean that”*—but another shadow, your boss (who you secretly resent), crawls from the orchestra pit and snaps its own neck. You drop the mic, but your thoughts still blare. Your mind spirals, and shadows keep forming, acting on every ugly, unfiltered thought you can’t suppress. You’re trapped, a passenger in your own mind, no control over the horrors your thoughts unleash.

instruction: Craft a second-person horror narration centered on loss of control, set in an abandoned theater, 100–250 words, no filler.
input: fear: loss_of_control, setting: abandoned theater
output: You stumble on a leather-bound prompt book backstage in the abandoned theater, its pages glowing faintly. The first line reads *“My left hand is no longer mine”*—your left hand twitches, then slams into the brick wall, breaking your knuckles. You scream, trying to close the book, but your right hand locks to the spine. Next line: *“My voice belongs to the stage”*—your mouth opens, reciting lines you’ve never heard, voice warping into a guttural growl. Your legs go rigid, forcing you to step onto the stage. Each line robs you of more control: your eyes roll back, your heart beats on its own, faster and faster. The curtain rises, and you’re a puppet, no say in your words, your movement, even your own heartbeat.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration focused on loss of control, set in an abandoned theater, 100–250 words, no filler.
input: fear: loss_of_control, setting: abandoned theater
output: You climb the abandoned theater’s creaky balcony, grabbing a rusted pair of binoculars left on a ledge. You peer through them, focusing on the stage—only it’s you, standing there, looking confused. Then the stage-you starts twitching, hands slamming into their own face. You yank the binoculars away, but your eyes lock to the lenses, can’t look away. Stage-you’s voice cracks, screaming lines you don’t recognize, their body contorting into impossible angles. You feel a twinge in your own hand—your fingers start curling on their own, matching stage-you’s movements. Your throat tightens, mirroring their gasps. The binoculars won’t drop, your body won’t listen, and you realize you’re watching yourself lose control, second by second, with no way to stop it.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a mirror maze focused on a jumpscare, where the narrator is searching for a missing friend.
input: Mirror maze, searching for missing friend, jumpscare from unexpected moving reflection.
output: You grip the cold metal handrail, calling your friend’s name again—your voice bounces off the warped glass, turning into a gurgling echo. The maze’s neon pink lights flicker, casting long, twisted shadows of your own figure across every surface. You round a corner, and there she is: your friend, pressed against a mirror, her face pale. You reach out to tap the glass… but her eyes snap open, black and empty, and she lunges forward—right through the mirror. You stumble back, heart hammering, only to see your own reflection behind you in another mirror: it’s grinning, its mouth stretched too wide, and it slams its palm against the glass so hard it cracks. You scream as it breaks through, cold fingers brushing your neck.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a mirror maze focused on a jumpscare, where the narrator is fixated on their own changing reflection.
input: Mirror maze, self-reflection fixation, jumpscare from simultaneous mirror figures.
output: You stop to adjust your hair in a mirror, frowning at the way your left eye seems slightly off. The maze’s dim blue lights hum, making the glass vibrate. You lean in closer, and suddenly your reflection’s mouth twitches—not in sync with yours. Blinking, you step back, but every mirror around you now shows a tall, hooded figure standing directly behind you. You spin around, but there’s nothing there. When you turn back, the figure in each mirror is leaning forward, its face hidden by the hood. Then, all at once, it yanks the hood back: a gaping, toothless maw where its face should be. You cry out, tripping over your own feet, as the figure slams its fists against every mirror at once, shards raining down around you.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a mirror maze focused on a jumpscare, where the narrator is trying to escape and mistakes a mirror for an exit.
input: Mirror maze, escaping, jumpscare from mirror that’s a hidden door with a monster.
output: You’ve been wandering the maze for 20 minutes, your breath coming in ragged gasps. The smell of mildew clings to the air, and every turn leads to another wall of glass. You spot what looks like an open doorway at the end of a corridor—finally, the exit. You sprint toward it, ignoring the way the lights flicker out one by one. As you reach it, you realize it’s a mirror, reflecting your desperate face. But wait—your reflection isn’t moving. You tap the glass, and it gives way. A cold, slimy hand grabs your wrist, yanking you forward. You’re pulled through the mirror into a dark space, where a creature with too many eyes stares at you, its mouth dripping with saliva. You scream as it lunges, its teeth sinking into your shoulder.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a mirror maze focused on a jumpscare, where the narrator uses a phone to navigate and captures a ghostly figure in photos.
input: Mirror maze, using phone for navigation, jumpscare from figure in photo appearing physically.
output: Your phone’s flashlight cuts through the dark maze, casting streaks of light across the mirrors. You snap a photo to mark your path, squinting at the screen. In the background of the photo, there’s a small, child-like figure standing in a corner, its face covered by a mask. You spin around, but the corner is empty. You take another photo—this time, the figure is closer, right behind you in the frame. You drop your phone, heart racing. When you pick it up, the figure is now standing directly in front of you, its mask slipping to reveal a rotting face. It screeches, lunging at you, and you fall back, hitting your head on a mirror. The glass cracks, and you feel cold fingers wrap around your ankle, dragging you toward the shards.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a mirror maze focused on a jumpscare, where the narrator follows a soft sound to a reflection that mimics then attacks.
input: Mirror maze, following soft sound, jumpscare from mimicking reflection attacking.
output: You hear a soft lullaby drifting through the maze, familiar but twisted. You follow it, your shoes scuffing the cold tile. The lullaby gets louder as you round a corner, leading you to a mirror that shows a young girl sitting on the floor, singing. You kneel down, and she kneels too—mimicking your every move. You wave, and she waves back. Then, you stand up, but she stays seated, her eyes turning black. She stands suddenly, her body contorting, and slams her head against the mirror so hard it shatters. She crawls through the broken glass, her nails scraping the floor, and lunges at you, her teeth bared. You scream, scrambling backward, as she grabs your arm, her cold skin burning like ice.

instruction: Write second-person psychological horror narration set in a corrupted garden
input: Your grandmother’s overgrown backyard garden, corrupted by blight and invasive flora, where every bloom holds a small, precious memory
output: You step into your grandmother’s overgrown backyard, the garden now corrupted—weeds twist like gnarled fingers around blackened rosebushes, forget-me-nots bloom in bruised clumps. You reach for a bloom that held your first birthday memory, and when your fingers brush its petals, a flash hits: you’re five, holding a lollipop, only for that memory to vanish entirely. A hollow ache remains, like a missing piece of your mind. Another bloom holds your first kiss, and it’s stolen next. You back away, but more blooms brush your wrists, each siphoning a tiny, happy thought you can’t reclaim. Your own mind blurs; you can’t recall your phone number, why you came here, just the growing terror of losing yourself whole.

instruction: Write second-person psychological horror narration set in a corrupted garden
input: An abandoned community garden, corrupted by toxic soil, that mirrors your deepest unspoken anxieties
output: You wander the abandoned community garden, corrupted by blighted dirt and plants that twist into shapes you can’t name. A patch of tomatoes grows into small, faceless figures that watch you, and you suddenly remember ruining your friend’s art project in seventh grade—something you buried deep. As you step over a root, the garden shifts: the old shed’s walls look like your childhood home, where you hid after that fight. Voices whisper, people you wronged, and you can’t move; the soil softens, trying to pull you under. These are just your own fears, but you can’t outrun them here, trapped in the garden that shows you the parts of yourself you hate.

instruction: Write second-person psychological horror narration set in a corrupted garden
input: A forest-edge garden at dusk, corrupted by time rifts, that traps you in a repeating moment of regret
output: You step into the corrupted forest garden at dusk, and something feels off: the air smells like rain and old regret. You turn to leave, but the path back is gone—only twisted ferns and scratchy blackberry bushes. You walk for hours, only to find the same gnarled oak and broken bench. Then you hear your own voice, from ten minutes prior, saying, “I should have never come here.” You realize you’re looped, trapped in the moment you entered. Every step forward brings you back, the sun never rises, the only sound your repeating words. You’re not sure if you’ll escape, or if you’ll stay here forever, replaying your own bad choice.

instruction: Write second-person psychological horror narration set in a corrupted garden
input: A neglected city garden, corrupted by ambient despair, that consumes personal identity
output: You enter the corrupted city garden, grass gray and flowerbeds empty of blooms, just pocked, lifeless soil. You pick up a small stone, and suddenly your name slips away. You touch the oak tree, and your birthday, your sister’s name, your first job—all gone. Vines wrap your legs, and you look down: your hands turn translucent, like smoke. You hear faint whispers, people who came before you, their identities rotting into the soil and plants. You try to scream, no sound comes; you’re disappearing, the garden waiting to swallow you entirely.

instruction: Write second-person psychological horror narration set in a corrupted garden
input: A private childhood garden, corrupted by your own repressed stress, where your thoughts manifest as dangerous flora
output: You stand in the corrupted garden behind your childhood home, where you hid as a kid. The roses here have thorns like tiny knives, and you realize these aren’t just plants—they’re your own anxious thoughts, grown solid. A vine twists toward your throat, and your mind yells: You messed up, you’ll never be good enough. You try to run, but every path loops back to the center, where more plants grow from your fears. You see ghosts: your old teacher yelling, your friend walking away, all made of leaves and thorns. You know this is your own mind twisted by the garden, but you can’t stop the growth, can’t silence the voices, and fear you’ll be trapped here with your own thoughts forever.

instruction: Generate a second-person horror narration centered on the fear of darkness, set on a frozen lake
input: You are solo ice fishing on a remote backcountry frozen lake when your only headlamp shuts off without warning
output: You freeze mid-reach for your emergency kit as absolute dark swallows every trace of your ice hole, the distant treeline, even the faint glint of your ice picks at your feet. The cold bites through your parka fast, but what makes your blood turn to slush is the slow, scraping drag of something moving under the ice, circling closer every time you hold your breath to listen. You can’t see the thin, crackling fissures spreading out from your stool, only feel the faint vibration of pressure from below, and the warm, damp breath that curls against your neck from the dark where no living thing should be standing.

instruction: Generate a second-person horror narration centered on the fear of darkness, set on a frozen lake
input: You went for a casual evening ice skating on the local public frozen lake when a sudden power outage kills all the shoreline floodlights
output: The last sliver of dusk vanishes in a heartbeat, leaving you adrift in total darkness out on the ice, your skates scratching at empty, unmarked surface under you. The other skaters’ shouts cut off abruptly, like someone muted the world, and you can’t tell if the faint, sliding thud you hear ten feet away is a friend calling out, or something long frozen in the lake dragging itself across the ice toward you. You stumble back, your blade catching on a hairline crack you can’t see, and a cold, clawed hand brushes your wrist from the void of dark next to you.

instruction: Generate a second-person horror narration centered on the fear of darkness, set on a frozen lake
input: You crashed your snowmobile halfway across an isolated frozen lake, your only headlight breaking on impact as full night falls
output: You pat your pockets frantically for a lighter, a phone, any tiny source of light, and come up empty, the dark so thick you can’t even see your own outstretched fingers. You’d mapped the lake’s thin ice patches earlier, but now you have no way of telling where solid ice ends and open, frozen-over sinkholes begin. The distant jingle of your lost snowmobile’s keychain echoes from the dark, moving closer, and you hear the low, gurgling voice of the teenager who drowned here last winter, calling your name to step forward into the dark.

instruction: Generate a second-person horror narration centered on the fear of darkness, set on a frozen lake
input: You hiked out to a half-sunken abandoned hunting cabin on a frozen lake to take photos, and the sun set faster than you expected
output: The last of the twilight bleeds out of the sky, and the dark wraps around you so tight it feels like a blanket soaked in ice water. The cabin you were exploring vanishes entirely behind you in the void, and you can’t retrace your steps back to shore, every footfall landing on ice that creaks and groans louder with every step. You hear the soft, wet sound of something crawling out of the lake behind you, its breath fogging against your ear, and you realize the dark isn’t just hiding what’s in the lake—it’s holding it, guiding it straight to you.

instruction: Generate a second-person horror narration centered on the fear of darkness, set on a frozen lake
input: You are doing late-winter ice thickness survey work on a remote frozen lake when a blizzard rolls in, blotting out all remaining light
output: Your GPS died ten minutes ago, your laser rangefinder is useless in the pitch dark, and all you can feel under your boots is ice that gets softer, slushier, with every step you take. The blizzard wind carries the sound of children laughing, high and sharp, from the dark expanse of the lake, even though no one has lived within ten miles of this spot for 40 years. You trip over something solid half-buried in the ice, and your gloved hand brushes the cold, preserved face of a child who went missing on this lake in 1978, right there in the dark you can’t see past.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on isolation in a mine shaft
input: Fear: isolation. Setting: abandoned mine shaft
output: You click the radio again. Static only. Three hours ago you fell through rotten timber. The headlamp casts a ten foot circle; beyond is pure black. No voices, no distant drill hum. You yell. The sound dies instantly, swallowed by rock. You count your breaths. 1172 now. You scrape your boot on the floor just to make noise. There’s no echo. No one heard you fall. No one is coming. The headlamp flickers once. You realize you haven’t heard a single living sound, not even a bug, since you landed. It’s just you, and the dark that’s waited here alone for a hundred million years.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on isolation in a working mine shaft
input: Fear: isolation. Setting: active working mine shaft
output: You turned left at the third cross drift just like the foreman said. The chalk arrows stopped twenty minutes back. You backtrack. No footprints. Not even your own. The ventilation fans went quiet ten minutes ago; the air tastes like dust and time. You press your palm to the rock wall. It’s cold. Unchanged. No one will notice you’re missing until shift change. Seven hours from now. You press your ear to the stone. There’s nothing. No thud of distant picks, no pipe rattle. Just heavy silence that presses on your eardrums until you start screaming just to break it.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on isolation in a collapsed mine shaft
input: Fear: isolation. Setting: partially collapsed mine shaft
output: The roof fell behind you twelve minutes ago. You’re trapped in a twelve yard stretch of tunnel. You saw a headlamp bobbing on the other side of the rubble once. You screamed. It stopped. It never came back. No one is digging. You don’t hear shovels. You don’t hear anyone calling your name. The rock shifts sometimes, soft groans. That’s the only sound. You check your watch. It stopped. You don’t know how long you’ve been here. You don’t know if anyone even knows you were here. The dark gets closer every time you blink.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on isolation in a deep exploratory mine shaft
input: Fear: isolation. Setting: deep exploratory mine shaft 4200ft below surface
output: Your suit beacon blinks steady green. It should ping the surface every seven seconds. It’s been two hours. No reply. No acknowledgement. You are deeper than any human has ever been. No one has ever stood where you are standing. No one will ever come this far just to look for you. You shine the lamp down the tunnel. It doesn’t end. It just goes forever into rock formed before life existed on Earth. You are the only living thing for two and a half miles in every direction. You don’t bother yelling. Nothing here can hear you.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on isolation in a historic mine tour shaft
input: Fear: isolation. Setting: boarded up historic mine tour shaft
output: You snuck in here with your friend an hour ago. You turned around for ten seconds. She’s gone. No scream. No footprints. The entrance you climbed through is gone. Solid rock where the ladder was. You run your hands over it. No seam. No crack. Your phone has no signal. No one knows you came here. The tour hasn’t run in 17 years. No one will find you. You sit down. The dark wraps around your shoulders. You realize you can’t remember what your friend’s voice sounded like. You can barely remember your own name.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a barn centered on paranoia, where the protagonist hides from a storm and hears whispers mirroring their unspoken guilt.
input: Barn, storm, unspoken guilt mirrored in whispers
output: You slam the barn door shut against the howling storm, rain drumming the tin roof like a thousand tiny fists. You huddle behind a rotting hay bale, breath ragged—you thought you’d escaped the guilt of leaving your sister behind, but now the barn breathes it back at you. A whisper curls around your ear, exactly your voice: She hated you for running. You spin, but there’s nothing, just swaying hay and creaking rafters. Another whisper, softer: They’re coming for you. You check the door latch three times, fingers trembling. The hay shifts, and you swear you see a shadow that’s your own, but taller, angrier. Every creak feels like a judgment, every rustle a reminder you can’t outrun the thoughts you’ve buried. You press your palms to your ears, but the whispers don’t stop—they’re inside your head now, aren’t they? The barn isn’t just a shelter; it’s a cage for your paranoia, and it’s locking you in.

instruction: Create a second-person horror narration in a barn focused on paranoia, where the protagonist discovers photos of their doppelgänger and feels constant surveillance.
input: Barn, doppelgänger photos, constant surveillance
output: You’re stacking firewood in the back of the barn when your boot kicks a rusted metal box. Inside are yellowed photos—each one of a person who looks exactly like you, standing in this same barn, same hay bales, same chipped paint on the wall. Your blood runs cold. You glance over your shoulder; the barn’s shadows stretch toward you, as if something’s peeking from behind the tractor. You flip to the last photo: it’s you, right now, stacking wood, taken just minutes ago. You drop the box, fumbling for your phone, but there’s no signal. Every time you blink, you swear you see a flash of your own face in the rafters. You check the barn doors twice, then three times, but they’re latched tight. The air smells like dust and metallic fear. You know you’re alone—aren’t you? But the photos don’t lie, and the feeling of eyes on your neck won’t fade. You’re not sure if you’re being watched… or if the thing watching is you.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a barn centered on paranoia, where the protagonist notices livestock avoiding them and suspects they hold a forgotten secret.
input: Barn, livestock avoiding protagonist, forgotten secret
output: You carry a bucket of slop to the goat pen in the barn, but when you approach, every animal freezes—goats, chickens, even the old mule in the corner. They don’t run; they just stare at you, eyes wide, as if you’re a stranger in your own skin. You set the bucket down, and the mule snorts, turning its back so fast it knocks over a water trough. Your hands shake. You can’t remember why you came out here at midnight, just a vague, gnawing feeling you left something important in the barn. You search the hayloft, finding only a crumpled note in your handwriting: Don’t trust the silence. The barn goes dead quiet, no clucking, no bleating. You spin—all the animals face the same corner, watching something you can’t see. You step toward them, but they bolt, trampling the bucket. The silence returns, thick enough to swallow you. You know they’re hiding something. But what if the secret isn’t theirs to keep? What if you’re the one who’s forgotten?

instruction: Create a second-person horror narration in a barn focused on paranoia, where the protagonist hears their name repeatedly called and begins to question their sanity.
input: Barn, repeated name calls, questioning sanity
output: You’re prying loose a rotting fence post in the barn when someone calls your name—soft, familiar, like your mom’s voice. You look up, but the barn is empty except for dust motes dancing in slanted sunlight. You shake it off, going back to work, but the call comes again, closer, from the hayloft. You climb the rickety ladder, heart pounding, but find nothing except a frayed scarf identical to the one you lost at 10. You climb back down, and the name echoes again, from the latched barn door—no one outside. You check every corner, every crate, but there’s no one. You sit on a hay bale, breathing hard. Is it a ghost? Or are you losing it? You hum your mom’s lullaby, and the call stops… for a minute. Then it starts again, but this time it’s your voice, calling your own name. You cover your ears, but it’s inside your head now. You can’t tell if you’re hearing things, or if the barn is making you say them. Either way, you’re not sure you can leave.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a barn centered on paranoia, where the protagonist finds a mask identical to their face and fears it’s stealing their identity.
input: Barn, identical mask, identity theft fear
output: You’re hauling Halloween boxes into the barn when you spot a wooden mask tucked under a hay bale. It’s carved to look exactly like you—same crooked smile, same scar above your left eyebrow, same tired eyes. You pick it up, fingers tingling. You set it on a crate, turning to grab another box, but when you glance back, the mask is facing you, as if it turned on its own. You laugh it off, but when you touch your face, the scar feels faint, like it’s fading. You put the mask on for a joke, but when you take it off, your reflection in the tractor’s mirror looks wrong—your smile is too crooked, the scar is gone. You panic, grabbing the mask, but it’s now missing the scar too. You check the barn doors, latched tight. Every time you blink, your reflection changes a little more, matching the mask. You hide the mask in a locked crate, but you can still feel it watching you. You’re not sure if the mask is becoming you… or if you’re becoming the mask. The barn feels smaller now, trapping you with the thing stealing your face.

instruction: Describe the moment the silo's automated system locks you in.
input: The silo's climate control system has malfunctioned.
output: The hiss of the seal is the loudest sound you've ever heard. The inner airlock door just shut, and the status panel by your hand flashes red: MANUAL OVERRIDE DISABLED. You pound on the thick steel, but your fists are whispers against it. A calm, synthetic voice fills the chamber. 'Atmospheric stabilization in progress. Please remain calm.' The air grows thick, heavy, forcing you to your knees. You can't draw a full breath; it's like inhaling syrup. Your vision tunnels. The system isn't trying to kill you, you realize with dawning horror. It's trying to 'save' you, recalibrating the air to a mix your body wasn't built for. Your limbs are lead. You can't even lift a hand as the voice cheerfully updates, 'Optimization at 87%.' Your thoughts are the only thing left moving, screaming inside a body that no longer obeys.

instruction: Narrate discovering the silo's walls are slowly, silently contracting.
input: You are conducting a routine structural inspection deep in the grain silo.
output: Your measuring tape snags. You frown, running a hand along the curved metal wall. It should be smooth. A hairline seam you've never noticed before now holds the tape's end. You measure the diameter again, from the same bolt to the opposite wall. The number is two centimeters smaller than in the logbook from yesterday. A cold trickle of sweat traces your spine. You press your palms against the cold steel. Is it your imagination, or is there a faint, sub-audible groan of stress? You look up the sheer wall, disappearing into darkness. The ceiling is lower. The walls are not settling. They are closing. A slow, inevitable embrace. The exit ladder is thirty meters up a surface that is now subtly, undeniably, concave.

instruction: Detail the experience of the silo's internal gravity failing and reversing.
input: A deep hum shakes the silo, and all warning lights go out.
output: The flashlight slips from your fingers. But it doesn't fall. It hangs, wobbling, in the air before your face. Your stomach lurches as your own feet leave the grating. You are floating, gently at first, in the absolute dark of the central shaft. Then the deep hum returns, vibrating in your bones, and an invisible hand shoves you upward—toward the distant, sealed roof. You flail, grabbing nothing. The gravity hasn't failed; it's inverted. You are falling upward. The roof rushes to meet you, a disk of black metal in the gloom. You scream, twisting your body, but physics has betrayed you. You are a puppet, and the silo has cut all the right strings.

instruction: Describe being trapped as a strange, sentient mold in the silo consumes your tools and begins to learn your patterns.
input: A bioluminescent fungus coats the lower levels of the abandoned missile silo.
output: The fungus glows a soft, sickly green. You dropped your wrench an hour ago; the mold flowed over it, dissolving the metal in silent, oily tendrils. Now, it's mimicking. A lump of fungus near the door has hardened into a rough, wrench-shaped silhouette. You try to retreat, but the path you cleared is gone, seamlessly filled in. The glow pulses softly, in time with your panicked heartbeat. You step left; a tendril lazily blocks your way. You step right; another anticipates you. It's herding you. Not with malice, but with the terrible, patient control of a system learning its new component: you.

instruction: Narrate the realization that the silo's countdown timer is not for launch, but for a total internal purge you cannot stop.
input: You are a technician who has accidentally triggered a dormant launch protocol.
output: The main console is alive, digits ticking down from 10:00. You've entered every abort code, pulled every emergency lever. Nothing. 'PURGE CYCLE INITIATED' glows steadily. This isn't a missile launch. It's a sterilization. You hear the first vents open high above, a sigh of pressurized gas. It's odorless, colorless. But the monitor shows the oxygen percentage plummeting, replaced by an inert, unbreathable nitrogen. Your breaths become useless gulps. The system is methodically, cleanly, replacing the atmosphere. You slump against the console, fingers scrabbling weakly at the unyielding keys. You are not being attacked. You are being deleted from the environment, a variable smoothed away by perfect, automated control.

instruction: Create a second-person horror story centered on a jumpscare, set in a phone booth with a highway distress scenario
input: Second-person horror narration, phone booth setting, jumpscare fear, 100-250 words
output: You’re huddled in a fogged phone booth on a rain-soaked deserted highway, dialing 911 after swerving to avoid a deer. The line crackles, not with a dispatcher’s voice, but a distorted, cold hiss: “I’ve been waiting for you.” You fumble to slam the receiver down, but it sticks to your palm, sticky and warm. The booth’s glass slams inward suddenly, not from wind—you whirl, and the deer you hit stands at the booth’s opening, its eyes wide and human, mouth stretched into a grin too big for its face, its cold breath fogging the space between you as it steps closer, hooves clicking fast on wet pavement.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror story set in a late-night mall parking phone booth with a prank call twist
input: Second-person horror narration, phone booth setting, jumpscare fear, 100-250 words
output: You duck into a scuffed payphone booth in the 24h mall’s empty parking lot at 1 a.m., planning a prank call to your roommate. Before you can insert a quarter, the coin slot clicks on its own, pushing a quarter out, and a raspy kid’s voice hisses “You found me” through the receiver before cutting to static. You dial your roommate anyway, no ringtone, just dead air. Your sleeve catches the booth’s metal door handle—sticky, wet red gunk. Glancing up at the booth’s cracked mirror, you don’t see your face: a faceless, grinning thing presses its cheek to the glass, fingers crawling through gaps as the mirror shatters inward, shards raining down on your shoulders.

instruction: Make a second-person jumpscare horror story set in a phone booth where you’re hiding from a stalker
input: Second-person horror narration, phone booth setting, jumpscare fear, 100-250 words
output: You bolt down a dark back alley, shoes slapping wet pavement, ducking into a scuffed phone booth to hide from the man yelling your name behind you. The booth’s overhead light flickers, then cuts out, leaving you in near darkness. A static-laced whisper of your name echoes through the receiver—you freeze, then hear fast footsteps thudding right outside the door. The receiver is ripped from your hand; you whirl, and your stalker stands in the booth’s opening, its head twisted 180 degrees so its chin rests on its shoulder, mouth stretched wide to show rows of sharp, black teeth, hands clutching the door frame as it leans in, breath reeking of rot.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror story set in a closed county fair’s old phone booth
input: Second-person horror narration, phone booth setting, jumpscare fear, 100-250 words
output: You wait for your ride at the shuttered county fair, ducking into a rusted, decades-old phone booth left by the ticket gate. The coin slot clicks on its own, dropping a quarter into the machine, and a faint carnival bell rings three times. You pick up the receiver, and a high-pitched clown laugh blares so loud it makes your teeth ache, cutting to dead silence immediately after. You lean forward to set the receiver down, and the booth’s floor drops a foot; you glance down, seeing a layer of grinning clown hands pulling at your shoes. The booth’s glass splits down the middle, and a clown with cracked painted skin presses its face through the gap, red nose glinting in faint moonlight, grin stretching past its ears.

instruction: Create a second-person jumpscare horror story set in a rural backroad’s weathered phone booth
input: Second-person horror narration, phone booth setting, jumpscare fear, 100-250 words
output: You’re lost on a dark rural backroad at midnight, your phone dead, so you duck into a weathered, creaky phone booth to call AAA. There’s no dial tone, just a low hum. You bang the receiver against the hook, and a woman’s scream echoes through the small space, bouncing off metal walls. You step back, tripping over the booth’s base, and when you glance up, the booth’s door is hanging open, no wind to move it. A woman in a tattered white wedding dress stands behind you, fingers long and cold wrapping around your neck, mouth full of shattered glass, eyes empty black pits as she pulls you out, her dress dragging on gravel.

instruction: Document a unique geometric horror entity manifesting on a rural steel cantilever bridge, aligned with SCP cosmic horror design principles
input: Setting: fog-shrouded 1960s road bridge, 2:17AM, no cell service, crumpled abandoned car on the east shoulder
output: Entity: The Truss Tessellation. Appearance: A shifting cluster of overlapping, razor-sharp equilateral triangles that replace the bridge’s support crossbeams, flickering between rusted steel, pale human skin, and raw exposed iron when viewed out of the corner of your eye. Behavior: It warps spatial perspective so every step taken on the bridge, no matter direction, moves you 11 inches closer to its central core. It only becomes visible when the ambient fog density reaches exactly 78%. Origin: 12 ironworkers fell into wet truss concrete during the bridge’s 1969 construction, their unmarked remains fused with the structural math of the span to form a sentient geometric anomaly. Horror: Each triangle edge leaves an invisible 1cm deep laceration that only bleeds once you are far enough from the entity to realize you are injured; it feeds on the breakdown of human spatial reasoning. Encounter: The only surviving witness reported crawling off the bridge on their stomach, losing 3 pints of blood, with no memory of how they reached the shore.

instruction: Document a unique geometric horror entity manifesting on a remote wooden footbridge, aligned with Junji Ito body horror design principles
input: Setting: rotting footbridge over a glacial melt river, golden dusk, distant campfire smoke drifting through the trees
output: Entity: The Non-Euclidean Railing. Appearance: A continuous 9-sided polygon that forms the entire span of the bridge’s guardrails, with no visible corners no matter what angle you view it from, its matte black surface absorbs all light that touches it. Behavior: It extends 2 inches outward every time you blink, mimics the voice of a lost loved one leaning against the rail to lure visitors within arm’s reach. Origin: A 1998 teen suicide pact where 9 teenagers jumped from the bridge in sequence, their collective unprocessed grief warped the wooden rail into a geometric prison holding their trapped consciousness. Horror: Skin contact with the railing immediately rearranges the cross-section of every bone in your body to match its 9-sided geometry, fusing you into the rail as a permanent new segment. Encounter: Park rangers have recovered 22 abandoned trail cameras pointed at the bridge, all footage cutting out the second any visitor steps within 3 feet of the guardrail.

instruction: Document a unique geometric horror entity manifesting on a crumbling concrete arch bridge, aligned with Silent Hill uncanny design principles
input: Setting: 1930s WPA arch bridge over a dry ravine, midday, no other pedestrians, bright unfiltered sunlight
output: Entity: The Plumb Line Wraith. Appearance: An infinitely thin, perfectly straight vertical line running dead center of the bridge span, with zero measurable width no matter how close you approach, casting a perfect square shadow even when the sun sits at a 47 degree angle. Behavior: It sways exactly 1.6 degrees back and forth per second, blocks all attempts to cross the center line no matter how wide you detour around it. Origin: A 1931 bridge inspector hanged himself from the arch after his miscalculations caused a construction collapse that killed 40 workers, his overwhelming guilt condensed into a fixed geometric anomaly tied to the bridge’s structural load math. Horror: Crossing the line erases all human perception of up and down, leaving visitors falling indefinitely even while standing on solid concrete. Encounter: Truck drivers report seeing the line only when their odometer hits a multiple of 13, all who have crossed it are found catatonic, muttering about “the line that was never supposed to be there”.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a tunnel
input: Setting: pedestrian underpass tunnel
output: You count each footfall to stay calm. 117 steps, that’s how long this tunnel always is. 115, 116, 117… you’re still inside. You stop. No exit glow ahead. You turn—no entrance behind either. Just damp concrete stretching both ways. You start walking again. 200 steps. 300. You hear breathing, perfectly matched to yours, one half-second delayed. You hold your breath. The delayed breath keeps going, soft and steady, right behind your ear. It never needed you to breathe at all. It was just matching you, for politeness, until you noticed.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a tunnel
input: Setting: abandoned rail tunnel
output: You duck in to wait out the rain. Phone dies instantly. You trace old graffiti with your thumb—same tag, same smudge, over and over, every ten feet. You laugh until you notice: each tag has your initials, your handwriting, the little cross you always dot your i with. You don’t remember painting any of them. You run. Every turn looks identical. When you finally collapse, you reach in your pocket. You’re holding a spray can. Your fingers are still sticky with black paint. The wall in front of you is blank. Your hand is already lifting.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a tunnel
input: Setting: highway road tunnel
output: Your car died mid-tunnel. Your daughter ran ahead for help ten minutes ago. You call her name. Only your echo answers, but each time it comes back younger, higher, exactly her voice. You follow it. Halfway, you find her tiny sparkly shoe. Then another. Then another. Every three steps, another identical shoe, placed neatly in the white line. When you glance back, you see your own work boots, placed exactly the same way, trailing behind you. You don’t remember taking them off. You don’t remember stopping walking.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a tunnel
input: Setting: water utility tunnel
output: You’re here to fix the pressure gauge. The lights flicker once. When they come back, every clock on the wall reads 3:17. You check your watch. It also reads 3:17. You walk for what feels like an hour. All the clocks still read 3:17. You lean against the wall and close your eyes. When you open them, you’re standing at the tunnel entrance. You reach for your radio. Your coworker’s voice comes through, calm: “Where’d you go? You just walked in thirty seconds ago.” You haven’t aged, but your boots are worn through at the soles.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a tunnel
input: Setting: mountain hiking tunnel
output: You took the shortcut tunnel back to camp. You pass a hiker slouched against the wall, hood up. You ask if they’re okay. They don’t move. You pass another. Same hood, same slump. Ten more. Twenty. You start running. Finally you see the exit light. Right before you step out, you glance at the glass reflection on the tunnel exit sign. You’re slouched. Hood up. And there’s someone running towards you, just like you were, about to ask if you’re okay. This loop has been running for three days.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a clock tower, focusing on the fear of isolation, where you're the last caretaker trapped after a blizzard cuts off all communication.
input: Clock tower, last caretaker, blizzard isolation
output: You huddle in the clock tower’s stone basement, the blizzard howling outside like a rabid beast that’s already eaten the town. Your radio died three days ago; the last transmission was a static-laced warning to stay inside. The tower’s gears groan every hour, a slow, mournful chime that echoes through empty stairwells. You found the 1927 caretaker’s log yesterday—entries ending with “It’s mimicking my footsteps now.” Tonight, you hear a scuff on the metal staircase above. You haven’t left the basement in 48 hours. The clock strikes midnight, and the chime warbles off-key, like someone’s holding one of the bells. You reach for your flashlight, but the bulb burns out. In the dark, you swear you feel a breath on the back of your neck, warm and stale, like the dust in the clock’s long-forgotten attic. You’re not alone. But no one’s coming to save you.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a clock tower, focusing on isolation, where you’re a journalist locked inside while investigating a missing restorer.
input: Clock tower, journalist investigating missing restorer, locked inside isolation
output: You slam your shoulder against the tower’s iron door again, but it doesn’t budge—someone jammed the deadbolt from the outside. You came here to find out why the clock restorer vanished last month, but now your phone’s dead, and the only sound is the clock’s relentless ticking. You climb the spiral stairs, your boots thudding against cold stone, until you reach the restorer’s workbench. Half-finished gears lie scattered, and a note scrawled in shaky ink: “It counts the seconds until you’re alone forever.” The clock strikes 3, and the bells ring so loud you cover your ears. When they stop, you hear a soft, rhythmic tapping from the bell chamber above. You climb the final ladder, flashlight shaking, and freeze. The restorer’s tool belt hangs from one of the bells, swaying gently. But there’s no wind inside the tower. The tapping starts again—this time, from behind you. You turn, but the ladder’s gone. The clock ticks on, and you realize you’re now the one who’ll never be found.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a clock tower, focusing on isolation, where you’re a teen who snuck in and was abandoned by friends, now trapped as night falls.
input: Clock tower, teen abandoned by friends, trapped at night isolation
output: You call your friends’ names for the tenth time, but the only reply is the clock’s ticking, loud enough to rattle your teeth. They left you here an hour ago, laughing as they slammed the tower door shut—said it was a prank. Now the sun’s gone, and the temperature’s dropping fast. You climb the spiral stairs, hoping to find a window to scream through, but each landing is empty, the walls lined with dust-covered portraits of past caretakers. The clock strikes 9, and the bells boom, making you stumble. When the noise fades, you hear a whisper, soft and familiar, like your best friend’s voice. “Up here,” it says. You rush to the top, but there’s no one—only a cracked mirror leaning against the bell frame. In the mirror, you see a figure behind you, tall and shadowy, its hand reaching for your shoulder. You spin around, but nothing’s there. The mirror’s reflection doesn’t change. The clock ticks on, and you realize the whisper wasn’t your friend. It was something that’s been waiting for someone alone in the tower.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a clock tower, focusing on isolation, where you’re the only survivor of a town-wide disaster, hiding in the tower as something hunts you.
input: Clock tower, sole disaster survivor, hunted isolation
output: You press your back to the clock’s massive brass gears, listening for the scrape of claws on stone. The flood took everyone else—your family, your neighbors, the whole town. You climbed the tower to escape the water, but now you’re trapped, the stairs below submerged. The clock ticks every second, a reminder of how long you’ve been alone. You found a tattered blanket in the caretaker’s closet, but it smells like rot. Tonight, you hear a splash from the stairwell. It’s coming up. You climb onto the gear platform, your hands slipping on grease. The clock strikes 12, and the gears shift, nearly knocking you off. You look down, and see it: a pale, waterlogged figure, its eyes milky white, clinging to the stairs. It looks like your mother. It calls your name, voice gurgling. You know it’s not her. But you’re so lonely, you almost reach out. Then it smiles, and you see its teeth are sharp, like shards of glass. The clock ticks on, and you realize you’ll die here, alone, or worse—join it.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a clock tower, focusing on isolation, where you’re a night watchman haunted by the ghost of a watchman who died alone in the tower decades ago.
input: Clock tower, night watchman, haunted by deceased lonely watchman isolation
output: You check the security cameras for the fifth time—all static. You’re the night watchman at the old clock tower, and the previous one died here in 1958, starved to death after the town forgot he was trapped. The clock ticks so loud it’s in your bones. You pour a cup of coffee, but when you turn around, there’s a man sitting in your chair, wearing a tattered 1950s uniform. He doesn’t look at you, just stares at the clock. “No one comes,” he says, voice like dry leaves. You back away, knocking over your coffee. When you look again, he’s gone. But his journal is on the desk, open to the last entry: “The clock counts the seconds until no one remembers you.” The clock strikes 2, and the bells ring off-key. You run to the bell chamber, and see the man hanging from one of the bells, his eyes wide. You scream, but no one hears—you’re 100 feet above the empty town. When you turn to leave, the door is locked. The man’s voice whispers in your ear: “Now you’ll be alone too.”

instruction: Generate a horror entity entry in the specified format.
input: Type: lovecraftian. Setting: a prison cell.
output: {"entity_name": "The Geometry of Guilt", "appearance": "Not a creature, but a non-Euclidean fault in reality centered on a single brick in the cell wall. The brick pulses with a sick, internal violet light. Looking directly at it causes the surrounding walls to visually unfold into impossible angles and recursive, prison-bar patterned spaces that extend to a silent infinity. A low, atonal hum vibrates from the stone.", "behavior": "The anomaly passively warps space and perception within the cell. Prolonged exposure causes inmates to physically fold into themselves; bones bending at angles nature forbids, skin developing geometric pressure sores that form perfect, intricate shapes. It 'judges' by imposing a literal, physical geometry upon the chaotic guilt of the human form.", "origin": "A fragment of a cosmic law, a principle of absolute order, accidentally embedded in the mortar during construction. It was activated and attuned by centuries of chaotic human suffering, becoming a lens that focuses cosmic regularity onto biological irregularity.", "horror": "The horror is of unnatural law and transformation. It represents a universe utterly indifferent to human biology, where concepts like 'justice' and 'order' have a pure, mathematical form that is utterly mutilating to flesh and sanity.", "encounter": "The cell appears normal until one notices the faint glow and the slight 'wrongness' of the walls. Staring too long triggers the spatial unfolding. Escape requires not looking at the central brick, but its influence warps depth perception, making the door seem miles away. Touching the brick results in immediate, irreversible geometric crystallization of the limb."}

instruction: Generate a horror entity entry in the specified format.
input: Type: lovecraftian. Setting: a prison cell.
output: {"entity_name": "The Warden's True Flesh", "appearance": "A pulsating, porous mass of meat and rusted iron that seeps from the ventilation grate. It has no fixed shape, but constantly forms and dissolves sadistic instruments—hooks, gags, barbed coils—from its own substance. A single, bloodshot eye the size of a dinner plate swims within its depths, blinking with a sound like a closing cell door.", "behavior": "It embodies the prison's institutional cruelty. It slowly, meticulously expands to fill the cell, not to crush, but to intimately violate. It probes orifices with cold iron filaments, whispers recorded reprimands and sentencing in a hundred garbled voices, and attempts to 'reform' the victim by reshaping their flesh into a posture of perpetual, obedient confinement.", "origin": "A psychic parasite born from the collective fear of punishment and the sadistic satisfaction of those who wielded absolute authority within the prison walls. It is not the warden, but the abstract concept of his power made into a hungry, thinking tissue.", "horror": "The horror is of violation and inescapable authority. It is the cell itself becoming an active, intelligent torturer. Its invasion is slow, clinical, and inescapable, a physical manifestation of the terror that the system is not just corrupt, but is alive and hates you personally.", "encounter": "It begins as a slow drip of warm, metallic-smelling fluid from the vent, followed by the soft extrusion of the mass. The cell grows colder. The entity is relentless but slow-moving. Physical resistance is futile, as severed parts regenerate. The only hope is absolute, silent stillness, mimicking death, which may cause it to lose interest and retract—temporarily."}

instruction: Write a second-person horror scenario set in a hospital ward centered on paranoia
