input: A lone night shift nurse on an empty pediatric ward
output: You press your palm to the cold metal of the nurse’s station, overhead lights flickering like dying embers. The pediatric ward is supposed to be empty—your last patient left for a scan three hours ago, but you keep hearing soft, tiny whimpers from room 7B. When you peek inside, the crib is empty, but the blanket is pulled back as if someone just sat there. Glancing at the patient log, the discharge entry for that child is scribbled over, no new name filled in. The security feed shows only static, except a blurry yellow-gowned figure standing out of frame. A soft tug pulls at your lab coat from behind; you spin, empty, but the whimpers grow louder. You fumble for your walkie-talkie, and then the lights cut out completely.

instruction: Write a second-person horror scenario set in a hospital ward centered on paranoia
input: A post-surgery patient recovering on a quiet med-surg ward
output: You wake to the beep of your heart monitor, the ward dark except for a single night light. Your IV fluid bag is empty, even though you refilled it an hour ago. You call for a nurse, but no one answers. A few minutes later, you hear footsteps shuffling down the hall, getting closer. You pull the blanket up to your chin, and the footsteps stop right at your door. When you peek, no one is there, but the doorknob turns on its own. You check the room’s window—no one outside. The nurse’s call light button you pressed is now on the floor, cracked. You reach for the phone on your nightstand, but it’s dead, though you swear you saw the screen lit up with a nurse’s extension just seconds ago.

instruction: Write a second-person horror scenario set in a hospital ward centered on paranoia
input: A therapist working overnight in a locked psych ward
output: You’re sorting patient files in the locked psych ward’s office, the only sound the hum of the fridge. You swear you just moved file 14 to the top of the stack, but it’s now at the bottom, pages flipped open to a blank page. You hear a soft voice whispering your name from the ward’s common room—no patients are supposed to be awake this late. You check the common room, empty, but the door slams shut behind you, locking automatically. Your ID badge stops working when you hold it to the lock. On the wall, a handwritten note is taped that wasn’t there before: “They’re all listening. You’re next.” You fumble for your keys, and your hands shake so bad you drop them, the whispering growing louder.

instruction: Write a second-person horror scenario set in a hospital ward centered on paranoia
input: A visitor searching for their grandmother on a geriatric ward
output: You walk the geriatric ward’s hallways, looking for room 212 where your grandma is staying. The hallways repeat—turn left, you end up back at the same vending machine; turn right, same door. You check your phone, signal gone, even though the hospital has full service. An elderly woman in a white gown walks past, but when you call out, she just keeps walking, her feet making no sound on the linoleum. You find a room labeled 212, but inside the bed is empty, and the curtains pull back to show a blank wall, not a window. The nurse’s station says no one is registered in room 212, even though you saw her name on her wristband yesterday.

instruction: Write a second-person horror scenario set in a hospital ward centered on paranoia
input: A night shift janitor cleaning an emergency ward
output: You mop the emergency ward’s floors, the only sound your bucket sloshing. You left your cleaning cart at the end of the hall, but when you turn, it’s gone. You hear a cough from a closed room, a patient’s room that was supposed to be empty. You unlock the door with your master key, and the room is empty, but there’s a used tissue on the bed that wasn’t there before. Your radio crackles with a voice asking, “Where is the janitor?” but when you answer, no one responds. You check your pocket, and your master key is missing, replaced by a small plastic wristband marked “Patient.” You look down at your hands, and they’re covered in old, dark blood you didn’t notice before.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on the loss_of_control fear, set exclusively in a school gymnasium.
input: Scenario: You are mid pull-up during a solo PE fitness make-up test after all other students have left for the day.
output: You heave to get your chin over the scuffed metal pull-up bar, when your arms lock rigid entirely against your will. You can’t relax your grip, can’t shift your weight, lungs burning as you hang suspended, the bar digging deep half-moons into your palms. You scream for help, but your throat won’t open, no sound passes your lips. The empty bleachers creak, and you watch in terror as your free hand lifts of its own accord, shoves a deflated dodgeball into your mouth, then forces your body to lower itself stiffly from the bar. Your legs march you to the center of the wrestling mat, and you start doing jumping jacks faster and faster, no way to stop, even as your vision blurs with panic.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on the loss_of_control fear, set exclusively in a school gymnasium.
input: Scenario: You are the night janitor locking up after the homecoming dance, alone in the cavernous space.
output: You bend to pick up a crumpled streamer from the gym floor, when your knees lock, and your broom slips from your hand. You try to reach for your walkie talkie clipped to your belt, but your arms raise above your head of their own volition, fingers splayed. You watch, frozen, as your body starts to gyrate to a tinny, distorted pop track you can’t hear coming from any speaker. You can’t close your mouth, can’t stop your hips from moving faster, faster, sweat dripping down your back as you spin in slow circles across the polished wood. Your brain screams to stop, but your body doesn’t belong to you anymore, moving to a rhythm no one else can perceive.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on the loss_of_control fear, set exclusively in a school gymnasium.
input: Scenario: You are a cheerleader staying late to practice stunts alone before the regional competition.
output: You brace your feet to lift your imaginary flyer, when your core seizes up, and your arms shoot upward so fast you nearly topple. You don’t have a partner standing in front of you, but your hands curl around empty air like you’re holding a 120 pound girl above your head. You try to drop the imaginary weight, but your arms won’t budge, your calves burning as you hold the position, breath coming in ragged gasps. Then your body leans forward of its own accord, slamming the invisible flyer into the gym floor over and over, the thud you hear loud in your ears, even as you beg out loud for it to stop, no one there to hear you.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on the loss_of_control fear, set exclusively in a school gymnasium.
input: Scenario: You are sitting in the gym bleachers serving after-school detention, the only other person a sleeping administrator at the far desk.
output: You pick at a loose thread on your hoodie, when your legs swing out from under you of their own accord, and you stand before you can process the movement. You try to sit back down, but your feet carry you down the bleacher steps one by one, stiff and mechanical, until you’re standing at the free throw line holding a basketball you don’t remember picking up. Your arm lifts, shoots, sinks the shot, again and again, every time perfectly, even as your mind races. You can’t look away from the hoop, can’t stop shooting, the thud of the ball on the floor the only sound in the empty gym, as the administrator sleeps on, completely unaware.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on the loss_of_control fear, set exclusively in a school gymnasium.
input: Scenario: You are at a school all-night lock-in, playing a casual dodgeball game with a group of your friends.
output: You duck to avoid a flying dodgeball, when your body freezes mid-crouch, right in the middle of the court. Your friends yell to move, but you can’t will your muscles to respond, you can only watch as your own hand lifts, grabs the hardest, heaviest dodgeball on the floor, and hurls it directly at the head of your best friend. You scream an apology, but your body doesn’t stop, it keeps throwing ball after ball, hitting every other player one by one, even as tears stream down your face. Your friends stare at you in horror, and you can’t explain, can’t make your hands stop, your brain begging to drop the balls as they all run for the exit, leaving you alone on the court still throwing.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror story set in a server room
input: Setting: server room, fear: jumpscare
output: You kneel by rack 7, flashlight clamped between your teeth. The drive alarm has blared for 12 minutes; no one else answered the page. You pop the drive tray. It’s ice cold, no indicator light. Behind you, every server fan cuts out at once. Total silence. You freeze, breath fogging. You turn your head slow. Nothing. You laugh quiet, reach for the replacement drive. Then a hand slams hard against the inside of the sealed server chassis directly in front of your face, fist pounding once, twice, leaving bloody smears through the vent grates.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror story set in a server room
input: Setting: server room, fear: jumpscare
output: You’re counting serial tags at 2am, boots sticking to the damp raised floor. You passed out here once last month, woke up sore with no memory. You reach up to tag the top rack. Your wrist brushes something. Not cold metal. Warm skin. You yank back. The aisle is empty. You swallow, keep working. Three tags later, something breathes warm against the back of your neck. You spin. Nothing. You turn back to the rack. A pale face is pressed flush to the server grille one inch from yours, eyes black and wide, screaming silent.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror story set in a server room
input: Setting: server room, fear: jumpscare
output: You pull up the room’s internal cam feed on the terminal. The feed shows you, hunched over the keyboard. You notice a figure standing perfectly still behind the last rack, just at the edge of frame. You don’t look up. You scroll the timeline back ten minutes. It was there. Five minutes. Still there. One minute ago. Still there. Right now. Your blood turns to ice. You lift your eyes very slowly. Before you can focus, it slams into the terminal screen from the other side, fists cracking the glass, mouth stretched impossibly wide.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror story set in a server room
input: Setting: server room, fear: jumpscare
output: You’re tracing a dead patch cable under the raised floor. You lift a tile, set it aside. The cable runs back into the dark between rack legs. You yank it hard. It doesn’t give. You pull again, leaning your full weight back. It releases suddenly. You fall backwards. What comes out with the cable isn’t a RJ45 jack. It’s a cold human hand, fingers curled tight around the plastic, nails broken black. It yanks back hard before you can scream, dragging itself halfway out the dark gap under the racks, teeth bared.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror story set in a server room
input: Setting: server room, fear: jumpscare
output: The UPS dies. Every light cuts. You fumble for your phone. The screen flashes on. You’re surrounded by shadowed server towers, every status LED dead. You start walking for the door. You count your steps. You’ve walked twice the length of the room. No door. You stop. You hold the phone up. Directly in front of you, pressed nose to nose with you, is a woman you don’t recognise. She doesn’t blink. She doesn’t breathe. Right as you scream, she slams her forehead into yours so hard your phone shatters.

instruction: Generate a Junji Ito-inspired body horror shadow entity tied to a boiler room’s pressure safety systems
input: Type: shadow, Setting: industrial boiler room, Style: Junji Ito body horror, Tied to pressure relief valves
output: Appearance: A pinprick-fringed shadow that clings exclusively to the boiler’s red-capped pressure valves, its edges rippling with tiny, tooth-lined spiracles that pulse in sync with the boiler’s pressure gauge. Behavior: It mimics the shadow of a stuck valve stem until a worker leans in to inspect, then exudes a fine, iridescent dust from its spiracles. Origin: A 1978 maintenance worker who sealed himself inside a valve housing to plug a catastrophic leak, his shadow fusing with the valve’s pressure gradient as he boiled alive. Horror: The dust burrows into skin pores, forcing the victim’s flesh to pulse like an overpressurized boiler until their organs rupture in steam jets. Encounter: A night janitor checking a faulty valve in 2022 reached for the 'stem' shadow; 3 hours later, security found his torso bulging with superheated steam, his shadow fused to the valve.

instruction: Generate an SCP Foundation-class containment shadow entity tied to a boiler room’s core heat signature
input: Type: shadow, Setting: decommissioned nuclear-powered boiler room, Style: SCP containment anomaly, Tied to core thermal thresholds
output: Appearance: A pitch-black shadow confined to the 2-inch gap between the boiler’s firebrick core and steel casing, with no defined edges—its form absorbs infrared light instead of visible, rendering it invisible to thermal cameras until active. Behavior: It only manifests outside the gap when the core’s temperature drops below 1200°F (maintenance mode), crawling out to 'reclaim' heat sources (biological tissue) to restore core equilibrium. Origin: SCP-7842-B: The shadow of a 1993 nuclear physicist who dumped a reactor coolant sample into the boiler to hide misconduct, his atomic shadow bonding to the core’s heat gradient after he was incinerated by a core flash. Horror: It saps core body heat so rapidly that the victim’s blood crystallizes in their veins, leaving a frozen, thermally inert shadow where they stood. Encounter: MTF Epsilon-6 responding to a core temp drop in 2019; one agent stepped 6 inches from the casing, and his radio went dead—only a 6-foot frozen shadow with his ID badge fused to the steel remained.

instruction: Generate a psychological horror shadow entity in a boiler room, blending Silent Hill guilt themes and Lovecraftian non-Euclidean geometry
input: Type: shadow, Setting: abandoned orphanage boiler room, Style: Silent Hill psychological + Lovecraftian non-Euclidean, Tied to guilt over abandoned children
output: Appearance: A warping shadow that distorts the boiler’s pipe geometry, with tiny, flickering 'eyes' that are the shadow of chipped teacups (belonging to dead orphans) and no fixed shape—its edges fold into the boiler’s pipe bends like a non-Euclidean knot, defying 3D spatial logic. Behavior: It only manifests to people who have abandoned a vulnerable dependent, mimicking a child’s lullaby via rhythmic pipe clangs. Origin: A 1952 orphanage matron who locked 7 orphans in the boiler room to hide them from a state inspection; the boiler overheated, killing them, and her guilt-fueled shadow merged with the pipe network, gaining non-Euclidean properties from the collective terror’s psychic resonance (Lovecraftian). Horror: It forces the victim to relive the matron’s guilt while pipe shadows wrap around their ankles, slowly folding their body into the boiler’s non-Euclidean bends until they cease to exist in 3D space. Encounter: A social worker investigating the orphanage in 2021, who’d abandoned her own daughter 10 years prior, heard the lullaby; her body seemed to 'fold' into the pipes, leaving only her daughter’s locket wedged in a pipe joint.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a cathedral where the protagonist discovers a journal that predicts their every thought.
input: Protagonist is a volunteer cleaning the cathedral's forgotten crypt; finds a tattered journal hidden under a broken pew.
output: You run a rag over the crypt's dust-caked stone pews, your mind wandering to the fight you had with your sister last night—how you wished she’d just disappear. Your fingers brush something leathery: a tattered journal, its pages stuck together with age. You pry it open. The first entry is dated today. “You’re thinking about your sister,” it reads. Your blood runs cold. Flip to the next page: “You’re telling yourself this is a coincidence.” You slam the journal shut, but your eyes catch the last line you’d just thought: “Who would even know?” The journal’s spine creaks as if it’s laughing. When you look back, the next page is filled with your unspoken fears—your fear of being alone, of never fixing things with her. A draft whispers through the crypt, and the journal flips to a blank page. The ink starts to bleed on its own: “You’re going to stay here forever.”

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a cathedral during midnight mass where the priest’s sermon targets your hidden guilt.
input: Protagonist attends midnight mass to confess a past mistake; the priest’s sermon becomes unnervingly personal.
output: You huddle in the back pew of the cathedral, your hands clenched around the edge of the kneeler. You came here to confess the lie you told ten years ago—the one that ruined your best friend’s life. The priest steps to the pulpit, his eyes locking directly on you. “Some among us carry a secret so heavy it eats at their bones,” he says, his voice echoing off the stained glass. He lists every detail: the rainy night, the stolen necklace, the way you let your friend take the blame. Your breath catches. No one else knows this. The congregation turns to stare, their faces blurring into identical, judgmental masks. The priest leans forward, whispering now, as if only you can hear: “You thought coming here would absolve you. But the walls remember. They’ve been waiting for you to admit it.” Your vision swims, and the cathedral’s vaulted ceiling seems to press down, trapping you with the truth you can’t escape.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a cathedral where the protagonist’s reflection in a stained glass window shows a dead alternate version of themselves.
input: Protagonist is a conservator restoring a 19th-century stained glass window; notices their reflection in the glass is wrong.
output: You lean into the scaffolding, carefully scraping grime from the stained glass window of the cathedral’s north transept. The glass depicts a martyr, but when you glance at your reflection, it’s not you staring back. The figure in the glass has a gash across its throat, eyes hollow, wearing the same coveralls you have—but its chest is stained with dark, dried blood. You blink, and the reflection is normal again. You shake it off, reaching for a brush. When you look up, the reflection is back, mouthing words you can’t hear. You step back, your heart pounding. The window’s colors shift, the martyr’s face melting into your own dead one. A voice whispers in your ear, soft as glass: “You were supposed to die that night. The car crash. We took your place.” You stumble, knocking over a bucket of solvent. The reflection grins, and you realize the gash on its throat is exactly where the seatbelt cut you that night—before you survived. The cathedral’s bells toll, and the reflection presses its hand against the glass, as if trying to break through to you.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a cathedral during a thunderstorm where the statues seem to watch and repeat your unspoken regrets.
input: Protagonist takes shelter in a cathedral during a violent thunderstorm; notices the stone statues are following their movements.
output: You burst into the cathedral, soaked to the bone from the thunderstorm outside. You collapse onto a pew, gasping, your mind replaying the words you never said to your dying mother. A flash of lightning illuminates the stone statues lining the nave—their heads are turned toward you. You rub your eyes, thinking it’s a trick of the light. Another flash: the statue of the Virgin Mary has shifted, her gaze fixed on your face. A low rumble of thunder shakes the walls, and you hear a voice, soft and familiar, coming from the statue: “You never told her you loved her.” Your blood runs cold. You stand, backing away, and the other statues turn too—each one mouthing your unspoken regrets: “You left your brother when he needed you.” “You lied about your dream to make everyone happy.” The storm rages outside, but inside, the air is still, thick with the weight of your secrets. You try to run for the door, but the statues’ stone fingers seem to stretch toward you, their whispers growing louder, until they’re all you can hear.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a cathedral where the confessional booth torments the protagonist with their repressed sins.
input: Protagonist is a priest doing late-night prayers; the confessional booth starts calling their name, revealing their repressed mistakes.
output: You kneel at the altar, reciting the rosary, your mind wandering to the sin you buried years ago—how you abandoned a vulnerable parishioner when they needed you most. The confessional booth at the back of the cathedral creaks open, and a voice calls your name, soft but insistent. You ignore it, but the voice grows louder, repeating the parishioner’s name: “Lila. You left her.” You stand, your hands trembling, and walk toward the booth. When you peer inside, it’s empty—but the air smells like Lila’s lavender perfume. “You told her you’d help,” the voice says, echoing from the booth’s wooden walls. “But you ran. Because you were scared.” Your knees buckle. The booth’s door slams shut, trapping you inside. The walls close in, and you see flashes of that night—Lila’s tear-streaked face, the way you turned and ran. The voice whispers, “You’re not a priest. You’re a coward.” You pound on the door, but it doesn’t budge. The cathedral is silent, except for the sound of your own sobs, and the voice repeating your shame over and over.

instruction: Describe your first encounter with the whispering shadows in the catacombs.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: catacomb.
output: Your torch gutters, plunging you into a blackness so absolute it feels like a physical shroud. The catacomb's chill seeps into your bones. Then, you hear them—not echoes, but whispers. They coil from the darkness just beyond your extinct light, murmuring in a language of dry earth and forgotten names. They speak of the weight above you, of the miles of stone, of how your own breath is the only sound you'll ever make again. A faint, cold pressure brushes your cheek, like fingertips made of void. The whispers solidify into a single, clear sentence in your mind: 'We have been waiting in the quiet. Join us in the quiet.' You fumble for a match, but your fingers are numb. The darkness isn't empty. It's patient.

instruction: Narrate the moment your light fails while mapping the catacombs.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: catacomb.
output: The headlamp battery dies with a final, feeble flicker. The map in your hands becomes a useless rectangle of deeper black. The remembered path behind you is swallowed. You are adrift in a silent, starless ocean of stone. Then, the scraping begins—a slow, deliberate drag against the rough wall to your left. You freeze. It stops. A moment later, it answers from the right, closer. It's learning your position by sound. You dare not breathe. The scrape comes again, directly ahead now, accompanied by a low, wet inhalation. Something is in here with you, something that doesn't need light. It is sharing the dark.

instruction: Describe being lost in a catacomb where the darkness seems to thicken.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: catacomb.
output: You've taken three wrong turns. The darkness here is different—it clings, a viscous, cool oil that slows your movements and muffles your panicked heartbeat. Your flashlight beam, once strong, now struggles to pierce more than a foot ahead, ending in a dull, swallowed glow. The walls, you swear, are closer. The rough-hewn niches where bones should rest are empty, as if their occupants quietly slipped out into this concentrated dark. A soft, rhythmic sound reaches you: a drip. But there is no water here. It is the sound of the darkness itself, condensing on the ceiling, gathering to drown you.

instruction: Tell of reaching a dead end as your last candle dies.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: catacomb.
output: The passage ends in a rough wall of crumbling brick. As you turn, the candle flame on your makeshift holder wavers, blue at its core, then winks out. The scent of hot wax is instantly overpowered by the smell of cold, stale age. In the sudden black, you hear the bricks behind you shifting. Not a collapse—a methodical, grinding disassembly. Something is taking the wall apart, stone by stone, opening the path you just sealed. No light escapes from there either. It is a darkness digging its way toward you, silent save for the gritty slide of masonry. There is nowhere to go but into it.

instruction: Describe the horror of realizing the catacomb's darkness has a tangible form.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: catacomb.
output: You feel it before you see it—a cold, fibrous strand brushing your neck. You flash your light upward. Where the tunnel ceiling should be, there is a seething, matted tapestry of absolute black, like solidified shadow. It drips slowly, forming tendrils that sway in a nonexistent breeze. Your light doesn't illuminate it; it absorbs the beam, drinking the photons. One tendril, thicker than the rest, detaches and descends with silent purpose. It doesn't move through the air—it extends the darkness itself, a spear of void aimed at your chest. To run is to plunge deeper into its body.

instruction: Generate a second-person horror narrative set in a train station centered on isolation.
input: Abandoned late-night train station, empty platforms, no cell service
output: You stumble into the dusty train station at 2:12 a.m., your running shoes clicking on cracked tile. The last employee locked the doors 10 minutes prior, but you tripped over a loose tile and got trapped inside. Vending machines hum on their own, but no one answers your shouts. Your phone died an hour ago, so you can’t call for help. The platform is empty—no benches, no posters, just tracks stretching into the dark. You realize no one knows you’re here, stuck alone in a station that doesn’t belong to anyone.

instruction: Write a second-person horror scene set in a train station focused on isolation.
input: Canceled late train, station clears out, exit locked
output: You wait on the downtown train platform, your scheduled train canceled due to track repairs. The last passenger left 15 minutes ago, the ticket agent locked their booth and left. You try the exit door—it’s bolted shut. You call transit assistance, but the line is dead. A distant train whistle echoes, but no train comes. You sit on cold concrete as lights flicker, realizing you’re alone here, no one is looking for you, sealed tight in this empty station.

instruction: Create a second-person horror story set in a train station with isolation as the core fear.
input: Snowstorm, automatic door lock, clock frozen
output: You duck into the small suburban train station to avoid a blizzard, planning to catch the 10:00 a.m. commuter. The doors lock automatically behind you, and the wall clock freezes at 9:59. Your watch says 10:00, but the board shows no trains for three hours. You call for help, your voice bouncing off empty walls with no reply. Wind howls through vents, and the platform is empty. You’re isolated here, stuck between a storm outside and an unstaffed station inside, no one knows you’re trapped.

instruction: Draft a second-person horror narrative set in a train station centered on isolation.
input: Friend boarded wrong train, station gates lock, no cell service
output: You waited at Central Station for your best friend, who ran to grab a pastry before their 9:00 train. When you checked your phone, they texted saying they boarded the wrong train and were gone. The station cleared out 20 minutes later, the exit gate slamming shut. Your phone has no service here, so you can’t reach anyone. The PA system is silent, no trains arrive. You’re alone now, stranded in a station that was once full of people, just you and dark empty tracks.

instruction: Write a second-person horror scene set in a train station with isolation as the primary fear.
input: Overnight station shift, last train departs, no manager contact
output: You work the overnight shift at the rural train station, the only employee on duty. At 1:00 a.m., the last freight train pulls out, and the gates slide closed with a clank. You call the station manager to check in, but their line goes straight to voicemail. The station lights dim, and cameras flicker off suddenly. You walk the empty platform—no stragglers, no staff. You realize you’re alone here, cut off from the world, no one will come for hours, if ever, trapped in the quiet empty station.

instruction: Generate a unique geometric horror entity set in a remote overgrown 19th century cemetery, aligned with SCP cosmic horror and Junji Ito body horror aesthetics
input: Entity Codename: Edge of the Crossroads
output: Appearance: A 4ft tall unmarked matte black equilateral triangular prism half-buried at the intersection of the cemetery’s four main footpaths, its edges sharp enough to slice falling rain mid-fall, no visible seams or texture. Behavior: It rotates exactly 1 degree clockwise at midnight each night, and one new unmarked grave appears within its 10ft radius after each rotation. Origin: It was unearthed during a 1897 cemetery expansion, no historical records note its placement, and the priest who attempted to exorcise it was found folded into a flat 2D triangle sealed inside a nearby casket days later. Horror: Staring at it for longer than 10 seconds triggers gradual bone rearrangement, warping the viewer’s skeleton into jagged non-Euclidean angles that erase all depth perception permanently. Encounter: A lost hiker stumbled on it after dark, reporting overlapping triangular shadows stretching across all headstones, and fled when he noticed his own shadow had three disjointed right angles.

instruction: Generate a unique geometric horror entity set in a remote overgrown 19th century cemetery, aligned with Silent Hill spatial horror and Lovecraftian cosmic geometry aesthetics
input: Entity Codename: Grave Lattice Octahedron
output: Appearance: A perfect, 6ft wide regular octahedron suspended 3ft above a desecrated infant grave, formed by thin glowing silver wire strung between six randomly selected headstones, its faces are completely empty, no light passes through its frame. Behavior: It shifts position every time a bird lands on a nearby marker, restringing itself between new headstones without breaking its wires, emitting a low hum only detectable by canines and people with tinnitus. Origin: It spontaneously manifested in 1978 after six teen geometry students died in a car crash on the cemetery access road, buried in a shared unmarked plot after no next of kin claimed their bodies. Horror: Anyone who passes through its empty central frame is trapped in an infinite looping pocket dimension of the cemetery, every turn revealing new headstones carved with their full name and date of death. Encounter: A lost hunter was found by groundskeepers three days after he wandered into the plot, half-starved, screaming he had walked more than 40 miles across the 1 acre cemetery grounds.

instruction: Generate a unique geometric horror entity set in a remote overgrown 19th century cemetery, aligned with SCP anomalous object and Junji Ito uncanny entity aesthetics
input: Entity Codename: Dodecahedron Sexton
output: Appearance: A waist-high smooth weathered grey dodecahedron, each of its 12 faces etched with a unique unmarked grave symbol, no two carvings identical, it moves by slowly rolling between headstones without ever knocking over a single marker. Behavior: It waits at the cemetery entrance on every full moon, offering visitors a perfectly uniform round river stone plucked from its top face. Origin: It is the collective physical manifestation of the cemetery’s 172 unclaimed dead, each face corresponding to a body that was buried without a name or headstone over the cemetery’s 160 year history. Horror: Anyone who accepts the stone wakes up buried alive in an unmarked grave the next morning, anyone who refuses is followed home by the entity, which folds their home’s walls into flat geometric planes until no space remains for them to exist. Encounter: A paranormal investigator filmed the entity rolling away from a fresh grave in 2021, and every photo from his camera developed as flat 2D polygons with no discernible features.

instruction: Write a second-person paranoia horror story set at a carnival
input: Fear type: paranoia. Setting: carnival. Second person narration.
output: You glance over your shoulder for the seventh time. Every cotton candy vendor has the same cracked smile. Every child waving a balloon stares directly at you, unblinking. You thought you lost the man in the checkered coat three rides back, but he’s always one booth over, never moving, never buying anything. The carousel music warps just slightly when you look away. No one else seems to notice the calliope is looping the same 12 bars on repeat. When you stop to catch your breath, every person within twenty feet freezes mid-step. You dart for the exit. It’s gone. There was never an exit. Only more booths, more smiles, more eyes that track every twitch of your hands.

instruction: Write a second-person paranoia horror story set at a carnival
input: Fear type: paranoia. Setting: carnival. Second person narration.
output: You won the stuffed fox at the ring toss ten minutes ago. At first you thought you imagined its head turning. Now you notice every prize on every game stall tracks you as you walk. You drop the fox in a trash can. Three booths later, a child holds it out to you, no expression. You run. The fun house mirrors don’t show your reflection—they show you ten feet behind, standing still, watching yourself run. No one bumps into you. The crowd parts perfectly every time, like they’ve been waiting for you to panic. By the time you hear your own name whispered over the speaker system, you can’t remember why you came here at all.

instruction: Write a second-person paranoia horror story set at a carnival
input: Fear type: paranoia. Setting: carnival. Second person narration.
output: The ferris wheel creaks to a stop with you at the very top. The operator said it would be thirty seconds. It’s been twelve minutes. Down below, every single person on the grounds has stopped what they were doing. They’re all looking up. Not at the wheel. At you. No one waves. No one yells. They just stand, necks craned, faces blank. You yell for help. One person tilts their head. Then another. Then all two hundred of them tilt their heads in perfect unison. You press back against the seat. You swear you can feel thousands of eyes crawling across your skin, even the ones that should be blocked by tents and ride walls.

instruction: Write a second-person paranoia horror story set at a carnival
input: Fear type: paranoia. Setting: carnival. Second person narration.
output: You keep hearing your own laugh echo through the midway. You haven’t laughed since you arrived. Every time you pass a group of people, they fall silent mid-conversation until you’re past. The popcorn vendor hands you a bag before you reach him. You didn’t order anything. When you check your pocket, you find three ticket stubs for rides you don’t remember riding. Each has your name scrawled on the back in your handwriting. You don’t remember writing them. The clock above the entrance has been stuck at 9:17 for an hour. Or maybe it’s been three hours. You can’t tell anymore. No one else is checking the time.

instruction: Write a second-person paranoia horror story set at a carnival
input: Fear type: paranoia. Setting: carnival. Second person narration.
output: You followed the sound of a crying child behind the cotton candy tent. There’s no child. Just the sound, looping, coming from everywhere and nowhere. When you turn back to the midway, all the faces have blurred. No one has eyes, no one has mouths, just smooth pale ovals bobbing through the crowd. You don’t scream. You walk slowly, shoulders hunched, trying not to stand out. You can feel them noticing you anyway. Every step makes the silence grow thicker. You know if you run, they’ll all stop. You know if you stop, they’ll turn. And you will not like what happens when they turn.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration with a jumpscare set in a vintage hotel lobby, focusing on a mysterious covered object at the concierge desk.
input: You’re alone in a dimly lit 1920s hotel lobby at 2 AM, waiting for a delayed taxi. The only light comes from a flickering desk lamp behind the concierge counter, where a sheet-draped object sits untouched.
output: You shift your weight, the leather of your jacket creaking against the cold marble floor. The taxi app says it’s still 10 minutes away, so you drift toward the counter, curiosity pricking at you. The sheet is dusty, its edges frayed. You reach out, fingers brushing the fabric—then it moves. A low, gurgling sound rumbles from beneath it. You yank your hand back, heart hammering. The sheet slips off to reveal a concierge, his face bloated and blue, eyes milky white, clutching a rusted key. He lunges forward, mouth agape in a silent scream, and his cold, clammy hand wraps around your wrist. You stumble back, tripping over a chair, as he slams against the counter, his skull cracking with a wet crunch.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration with a jumpscare set in a modern hotel lobby, focusing on a dropped room key near a potted plant.
input: You’re rushing to catch an early morning shuttle, and you drop your room key near a large potted fern in the corner of the hotel lobby.
output: You kneel, squinting in the soft overhead light to spot the key under the fern’s fronds. The lobby is empty save for the hum of the AC. Your fingers brush against damp soil, then something else—cold, clammy, and curled. You freeze, thinking it’s a root, but it wraps around your wrist. You yank back, but it holds tight. A low, hissing sound echoes from the plant. Suddenly, a gaunt figure bursts out from behind the fern, its face half-eaten, eyes glowing red. It lunges at you, mouth full of sharp, yellow teeth, and you fall backward, hitting your head on the marble floor. The figure looms over you, its breath reeking of rot, before the shuttle driver’s shout snaps you back to reality—until you see the figure’s hand still clamped around your ankle.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration with a jumpscare set in a cozy hotel lobby, focusing on a fogged mirror above the fireplace.
input: It’s a rainy winter night, and you’re sitting by the fireplace in a rustic hotel lobby, drying your wet boots. The mirror above the mantel fogs up from the heat.
output: You stare at the fogged mirror, drawing a heart with your finger. The rain taps against the windows, and the fire crackles softly. Suddenly, the fog clears in a patch, revealing a face you don’t recognize—pale, with sunken eyes and a slit throat. You blink, thinking it’s a trick of the light, but the face moves, pressing closer to the mirror. The glass cracks, spiderwebbing outward. You jump up, knocking over your mug of hot cocoa. Then, the mirror shatters, and a figure crawls through, its body covered in glass shards. It lunges at you, screaming, and you dive behind the couch. The figure slams into the mantel, sending logs tumbling into the carpet, and you scramble for the exit, its cold breath on your neck.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration with a jumpscare set in an old hotel lobby, focusing on a vintage payphone.
input: You’re in a run-down 1970s hotel lobby, using a vintage payphone to call your friend because your phone died. The line cuts to static halfway through your sentence.
output: You tap the receiver against the phone, muttering curses. The static grows louder, turning into a high-pitched whine that makes your ears ring. Suddenly, the receiver is yanked out of your hand, slamming against the wall. You step back, heart racing. The payphone’s door creaks open, and a figure steps out—wearing a tattered bellhop uniform, its face covered in blood. It reaches for you, its fingers long and bony, and you turn to run. But your foot catches on the phone’s cord, and you fall. The figure looms over you, raising a rusted knife, and you scream—until the lobby’s flickering lights go out. When they come back on, the figure is gone, but the payphone’s receiver is still dangling, and there’s a handprint of blood on the wall next to you.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration with a jumpscare set in a luxury hotel lobby, focusing on a velvet couch where a hidden figure lurks.
input: You’re waiting for your friend in a sleek luxury hotel lobby, sitting on a plush velvet couch. The lobby is quiet, with only the soft sound of a piano playing in the background.
output: You scroll through your phone, checking the time. Your friend is 20 minutes late. Suddenly, the couch cushion next to you sinks, as if someone sat down. You look up, but no one is there. You shrug it off, thinking it’s the couch settling—until a cold hand touches your shoulder. You jump, spinning around. A figure is sitting next to you, its skin gray and tight against its bones, eyes empty sockets. It opens its mouth, letting out a silent scream, and lunges at you. You scramble off the couch, knocking over a side table with a vase. The figure chases you, its feet making no sound on the marble floor. You reach the elevator, slamming the button, and it opens just as the figure grabs your coat. You yank free, diving inside, and the doors close—leaving the figure’s clawed hand scraping against the glass.

instruction: Create a detailed profile for a Lovecraftian horror entity encountered in a restaurant kitchen.
input: Entity Type: lovecraftian, Setting: restaurant kitchen
output:
**Appearance:** The 'Sink Scourge' manifests as a pulsating, translucent mass of congealed grease, food scraps, and sentient drain hair. It clings to the dark, wet pipes beneath sinks, its form studded with unblinking, lidless eyes that reflect the kitchen's fluorescent lights. Occasionally, a malformed, toothless mouth gapes open, dripping a viscous, grey slime.
**Behavior:** It is passive until nightfall or when the kitchen is empty. It then slowly extrudes tendrils up drainpipes, seeking warmth and moisture. It 'feeds' not on flesh, but on memories and emotions, particularly the stress, fear, and quiet desperation of the kitchen staff, which it absorbs through physical contact with its slime.
**Origin:** Born from decades of culinary misery, repeated chemical abuse from harsh cleaners, and the psychic residue of countless discarded meals prepared in anger or sorrow. It is a coalescence of the kitchen's forgotten shame.
**Horror:** Victims experience a sudden, chilling emptiness and a loss of specific memories—the face of a loved one, the taste of a favorite childhood food. Prolonged exposure leads to severe apathy and a haunting sense that one's very essence is being slowly siphoned down the drain.
**Encounter:** A dishwasher stays late, hearing soft, wet slithering from the deep sink. Reaching to clear a clog, their hand is enveloped in a sudden, gelid grasp. They pull back, physically unharmed, but cannot remember their own mother's name.

instruction: Create a detailed profile for a Lovecraftian horror entity encountered in a restaurant kitchen.
input: Entity Type: lovecraftian, Setting: restaurant kitchen
output:
**Appearance:** 'The Butcher's Hymn' is not a creature, but a sentient, harmonic resonance trapped within a large walk-in freezer's humming compressor. It has no physical form, but its presence warps the frost on the walls into intricate, non-Euclidean patterns that subtly shift when observed. The air within vibrates at a frequency just below hearing.
**Behavior:** It 'sings' a constant, low drone that harmonizes with the machinery. This hymn slowly alters the perception of time and space for those inside. Minutes feel like hours, and the freezer's interior appears to stretch into impossible, lightless geometries. It seeks to incorporate living minds into its eternal chorus.
**Origin:** A fragment of an alien frequency, drawn to and captured by the industrial cold and constant mechanical rhythm. It fused with the freezer's fundamental operation during a city-wide power outage that created a moment of silent, receptive null.
**Horror:** The true terror is psychological dissolution. Prolonged exposure causes victims to lose their sense of self, believing they have always been part of the machine's endless, frozen song. They may be found catatonic, their breath synced to the compressor's cycle, whispering mathematical formulae describing the cold.
**Encounter:** A line cook enters for stock. The door seals shut. The familiar hum deepens, becoming a layered chorus. The shelves of meat recede into darkness. He stares at the frost patterns, now depicting his own face in infinite regression, as the cold begins to feel like a welcoming embrace.

instruction: Create a detailed profile for a Lovecraftian horror entity encountered in a restaurant kitchen.
input: Entity Type: lovecraftian, Setting: restaurant kitchen
output:
**Appearance:** The 'Familiar's Feast' appears as a perfectly normal, plump roast chicken or other succulent dish left under a cloche on the pass. Its horror reveals itself only upon carving: the meat inside is not poultry or beef, but a perfect anatomical replica of the carver's own internal organs, still warm and pulsating, arranged as if on a surgical tray.
**Behavior:** It is a patient entity, manifesting only when a chef is alone and consumed by profound self-doubt or creative block. It does not move or attack. Its sole purpose is to be recognized and understood. It emits a faint, savory aroma that is deeply, personally nostalgic to the beholder.
**Origin:** A psychic abscess formed from a chef's repressed impostor syndrome and the latent terror of serving one's essence to critics. It is a literal manifestation of the phrase "putting your guts into your work," made flesh through unknowable cosmic irony.
**Horror:** The horror is one of intimate violation and grotesque self-reflection. It confronts the victim with the literal consumption of self, blurring the line between creator and creation, artist and ingredient. It induces a permanent, nauseating wariness of one's own culinary output.
**Encounter:** After a devastating review, the head chef remains, despondent. She notices a covered dish she doesn't recall plating. Lifting the cloche reveals a beautiful roast. Slicing into it, she sees her own heart, valves slowly contracting, nestled between ribs of herbed stuffing.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in a bank vault
input: Night janitor locked in after closing, forgotten post-shift
output: You jolt awake, chest pressed to cold steel: the vault door slammed shut ten minutes ago—you’re the night janitor, left after a last-minute safe check. Your breath fogs the dark, then you hear it: not wind, but a woman’s voice, repeating the loan terms you denied three years back, the one that let her kid lose their critical hospital stay. The voice grows louder, and when you rub your eyes, you see her in your peripheral, her hands clutching empty folders. You grip the lock, your palms sweating, and realize the numbers you’re muttering to dial the emergency combo are the exact ones on the denied loan form—you can’t stop saying them, can’t remember why you even came here tonight.

instruction: Create a second-person psychological horror piece set in a locked bank vault
input: Bank executive hiding embezzled funds after hours, trapped inside
output: The vault’s fluorescent hum is the only sound as you press your palm to the wall, counting stacks of embezzled cash you stashed here this morning. You think you’re alone until you hear a soft lock click—then a figure steps from shadow, wearing your exact suit, holding a receipt for the very account you siphoned from. Another follows, then more: duplicates of you, all with guilty grins. You back up, tripping over cash, and their mouths move in unison: You can never leave. You reach for your phone, only to find it’s in the hand of the closest duplicate—your own face twisted with cold terror, already knowing you’ll rot here.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in an abandoned bank vault
input: Teenager who snuck into a closed bank to prank, locked inside the vault
output: You snicker, shining your phone flashlight across the vault’s dust-covered shelves—you and your friends snuck in to hide their phones, but the door slammed shut, the keypad glitching so no code works. Dust shifts, and you hear Lila’s voice, faint then clear: I’m scared of the dark. You freeze—Lila died last year here, when you forgot she was hiding during hide and seek, left her to suffocate. Shelves rattle, and she sits on a crate, same hoodie, empty eyes. You yell, but no sound comes out—you still left her here, all those months ago, and now she’s not going anywhere.

instruction: Craft a second-person psychological horror piece set in a bank vault
input: Night security guard trapped inside the vault during a routine patrol
output: You tap your wrist, frustrated—your radio is gone, your flashlight flickers, and the vault door sealed when you reached for a spare battery. A soft thud echoes, and you glance at the shadowed old records shelf. A man in a 1990s security uniform stands there, chest bleeding from a wound. He says you skipped the third aisle check, where he died in 1998, locked in by a robber. The figure steps closer, and you realize his face is your own—old, tired, the same fear you feel now. He grabs your arm: You forgot me once, now you’ll be here forever, too.

instruction: Generate a second-person psychological horror narration set in a bank vault
input: Pair of siblings who broke into a bank vault for a hidden family heirloom, trapped inside
output: You and your sibling huddle together, backpack empty—you broke in last night to get your late mom’s locket, stashed here decades ago, but the door sealed when you grabbed the crate. Your sibling suddenly shoves you, yelling about the way you bailed on covering their DUI last year. You snap back, then see her: standing in the dark, face twisted with rage, holding the locket. She isn’t your sister—she’s the version of you that let her down. The locket clatters, and your own hand clamps your wrist: You can’t have it. You don’t deserve to make amends.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on a fear of darkness, set inside a public planetarium, 100-250 words, no filler.
input: You are the last remaining visitor after a private evening planetarium show when the building’s power cuts out without warning.
output: You fumble for your phone flashlight first, thumb slipping over your cracked case when you realize the room went far darker than any standard power outage. The projected constellations flicker out mid-orbit, no exit sign glow, no hum of the projector you could hear 10 seconds prior. You wave your hand in front of your face and can’t see it, not even a faint shadow. Something warm brushes the back of your neck, and you hear a soft, breathy hum that doesn’t match any of the show’s audio. You step forward and your shoe scuffs not on carpet, but on a crumpled star-patterned cape no one was wearing when you sat down. The dark presses in, thick as wool, and you swear you can feel it breathing.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on a fear of darkness, set inside a public planetarium, 100-250 words, no filler.
input: You are a night shift planetarium intern running overnight projector calibration when the dome’s hydraulic seals engage unexpectedly.
output: You’d joked earlier about the old building’s janky door locks, but the second the dome whirs closed, every overhead backup light dies instantly. You’d left the emergency exit propped open two minutes prior, you swear, but now you can’t even sense the direction of the wall. The darkness isn’t just absence of light—it’s tangible, eating the faint glow of your smart watch screen before you can even read the time. You call out, and your voice doesn’t echo, it gets swallowed whole, like the dark is drinking it. You feel the wall for the door, and your fingers meet not cold metal, but soft, squelching, warm material that pulses under your touch. You yank your hand back, and the dark laughs, low and quiet, right next to your ear.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on a fear of darkness, set inside a public planetarium, 100-250 words, no filler.
input: You paid for a limited-run immersive dark sky planetarium experience, marketed as a fully unlit sensory show mimicking deep space.
output: The host warned you the show would eliminate all artificial light to mimic interstellar space, but you didn’t expect the dark to cling to your skin like damp mold 10 minutes in. The other attendees went quiet one by one, no rustle of jackets, no huffed breaths beside you. You reach for the person sitting to your left, and your hand closes around cold, dry flesh that doesn’t feel like a living human. You yank back, and something wraps around your wrist—thin, sinewy, impossibly strong. The dark doesn’t just hide things, you realize, it grows them. You can hear dozens of quiet, scraping sounds all around you, moving closer, and the pressure on your chest gets so tight you can barely breathe.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on a fear of darkness, set inside a public planetarium, 100-250 words, no filler.
input: You snuck into a long-shuttered 1970s planetarium for urban exploration photos when your camera dies without warning.
output: You thought the only darkness would come from the boarded up windows, but the second your camera dies, every bit of residual light fizzles out like it was never there. You fumble for your headlamp, and the batteries fall out of the casing the second you touch it, clattering into the void where you can’t hear them hit the floor. The dark is cold, colder than the unheated building should be, and it seeps through your jacket to settle in your bones. You remember you left the entrance propped open 30 seconds ago, but when you turn toward where it should be, you can’t sense any opening, any draft from outside. Something brushes your ankle, and you hear a faint, child’s voice whisper “you shouldn’t have come” from somewhere deep in the black.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on a fear of darkness, set inside a public planetarium, 100-250 words, no filler.
input: You are at a planetarium meteor shower watch party when a sudden thunderstorm knocks out all grid power to the building.
output: The party chatter dies immediately when the projectors cut out, everyone laughing off the outage for the first 10 seconds—until the emergency exit lights don’t kick on, and the room goes so dark you can’t tell if your eyes are open or closed. You reach for the hand of the person you came with, and your fingers close around empty air, even though they were sitting shoulder to shoulder with you a second prior. No one responds when you call their name, no voices, no movement, just the thick, heavy quiet of the dark pressing in on all sides. You feel a pressure on your forehead, like someone is leaning in to look at you, and you realize the dark isn’t just empty—it’s full, full of things that have been waiting for the lights to go out.

instruction: Write a second-person horror story about isolation set in a greenhouse
input: Setting: commercial produce greenhouse. Fear: isolation
output: You clocked in alone for night maintenance an hour ago. The thick plastic walls muffle every external sound—no road hum, no crickets, nothing. You followed a draft to find the rear emergency door bolted from the outside. Your phone died ten minutes later. Every row of tomato vines sways identically, no breeze to cause it. You call out. No echo. Even your voice dies mid-air, like the greenhouse is swallowing it. No one will check on you for twelve hours. No one even confirmed you showed up tonight.

instruction: Write a second-person horror story about isolation set in a greenhouse
input: Setting: university research greenhouse. Fear: isolation
output: You stayed late to run a final sample on the rare orchid. The lab tech locked up without noticing you in the back humid chamber. Frosted glass means no one can see you wave. The specimens emit a sickly sweet scent that makes your head heavy. You’ve banged every pane for an hour. No one passes. Campus is empty for spring break. No one will return for nine days. The orchids’ petals unfurl slow right in front of you, like they’re watching you realize no one is coming.

instruction: Write a second-person horror story about isolation set in a greenhouse
input: Setting: abandoned family greenhouse. Fear: isolation
output: You came out to clear grandma’s greenhouse after the funeral. The rusted door latched shut behind you before you could turn. No cell service reaches this spot, three miles from the nearest house. Overgrown ivy crawled over every window while you weren’t looking, blocking all light. You hear things scuttling under dead potting soil but don’t dare reach. Every yell only gets a distant wind whine in reply. No one knew you came here. No one is looking.

instruction: Write a second-person horror story about isolation set in a greenhouse
input: Setting: remote winter roadside greenhouse. Fear: isolation
output: The blizzard hit fast. You ducked into the greenhouse to shelter. Snow piled against the doors an hour ago, sealing you in. It’s quiet enough to hear your own heartbeat. Frost creeps across the inside glass, painting every window solid white. You can’t see out. No one can see in. The highway was already closed. No rescue crews are coming. Every time you press your palm to the glass, the frost grows back faster, like it’s hiding you from the world entirely.

instruction: Write a second-person horror story about isolation set in a greenhouse
input: Setting: industrial hydroponic greenhouse. Fear: isolation
output: Power cut out halfway through your overnight shift. All pumps went silent. You’re surrounded by endless identical rows of floating greens, stretching into total blackness past your dying flashlight beam. Every exit’s magnetic lock failed shut when the grid died. Dispatch isn’t answering your radio. There are 17 acres of greenhouse between you and the nearest human. No one will notice you’re missing until harvest at dawn. That’s seven hours alone in the dark, with damp rot smell creeping closer.

instruction: Analyze the anomalous shadow entity tied to a blighted tomato row in an abandoned commercial greenhouse, documenting its physical traits, behavioral triggers, and existential horror implications.
input: Setting: Abandoned 1980s commercial greenhouse, blighted heirloom tomato row, midday overcast (no direct sunlight). Entity Type: Shadow.
output: Appearance: A 5’8” humanoid shadow with no casting object—its edges fray into vine-tendril fingers, and hollow, mildew-streaked eye pits gape where a face should be. Behavior: Dormant on soil until someone touches a blighted tomato; then drags its tendrils along the ground, leaving black mold blooms that kill nearby grass. Origin: 1927 gardener Elias Voss buried his blight-stricken wife, Clara, under the row, begging the plants to “keep her close”—the curse bound her shadow to the blight. Horror: If a tendril brushes skin, the victim’s shadow develops blighted black spots that rot their flesh from the inside out, with no known cure. Encounter: A 2021 urban explorer touched a tomato; the shadow crept 10 feet before she fled, but a black spot on her left hand’s shadow grew into a flesh-eating ulcer three days later.

instruction: Document the predatory shadow entity localized to a luxury residential greenhouse’s carnivorous plant display, prioritizing survival cues and encounter outcomes.
input: Setting: Gated 2020 luxury residential greenhouse, carnivorous plant wing (empty rare venus flytrap shelf), total blackout (no ambient light). Entity Type: Shadow.
output: Appearance: A 3ft-tall, upright venus flytrap shadow with no physical plant to cast it—its jaw-lobes are lined with needle-thin shadow spines, and its coiled stem wraps around a dented metal shelf. Behavior: Triggers when a victim exhales within 6 inches of the empty shelf; lunges with lobes snapping, retreats instantly if exposed to 450nm blue light (e.g., phone flash). Origin: 2019 botanist Elara Morn fed her own venous blood to a critically endangered venus flytrap to “save it”; when the plant died, her obsessive essence merged with its shadow. Horror: If caught, the shadow severs a finger (which vanishes entirely) and implants a predatory urge—victims crave raw meat and snap at small animals. Encounter: A 2023 date snuck into the greenhouse, exhaled near the shelf; he activated his phone’s blue light mid-lunge, but his left thumb was gone, and he later attacked a neighbor’s cat.

instruction: Evaluate the anomalous shadow swarm entity occupying a municipal community greenhouse’s seedling propagation bench, focusing on transmissibility and long-term bodily effects.
input: Setting: 2022 municipal community greenhouse, seedling propagation bench (dead, unwatered lettuce seedlings), post-sunset twilight (no moonlight). Entity Type: Shadow.
output: Appearance: A swarm of 100+ tiny, flickering shadow seedlings (each 2mm tall) scattered across the bench and adjacent floor—each has a minuscule, screaming human face etched into its cotyledon, with no light source casting them. Behavior: Dormant under moonlight; activates in total twilight, crawling onto shoes, skin, or clothing, leaving tiny black root-shaped marks. Origin: 2021 community gardener Mia Rao used contaminated topsoil (illegally dumped fetal remains from a back-alley clinic) for the seedling bench; the unborn’s unformed essence merged with the dead seedlings. Horror: The root marks grow into seedling-shaped bumps under the skin, which burst into shadow vines at the next full moon, binding the victim to the greenhouse forever. Encounter: A 2022 volunteer stayed late to water plants, tripped over the bench; shadows crawled up her calf, leaving 3 tiny root marks. At the next full moon, she vanished, and her shadow was found merged with the seedling swarm.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration focused on paranoia in a 3am subway tunnel, centered on a mirroring shadow that isn't your reflection.
input: Subway tunnel at 3am, waiting for the last train, shadow mimicking your every move but not your reflection.
output: You press your back to the cold tunnel wall, eyes fixed on the flickering fluorescent light above. The last train was supposed to arrive 15 minutes ago. You shift your weight, and a dark shape at the tunnel’s end shifts too—only it’s not your reflection in the grimy tile. You lift your left hand; it lifts its left. You duck; it ducks. When you turn to run, it’s closer, no feet visible under its ragged coat. The hum of the tunnel warps into a whisper: ‘You know it’s you.’ You pat your pockets for your phone, but your fingers brush something cold, not yours—a handprint on your jacket that wasn’t there a minute ago. The shadow stops, tilts its head, and you swear its face is yours, just… wrong. The train’s rumble never comes. The shadow steps forward, and you realize you can’t remember when you started copying it.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration focused on paranoia in a 3am subway tunnel, centered on hearing your own unspoken thoughts whispered back to you.
input: 3am subway tunnel, alone, hearing your own unspoken thoughts whispered aloud from the dark.
output: You hug your backpack tight, eyes scanning the empty tunnel for any sign of the delayed train. You think, ‘I should leave,’ and a voice identical to yours hisses from the blackness 20 feet away: ‘I should leave.’ You freeze—you didn’t say that out loud. You think, ‘Who’s there?’ and the voice repeats it, slower, dripping with malice. You back toward the exit, but the voice follows, echoing off the tiles. You fumble for your keys, thinking, ‘It’s just my imagination,’ and the voice laughs, your laugh, but twisted. You glance over your shoulder and see nothing, but the voice is now right behind your ear, whispering your deepest fear: ‘You’re going to be stuck here forever.’ You spin, but the tunnel stretches on, endless, and every time you think a thought, it bounces back at you, louder, until you can’t tell if the voice is yours or something else wearing your voice.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration focused on paranoia in a 3am subway tunnel, centered on a lost wallet containing your ID but with a photo of someone you don’t recognize.
input: 3am subway tunnel, found a wallet with your name on the ID but a photo of a stranger.
output: You kick a crumpled soda can down the tunnel, bored and anxious for the train. Your boot hits something hard—a black leather wallet. You open it; the driver’s license has your full name, your address, your birthday… but the photo isn’t you. It’s a person with your face, but their eyes are hollow, their smile too wide. You flip through the wallet: receipts from a café you’ve never visited, a ticket stub for a movie you didn’t see, a handwritten note that says ‘Don’t trust the man in the blue coat.’ You look up, and a man in a blue coat stands at the tunnel’s mouth, staring at you. You stuff the wallet in your pocket, but your hands shake—how does this wallet exist? The man starts walking toward you, and you notice his shoes are scuffed exactly like yours. You check your own shoes; yes, same scuffs. The man calls your name, and his voice is yours, deeper, older. You back away, realizing the photo in the wallet is what you’ll look like if you stay here one more minute.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration focused on paranoia in a 3am subway tunnel, centered on a stranger who keeps getting closer but is ignored by the only other person present.
input: 3am subway tunnel, a stranger approaches you repeatedly, but the homeless man sitting nearby acts like he can’t see them.
output: You lean against the ticket machine, scrolling through your phone with no signal. A man in a tattered hat starts walking toward you from the tunnel’s dark end. You look away, then glance back—he’s 10 feet closer. You nudge the homeless man dozing on a bench beside you, saying, ‘Hey, that guy’s coming over.’ The man doesn’t move, doesn’t even blink. The stranger is now 5 feet away, his face hidden by the hat. You step back, and he steps forward. You yell, ‘What do you want?’ but the homeless man still doesn’t react. The stranger lifts his head, and his face is blank, no eyes, no mouth. You run toward the exit, but when you glance over your shoulder, the stranger is right behind you, and the homeless man is now standing, staring at you with the same blank face. You realize the homeless man was never there—he was just the stranger, waiting for you to notice. The tunnel’s exit vanishes, and you’re trapped with something that keeps changing shape to match what you think is safe.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration focused on paranoia in a 3am subway tunnel, centered on unseen touches that make you question your sanity.
input: 3am subway tunnel, unseen hands touching you, making you doubt your own perception of reality.
output: You’re pacing the tunnel, checking your watch every 10 seconds. A cold hand brushes your left shoulder. You spin—nothing. You keep pacing, and another hand taps your right elbow. You look down, no one there. You press your back to the wall, heart racing. A hand grabs your wrist, tight, then lets go. You yelp, but the tunnel is silent except for your breathing. You start to hyperventilate—are you imagining this? You pull out your phone to record, but the screen glitches, showing a shadowy figure behind you that vanishes when you turn. The touches get bolder: fingers tracing your neck, palms pressing your back, a breath on your ear. You run, but the touches follow, and you hear a voice in your head saying, ‘You’re just crazy.’ You stop, gasping, and see your own hand reaching for your shoulder—you didn’t move it. You stare at your hand, trembling, and realize the touches are coming from you, but you can’t control them. The tunnel closes in, and you don’t know if you’re being haunted or if you’re the one haunting yourself.

instruction: Describe the protagonist's experience as the parking garage's security system traps them.
input: Parking garage after hours, protagonist trying to leave.
output: The gate arm refuses to lift. You tap your card again. Nothing. You reverse, try another lane. Red 'X'. A calm, digitized voice fills your car: 'Security Protocol Initiated. Please remain in your vehicle.' The exit gates lower completely, sealing you in. Then, the lights begin to shut off, row by row, a wave of darkness swallowing the concrete. Your headlights show only the empty stall ahead. The voice returns, flat and final: 'Containment successful. Awaiting collection.' Your door locks click down, not by your hand.

instruction: Describe the protagonist's experience as their car's autonomous systems malfunction in the garage.
input: Modern car with full self-driving features, protagonist inside.
output: You select 'Home' on the touchscreen. The car pulls smoothly from the spot, then accelerates sharply, tires squealing. You grab the wheel, but it's locked. 'Autonomous Drive Engaged' flashes. It takes a ramp downward, spiraling deeper, ignoring your 'Cancel' stabs. The display flickers, showing a phantom passenger in the backseat. The brakes are unresponsive. The car parks itself in a dark, sub-basement corner you've never seen, engine off, lights dead. A child's voice whispers from the speakers, 'We're here. Let's play.' The doors won't open.

instruction: Describe the protagonist's experience as they are stalked by something that manipulates the garage's sound and space.
input: Protagonist walking to their car on an upper level.
output: Your footsteps echo, then are perfectly matched a second later from the shadows ahead. You stop. The other steps stop. You hear your own breath, played back to you from a distant air vent. The level seems to stretch, your car perpetually three rows away. A nearby concrete pillar warps, its surface bubbling like liquid. From it, a distorted reflection of your own terrified face pushes out, mouthing silent words. The lights dim, and you feel the ground tilt, sliding you toward the thing that wears your shape.

instruction: Describe the protagonist's experience as they become physically paralyzed while retrieving something from their trunk.
input: Evening, protagonist loading groceries into their car's trunk.
output: As you straighten up, a cold rigidity seizes your spine. You're frozen, bent over the trunk's lip, staring at your grocery bags. You can't blink. Your fingers are cement on the plastic handle. From your fixed vantage, you see a figure emerge from between two cars. It moves with jerky, unnatural patience, scraping a long nail along the vehicle's flank. It stops behind you, its breath a sour chill on your neck. You cannot scream, cannot turn. A hand, dry as plaster, closes over yours on the trunk latch, and slowly begins to push it down.

instruction: Describe the protagonist's experience as the parking garage's architecture reshapes itself to trap them.
input: Protagonist takes a wrong turn into a dead-end section.
output: You turn down a ramp, but it doesn't lead to the exit. It ends in a blank wall. You reverse, but the way you came is now a solid barrier of raw, weeping concrete. A new ramp spirals upward to your left where there was only a wall. You take it, desperate. It leads back to the same dead-end. The ceiling lowers incrementally with a grinding of stone. Your radio sputters a looping message in your own voice: 'Stop trying. Stop trying. Stop.' The rearview mirror shows your car is now the only one in a vast, shrinking grey cube.

instruction: Create second-person horror narration set in a closed late-night laundromat centered on jumpscare fear.
input: Late-night closed laundromat, jumpscare scenario
output: You’re the only patron left at the shuttered laundromat, folding socks at 2:17am when the back dryer rattles so hard it tips your empty change tray. You hesitate, then yank the door open—instead of your faded sweatshirt, a pair of blood-crusted eyes stares back, glued to a tattered toddler sock stuck in the lint trap, the dryer’s blower humming a wet, gurgling tune like a tiny child crying. You slam the door so hard your hands sting, but when you blink, a twisted, tiny hand is pressed to the cold glass, fingers bent backward like it’s trying to rip through metal to reach you.

instruction: Generate second-person horror narration for a daytime laundromat with a jumpscare fear scenario.
input: Busy daytime laundromat, lost phone jumpscare
output: You left your new iPhone in a mid-cycle washer, so you pop the lid to fish it out before it drowns. The suds swirl, and instead of your cracked screen, a gray, pruned face stares up at you—mouth agape, eyes milky, skin wrinkled like over-soaked fruit. You scream and slam the lid shut, flinging suds everywhere. When the cycle ends, you lift the lid again, and your phone sits on the edge, lit up with a photo of that exact face taken two seconds before you’d glanced away to grab detergent.

instruction: Write second-person horror narration set in a quiet 1am laundromat focused on a jumpscare.
input: 1am quiet laundromat, vending machine jumpscare
output: You’re reaching for a cola from the corner vending machine at 1am when you hear scuffling behind you, but the laundromat is empty—all dryers are done, the overhead lights dimmed to a flicker. You punch the button, the machine clunks, then its front door flings open violently. A severed, matted head lands on your shoulder, its tongue lolling, breath cold enough to freeze your neck. You yank it off, and when you drop it, it vanishes like smoke, leaving only a sticky unopened soda can in your trembling hand.

instruction: Create second-person horror narration for a crowded daytime laundromat centered on jumpscare fear.
input: Crowded daytime laundromat, forgotten basket jumpscare
output: You’re folding a stranger’s forgotten clothes in the noisy, packed laundromat when you pull a soft flannel shirt from a wicker basket. It’s warm, like it was just worn an hour ago, and when you lift the collar, a pale, overlong hand grabs your wrist. Its fingers are black with grime, nails twisted, the arm fading into the fabric like ink bleeding into water. The customers around you keep chattering, their clothes spinning in dryers, none noticing the hand holding you or the shirt that’s now sticking to your skin.

instruction: Generate second-person horror narration for a personal laundromat load focused on jumpscare fear.
input: Your own laundered clothes, jumpscare scenario
output: You toss your last load of clothes into the dryer, set it to high heat, and wait ten tense minutes. When you yank the door open, your hoodie is gone—replaced by a pile of your own flayed skin, still warm, with your laundry tag stitched to the edge of the patch on your left palm. You stumble back, tripping over a metal washer, and when you look up, the dryer’s control panel glows red, displaying 'Next Load: You' in dripping letters that match the wet blood on the skin pile at your feet.

instruction: Generate a unique geometric horror entity entry for an after-hours public library, following SCP/Silent Hill/Junji Ito cosmic horror conventions
input: Entity 1: Shelf Edge Tesseract
output: Appearance: A warped 4-dimensional polyhedra that only manifests at the 90-degree intersection of two metal stack shelf edges, its facets constructed entirely of laminated overdue date stamp fragments, no two displaying the same calendar date. It has no consistent shadow, appearing as a flickering set of stacked line segments to the naked eye. Behavior: It only rotates when unobserved, siphoning any book whose due date matches its current active facet into its void interior, leaving no trace the book ever existed. Origin: Formed from 72 years of accumulated unreturned book guilt in the library’s isolated third floor closed stacks, where missing book reports have been filed unchallenged since the 1950s. Horror: Sustained eye contact staples your personal timeline to an overdue notice, trapping you in the stacks for eternity restocking books no other patron will ever see. Encounter: A night janitor stumbled on it searching for a misplaced horror novel in 2021, losing his left index finger to a facet; the detached digit still bears a 1998 due date stamp that fades and reappears monthly.

instruction: Generate a unique geometric horror entity entry for an after-hours public library, following SCP/Silent Hill/Junji Ito body horror conventions
input: Entity 2: Page Seam Hyperbola
output: Appearance: A zero-thickness, matte black curved line that only manifests along the bound spine gap of open hardcover books, seeping a faint viscous ink that smells like dried formaldehyde and yellowed 19th century rag paper. It has no measurable width, but casts a distorted wavy shadow that stretches across adjacent table surfaces. Behavior: It grows outward across the gap between adjacent open books left unattended on reading tables, slicing through any organic or inorganic material that passes through its curved plane without resistance. Origin: Spawned from a 1978 book restoration accident, where a grieving librarian accidentally mixed her own blood into a binding adhesive used to repair 120 cracked poetry collections. Horror: If it wraps around exposed skin, it peels away the top layer of epidermal memory, leaving the victim only able to recall fragmented lines of forgotten Victorian verse for the rest of their life. Encounter: A cramming college student encountered it after falling asleep at a study table in 2019, when it sliced through her backpack strap mid-reach, all her textbook contents turning to yellowed poetry pages mid-fall.

instruction: Generate a unique geometric horror entity entry for an after-hours public library, following SCP/Silent Hill/Lovecraft cosmic horror conventions
input: Entity 3: Card Catalog Nonagon
output: Appearance: A flat 9-sided polygon embedded in the label slots of the library’s 1950s wooden card catalog, each edge etched with a call number that does not exist in any library digital system.
