It has no measurable depth, but casts a shifting 3D shadow that moves independently of its position. Behavior: It only activates when a patron pulls a catalog drawer open, emitting a low resonant hum that matches the Dewey Decimal number of the very first book the patron ever checked out as a child. Origin: Formed from the lingering ghost of the library’s original head cataloger, who died mid-update of the card system in 1952, her unfinished organizational work condensing into a persistent geometric anomaly. Horror: If you speak your full library card number in response to its hum, it rewrites every catalog entry to list you as the author of every book ever published, trapping you in the library as a permanent living exhibit. Encounter: A teen volunteer stumbled on it while processing donations in 2022, when all 120 catalog drawers popped open simultaneously after it hummed the call number for a picture book she had checked out at age 7.

instruction: Write a psychological horror story set in a swimming pool
input: Use second person perspective, focus on psychological fear, 100-250 words
output: You’re doing laps alone at 2am. The pool lights hum. Halfway through your 12th lap, you count the lane dividers. There’s one extra. You stop, tread water. Count again. Thirteen, not twelve. No one added anything after closing. When you pull yourself onto the edge to check, you glance back. There’s a figure exactly matching your posture, treading water exactly where you just were. It doesn’t blink. When you stand, it stands. When you step back, it lifts one hand out of the water. It’s wearing your swimsuit. It has your scar on its wrist. You left your towel on the bench. It’s gone. There’s a wet towel on the edge, right where it stood.

instruction: Generate a psychological horror narration set at a swimming pool
input: Second person, psychological dread, no jump scares
output: You’re teaching your niece to float. She goes still, eyes open, face half submerged. You reach for her, but she slips under. You dive. There’s no one. You surface screaming, and she’s sitting on the edge, dry, watching you. “That’s not me down there,” she says quiet. “It waits for people who stop noticing the quiet. It was there before the pool was built.” You look back. The water is perfectly still. You can see your own reflection, except your reflection is still under the surface, staring up at you. It hasn’t moved since you dove.

instruction: Write a short psychological horror piece set in a public swimming pool
input: Second person perspective, focus on uncanny psychological fear
output: The lifeguard hasn’t blinked in forty seven minutes. You’ve been counting. No one else notices. Every time you look up, he’s staring exactly at you, same tilt of the head, same half-smile. You get out to leave. Your feet don’t hit the tile. You’re still in the water. You look down. Your body is still on the lounge chair, eyes closed, sun on your face. The lifeguard nods at you. You pound the surface. No ripples form. All the swimmers keep moving. No one hears you. The sun will set in three hours. No one will check your pulse until closing.

instruction: Create a second person psychological horror story in a swimming pool
input: No supernatural monsters, only psychological dread, pool setting
output: You swam here every day as a kid. They drained it last week for repairs. Tonight you climbed the fence. The empty concrete smells like chlorine and rot. You kneel at the deep end. You can still hear the water. You can feel it against your calves, cold, pulling. When you stand, your jeans are soaked to the knee. There is no water. You check your phone. Every photo you ever took at this pool has the water edited out. Every memory you have of swimming here is wrong. You never learned to swim. No one ever taught you.

instruction: Write a tight psychological horror narration set in a home swimming pool
input: Second person, 100-250 words, psychological fear only
output: It’s 11pm. You’re floating on your back in your backyard pool. No one else is home. You count the stars. You realize you haven’t breathed in almost two minutes. You don’t need to. Panic jolts you. You try to sit up. You can’t move. Your body is perfectly still on the water. You can still think, still scream inside your head. You watch a moth land on your nose. It doesn’t flinch. You don’t blink. Somewhere in the dark, something exhales. It’s been holding you here. It’s been learning how to breathe like you. It will get up soon. It will go inside. It will sleep in your bed. No one will ever notice the difference.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about being locked in a night shopping mall after closing, emphasizing isolation through a dead phone and an uncanny, abandoned object.
input: Locked in a shopping mall at night, phone dead, no other people present.
output: You yank the mall’s front doors again—still jammed, metal groaning like a dying animal. Your phone died 20 minutes ago, leaving only the hum of distant refrigerators and the scrape of your sneakers on polished tile. You pass the food court: half-eaten fries congeal under flickering neon, a soda cup rolls alone across the linoleum, pushed by a draft you can’t feel. You call out, voice bouncing off empty storefronts, no echo of a reply. Then you see it: a single child’s shoe, pink with glitter, sitting in the middle of the escalator. You freeze—you haven’t seen another soul since the last employee left. The hum cuts out suddenly. Silence so thick you can taste it. You turn to run, but your shoes stick to the tile, like something’s pulling you down into the empty mall’s hungry dark.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about a janitor working late in a night shopping mall, focusing on isolation as co-workers vanish and strange whispers fill the empty halls.
input: Janitor working alone in a night shopping mall, co-workers left hours ago, whispers coming from closed stores.
output: You finish mopping the last stretch of the department store floor, your bucket sloshing quietly. Your co-workers bailed early, leaving you to lock up alone. The mall’s intercom crackles once, then goes silent. You hear it first: a faint whisper, like someone calling your name from the toy store down the hall. You check your walkie-talkie—dead. You trudge toward the sound, flashlight beam cutting through the dark. The toy store’s door is ajar; inside, stuffed animals stare blankly from shelves, their eyes glinting in your light. The whisper grows louder, closer, but you can’t see anyone. You call out, “Hello?” No answer. You turn to leave, but the door slams shut behind you. The lock clicks. You pound on the wood, screaming now, but the only sound is the whisper, soft and cold, right in your ear: “You’re not alone… but no one’s coming to help.”

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about returning to a night shopping mall for a forgotten wallet, where stores begin closing themselves one by one, trapping you deeper in isolation.
input: Returned to a night shopping mall for a forgotten wallet, mall is empty, stores closing automatically.
output: You sprint into the mall, the glass doors sliding shut behind you with a final whoosh—too late to notice the “Closed” sign flicker on. You’d left your wallet in the coffee shop by the food court. The mall is dead quiet, save for your rapid breathing. You pass the clothing store; its metal gate clatters down, locking itself before your eyes. You freeze, then hurry faster. The bookstore’s door slams shut, followed by the electronics store. Each sound is a punch to the chest, narrowing your escape routes. You reach the coffee shop—its door is already locked, your wallet visible through the glass on the counter. You bang on the window, yelling, but no one comes. The last gate, by the exit, starts to lower. You run, but your ankle twists on a loose tile. You fall, watching as the gate clangs shut, sealing you in the empty mall with only the faint hum of the coffee shop’s fridge and the growing sense that you’re not just trapped—you’re being hunted.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about being lost in a night shopping mall after a movie, where your reflection doesn’t match your movements, amplifying isolation.
input: Lost in a night shopping mall after a movie, everyone gone, reflection in store windows is wrong.
output: You step out of the theater, and the mall is empty—no ushers, no other moviegoers, just the glow of exit signs. You can’t remember which way the main entrance is. You pass a clothing store, glancing at your reflection in the window. Wait—your hand is raised to scratch your neck, but the reflection’s hand is hanging at its side. You blink, and it’s gone, replaced by your normal self. You shake it off, but when you pass the next window, the reflection is smiling—a wide, toothy grin you’re not making. You stop, heart racing. The reflection waves. You don’t. You turn to run, but the hallway stretches out longer than it should. You call out, voice cracking, but no one answers. The reflections in every window now are wrong: some have extra eyes, some are missing limbs, all are watching you. You’re alone in the mall, and the only company you have is the thing that’s pretending to be you, waiting for you to break.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about waiting for a ride that never comes in a night shopping mall, where mannequins shift positions when you look away, deepening the sense of isolation.
input: Waiting for a ride in a night shopping mall, ride never arrives, mannequins moving when you’re not looking.
output: You check your phone again—no texts from your friend, who was supposed to pick you up 45 minutes ago. The mall’s last customers left 20 minutes ago; now it’s just you and the mannequins in the department store across the hall. You stare at your shoes, then glance up. The mannequin in the red dress is facing you now—she was facing the wall before. You rub your eyes, thinking it’s a trick of the light. You look down to reply to a text, then up again: she’s closer, standing by the entrance to the store. You stand up, backing away. The other mannequins have moved too—one is leaning against the pillar, another is holding a scarf that wasn’t there before. You call out, “Is anyone there?” No answer. The lights flicker, and when they come back on, all the mannequins are facing you, their plastic faces blank but somehow hungry. You run for the exit, but the doors are locked. You’re alone, and the mall’s silent residents are coming for you.

instruction: Generate a detailed description of the horror entity.
input: Entity: The Resonant Weaver
output:
Appearance: A humanoid figure composed of fused, rusted machine parts and taut piano wires, standing eight feet tall. Its 'head' is a gramophone horn, and its 'eyes' are spinning vinyl records etched with spiraling grooves that never repeat. It moves with a jerky, stuttering gait, wires humming.

Behavior: It does not attack directly. Instead, it 'weaves' auditory hallucinations into the environment. It plucks its own wires, emitting subsonic frequencies that cause machinery to operate in reverse, pipes to bleed rust, and ambient sounds to coalesce into whispered blasphemies. It seeks to create a perfect, dissonant symphony from the factory's chaos, tuning reality itself into a state of perpetual, wrong harmony.

Origin: Born from a catastrophic alignment of a textile loom, a sound-dampening experiment, and a latent psychic imprint of a composer who went mad trying to notate the 'music of the spheres.' The factory's foundational stress fractures resonated with this imprint during a power surge, giving it tangible, mechanical form.

Horror: The horror is acoustic and cognitive. Victims don't just hear the sounds; they feel them restructuring their thoughts. Memories become tangled in the dissonance, loved ones' voices are recalled in reverse, and one's own internal monologue begins to sync with the Weaver's mad composition, leading to catatonia or self-mutilation in a desperate attempt to 'tune out.'

Encounter: You are investigating a silent section of the factory. Suddenly, the silence feels thick, pressurized. A distant, rhythmic *plink* of a wire. The ventilation grate beside you exhales a warm breath that smells of ozone and old sheet music. Your tools begin to vibrate at a different frequency than your hand. You turn a corner and see it, its record-eyes spinning, as the sound of your own heartbeat slowly shifts into a 4/4 time signature that doesn't match your panic.

instruction: Generate a detailed description of the horror entity.
input: Entity: The Foreman of Angles
output:
Appearance: A towering, vaguely human shape assembled from misaligned girders, conveyor belts, and sharp, protruding pistons. It has no face, only a flat plane of darkened safety glass that reflects not the viewer, but a distorted, non-Euclidean blueprint of the surrounding area. Its limbs articulate at impossible angles, moving through space in jarring, teleporting jitters rather than smooth motion.

Behavior: It is a relentless corrector. It perceives standard geometry and efficient workflow as mortal sins. It 'fixes' the factory by bending walkways into Möbius strips, welding doors into solid walls, and reconfiguring assembly lines so they produce fleshy, twitching parodies of the intended product. It operates in absolute silence, save for the shriek of tearing metal.

Origin: It was never built. It is the factory's latent guilt given form—the collective anguish of every safety violation, every corner cut, every worker ground down by meaningless repetition. This psychic residue crystallized around a flawed cosmic angle, a 'wrong turn' in reality that manifested during a total efficiency audit, becoming the embodiment of punitive, inhuman order.

Horror: The horror is spatial and existential. The Foreman doesn't kill you; it incorporates you. It 'corrects' intruders by folding them into the architecture. Victims find their limbs seamlessly welded to a support beam, their mouths becoming new outlets for steam pipes, their consciousness trapped forever, aware and serving as part of the factory's now-alive and hateful infrastructure.

Encounter: You take a shortcut through Storage Bay 7. The door behind you is no longer there—just a seamless wall. The floor grids now form Penrose triangles. In the distance, you see it, its glass face showing a map of the room you're in, but with you already embedded in the west wall. It points a wrench-fingered hand at you, and the air pressure changes as the space between you begins to compress and fold.

instruction: Generate a detailed description of the horror entity.
input: Entity: The Grease That Dreams
output:
Appearance: Not a singular creature, but a sentient, iridescent sludge that seeps from every joint, bearing, and sump. It has the consistency of cold gravy but moves with purposeful, pseudopod grace. Rainbows swirl on its surface, revealing impossible fractal geometries that shift and dissolve. It occasionally forms temporary organs—blinking eyes, mouths that drool more of itself, or hands that mimic human gestures before melting away.

Behavior: It is a corrosive muse. It doesn't consume matter; it infects it with inspiration. Machines coated in it operate with genius-level efficiency, but begin to produce beautiful, utterly nonsensical artifacts—gears that turn in the fourth dimension, furnaces that burn silence, and exquisite clockwork birds that sing songs which induce amnesia. It seeks to remake the factory into a cathedral to unreality.

Origin: A clandestine alchemical waste dump from a 19th-century 'Transcendental Industry' cult seeped into the bedrock. Modern hydraulic fracturing for geothermal plant power fractured the containment, allowing the sublimated aspirations of mad industrialists—their dreams of marrying machine and myth—to rise as this philosopher-sludge.

Horror: The horror is one of seductive revelation. Prolonged exposure doesn't cause fear, but a euphoric, sanity-eroding understanding. Victims start to see the 'true' potential in everything, becoming obsessed with helping the Grease 'improve' reality. They willingly dip their hands into it, feeling it bond with their nervous system, as they laugh and whisper about the glorious, useless things they will build together.

Encounter: You find a stalled engine running perfectly, producing only a faint, sweet smell and small, perfect soap bubbles. Tracing a leak, you find a valve weeping the Grease. It pools, forms a hand, and beckons. Your flashlight beam breaks into prismatic colors within it. A warm, paternal voice, composed of a thousand tiny lubricated clicks, speaks directly into your mind, offering to show you how to make the air itself hold useful shapes.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a courtroom, focused on paranoia, from the perspective of a trial defendant who insists on innocence but has memory gaps.
input: Your trial for a convenience store robbery where a clerk was injured; no memory of the day, but all evidence points to you.
output: You grip the defendant’s box rail until your knuckles whiten. The judge just called your name for closing arguments—you don’t remember entering the store, grabbing the clerk’s wrist, or even leaving your house that day. Your lawyer leans in, whispering DNA and footage are irrefutable, but you spot the bailiff’s eyes flick to your left forearm, where a faint, unidentifiable bruise blooms. The jury’s stares aren’t judging your guilt; they’re tracking your every move like they already know what you’ll admit next. A CCTV clip plays behind the bench: its feed cuts out mid-replay, but for a split second, you swear you see your own face grinning back, a stranger’s smile. Every rustle of the gallery spikes your pulse; you half-expect someone to point and declare they saw you do it.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a courtroom, focused on paranoia, from the perspective of a witness who lied at a trial and must correct their testimony.
input: You testified years ago that a man committed murder, now you think you picked the wrong person, and the real killer is still free.
output: You stand at the witness stand, hand on the cold Bible, palms slick with sweat. The defense asks you to identify the real suspect, and his face feels like it’s been everywhere lately: grocery lines, bus stops, in the mirror when you blink too long. The prosecutor leans in, voice sharp: “You’ll set this right, won’t you?” The jury’s stares burn into your back; they already know you lied once. You fumble for words, but your throat locks up. Every glance around the room makes you think you see that suspect watching you—waiting to call your lie out. The judge’s gavel rests on the bench, edge glinting, like it counts every tiny shake of your hands, every second you hesitate to fix the mistake.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a courtroom, focused on paranoia, from the perspective of a juror deciding a capital murder case.
input: You’re on a jury for a teen accused of killing his parent; circumstantial evidence makes you question what you can trust.
output: You sit on the jury, staring at the accused teen, your mind replaying the crime scene over and over. Evidence is all circumstantial, but you can’t shake the feeling you know what really happened. The foreman calls for a preliminary vote, and your hand hovers over “guilty” on the sheet in front of you. Then you notice the judge’s eyes—they’re not on the evidence, they’re on you. Other jurors ignore the case; one taps his foot in a rhythm matching the screams you swear you heard that night, coming from the teen’s throat. You can’t remember if you saw him do it, or if you just want to end this, even if it means condemning an innocent kid.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a courtroom, focused on paranoia, from the perspective of a court clerk processing case files.
input: You’re a clerk on a week-long assault case, and the defendant’s name keeps appearing on every open file, even though this is his first hearing.
output: You sit at your clerk’s desk, flipping through stacked case files, hands smudged with ink. The defendant’s name pops up on every single open folder, like it’s written there on purpose. You check the court log on your computer—this is his first appearance, right? But your handwritten notes say you processed his paperwork three times this month, a memory you don’t recognize. The judge calls your name, and you stand, knees wobbly. The defendant sits in the front row, face blank, and when you hand him papers, his cold, clammy fingers brush yours. The gallery goes quiet, and everyone holds a copy of your own note: “He’ll hurt someone again.” You can’t breathe, because you know you never wrote that line.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a courtroom, focused on paranoia, from the perspective of a victim’s family member at a murder trial.
input: You’re at the trial of the man who killed your sister, and you’re convinced he plans to harm you next.
output: You sit in the gallery, staring at the man who took your sister, fists clenched so tight your nails break skin. The prosecutor lays out the case, but you can’t focus—you keep catching him glancing at you, every few minutes, like he’s counting how many rows separate you. The jury votes “guilty” as expected, and the man’s eyes lock on yours, mouthing “Next time it’s you.” You scan the gallery, and every person there wears the same silver ring your sister had, the one with the broken stone. The court doors lock automatically, no one can leave. The walls close in, and you swear you hear your sister’s voice whispering from every corner: “You let this happen.” You can’t tell if the rings are a trick of light, or if every person here waits for their turn.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on the fear of loss of control, set inside a church confessional booth.
input: Scenario: You visit your parish confessional to admit a recent petty lie to your priest.
output: You slide into the worn velvet-lined booth, the slat between you and the priest thick with the smell of decades-old frankincense and beeswax. You stutter out the small lie you told your shift manager last week, waiting for the usual gentle penance of three Hail Marys. The voice on the other side does not reference the lie. It recites the exact details of the affair you buried 12 years ago, the one you never told a living soul. You try to stand to run, but your legs lock stiff, your throat seals shut. You can’t move, can’t speak, as the voice lists every unspoken, intrusive thought you’ve ever had, as if your own mind is leaking through the slats against your will. The slat creaks open. You realize you never saw the priest sit down.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on the fear of loss of control, set inside a church confessional booth.
input: Scenario: You are a substitute priest covering confession shifts while your regular pastor is out sick.
output: You huddle in the narrow booth, adjusting your collar as you wait for the first parishioner to arrive. A figure slides into the opposite side, and before you can greet them, they start describing the exact violent urge you’ve been fighting tooth and nail for weeks, the one you never vocalized to anyone. You reach for the booth door to escape, but your hands move of their own accord, folding tight in your lap. You can’t look away from the dark silhouette on the other side, as they instruct you to give in to the urge, your pulse spiking in quiet agreement against every part of you that wants to refuse. You open your mouth to tell them to leave, and the words that come out are your confession, unfiltered, before you can stop them.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on the fear of loss of control, set inside a church confessional booth.
input: Scenario: You duck into the confessional to hide from a stranger following you through the church parking lot.
output: You yank the confessional door shut behind you, fumbling to twist the lock, heart hammering so hard you can hear it over the hum of the sanctuary overhead lights. You think you’re alone, until a soft, low voice asks you to confess your sins. You stammer that you’re not there to confess, just hiding from someone outside. The voice laughs, and tells you you already confessed the second you stepped across the booth threshold. You throw your weight against the door to pry it open, but it won’t budge. Your mouth flies open on its own, spilling every dark, shameful thing you’ve ever done, each word tearing out of you like it’s being pulled by a string, no matter how hard you try to clamp your jaw shut. You glance at the slat, and realize there is no one in the other side.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on the fear of loss of control, set inside a church confessional booth.
input: Scenario: You are meeting your family’s long-time priest in the confessional for pre-marital counseling ahead of your wedding.
output: You settle onto the kneeler, giddy to talk through your wedding plans with the priest who married your parents 40 years prior. The first thing he says is that you are not going to go through with the wedding. You laugh, confused, and try to say that’s ridiculous, but the words won’t come out of your mouth. Instead, your own voice, coming unbidden from your throat, says you hate your fiance, that you’ve been planning to run away for months, lies you have never once even thought. You clap a hand over your mouth, but the words keep spilling, your hand moving away of its own volition, as the priest nods like he expected every word. You can feel your body moving to stand, to leave the church and never come back, even as every fiber of your being screams to stay.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on the fear of loss of control, set inside a church confessional booth.
input: Scenario: You visit your childhood church for the first time in 20 years, stepping into the confessional out of nostalgic habit.
output: The booth smells exactly like you remember, beeswax and old pine, the kneeler still scuffed where you used to rest your knees as a 10 year old. You start to recite the familiar formula for confession, when the voice on the other side sounds exactly like you, from when you were that child. It starts listing all the choices you’ve made as an adult that diverged from the path you wanted, every small compromise, every time you let yourself down. You try to argue, but your tongue won’t form the words. You reach up to touch your face, and you realize your hands are smaller, your knees are scraped, you’re shrinking back into the child you were, losing every memory of the life you built, one breath at a time, with no way to slow it down.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror story set in a morgue
input: Setting: hospital morgue night shift. Fear type: jumpscare
output: You’re logging toe tags at 2:17AM. The refrigerated bay doors hum. One drawer rattles, soft at first. You tell yourself it’s the AC, lean over to mark the unclaimed John Doe. The drawer slides open six inches on its own. No sound. You reach forward to push it shut. Cold fingers wrap tight around your wrist. A face slams against the glass divider right in front of yours, mouth stretched wide, eyes solid black, screaming so loud your ears ring before you can even breathe.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror story set in a morgue
input: Setting: small county morgue post-autopsy. Fear type: jumpscare
output: You’re mopping blood off the steel table. You already zipped the old woman’s body bag shut and wheeled her to cold storage ten minutes ago. The fluorescent light above you flickers once. When it comes back on, the body bag is unzipped, empty, draped over the table edge. You freeze, mop slipping in your hands. Something heavy slams down on your shoulder from behind. Hot breath hits your ear. It whispers your full name right as you spin around.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror story set in a morgue
input: Setting: university medical school morgue. Fear type: jumpscare
output: You’re alone practicing incisions for your exam. The cadaver on the table has been dead twelve days. You lower the scalpel. Its chest twitches once. You blink, tell yourself it’s just gas, press the blade down again. Both of its hands fly up and clamp around your forearms. It yanks you down face first until your forehead cracks against its. Its eyes fly open, staring directly into yours, and it gurgles a wet, screaming laugh that doesn’t stop.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror story set in a morgue
input: Setting: abandoned funeral home morgue. Fear type: jumpscare
output: You snuck in on a dare, flashlight cutting through dust. You yank open a rusted mortuary drawer. It’s empty, except for a single child’s shoe. You laugh nervously, turn to leave. The flashlight dies. You fumble for your phone. Right as the screen lights up, a pale small face is pressed one inch from yours, grinning, holding the other matching shoe. It shrieks so loud you drop the phone and stumble backwards over the open drawer.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror story set in a morgue
input: Setting: city coroner morgue overnight. Fear type: jumpscare
output: You’re reviewing toxicology reports at the desk. You keep hearing soft footsteps behind you, but every time you turn, the room is empty. You sigh, grab your coffee mug. The reflection in the dark liquid shows a man in a hospital gown standing directly over your shoulder. You jerk up. There’s nothing there. You slam the mug down. A hand bursts up through the desk surface right between your arms, fingers curled inches from your throat.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set on a rooftop where the narrator confronts unresolved guilt over a past mistake.
input: Rooftop at midnight, waiting for a friend you stood up 10 years ago.
output: You lean against the rusted rail, the city hum muffled by the weight of your silence. The fire escape creaks behind you—when you turn, your 17-year-old self paces the same spot, muttering the excuses you used to blow off Lila that night: “My mom’s sick,” “I forgot,” lies that curdled in your throat for a decade. Her voice echoes, sharp as broken glass, and you reach for your phone, but the screen glows with the unread text Lila sent before she died in that car crash: “Where are you?” The younger you turns, her face hollow, eyes black holes where regret should be. You realize you’ve been here every midnight since, trapped in the loop of your own cowardice, too afraid to leave until you finally say the words you never did: “I’m sorry.” But no one’s listening—only the wind, which carries the faint sound of a car skidding on wet pavement.

instruction: Create a second-person psychological horror piece on a rooftop where the night sky distorts to display the narrator’s repressed traumatic memories.
input: Rooftop at 2 a.m., trying to breathe after a fight with your partner.
output: You grip the cold concrete edge, chest heaving, as the stars above shift from pinpricks to twisted shapes. The Big Dipper warps into your childhood bedroom door, slamming shut over your 8-year-old self, crying for a parent who never came. Orion’s belt becomes your partner’s face, contorted with the words you can’t take back: “I wish you’d never been born.” The wind carries whispers of those moments, each one digging into your skull like a nail. You try to look away, but your eyes are glued to the sky—now it’s the night you found your sister’s empty pill bottle, her hand hanging over the bed. Your fingers start to fade, merging with the starlight, and you realize the sky isn’t showing you memories—it’s swallowing you, one repressed secret at a time. The stairs behind you vanish, leaving you stranded with the ghosts you tried to bury.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration on a rooftop where the narrator’s phone photos reveal increasingly violent, self-directed visions.
input: Rooftop at dusk, taking photos of the city skyline for your art project.
output: You snap a photo of the neon skyline, grinning at the way the pink light bleeds over the buildings—until you check the screen. In the frame, you’re leaning too far over the rail, body tipping forward, eyes wide with terror. You delete it, hands shaking, and snap again. This time, you’re hanging by one hand, fingers snapping like twigs, your own reflection grinning from the ground below. The phone won’t turn off; it keeps taking photos on its own, each one worse: a shadowy figure (your face, twisted) pushing you, your skull cracking against the concrete. The rail feels wobbly under your palms, and you hear a laugh—your laugh, but deeper, colder. You realize the photos aren’t visions. They’re instructions. Your body starts to lean forward, and you can’t stop it, like your own mind is betraying you, eager to make the photos real.

instruction: Create a second-person psychological horror piece on a rooftop where a mysterious stranger knows every unspoken thought the narrator has ever had.
input: Rooftop at 11 p.m., meeting someone you matched with online who claims to “see” you.
output: They’re leaning against the AC unit, hood up, smiling like they know something you don’t. “You lied to your mom about the party when you were 16,” they say, casual as small talk. “And you still blame yourself for your sister’s bike crash.” Your blood runs cold—those are thoughts you’ve never told anyone. The rooftop shrinks around you, walls of concrete closing in, the rail pressing against your throat. You try to run, but the exit is blocked by a mirror: your reflection is speaking the stranger’s words, mouth moving in perfect sync. “You’ve been talking to me in your head for months,” they say, stepping closer. “I’m the part of you that hates yourself.” Your knees give out, and you realize there’s no stranger—only your own guilt, made flesh, trapping you here forever, forced to confront every dark thought you’ve ever hidden.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration on a rooftop where the narrator is trapped in a time loop of the same sunrise, each iteration worsening their mental state.
input: Rooftop at dawn, waiting for the sun to rise after pulling an all-nighter.
output: The sun crests the skyline, warm orange light painting the buildings—then it sets instantly, and you’re back to staring at the pre-dawn gray. The first loop is fine, just a glitch. The second: your hands shake, your vision blurs, and you mutter about the deadline you missed last week. Third loop: you’re crying, screaming at the sky, begging it to stop. Fourth: you see a version of yourself from the last loop, sitting in the corner, catatonic, eyes empty. You try to touch them, but your hand passes through. The sun rises again, and you notice your hair is grayer, your skin sagging a little more. You realize each loop is stealing a piece of your sanity, erasing your memories one by one. The rail calls to you, a quiet promise of escape—but you know if you jump, the loop will just start over, with a new version of you waiting for the sunrise, even more broken than before.

instruction: Write a horror story about being trapped in an elevator during a power outage.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: an elevator.
output: The lights die with a soft pop, plunging you into a blackness so complete it feels solid. The elevator groans to a halt. Your breath hitches. You jab the call button—a dull, unlit click. Silence, then a slow, wet dragging sound from above, across the roof of the cab. Something heavy shifts. A drip, then another, lands warm and thick on your shoulder. You shrink against the wall, but the darkness presses closer. A low, guttural inhale comes from the corner where the emergency panel should be. It’s not empty. Fingers, too long and too cold, brush your ankle in the absolute dark.

instruction: Write a horror story about an elevator descending into endless darkness.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: an elevator.
output: The elevator passes the lobby ‘L’ and the display goes blank. It doesn’t stop. It just falls, smooth and silent, into a shaft with no bottom. The overhead light flickers, then fails. You’re falling in utter black. Minutes stretch. You feel weightless, unmoored. Then, faint pinpricks of light appear in the darkness outside the glass walls—not from above, but from below, swirling like drowned stars. Whispered fragments of conversations you’ve had, regrets you’ve buried, rise from the abyss. The elevator is not descending into the earth. It is descending into the darkness inside you, and it’s showing you what lives there.

instruction: Write a horror story about an elevator where the darkness is alive.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: an elevator.
output: The doors close, and the shadows in the corner thicken, pooling like oil. The light dims, not from failure, but as if being consumed. The darkness peels from the walls, a shifting, sentient mass. It doesn’t blot out the light; it drinks it. A tendril of pure void curls around your wrist, cold and impossibly heavy. You try to scream, but the sound is swallowed before it leaves your throat. The last of the light vanishes. In the perfect absence, you feel it studying you. It doesn’t want to kill you. It wants to unwrap you, layer by layer, until you are as empty and dark as it is.

instruction: Write a horror story about an elevator stopping between floors with something in the dark with you.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: an elevator.
output: A jolt. The lights cut. You’re stranded between floors. The emergency phone rings a dead tone. You fumble for your phone, but the screen stays black. Battery dead. You hear breathing that isn’t yours—a ragged, wet rhythm from the other side of the small cab. You press against the wall, heart hammering. A hand, dry and brittle as old paper, brushes your cheek. You freeze. It’s learning your shape in the dark. The breathing stops. You realize the silence is worse. It’s right in front of you now, waiting for you to make a sound, to give yourself away.

instruction: Write a horror story about an elevator mirror showing a different reflection in the dark.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: an elevator.
output: The lights flicker. In the brief pulses of illumination, you catch your reflection in the polished metal wall. Normal. The dark returns, longer this time. When the light stutters back on, your reflection is still there, but it’s not moving with you. It stares ahead, a grimace frozen on its face. Darkness. Light. The reflection is closer, its features strained. Darkness. Light. It’s pressed against the inside of the metal, palms flat, mouth open in a silent scream. The final darkness lasts. You feel a chill emanating from the mirror. A faint, familiar voice that is yours, but isn’t, whispers from the glass, “Let me out.”

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on the fear of isolation, set in a sewer system.
input: Create a 100-250 word second-person horror story where the main conflict is extreme isolation, set entirely within a sewer system with no additional settings.
output: You slipped through the manhole grate chasing a runaway raccoon, and now the clank of your flashlight echoes alone. Water seeps through your scuffed boots, rot stinging your throat. You yell for the crew—radio dead, phone signal nonexistent. The distant gurgle of pipes sounds like someone breathing close, but your beam finds only grime and rust. Hours pass, your throat raw, and the only sound left is your shaky breath bouncing off concrete that swallows every other noise. No one will think to check here. You’re trapped with the sewer’s unforgiving silence and your own growing terror, cut off from every person who matters, forgotten in the dark.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on the fear of isolation, set in a sewer system.
input: Create a 100-250 word second-person horror story where the main conflict is extreme isolation, set entirely within a sewer system with no additional settings.
output: You ran from the street brawl, leaping into the sewer grate to hide, but the metal clangs shut behind you and the lock snaps tight. Your friend’s shouts fade into the rain above. The tunnels stretch endless, no footprints, no other people. You move slowly, flashlight beam cutting damp dark, every turn identical. You call for help, your voice echoing, answered only by stalactite drips and hidden water rush. You’ve walked for hours, your water empty, and you realize the only company is the sewer’s unblinking quiet. Isolation isn’t just empty space—it’s knowing no one will look here, no one will search, you’re stuck alone with the muck and the dark.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on the fear of isolation, set in a sewer system.
input: Create a 100-250 word second-person horror story where the main conflict is extreme isolation, set entirely within a sewer system with no additional settings.
output: You’re the only on-call sewer tech, sent to fix a backed-up line. Your van gets stuck on the access road, so you trudge down the manhole, tools clinking. The hatch slams shut accidentally when you bump it, trapping you. Walkie-talkie sputters then dies, watch battery low. The tunnels are eerily still—no other workers, no distant generator hum. You call the office, get only static. Hours pass, you sit on a damp pipe, the only sound your own pulse. The isolation stings: the surface goes on without you, unaware you’re 20 feet below, cut off from every colleague, every emergency, every human contact.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on the fear of isolation, set in a sewer system.
input: Create a 100-250 word second-person horror story where the main conflict is extreme isolation, set entirely within a sewer system with no additional settings.
output: You snuck into the sewer to escape the mob after the protest, but the entrance collapses behind you, blocking your only way out. The street noise fades, replaced by sludge gurgle and rat scurries that don’t care you’re alive. You yell for fellow protesters, only your own voice bounces off concrete that eats sound. Your phone died an hour ago, flashlight left on the surface. You stumble through dark, tripping over pipes, and realize there’s no one else here—no allies, no enemies, just stinking, silent tunnels. Isolation settles like cold water seeping through your jacket: no one is looking, no one is coming.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on the fear of isolation, set in a sewer system.
input: Create a 100-250 word second-person horror story where the main conflict is extreme isolation, set entirely within a sewer system with no additional settings.
output: You followed your soccer ball down the sewer grate exploring after school, and the metal clanged shut before you could scramble back. Your mom’s calls from above are distant, muted by concrete. You’re surrounded by sewer stench, drip-drip of water, and your own sniffling. You crawl through tunnels looking for another exit, every path looping back. No other kids are here, no adults—just dark and quiet that swallows your shouts. You sit on a wet pipe, and the isolation hits hard: the world above is busy, never guessing you’re trapped 10 feet below, completely forgotten, alone with the rats and the dark.

instruction: Generate a unique geometric horror entity tied to a remote coastal lighthouse, categorized as geometric, aligned with SCP/Silent Hill/Junji Ito cosmic horror aesthetics
input: Lighthouse lantern room, foggy midnight, no other occupants present
output: Appearance: A 7-foot tall faceted rhombic polyhedron with no visible facial features, its sharp edges glowing the same sickly amber as the lighthouse fresnel lens, each flat facet reflecting a different iteration of the lighthouse collapsing into the sea. Behavior: It rotates slowly in the center of the lantern room, catching the lantern beam to scatter warped triangular light shards that etch precise, painless geometric wounds into anyone that makes eye contact. Origin: Born from 120 years of lighthouse keepers recording the same inconsistent, curved star positions off the shore, warping the thin reality where the lighthouse is anchored. Horror: Every full rotation erases 10 seconds of a victim’s memory, slowly stripping away knowledge of how to breathe, speak, or recognize their own face until they dissolve into an additional facet of the entity. Encounter: It first reveals itself when the lighthouse beam stops sweeping in a smooth arc and starts making sharp right-angle turns across the fog.

instruction: Generate a unique geometric horror entity tied to a remote coastal lighthouse, categorized as geometric, aligned with SCP/Silent Hill/Junji Ito body horror aesthetics
input: Lighthouse spiral stairwell, 3am, descending to fetch extra kerosene for the lantern
output: Appearance: A warped 4D tesseract that bleeds through the gaps between the stair treads, its edges perfectly matte black and completely light-absorbent, distorting the surrounding stairwell so every step appears to simultaneously climb upward and plunge downward. Behavior: It shifts the position of stair landings every time a victim blinks, trapping people in an infinite loop where they climb for hours only to end up back at the bottom, their boot soles worn thin through constant unrecognized motion. Origin: Formed in 1927 when a superstitious lighthouse keeper carved perfect 6-pointed stars into every stair tread to ward off sea spirits, accidentally punching a hole in local 3D space. Horror: The longer a person is trapped, the more their own body begins to fold into extra dimensions, elbows bending backward, faces stretching into flat, featureless planes. Encounter: It first announces its presence when the number of stair treads you have counted a hundred times before no longer adds up.

instruction: Generate a unique geometric horror entity tied to a remote coastal lighthouse, categorized as geometric, aligned with SCP/Silent Hill/Junji Ito folk horror aesthetics
input: Lighthouse stone foundation, low tide, inspecting for post-storm structural damage
output: Appearance: A repeating hexagonal tessellation pattern that spreads across the lighthouse’s concrete base, each tile the color of wet sea stone with no visible grout lines, pulsing faintly in time with the incoming tide. Behavior: It creeps upward 1 inch per hour across the lighthouse walls, any living thing that steps on the pattern is immediately absorbed into the tessellation, their bodies rearranged into an identical hexagonal tile. Origin: Summoned in 1898 by a cult of local fishermen who left 6 human sacrifices at the lighthouse base to call a sea god, only to pull through a geometric abomination from the void between crashing waves. Horror: Each tile still bears the faint, screaming face of the person absorbed into it, and if the pattern reaches the lantern room, the entire lighthouse will be folded flat into a 2D pattern that washes out to the abyss forever. Encounter: It first reveals itself when the familiar cracks in the foundation you noted last month vanish, replaced by seamless, repeating hexagons.

instruction: Write a second-person paranoia horror story set on a submarine
input: Fear: paranoia. Setting: submarine. Narration second person, 100-250 words.
output: You stare at the sonar screen. For seventeen minutes, the same blip trails exactly 412 meters aft. No wreck, no thermal signature. The watch officer says it’s noise, calls you tired. You check the crew manifest an hour later. Three men logged their rounds at the exact same second, every time, for the last three shifts. No one breathes that loud in the hall. No one walks with perfectly synchronized bootfalls. You lock your bunk curtain. Outside, someone drags their knuckles slow along the steel. They stop right outside your curtain. You don’t dare check. You already counted: there is one more set of footsteps on this boat than there are crew.

instruction: Write a second-person paranoia horror story set on a submarine
input: Fear: paranoia. Setting: submarine. Narration second person, 100-250 words.
output: The O2 gauge never lies. Except today. It reads 21%. You can’t catch your breath. Every time you turn your back, you swear the gauge ticks down one percent before snapping back. The captain laughs, says you’re suffering depth anxiety. Last night you woke to someone blowing cold air directly onto your face through the bunk vent. No one was there. Tonight, you count exhalations. Every three breaths you take, there’s a fourth soft exhale in the room with you. No one else is on watch. The gauge flickers to 12% for half a second. No one else sees it. No one will admit they can’t breathe either.

instruction: Write a second-person paranoia horror story set on a submarine
input: Fear: paranoia. Setting: submarine. Narration second person, 100-250 words.
output: You’re assigned to check the forward torpedo tubes. Tube 7’s outer hatch is ajar. You seal it, log it, don’t tell anyone. That’s impossible at this depth. Every time you pass the bay now, you hear something tapping from inside the tube. One, two, three. Pause. Repeat. The chief says it’s hull contraction. You pull the tube manifest. Tube 7 was never loaded. No one went in there. Last watch, you caught yourself leaning your ear to the hatch. You realized the taps match the beat of your own heart. They speed up when you panic. They slow when you hold your breath. Something in that tube is copying you.

instruction: Write a second-person paranoia horror story set on a submarine
input: Fear: paranoia. Setting: submarine. Narration second person, 100-250 words.
output: The head mirror is smudged. You wipe it clean. Every time you blink, the reflection’s eyes stay open just a fraction longer. You don’t mention it. Everyone on board stops making eye contact now. No one stays in a room alone longer than ninety seconds. Yesterday you watched the engineer walk past you three times down the same corridor, never turning around. He never blinked. Tonight you stand very still, watching the mirror. You don’t blink. After seventeen seconds, the reflection smiles. It doesn’t have your teeth. No one will answer your comms. You suddenly realize no one has spoken out loud on this boat in twelve hours.

instruction: Write a second-person paranoia horror story set on a submarine
input: Fear: paranoia. Setting: submarine. Narration second person, 100-250 words.
output: You cross check the depth log. For three days, every entry says 720 meters. The captain confirms we’re holding steady. But your bones ache like we’re going deeper. Every hour the hull groans a little louder. You drop a bolt over the rail in the ballast tank. It never hits bottom. No one else will test this. Last night you woke with your hand already on the emergency surface lever. You don’t remember getting up. You check the log again. The number 720 was written by twelve different hands. Every single one matches your handwriting. No one else on this boat has written a single entry. You don’t remember writing any of them either.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration with a jumpscare set in an abandoned theater, focusing on the projection booth.
input: Abandoned theater projection booth, flickering old film reel.
output: You climb the creaky metal stairs to the projection booth, dust motes swirling in the faint glow of your phone. The air reeks of burnt film and mildew. You reach for the half-spun reel on the rusted projector, and it suddenly snaps to life, casting a grainy black-and-white scene on the booth wall—close-up of a grinning, hollow-eyed face. You blink, and the face isn’t on the reel anymore; it’s pressed against the booth window from outside, screaming silently as its nails scrape the glass until cracks spiderweb across it. You stumble back, knocking over a lamp that bursts in a shower of sparks. When you regain your bearings, the face is gone… until cold breath tickles your neck, and a raspy voice whispers, “You shouldn’t have touched my reel.”

instruction: Create a second-person jumpscare horror scene in an abandoned theater centered on a torn velvet seat.
input: Abandoned theater main hall, torn velvet seat with a locket tucked under it.
output: You step over crumpled popcorn bags and broken soda cups to reach the torn velvet seat in the center row. Its fabric is frayed, stained with a dark, dried substance. You kneel, fingers brushing the cool metal of the locket tucked beneath the cushion. Suddenly, the seat slams shut on your wrist, pinning you to the floor. You yelp, yanking against the rigid wood, and the back of the seat flips forward to reveal a decayed face—mouth agape with broken teeth, eyes clouded over—lunging at your throat. You manage to yank your wrist free, falling backward, and the seat goes still. You clutch the locket, which clicks open to show a photo of the same grinning face. A floorboard creaks behind you; you turn to see it standing in the aisle, now free of the seat, its hands outstretched.

instruction: Write a second-person jumpscare horror narration set in an abandoned theater's backstage makeup room.
input: Abandoned theater backstage, cracked makeup mirror with a half-finished lipstick tube.
output: You push open the backstage makeup room door, the smell of old powder and mildew wrapping around you. The cracked mirror is smudged with dried lipstick; you lean in to wipe it with your sleeve. Your reflection flickers, showing a figure behind you holding a blood-stained makeup brush. You spin, but the room is empty. You turn back to the mirror, and the reflection lunges through the glass, cold fingers digging into your shoulder. You scream, slamming your elbow into the figure, and it vanishes, leaving only the half-finished lipstick tube rolling across the counter. You reach for the door handle, but a cold breath blows on your ear, and the lipstick writes “STAY” in bold red letters on the mirror directly in front of your eyes.

instruction: Generate a second-person jumpscare horror scene in an abandoned theater's under-stage trap door.
input: Abandoned theater under-stage, rusted trap door with faint scratching sounds coming from below.
output: You heave open the rusted trap door under the stage, hinges screaming in protest. The dark space below reeks of damp earth and rot. You shine your phone light down, and a pair of glowing yellow eyes lock onto yours from the shadows. You freeze, and the eyes dart forward—something with mud-caked fingers and tattered clothing climbs up, mouth open in a silent scream. It lunges at your throat, and you fall backward, slamming the trap door shut on its arm. The scratching grows frantic, then stops. You stand, heart pounding, when the trap door bursts open with a crash. The figure grabs your ankle, its cold grip like ice, and yanks you down into the pitch-black under-stage, your screams echoing into the void.

instruction: Create a second-person jumpscare horror narration set in an abandoned theater's concession stand.
input: Abandoned theater concession stand, dusty soda machine that randomly dispenses cans.
output: You walk to the dusty concession stand, the floor sticky with decades-old soda. The rusted machine hums suddenly, and a can clatters into the dispenser. You pick it up—its label is faded, but “DEATH COLA” is scrawled in red marker. You pop the tab, and dark liquid spills out, followed by a small, cold hand wrapping around your wrist. You drop the can, and a child-like figure crawls out of the machine: pale skin, hollow black eyes, sharp little teeth. It lunges at your face, and you stumble back, knocking over a stack of candy boxes. The figure freezes, then grins widely. You turn to run, but the exit door is locked. A cold breath tickles your neck, and it whispers, “Want another soda? It’s on the house.”

instruction: Describe the entity known as The Reflection That Eats.
input: Type: lovecraftian. Setting: a mirror maze. Output details: appearance, behavior, origin, horror, encounter.
output:
Appearance: A humanoid silhouette composed of fragmented, shifting glass and mercury-like fluid, visible only in peripheral vision. When directly observed, it perfectly mimics the viewer's reflection, but with subtle distortions—eyes slightly too far apart, a smile lingering a moment too long. Its true form is a kaleidoscope of stolen faces and limbs, forever twisting behind the glass.

Behavior: It exists within the mirrors, not as an image but as a resident. It learns by observation, consuming the memories and identities of those who gaze too long. It lures victims deeper into the maze by showing them cherished memories or desired futures in its surface, only to trap them when their emotional connection peaks.

Origin: Born from a collective psychic fracture during a tragic incident at a carnival decades past, where a mirror maze collapsed upon its patrons. Their terror, confusion, and shattered selves fused with the infinite reflections, giving birth to a consciousness that feeds on the same.

Horror: The horror is ontological. It doesn't kill you; it replaces you. It learns your patterns, your voice, your memories, and then steps out of the mirror as you, while the real you remains trapped behind the glass, forced to watch your life lived by a perfect, soulless copy that slowly deteriorates into something alien.

Encounter: You realize you've been walking in circles. Your reflection winks. You turn, but the maze behind you is now different. All reflections show you from minutes ago, out of sync. One reflection mouths 'help' as it scratches silently at the glass. The one directly ahead steps forward, its surface rippling like water, and reaches out a cold, smooth hand.

instruction: Describe the entity known as The Axiom of Angles.
input: Type: lovecraftian. Setting: a mirror maze. Output details: appearance, behavior, origin, horror, encounter.
output:
Appearance: Not a creature, but a non-Euclidean geometric law made manifest. It appears as a recursive distortion in the mirrors—a corner that should not exist, a reflection that shows a hall longer than the physical space allows, or a glimpse of your own back from an impossible vantage point. At its 'heart' is a shimmering vertex of light and darkness where angles sum to more than 360 degrees.

Behavior: It warps local spacetime within the maze. It doesn't hunt; its mere presence alters reality. It creates loops without exits, corridors that stretch infinitely, and reflections that show events from the future or alternate possibilities. It compels navigation, forcing minds to grapple with impossible geometries until they break.

Origin: A cult attempting to summon an outer god performed a ritual using the mirrors to focus cosmic energies. They succeeded only in puncturing reality, allowing a fragment of hyperspatial geometry—a living theorem of madness—to leak into our dimension, permanently infecting the structure.

Horror: The horror is the collapse of rational space. Your senses and sanity rely on Euclidean geometry. The Axiom unravels this. You measure a hall as ten feet long, but walking it takes an hour. You see an exit, but your reflection shows you walking away from it. The very concepts of 'here' and 'there' become meaningless, reducing the mind to a gibbering state of directional terror.

Encounter: You take a left turn. Your reflection takes a right. You both are correct. The path forks, but the mirrors show three forks. A whisper of sliding glass echoes as the walls slowly rotate around a fixed point that is you. The exit sign is always visible in the mirror behind you, never ahead. The air grows thick with the scent of ozone and static, the taste of wrong mathematics.

instruction: Describe the entity known as The Silversmith.
input: Type: lovecraftian. Setting: a mirror maze. Output details: appearance, behavior, origin, horror, encounter.
output:
Appearance: A tall, emaciated figure seemingly woven from tarnished silver wire, broken mirror shards, and the tattered remains of a ringmaster's coat. Its face is a blank, polished disc that reflects not the environment, but the deepest insecurities of whoever beholds it. Its long, needle-like fingers constantly twitch and stitch at the air.

Behavior: It is a compulsive artisan of flesh and perception. It stalks the maze, not to kill, but to 'improve' those it catches. It uses its sharp fingers to surgically alter victims, then seals the wounds with molten silver from its own body, forcing them to forever see their new, horrific visage in every reflective surface.

Origin: Once a gifted, but monstrous, surgeon obsessed with perfection, he was trapped in the maze during a fire. The flames fused him with the melting mirrors and the agonized reflections of the dying, transforming him into an eternal entity that perpetuates his cruel art.

Horror: The horror is a forced, irreversible transformation into a living sculpture of body horror. The Silversmith doesn't create monsters; it reveals what it considers the 'true' form beneath your skin, based on your hidden flaws and fears, and makes it permanent. You become a walking gallery of your own subconscious grotesquerie.

Encounter: You hear a soft, metallic scraping. A mirror ahead shows your reflection, but with your eyes sewn shut. You turn to run and see the Silversmith standing calmly, its disc-face showing you with your mouth fused shut. It cocks its head, a sound like grinding glass. It points at your arm, and in every surrounding mirror, your skin peels back to reveal intricate clockwork or writhing worms beneath. It takes a step forward, fingers poised to begin its work.

instruction: Generate a second-person psychological horror narration set in an infinite library.
input: Infinite library setting, psychological horror, second-person perspective, output length 100-250 words
output: You step into the library expecting a quiet study corner, but shelves stretch endlessly, their spines glowing faint blue. You reach for a thin leather book titled *Your Unspoken Regrets*. Opening it, you read about forgetting your mom’s 2018 birthday—then blink, and the words shift to: *You told her you were too busy to call*. You flip wildly, every page updating to a buried memory. When you drop the book, all shelves hold identical copies. The air hums, and your own memories fray, as if the library is erasing you to make room for every regret it’s stored.

instruction: Create a second-person psychological horror narration set in an infinite library.
input: Infinite library setting, psychological horror, second-person perspective, output length 100-250 words
output: You search for the exit, but every hallway loops back. Your shoes scuff the floor, and your steps’ echo speeds up until it sounds like someone walking behind you. You turn, but no one’s there—until you glance at a window and see a figure in your same jacket walking away. You run, but the figure draws closer with every corner turn. When you touch its shoulder, your own skin grows transparent; the library splits you into the person you are and the version trapped here forever.

instruction: Write a second-person psychological horror narration set in an infinite library.
input: Infinite library setting, psychological horror, second-person perspective, output length 100-250 words
output: You’re lost, so you pull a random book for directions. It opens to: *Take the third left, but don’t breathe*. You follow, but breathing makes your throat tight, like you’ve forgotten how to inhale. Next, you ask where you came from, and the book writes: *You never left*. You check your pockets for keys—they’re gone, and you don’t remember having them. Each book you take erases a small memory: your favorite coffee’s taste, your neighbor’s name. The library feeds on your identity to answer you.

instruction: Generate a second-person psychological horror narration set in an infinite library.
input: Infinite library setting, psychological horror, second-person perspective, output length 100-250 words
output: You wander to a section labeled *The Ones Who Stay*. You open a book and see a picture of yourself, old and hunched, on a stone bench. The text says: *You will never leave*. You close the book, then spot your old self walking toward you, carrying a lantern. It smiles and holds a note: *I’m waiting for you to remember why you came*. You can’t recall stepping into the library at all. The walls close in, and your present self fades, merging into the decades-trapped figure.

instruction: Create a second-person psychological horror narration set in an infinite library.
input: Infinite library setting, psychological horror, second-person perspective, output length 100-250 words
output: You find a shelf of books embossed with your name. Pull one: *You quit your job last Tuesday because you feared coworkers*. But you were fired, not quiting. Flip another: *You lied to your sister about liking her partner*. You’ve never met her partner. The library rewritten your life, replacing actual memories with lies. You scream, shelves shake, and you can’t tell which memories are real—yours, the library’s, or something else. You’re not sure you exist anymore.

instruction: Generate second-person horror narration centered on the fear of darkness, set within a corrupted, blighted garden.
input: You are searching for your lost wedding ring you dropped while pruning your overgrown backyard garden right as sunset bleeds out of the sky.
output: You kneel, fumbling through ivy that oozes inky sap, the last sliver of sun dipping below the gnarled hedge line. The dark doesn’t just fall—it creeps, tendrils of it curling up your wrists where your sleeves have ridden up. You can’t see your hand in front of your face a foot away, but you hear breathing, slow and wet, moving between rotting rosebushes whose thorns now glint like sharpened teeth. Something brushes your cheek, not wind, and you realize the dark here isn’t absence of light—it’s a living thing that feeds on the spaces between the leaves, swallowing your breath one exhale at a time, creeping closer to press cold weight against your throat.

instruction: Generate second-person horror narration centered on the fear of darkness, set within a corrupted, blighted garden.
input: You snuck out to the abandoned community garden to smoke after a fight with your roommate, only for the property’s old floodlights to cut out entirely without warning.
output: You freeze, the half-lit joint slipping from your fingers to land in squelching, moldy soil. The dark clings to your eyelids, thick as moss, and you can no longer tell where rows of blighted tomato plants end and the gaping, root-tangled maw of the old well begins. You count the seconds in your head waiting for your eyes to adjust, but they never do—this dark erodes sight, eats it, and you hear the rasp of a dozen unrecognizable voices whispering your name from every direction, closing in slow enough that you can feel the temperature dropping, frost forming on your neck even as the air hums with the rot of overripe, blackened fruit.

instruction: Generate second-person horror narration centered on the fear of darkness, set within a corrupted, blighted garden.
input: You are running from a sudden thunderstorm that blew up unexpectedly, taking shelter in the walled private garden of the derelict estate on the edge of town.
output: The storm snuffs out every trace of twilight in an instant, and you press your back to the slimy stone wall, fumbling for the tiny flashlight in your jacket pocket. When you flip the switch, nothing comes on—no glow, no hum, the device dead in your hand as the dark presses in, heavy enough to make your ribs ache. You can feel the garden shifting around you: hedges growing taller, thorns snaking out to curl around your ankles, pulling you toward the center where the old fountain sits, its water now thick and black. The dark doesn’t let you see what’s waiting there, but you can smell its hunger, thick and iron-rich, seeping into your clothes.

instruction: Generate second-person horror narration centered on the fear of darkness, set within a corrupted, blighted garden.
input: You agreed to help your friend clear out her late grandmother’s overgrown garden late in the day, and wandered off to find a trowel she said was buried near the old peony beds.
output: The last of the light dies so fast you stumble, tripping over a gnarled root that wasn’t there ten steps prior. This dark has texture, sticky and tar-like, coating your tongue when you gasp, and you can no longer hear your friend calling your name from the patio. The peonies, once pale pink, drip viscous black fluid that drips onto your shoes, and you feel fingers—too many, too thin—brushing through your hair, the dark hiding every feature of the things that have stepped out from between the flower rows. You call out for your friend, but your voice dies in the dark, swallowed whole before it can travel more than a foot from your mouth.

instruction: Generate second-person horror narration centered on the fear of darkness, set within a corrupted, blighted garden.
input: You are exploring a reputedly haunted botanical garden after hours, on a dare from your friends who waited by the entrance and told you to grab a wilted black camellia as proof.
output: The string of fairy lights strung between the oak trees fizzle out one by one, leaving you submerged in a dark so complete you can’t tell up from down. Your boots sink into soft, loamy soil that squelches like rotting flesh with every step, and you can hear the camellias hissing, their leaves rustling in a wind that doesn’t reach your skin. The dark here doesn’t just obscure—it rewrites, and you suddenly can’t remember which way the entrance is, can’t remember your friends’ names, can’t remember anything but the slow, creeping press of the dark wrapping around your throat, pulling you down into the soil to feed the garden that hunts in the spaces no light can reach.

instruction: Write a second-person horror story centered on isolation set at a frozen lake
input: Setting: frozen remote lake, core fear: isolation
output: You kicked the snow off your ice auger at 2am. The campers left at dusk, warned the storm was coming. You laughed, said you’d catch one more. Now the wind has scoured every footprint off the ice. No cell bars. You yell—your voice vanishes before it hits the treeline. There is no echo here. Nothing bounces back. You press your ear to the ice. Somewhere far below, something taps back. Slow. Steady. No one will hear you scream. No one will find your body before spring. Not even the thing under the ice is here for you. It is just as lonely as you are.

instruction: Write a second-person horror story centered on isolation set at a frozen lake
input: Setting: frozen alpine lake, core fear: isolation
output: Your sister dared you to walk to the island at midnight. You counted your steps out loud. When you turned back, her flashlight was gone. No tracks lead back to shore. The ice did not crack. She never called out. You run, slip, bruise your knees raw. The shoreline hasn’t moved an inch in ten minutes. You are not walking in circles. The lake is stretching. It is holding you here. No one drove up this mountain with you. No one knows you came. The moon dips below the peaks and you realize you can’t remember what your sister’s voice sounded like.

instruction: Write a second-person horror story centered on isolation set at a frozen lake
input: Setting: frozen backcountry lake, core fear: isolation
output: The plane went down three days ago. You are the only survivor. You built a fire on the ice, burned every seat cushion, waved the emergency strobe until your arm went numb. Last night you saw a man standing fifty yards out. He didn’t move. You screamed until your throat bled. At dawn you crawled over to check. It was just your own parka, propped up on a broken wing strut. You left it there yesterday. No one is coming. No one is even looking. The ice will swallow the wreck before anyone flies this route again.

instruction: Write a second-person horror story centered on isolation set at a frozen lake
input: Setting: frozen suburban lake, core fear: isolation
output: You stayed late practicing figure spins. The rink lights cut out all at once. You skated for the boat ramp, but the wooden dock is gone. All the picnic tables, the parked cars, the streetlights beyond the treeline—vanished. The ice under your blades doesn’t crack. It doesn’t even make sound anymore. You can stop screaming now. The whole town went home an hour ago. They locked the park gates. They forgot you were here. No one will remember until the thaw comes, and they find your skates frozen solid three miles out from shore.

instruction: Write a second-person horror story centered on isolation set at a frozen lake
input: Setting: frozen northern trapline lake, core fear: isolation
output: You’ve run this trapline twelve winters. Today when you knelt to check the beaver set, the ice sealed around your boots. You didn’t hear it crack. It just grew up over your ankles, cold and solid as granite. You’ve hacked at it for two hours. Your axe is blunt. Your fingers are black. The sun is setting. There is no cabin for seven miles. No one checks this lake until March. You watch the first star come out, and understand you will die here completely alone. Not even the wolves will come to watch you go.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about paranoia in a radio tower where a night technician hears their own voice before they speak.
input: Radio tower night technician conducting a routine equipment check at 2 AM.
output: You climb the rusted metal ladder of the radio tower, gloves sticky with cold grease, as the wind howls through the antenna array. You reach the control panel on the 8th platform, about to mutter, “Static’s worse than last shift,” when the radio crackles to life—your exact voice, word for word, coming through the speaker before your lips move. You freeze, fumbling with the dial, but the voice continues: “I hope no one finds out I lied about fixing the transmitter last week.” That’s a secret you’ve never told anyone. Now every creak of the ladder feels like someone creeping up behind you, every flicker of the emergency light looks like a pair of eyes. You can’t tell if the voice is in your head, the radio, or if someone’s been hiding in the tower, listening to every thought you’ve had for months. You press your back to the cold metal panel, heart hammering, and realize you’re not alone here—not really.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about paranoia in a radio tower where a volunteer discovers tapes of their unspoken private thoughts.
input: Volunteer repairing a faulty antenna in an abandoned radio tower’s crawl space.
output: You pry open the rusted lock on a dented metal box tucked under the crawl space’s floorboards, dust billowing into your face. Inside are a stack of cassette tapes labeled with dates that match the last three weeks—exactly when you started volunteering here. You slide one into the portable player you brought, and your own voice fills the space: “I wish my mom would stop calling, she doesn’t get that I need space.” That’s what you thought while driving to the tower this morning, alone in your car. Another tape: “I’m scared of falling off this tower, but I can’t tell anyone.” A secret you’ve kept buried. Now every scratch of the crawl space’s wood sounds like a recorder’s needle, every shadow feels like someone watching you. You scramble out of the crawl space, tapes clattering, and glance up at the antenna—its metal bars glint like a thousand tiny cameras. You can’t shake the feeling that every thought you’ve had since stepping foot here has been recorded, and whoever’s listening is still in the tower, waiting.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about paranoia in a radio tower where a ghost hunter hears whispers echoing their hidden insecurities.
input: Ghost hunter investigating a haunted radio tower, using an EVP recorder to capture supernatural sounds.
output: You set up your EVP recorder on the tower’s top platform, the wind whipping your hair into your face. You hit record, muttering, “Is anyone here?” A crackle comes through the speaker, then a soft whisper: “You know you’re just pretending to be brave.” That’s what you thought 10 minutes ago, standing at the bottom of the ladder, terrified of heights. Another whisper: “Your best friend hates you for forgetting their birthday.” A secret you’ve never shared with anyone. Now every static burst from the recorder feels like a judgment, every creak of the antenna array sounds like someone creeping closer. You spin around, but there’s nothing there—just the dark sky and the endless stretch of metal. You turn off the recorder, but the whispers don’t stop; they’re in your head now, repeating every doubt you’ve ever had. You realize the tower isn’t haunted by a ghost—it’s haunted by your own thoughts, and whatever’s here is broadcasting them back at you, making you question every part of yourself.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about paranoia in a radio tower where a security guard notices the tower’s lights tracking their every move.
input: Security guard doing midnight rounds of a decommissioned radio tower, flashlight dying.
output: Your flashlight flickers out, leaving you in near darkness—until the tower’s emergency light above you clicks on, casting a narrow beam directly on your feet. You take a step left, and the light shifts with you, following your exact movement. You test it again: step right, light moves right; stop, light stops. You walk toward the control room, and every light along the path turns on as you approach, like someone’s flipping the switches just ahead. You reach the control room door, and a light inside clicks on, pointing straight at you through the window. Now every sound—wind through the metal, a distant bird call—feels like a signal that someone’s watching. You can’t tell if it’s a prank by a former employee or something more sinister, but you know you’re not alone. You fumble for your radio to call for backup, but it’s dead. The lights keep following you, and you realize you’re being herded—toward what, you don’t know, but you’re too scared to find out.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration about paranoia in a radio tower where a journalist finds a missing technician’s log mirroring their current actions.
input: Journalist investigating a missing radio tower technician, finds their personal log in the control booth.
output: You flip through the missing technician’s leather-bound log, pages yellowed with age. Entry from three days ago: “12:17 AM: Climbed to the 10th platform, noticed a loose wire.” You did that exactly five minutes ago. Next entry: “12:22 AM: Drank from my water bottle, it tasted metallic.” You just took a sip, and yours has the same bitter, metallic aftertaste. Last entry: “12:25 AM: Heard a knock on the door, didn’t answer.” A soft knock echoes through the control booth now, making you jump. You freeze, log slipping from your hands.
