input: After-hours third-party audit, failed vault backup generator, no external access signal
output: You blink as the overhead vault lights sputter out entirely, the heavy steel door thudding shut behind you when you stumbled in to cross-check high-value serial numbers. The backup generator never kicked on, and the only light left is the faint, fading glow of your dying work phone screen. You reach for the vault door handle, and your hand brushes something cold, soft, not metal. It breathes, slow, against your wrist. You tell yourself it’s just stacked cash bags, but the sound of fabric shifting doesn’t match the crinkle of bills, and it drifts closer, no footsteps, just the press of shadow swallowing the last of your phone light before you can dial 911.

instruction: Write a tight second-person horror narration focused on the fear of darkness, set inside a locked bank vault, 100-250 words, no filler.
input: Night janitorial security check, faulty vault motion sensors, missing flashlight batteries
output: You step into the vault to log the overnight cash count, and the motion sensor lights cut out the second you cross the threshold. You fumble for the flashlight on your belt, only to find the casing cracked, batteries gone without a trace. You know the vault only has three rows of safety deposit boxes stacked against the far wall, but you can hear whispers coming from the empty corner where no storage sits. You take one step backward, and your shoulder hits a warm, solid figure that wasn’t there when you walked in. It doesn’t move, doesn’t breathe, but you can feel its eyes on you even when there’s no light to see anything at all.

instruction: Write a tight second-person horror narration focused on the fear of darkness, set inside a locked bank vault, 100-250 words, no filler.
input: Failed bank heist, hiding from responding officers, dead emergency glow stick
output: You’d crawled into the vault to hide from cops, slamming the vault door shut behind you to lock them out. The half-dead glow stick you’d stuffed in your pocket fizzles out mid-cheer, leaving you in total, absolute darkness so thick you can taste it. You count the stacks of cash you stuffed in your duffel, and come up with one extra bundle, warm, twitching in your hand. You drop it, and it hits the concrete with a wet thud, not paper. You hear the vault lock click, but it was already locked, and something drags the tip of your boot with a cold, bony finger.

instruction: Write a tight second-person horror narration focused on the fear of darkness, set inside a locked bank vault, 100-250 words, no filler.
input: Retrieving a family heirloom from a safety deposit box, jammed vault door, no cell service
output: The bank manager promised you’d be in and out in two minutes to grab your mother’s old locket from your safety deposit box, but the vault door jammed the second you stepped inside, all internal lights dying instantly. You call for help, but the thick steel swallows your voice before it can reach the hall. You reach out to feel for your box number, and your fingers trace the grooves of a box that has your full name, your date of death, etched into the metal, dated for today. A hand rests on your shoulder, and you can’t see it, but you can feel its cold breath on the back of your neck.

instruction: Write a tight second-person horror narration focused on the fear of darkness, set inside a locked bank vault, 100-250 words, no filler.
input: Vault access technician performing lock maintenance, unexpected city-wide power surge
output: You’re halfway through recalibrating the vault’s new biometric lock when a city-wide power surge fries every internal circuit, plunging the space into total darkness that no emergency backup light can cut through. You know you left your tool kit by the door ten feet away, but when you reach for it, your hand passes through the space where it should be, and you realize the vault is bigger now, much bigger, stretching out into endless dark you can’t see the end of. You hear the scrape of metal on concrete, moving toward you, and you know whatever is in the dark doesn’t want you to leave alive.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on isolation in a planetarium
input: Fear: isolation. Setting: planetarium
output: You lock up after the final late show, running the shutdown checklist. The dome goes black, then the star projectors kick back on alone. No constellation labels, no narration—just every single star, sharp and unblinking. You radio security, no signal. You pound the exit doors, they don’t budge. No one is scheduled back for 14 hours. Up above, the stars don’t twinkle. They stare. You realize none of these formations exist on any sky chart. This is not Earth’s sky. You are the only human that has ever looked at it.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on isolation in a planetarium
input: Fear: isolation. Setting: planetarium
output: You hid here for a middle school field trip dare, counting to a hundred before standing. All lights are out. The bus left 47 minutes ago. Doors bolted from outside. You yell until your throat burns, no reply. The projector whirs on, casting one single faint dot high in the dome. It doesn’t move. You watch it for an hour. It grows brighter. It is approaching. No one wrote your name on the headcount. No one is coming to look. That dot will reach you long before anyone notices you’re missing.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on isolation in a planetarium
input: Fear: isolation. Setting: planetarium
output: You’re the overnight tech testing the new deep space simulator. It boots into empty black void, no menu, no exit prompt. You yank the main power cord. The dome stays lit. You call your supervisor—line dead. Every exit seal has locked tight. The view drifts slow, centering on one tiny pale blue dot. Earth. It shrinks every ten seconds. No one else is in the building. No one else knows the machine didn’t just simulate the view. It took you with it.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on isolation in a planetarium
input: Fear: isolation. Setting: planetarium
output: You slipped in after closing to sit alone away from the city noise. Then you hear breathing. Not yours. You scan every row—every seat empty. The breathing grows louder, surrounding you, no source. Stars blink out one by one until only the one directly above you glows. You are not alone with something. You are the only thing here that is alone. Every empty chair holds something that came before you, that will stay after, that is only watching you breathe.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on isolation in a planetarium
input: Fear: isolation. Setting: planetarium
output: The tornado warning cut the show mid-demo. The crowd panicked, rushed the exits, you got knocked down and trampled underfoot. When you wake, the dome is cold. No emergency lights. No one came back for you. You can’t reach your crushed phone. You crawl along the seat rows, each one empty. Except when you reach behind you. Ice-cold fingers close tight around your wrist. They do not let go. No one will hear you scream through the soundproof dome walls.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a greenhouse centered on paranoia, where the plants appear to be observing your every move.
input: Late evening, tending to a rare tropical carnivorous plant in a secluded greenhouse wing.
output: You adjust the misting nozzle above the pitcher plant, its red, frilled lips glistening like stained glass. The greenhouse hums, but beneath it, a quiet rustle follows every step you take. You glance over your shoulder—nothing, just rows of ferns swaying, but their fronds seem to turn toward you as you walk away. When you lean in to check the plant’s soil, your reflection in the glass panel behind you doesn’t mirror your tilt; it holds still, eyes fixed on your back. You spin around, but the glass shows only your wide, panicked face. The pitcher plant’s trap snaps shut suddenly, not on a bug, but on a stray leaf that drifted too close. You step back, heart thudding. Every shadow between the pots feels like a gaze. You reach for the door handle, but your hand freezes—you swear you see a vine slowly coiling around the doorknob, as if trying to keep you here.

instruction: Craft a second-person horror piece set in a greenhouse focused on paranoia, involving unexplained writing and disembodied whispers.
input: Overnight shift fixing a leaky irrigation line in a commercial greenhouse.
output: You kneel to tighten the pipe under the tomato trellises, the scent of damp soil and ripe fruit clogging your throat. A whisper brushes your ear—your name, soft and breathy. You jerk up, scanning the empty rows. No one’s here; the night guard left an hour ago. You resume work, but the whispers start again, coming from every direction at once. When you stand to wipe your hands, you see it: your name scrawled thickly in oozing sap on a wooden bench. You rub it away with your sleeve, but when you turn back, it’s reappeared, this time longer—“Don’t look behind you.” Your blood runs cold. You spin, but only the tomato vines sway, their tendrils reaching toward you like groping fingers. You fumble for your radio, but it’s dead. Every creak of the greenhouse frame sounds like footsteps. You start to run for the exit, but another sap message blares from the wall: “We’ve been waiting for you.”

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a greenhouse focused on paranoia, where objects shift when you’re not looking.
input: Taking cuttings from heirloom roses in a family-owned greenhouse on a rainy afternoon.
output: You snip a rose cutting, placing it in a labeled pot on the worktable. When you turn to grab another pot, the first one is gone. You scan the floor—nothing. You shake your head, blaming the rain drumming on the roof for distracting you. You snip another cutting, set it down, and turn to get rooting hormone. When you look back, the pot has moved to the far end of the table, its label smudged beyond recognition. Your hands start to shake. You glance at the rose bushes; their thorns seem sharper, their blooms turned away from you. You reach for your phone to call your mom, but it’s not in your pocket. You check the bench—there it is, face down, screen cracked. When you pick it up, a photo you don’t recognize glares back: you, from behind, standing in the same spot, a shadowy figure leaning over your shoulder. You spin around, but the greenhouse is empty. The roses rustle, as if laughing.

instruction: Create a second-person horror piece set in a greenhouse centered on paranoia, involving fungal spores and manipulated thoughts.
input: Researching a mysterious fungal blight in a university greenhouse lab.
output: You peer through the microscope at the fungal spores, their threadlike tendrils twisting like tiny snakes. The air smells of rot and damp wool. You rub your eyes, feeling a faint tickle in your sinuses. When you look up, the lab notebook in front of you has a page you don’t remember writing: “They’re in your head. Leave now.” You flip back—all the previous entries are in your handwriting, but the words are slanted, desperate. You stand to open a window, but the latch is stuck. The spores drift through the air, settling on your arms. You start to forget why you’re here. Did you come to study the blight, or to escape it? A voice in your head whispers, “Stay. We need you.” You glance at the infected plants; their leaves are covered in black mold, and each one has a tiny, eye-like spot staring at you. You fumble for the door, but your legs feel heavy. The spores are crawling up your neck, and you can’t tell if the whispers are in your head or coming from the mold.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a greenhouse focused on paranoia, where the structure itself seems to trap and watch you.
input: Closing up a community greenhouse after a volunteer shift as dusk falls.
output: You lock the main door, slipping the key into your pocket. But when you turn to leave the vestibule, the door swings open again—unlocked. You lock it twice more, but each time, it creaks open a crack. You check the windows; all are latched, but their panes reflect a figure standing behind you. You spin around, but only empty shelves of seed packets greet you. The greenhouse heater kicks on, blowing hot air that smells like wet dirt and something metallic. You hear a floorboard creak in the back. You grab a trowel from the bench, creeping toward the sound. The rows of lettuce and kale seem to close in around you, their leaves brushing your arms. When you reach the back, you find your keys on the floor—you swear you put them in your pocket. You bend to pick them up, and a shadow falls over you. You look up, but the rafters are empty. The main door slams shut, locking itself. You pound on it, screaming, but the glass reflects only your terrified face, and the plants seem to lean in closer, as if waiting to see what happens next.

instruction: Describe a character's experience of becoming trapped in a subway car as it begins to flood.
input: Setting: a subway tunnel at 3am. Fear: loss_of_control.
output: The lights flicker and die as the train jolts to a stop, a low groan of metal echoing in the dark. Then you hear it: the hiss, then the gurgle. Cold, oily water seeps under the doors, rising with terrifying speed. You scramble onto a seat, but it's no use; the water is at your knees, your waist, a black tide smelling of rust and decay. Your phone light dies, swallowed by the absolute dark. The water presses in, numbing and heavy. You beat your fists against the emergency glass, but the tunnel is a sealed tomb. The water reaches your chin, your lips. A final, gasping breath fills your mouth with the taste of stagnant filth before the cold blackness closes over your head, and you feel your body go limp, carried by a current you cannot fight.

instruction: Describe a character's experience as the subway train's automated system malfunctions, ignoring all commands.
input: Setting: a subway tunnel at 3am. Fear: loss_of_control.
output: You're the only passenger. The train slows, then accelerates violently, throwing you against a pole. The intercom crackles. 'Next stop: Morgan Street.' But Morgan Street was demolished years ago. You lunge for the emergency brake. It depresses with a hollow click, doing nothing. The train screams through a curve, wheels shrieking. You hammer the driver's cab door, but it's locked. The digital display flickers: DESTINATION: UNKNOWN. SPEED: 87 MPH. The lights strobe, revealing glimpses of the tunnel wall rushing inches from the window. You're a prisoner in a metal projectile, hurtling deeper into the earth on a track to nowhere, every system ignoring your desperate, physical pleas for it to stop.

instruction: Describe a character's experience as they are pursued by something unseen that manipulates the tunnel environment.
input: Setting: a subway tunnel at 3am. Fear: loss_of_control.
output: You took a shortcut through the service tunnel. Now, you hear the wet scuttling behind you. You run, but the grimy floor suddenly softens, gripping your shoes like tar. The single overhead bulb ahead shatters. Darkness. You fumble along the wall, but the bricks are warm, pulsing. A ventilation grate slams shut ahead of you. You turn—the way back is now a solid concrete wall that wasn't there before. The scuttling is right behind you now. You press against the new wall, feeling it yield like flesh. Something brushes your ankle. You can't move. The tunnel itself is herding you, a living throat closing around its morsel.

instruction: Describe a character's experience as they witness other passengers being silently replaced by identical copies.
input: Setting: a subway tunnel at 3am. Fear: loss_of_control.
output: The train stalls. The woman across from you gets up to check the door. When she sits back down, her face is subtly wrong—the smile a millimeter too wide. You blink, and now the sleeping man by the window is awake, staring with her exact same smile. A chill grips you. You stand, but your legs feel heavy. You look down the car. Every passenger is now turned toward you, wearing that identical, placid expression. The doors won't open. You back away, but they stand in unison, moving with perfect, synchronized grace, closing in. You try to scream, but your jaw won't obey. You can only watch as your own hand rises, unprompted, to mimic their wave.

instruction: Describe a character's experience as the geometry of the subway tunnel becomes non-Euclidean and inescapable.
input: Setting: a subway tunnel at 3am. Fear: loss_of_control.
output: You step off the stalled train to find a station that shouldn't exist—just an endless platform stretching in both directions. You choose a direction and walk for what feels like hours. The tunnel curves, but you emerge onto the same platform, facing the train you left. You try the opposite direction. This time, the ceiling dips suddenly, forcing you to crawl. You emerge into a vast, dark cavern where tracks crisscross in impossible mid-air loops. A train whispers past on a vertical track. North, south, up, down—they've lost all meaning. You are a rat in a cosmic maze, every law of space revoked, and you know, with cold certainty, you will walk these impossible angles forever.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on a jumpscare in a parking garage.
input: You are a late-night janitor working the third shift at a downtown office parking garage, and you forgot your phone inside the lobby when you came down to check your car.
output: You fumble for your keys in the dim, flickering light of the third-level parking garage, your boots echoing off cold concrete walls. You’d run back inside 10 minutes prior to grab your forgotten work phone, now alone on the floor. A distant metal clank makes you freeze, your breath catching. You turn, squinting into the darkness, and see nothing at first—until a mannequin propped against a pillar suddenly lurches forward, its glass eyes wide, plastic arms outstretched. You scream, dropping your keys, as it’s just a leftover haunted house prop from last month’s festival, but its sudden, unanticipated movement makes you stumble, hitting your hip hard on a nearby car bumper. Your heart races as you bend to retrieve your keys, still reeling from the jump.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on a jumpscare in a parking garage.
input: You’re waiting for your roommate in a residential apartment parking garage, wearing noise-canceling headphones to tune out the distant vent hum, and it’s raining outside.
output: You lean against your sedan in the dim residential parking garage, rain tapping the roof, noise-canceling headphones blaring an indie playlist to drown out the space’s low hum. You check your phone: your roommate texted they’re 5 minutes late. A sudden loud thump makes you yank off the headphones abruptly. There’s no one there, but then a plastic bag caught on a car door handle flutters violently, as if grabbed from behind. You jump back, heart hammering, until you register the wind gusting through the garage’s open ramp. You grab your chest so hard you wince; the movement was so fast it felt like something unseen lunged straight for you, turning a mundane wait into a jolting shock.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on a jumpscare in a parking garage.
input: You’re loading groceries into your SUV at 9pm in a downtown basement parking garage, and the elevator is out of order so you took the stairs down alone.
output: You haul three heavy grocery bags to your SUV in the damp basement parking garage, the air smelling like old oil and mildew. The elevator’s broken, so you’d taken the stairs down alone, your arms strained. You drop the bags for a second to unlock your car, grunting as you reach for the handle. Suddenly, a sharp, high-pitched static blares from the cracked security camera above you, and a figure covered in a tattered tarp lurches from behind a parked van, yelling a prank scream. You stumble back, tripping over a concrete curb, and one bag splits, spilling apples across the floor. The part-time attendant laughs from the ramp above, but your heart’s still racing—you’d thought for a second it was something real.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on a jumpscare in a parking garage.
input: You’re searching for your toddler’s lost stuffed bear in the grocery store parking garage, after loading your car with snacks for the week.
output: You kneel on the cold concrete of the grocery store parking garage, searching between parked cars for your toddler’s stuffed bear—you’d realized you left it in the cart when loading bags. The sun’s almost set, casting long, twisted shadows across the lot. You reach under a sedan, and something soft and fuzzy brushes your hand unexpectedly. You yank back, screaming, as a matted stray cat hisses and bolts, its fur sticking to your jeans. You’re breathing hard, wiping your hand, when you spot the bear under the next car, its button eye glinting in the fading light. The cat’s sudden movement had been so close it felt like it had lunged right at your face, leaving you shaken for minutes.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration centered on a jumpscare in a parking garage.
input: You’re locking your car after a late-night concert in an arena parking garage, your hands still sticky with soda and your ears ringing from the music.
output: You fumble with your car keys after the late concert, locking your door in the dim arena parking garage, your hands still sticky with spilled soda. A group of fans laughs as they walk toward the exit down the same row. You turn to head to the ramp, and something cold and wet brushes your neck sharply. You spin, heart pounding, expecting a hand or a stranger—instead, it’s a tinsel-covered Christmas decoration hanging from a light fixture, swung by a gust of wind. You gasp, clutching your chest, as the fans ahead glance back confused. The tinsel stuck in your hair feels ridiculous, but your adrenaline’s still spiking; the sudden, unexpected contact had felt like a threat, pure and simple.

instruction: Generate a unique geometric horror entity entry set in a run-down 24-hour urban laundromat, aligned with SCP/Silent Hill cosmic body horror lore
input: Entity 1: The Hem Folding Triad
output: Appearance: A cluster of 3 interlocking right-triangular linen scraps, each no thicker than a human hair, edges impossibly sharp enough to slice through lint without resistance, floating 6 inches above the floor between dryer units. Behavior: It drifts along the base of dryer rows, systematically folding loose clothing hems into perfect 90-degree creases at a rate of one fold every 17 seconds, never deviating from its linear patrol path. Origin: Spawned in 2018 when a grieving widow washed her late firefighter husband’s work uniform 13 consecutive times in a broken dryer that repeatedly folded the collar at a wrong, harsh angle. Horror: It does not lash out, but will begin folding the exposed edge of skin at a person’s shirt line if they stand within 2 feet of it, leaving no pain until the victim notices their forearm has been tessellated into sharp, even folded segments. Encounter: A night attendant first documented it when it left a 1mm deep, perfectly straight crease mark on their wrist as they leaned over to empty a lint trap.

instruction: Generate a unique geometric horror entity entry set in a run-down 24-hour urban laundromat, aligned with SCP/Silent Hill cosmic body horror lore
input: Entity 2: The Grout Tessellator
output: Appearance: A palm-sized rhombic dodecahedron forged from cracked gray laundromat floor grout, each face bearing a unique uneven texture, shifting in position only when the observer blinks, never casting a shadow. Behavior: It rolls exclusively along the grout lines between square floor tiles, filling in any cracks or gaps with glossy black resin that spreads outward in flawless hexagonal patterns across the tile surface. Origin: Spawned in 2021 when the laundromat owner cut corners retiling flood damage, installing 13 misaligned tiles that left mathematically impossible gaps between the grout lines. Horror: If it makes contact with a person’s shoe sole, it will begin re-tessellating the bones of their feet into interlocking hexagonal fragments, causing no pain but rendering the victim completely unable to step outside the existing grout lines. Encounter: A teen doing laundry after school first encountered it when it sealed a puddle of spilled detergent into a perfect hexagonal plate, and they realized their feet were glued to the grout when they tried to run away.

instruction: Generate a unique geometric horror entity entry set in a run-down 24-hour urban laundromat, aligned with SCP/Silent Hill cosmic body horror lore
input: Entity 3: The Static Load Tetrahedron
output: Appearance: A floating 4-sided solid constructed from compacted dryer lint, crackling pale blue static fizzing along its razor-sharp edges, each face bearing a different density of lint, only visible in low laundromat light. Behavior: It hides inside running dryers, manifesting exclusively when a load of laundry has an odd number of paired socks, tucking itself between folded clothing items until the dryer door opens. Origin: Spawned after 12 years of unclaimed missing sock pairs and accumulated vent lint coalescing into a stable geometric form, fed by the static electricity of hundreds of dryer cycles. Horror: If a person reaches into the dryer while it is present, it will press one sharp edge to their palm, erasing one core memory for each of its four corners, each lost memory folding into a new missing sock the victim will never recover. Encounter: A regular patron first documented it when they pulled their laundry out of a dryer, found 17 perfectly folded mismatched socks on top of the load, and realized they could no longer remember their own home address.

instruction: Write a psychological horror story set in a public library after hours
input: Fear type: psychological. Narrate in second person, 100-250 words
output: You’re restocking returned novels when you freeze. Every book in the cart has your full legal name scrawled on the last page. Same blue ballpoint, same shaky left-handed slant you use when you’re tired. You didn’t touch these. No one else was here. You yank out the book you returned this morning—same handwriting, same name, dated three hours before you even arrived for your shift. The overhead lights hum. When you glance up, every study carrel has someone sitting perfectly still, facing away. None of them move. You don’t remember locking the front door. You don’t remember being hired here.

instruction: Write a psychological horror story set in a public library after hours
input: Fear type: psychological. Narrate in second person, 100-250 words
output: You snuck in after closing to finish your thesis, certain no one would find you. An hour in, you hear pages turning, steady, one every twelve seconds. You walk the stacks. No one is there. The turning doesn’t stop. You count: twelve seconds exactly, every time. You hold your breath, count along. On the seventieth turn, it skips. It waits. You breathe out. It turns the page exactly when your exhale ends. Now it matches every breath you take. You stop breathing. The page doesn’t turn. You don’t dare breathe again.

instruction: Write a psychological horror story set in a public library after hours
input: Fear type: psychological. Narrate in second person, 100-250 words
output: You pull a card from the old catalog for a book on local missing persons. The back has your childhood home address, your first phone number, the scar on your left knee. Each subsequent card you yank out is just details about you. Not birth records. Not police files. The things no one ever knew. The time you lied about your dog dying to skip school. The name you whispered to the dark when you were seven. There are 14,297 cards in this drawer. You realize you are reading the catalog entry for yourself. No one wrote these. They just were. And the due date is tonight.

instruction: Write a psychological horror story set in a public library after hours
input: Fear type: psychological. Narrate in second person, 100-250 words
output: You’re mopping the main hall when you glance at the ground floor window. Your reflection is still standing at the service desk, ten feet behind you. It doesn’t move when you turn. It just watches you, same slouched posture, same coffee stain on your uniform sleeve. You walk slowly back. It doesn’t blink. You reach out. Your hand passes right through where it should be. When you look down at your own hands, they are transparent. The reflection at the desk lifts your mop. It starts mopping. No one will notice it isn’t you tomorrow.

instruction: Write a psychological horror story set in a public library after hours
input: Fear type: psychological. Narrate in second person, 100-250 words
output: You head for the exit when you hear footsteps behind you. You stop. They stop. You walk fast. They walk fast. You spin. Empty stacks. You take one step backward. Somewhere down the aisle, one step echoes back. You step left. Left echo. Step right. Right echo. You don’t run. You know what happens when you run. You test it: you hum a quiet note. The exact same note hums back, delayed half a heartbeat. It isn’t copying you. It’s remembering every movement you will make, before you make it. You can’t leave. It already knows every path you could take.

instruction: Create a second-person psychological horror narration set in a subway tunnel at 3am, unique scenario, 100-250 words
input: Setting: Subway tunnel at 3am, psychological horror theme
output: You press your palm to the cold tunnel wall, breath fogging stale air. The last train left 20 minutes ago, your phone dead. A faint whisper wraps your skull—exactly your self-talk tone, but it says things you hide: I know you lied about the traffic, I know you regret coming here. You blink, but the whisper won’t fade. The tracks glint, and you see your reflection walking ahead, hunched, muttering the same lines back. Your hands shake; patting your pocket, the lighter you had is gone, replaced by a scrap with your full name scrawled in your handwriting—yours, but sharpened, as if someone copied you for years.

instruction: Create a second-person psychological horror narration set in a subway tunnel at 3am, unique scenario, 100-250 words
input: Setting: Subway tunnel at 3am, psychological horror theme
output: Your shoes clack the concrete platform, lit only by a dim emergency bulb. The tunnel hums, and you can’t shake the feeling you’ve been here before. You check your watch: 2:59am. A second later, it flips to 3:00am—again. You turn: the train you exited waits, doors open, empty. But the seats hold your coat, backpack, and dead phone placed exactly how you left them an hour prior. When you step close, your phone lights up, showing a photo you never took: you here, holding a camera you don’t remember. You realize you can’t recall why you came, like two hours of your life were erased.

instruction: Create a second-person psychological horror narration set in a subway tunnel at 3am, unique scenario, 100-250 words
input: Setting: Subway tunnel at 3am, psychological horror theme
output: You huddle against the tunnel wall, teeth chattering, when you hear your sister’s voice calling your name. She died two months ago. You scan the dark, and there she is—same frayed hoodie, same limp in her left leg—halfway down the tracks. You run toward her, but she doesn’t move. When you reach her, she turns, and her face is yours. Her voice is your voice: You left me. You forgot me. You freeze, because you didn’t forget—you grieved. The closer you stand, the more her hazel eyes match yours, even the tiny gold fleck in the right. You stop; you don’t know if either of you is real.

instruction: Create a second-person psychological horror narration set in a subway tunnel at 3am, unique scenario, 100-250 words
input: Setting: Subway tunnel at 3am, psychological horror theme
output: The tunnel is quiet except for your ragged breathing, until you hear your mother’s laugh—warm, familiar, exactly how it sounded when you were seven and sick. She died then, but here it is, clear. You wave your dead phone’s flashlight around, and the beam catches a shadow behind a pillar: your seven-year-old self, scraped knees, holding a teddy bear you left at home years ago. Your little self says, You promised you’d come back for me. You feel a hollow ache you buried since that day, when you ran away and pushed that memory away forever.

instruction: Create a second-person psychological horror narration set in a subway tunnel at 3am, unique scenario, 100-250 words
input: Setting: Subway tunnel at 3am, psychological horror theme
output: You trudge the tunnel, your phone’s last bar clinging to a map, when steps fall behind you—exactly the tempo of your boots, like copied from your gait. You spin: empty, only a faint glow. You keep moving, and steps quicken with your panic. When you whip around again, there’s someone: same height, same faded jacket, same scuffed sneakers as you. Their face is a blur, no features. They lift a hand holding your work ID—the one you hid two weeks ago when you planned to quit and disappear. They say, I’m the part of you that knows you’re running, that you can’t outrun yourself. You freeze, because you never admitted how scared you are of change.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a parking garage, centered on the core fear of overwhelming, consuming darkness.
input: You leave your 11pm retail shift, walking to your 3rd level parking spot immediately after the garage’s main power trips.
output: You fumble for your phone flashlight, but it dies the second you tap the screen. The low hum of the ventilation system cuts out entirely, leaving only the echo of your own breath bouncing off concrete pillars. You swore the exit was 20 feet ahead of you 10 seconds ago, but now there’s no visual cue, no faint glow of exit signage, just thick, cloying dark that tastes like rust when you part your lips to call for help. A soft scraping sound drags across the pillar to your left, too slow, too deliberate to be a rat. You pat your pocket for your car keys, only to find the fabric empty, and the darkness presses in tight enough that you can feel it resting against your cheek, warm and alive.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a parking garage, centered on the core fear of overwhelming, consuming darkness.
input: You return to a downtown parking garage 20 minutes after locking your car to retrieve the work laptop you left on the passenger seat.
output: You hit your key fob’s unlock button out of habit, but there’s no flash of taillights, no beep, only silence. The overhead LED lights start popping one by one in a straight row, swallowing the entire B2 level in inky, unbroken dark. You take one tentative step forward, and your shoe connects with something soft that lets out a quiet, guttural whimper. You freeze, because you came to the garage alone. The darkness doesn’t just hide things, it breathes, warm and damp against the back of your neck, and you suddenly can no longer remember what direction you entered from. The glowing green exit signs you swore were lit a minute ago are gone, no hum, no faint luminescence, just endless, hungry void.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a parking garage, centered on the core fear of overwhelming, consuming darkness.
input: You stop to help a disoriented elderly stranger in the B2 parking garage who says they can’t find their vehicle, right as a rolling blackout hits the entire structure.
output: The stranger vanishes the exact second the last overhead light fades out. You call out their name, the one they gave you 30 seconds prior, but there’s no reply, only a distant, slow drag of heavy tires on concrete that don’t sound like they’re moving closer or farther away. You pat your pockets for your phone to call security, only to find it’s gone, slipped out of your pocket when you leaned in to talk to the stranger. The darkness wraps around your wrists like cold, clammy fingers, pulling you slow toward the ramp you can’t see. You hear your car alarm blare somewhere far off, but it doesn’t sound like beeping, it sounds like screaming, and the dark thickens until you can’t lift your arms to push it away.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a parking garage, centered on the core fear of overwhelming, consuming darkness.
input: You walk to your car in the hospital parking garage at 2am after sitting through a 6 hour emergency surgery for your younger sibling.
output: You’re exhausted, your eyes burning from staring at sterile hospital walls for hours, so when the lights cut out you assume it’s a routine flicker, until 10 seconds pass and no power returns. You fumble for your phone, the flashlight flickers on for half a second, just long enough to show you a dozen shadowy figures standing 3 feet away from you, all perfectly still, before the light dies completely. The darkness presses into your eye sockets, making black spots dance across your vision, and you can hear all of them breathing in unison, matching the thud of your hammering heartbeat. You can’t move, can’t speak, and the dark keeps getting heavier, like it’s slowly lowering you down into the cold concrete under your sneakers.

instruction: Write a second-person horror narration set in a parking garage, centered on the core fear of overwhelming, consuming darkness.
input: You park on the desolate B3 level of a concert parking garage, lingering to send a text to your friend before the show starts when all lights abruptly fail.
output: You hit the lock button on your key fob out of muscle memory, and the soft click echoes so loud it makes your ears ring. You can hear small, scuttling sounds all around you, coming from every direction, too fast, too coordinated to be rats. The darkness doesn’t just obscure, it distorts: you hold your hand up to your face, and you can’t see it even an inch from your nose, no silhouette, no faint outline at all. You remember there was a security camera mounted above your parking spot, but there’s no little red recording glow, no sign anyone is watching. Something soft as long hair brushes your cheek, and you realize the darkness is no longer just absence of light, it’s a living presence, crawling into your open mouth when you gasp.

instruction: Write a second-person horror story about isolation set in a laundromat
input: Setting: 24hr laundromat, 2:17am, last patron left 40 minutes prior. Fear: isolation
output: You fold the last sock when every dryer thuds dead at once. No hum, no overhead glow—only the exit sign’s red bleed. You yank the front door. It doesn’t rattle, it doesn’t resist: it’s welded solid. No crack under it, no draft. You pound the glass. No cars pass. No streetlights glow beyond the lot. You check your phone. No signal, no timestamp, just a black screen that reflects only you. No one is coming. No one even knows you’re here. The dryers start spinning again, slow, empty.

instruction: Craft a second-person isolation horror scene set inside a laundromat
input: Setting: suburban laundromat, rainy Tuesday afternoon, you came in to wash bed linens after a breakup. Fear: isolation
output: You’ve been staring at the same dryer cycle for twelve minutes when you notice someone leaning against the back wall. You wave. They don’t move. You walk over. It’s just a crumpled hoodie draped over a cart. This happens three more times. When you finally reach for your basket, you realize the rain stopped. No cars are parked outside. No birds. The street beyond the windows is just blank grey. You scream. The sound dies three feet from your mouth. No one will hear it. There is no one left to hear anything.

instruction: Generate a second-person horror narration about isolation in a laundromat
input: Setting: all-night laundromat, 3:41am, you missed the last bus. Fear: isolation
output: You count the washers to pass time: seventeen. Ten minutes later, you count again. Eighteen. You walk the row. No one added one. You try the back exit. It opens into the same laundromat, same row of washers, same half-empty soda can on the counter you set down an hour ago. You text your roommate. It sends. No reply. You call. It rings forever. No voicemail picks up. The machines hum louder. They are not washing clothes. They are waiting for you to stop trying to leave. There is nowhere else to go now.

instruction: Write a tight second-person isolation horror scene set in a laundromat
input: Setting: small town laundromat, you arrived at 11pm to wash work uniforms. Fear: isolation
output: You pull your jeans from the dryer, fold them, then turn to find the exact same pair tumbling again. This repeats seven times. The clock above the door hasn’t moved from 11:27. You bang on the window. Every street light for three blocks goes out one by one, like someone snuffing candles. You don’t remember what you did before you came here. You don’t remember anyone’s name. Only the hum of the machines. Only the quiet, endless dark outside that will never, ever bring another person through that door.

instruction: Create a second-person horror story focused on isolation set at a laundromat
input: Setting: roadside laundromat, middle of winter, you stopped during a cross country drive. Fear: isolation
output: The payphone on the wall rings. No one else is here. You pick up. It’s just your own breathing, one second delayed. You hang up. It rings again. Same thing. Your car keys are gone. Your wallet is gone. All the clothes you brought are gone. The snow outside piles so high it blots out the sky. You sit on the cold tile. No one will drive past. No one is looking for you. The dryers hum. The phone rings. You will be here forever, just you, and the quiet, endless waiting.

instruction: Generate a shadow entity tied to unresolved library debts in an after-hours public library, blending SCP cosmic dread and Silent Hill psychological unease
input: Setting: public library after hours; Entity type: shadow; Constraint: unique origin tied to unclaimed overdue fines; Tone: SCP/Silent Hill
output: Appearance: A formless shadow that coalesces from smudges on the spines of books with 10+ years overdue, its tendrils made of faded overdue notice ink that glistens like fresh blood. Behavior: Drifts between the basement stacks, pausing only at books with handwritten fine slips tucked in pages; it emits a low, rustling hum like turning brittle paper. Origin: A 1927 head librarian who embezzled $2,000 in fine funds to pay for her sick sister’s treatment; when the fraud was exposed, she hung herself in the stacks, cursing herself and the books that “owed” her. Horror: Contact with its tendrils blooms permanent “FINES DUE” stamps on skin, each stamp multiplying until the victim is compelled to steal non-existent books and “return” them until they starve. Encounter: A teen hiding after a study session feels a cold tingle on his wrist; glancing down, he sees a stamp for $12,742, and hears the shadow’s hum growing louder from the basement.

instruction: Generate a shadow entity born from unremembered book marginalia in an after-hours public library, leaning into Junji Ito-style body horror and Lovecraftian memory erosion
input: Setting: public library after hours; Entity type: shadow; Constraint: unique origin tied to forgotten author marginalia; Tone: Junji Ito/Lovecraft
output: Appearance: A lanky, humanoid shadow whose torso gapes open like a yawn, its inner cavity a swirling void of faded pencil marks and half-erased notes; its “eyes” are smudges of ink that drip onto the floor, leaving tiny, pulsing dots. Behavior: Lurks exclusively near the “Lost and Found Books” shelf, where misfiled or abandoned volumes collect; it stretches toward anyone who pauses there, as if trying to whisper. Origin: A 1952 philosophy professor who wrote 300 pages of marginalia in a rare Husserl manuscript, only to have the book misfiled the day he died; his fragmented consciousness fused with the library’s shadows, feeding on unremembered book memories. Horror: If it touches a victim, their memories of beloved books dissolve first—they forget the plot of their childhood favorite, the sound of their parent reading bedtime stories—then their own identity fades into a walking shadow. Encounter: A retired librarian visits to find a lost poetry book; she pauses at the shelf, feels a cold breath on her neck, and suddenly can’t recall why she ever loved reading.

instruction: Generate a shadow entity from censored banned book passages in an after-hours public library, channeling Silent Hill’s psychological dread and SCP’s contained horror
input: Setting: public library after hours; Entity type: shadow; Constraint: unique origin tied to redacted banned book content; Tone: Silent Hill/SCP
output: Appearance: A patchwork shadow made of missing text—holes where words were redacted from banned books, its edges frayed like torn paper; its “fingers” are thick, black lines of redacted ink that leave burn marks on wood. Behavior: Patrols the library’s hidden “Banned Books Archive” in the attic, dragging its fingers across the floor; it emits a high-pitched whine like a printer jammed with forbidden pages. Origin: A 1978 school board that publicly burned a LGBTQ+ memoir, *Half-Light*, after banning it; the author’s grief and rage coalesced into the shadow, which seeks to “un-redact” the truth but destroys anyone who stands between it and the book’s remaining copies. Horror: Contact with its fingers leaves redacted patches on skin—areas where private memories (like coming out, first love) are erased, leaving only blank, numb skin. Encounter: A grad student researching banned memoirs sneaks into the attic; she touches a surviving copy of *Half-Light*, and the shadow lunges, leaving a redacted patch on her forearm where her memory of coming out to her mom once lived.
